Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                     2
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

                                  EXCUTIVE SUMMARY
Project Background
The Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province was approved for
investment policy by the Thanh Hoa Provincial People’s Council under Resolution No.
460/NQ-HĐND dated December 14, 2023, with the objective of building and upgrading
infrastructure and reorganizing production in order to develop the fisheries sector of Thanh Hoa
Province towards enhanced value, international integration, and climate change adaptation. The
project consists of 02 components, including:
- Component 1: Investment in developing infrastructure for seafood exploitation and
aquaculture, including the following 9 subprojects: Subproject 1 - Upgrading and expanding
Hoang Truong fishing port; Subproject 2 - Upgrading and expanding fishing port combined
with storm shelter for fishing boats in Lach Truong commune, Hoa Loc district; Subproject 3 -
Upgrading infrastructure of aquaculture areas in Nga Thuy, Nga Tan, Nga Tien communes,
Nga Son district; Subproject 4 - Upgrading infrastructure of aquaculture areas in Hoang Yen
commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject 5 - Upgrading infrastructure of aquaculture areas in
Hoang Phong commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject 6 - Upgrading infrastructure of
aquaculture areas in Hoang Luu commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject 7 - Upgrading
infrastructure of aquaculture areas in Hoang Chau commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject 8
- Upgrading infrastructure of aquaculture areas in Da Loc commune, Hau Loc district;
Subproject 9 - Upgrading infrastructure of the aquaculture area of Quang Trung commune,
Quang Xuong district
Investment in developing fisheries and aquaculture infrastructure, including 09 subprojects: (1)
Subproject 1 - Upgrading and expansion of Hoang Truong fishing port subproject; Subproject
2-Upgrading and expansion of Lach Truong fishing port and storm shelter anchorage in Hoa
Loc commune, Hau Loc district; Subproject 3- Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Nga
Thuy, Nga Tan, and Nga Tien communes, Nga Son district; Subproject 4- Upgrading
aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Yen commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject 5-
Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Phong commune, Hoang Hoa district;
Subproject 6 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Luu commune, Hoang Hoa
district; Subproject 7 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Chau commune, Hoang
Hoa district; Subproject 8 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Da Loc commune, Hau Loc
district; Subproject 9 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Quang Trung commune, Quang
Xuong district.
- Component 2: Project management.
Legal and technical basis for ESIA
The environmental assessment conducted during project preparation confirmed that the
project’s environmental risk is classified as Medium. The applicable Environmental and Social
Standards (ESS) for the project are as follows: ESS1: Assessment and Management of
Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts; ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and
Information Disclosure; ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions; ESS3: Resource Efficiency and
Pollution Prevention and Management; ESS4: Community Health and Safety; ESS5: Land
Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use, and Involuntary Resettlement; ESS6: Biodiversity
Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources; ESS8: Cultural
Heritage; This Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA, this document) is prepared
in accordance with the Environmental and Social Framework (ESF). The ESIA covers the
proposed works under Component 1 of the project.
Project description



Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            3
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

The scope of construction investment and upgrading of project items is summarized in the table
below:

   Subproject                                      Investment scope

 Subproject 1: Port area:
 Upgrading and
               Upgrading and constructing port facilities including:
 expanding
 Hoang Truong    New docking berth: length 330 m, width 10 m, deck elevation +2.35 m, bottom
 fishing port  elevation from -2.9 m to -4.0.
                   Substructure embankment: 330 m long.
                   Berth yard embankment: 520 m
                   Wharf yard: Reinforce the wharf yard surface with an approximate length of L =
                  800.0 m and a width of about 35.0 m.
                   Wharf canopy system: total length 210 m, width 10.0 m; total construction area
                  2,100 m².
                   Water collection ditch: Construct a new water collection ditch system, length
                  about L = 330.0m.
                    Dredging the water area in front of the port: Length of about L=800.0m, width of
                  2-20m, dredged bottom elevation from -2.9m to -4.0m. Estimated dredging volume
                  is 7,266.44m3.
                  - Channel layout: width is 40m. width of 40 m; channel curvature radius 150 m;
                  turning basins diameter for fishing vessel areas: <12 m, D=40 m; for fishing vessel
                  <17 m vessels D=55 m; for fishing vessel <24 m, D=75 m.
                  Logistics facilities: construct the following new projects
                   New administration building: 1 building with 3 storeys, total floor area of 300
                  m².
                   Parking building in administration area: 1 storey building, area of 60 m².
                   Port entrance gate: 03.
                   Fence surrounding the fishing port area: Length approximately 180.0 m.
                   2 toilets with area of 60 m² each.
                  Technical infrastructure works:
                     Construction of a clean water supply system, stormwater drainage, fire
                  protection and fighting, electricity supply, lighting systems, and greenery along
                  transport route.
                   Construction of 1 WWTP with a capacity of 200 m3/day; AO technology; treated
                  wastewater quality: Column B, QCVN 40:2011/BTNMT (Kq=0.9, Kf=1.1);
                  Wastewater sources: (i) Production wastewater: generated from fish sorting
                  activities, wharf washing, and ship-hold cleaning; Domestic wastewater: from
                  activities of local residents the the port after preliminary treated via septic tanks.

Environmental and social conditions
In order to assess the quality of the baseline environment in the subproject area, the Project
Owner has coordinated with the environmental monitoring unit, KGZ Investment Joint Stock
Company (with VIMCERTS 320 certificate) to conduct surveys, measurements, and sampling
for environmental analysis (air, surface water, groundwater, wastewater, soil and sediment) at
each sub-project. Environmental sampling was carried out on October 23, 24 and 30, 2024.
Information on the quality results of environmental components in the project area is as follows:
Current status of air quality and noise
The results of ambient air quality monitoring in the subproject area show that all indicators are
within the permissible limits of QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on
Ambient Air Quality (1-hour average). Noise levels in the project area are also within the
allowable limits according to QCVN 26:2010/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on
Nois.
This result shows that the ambient air quality in the project areas is still relatively good, with
no signs of pollution.
Current status of groundwater quality
The analysis results indicate that the quality of groundwater in the areas of subprojects 1, 2, 3,
5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 is relatively good, with no signs of pollution. At the sampling positions, most
analyzed parameters are within the permissible limits of QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT – National
Technical Regulation on Groundwater Quality. However, at sample NN4 in the area of
subproject 4, the Ammonium (NH₄⁺) parameter exceeds the standard by 2.44 times compared
to QCVN 09:2023/BTNMT, indicating localized groundwater pollution the subproject 4.
Current status of surface water quality
According to the surface water quality monitoring results, most parameters at all monitoring
points are below the permissible limits set by QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT – National Technical
Regulation on Surface Water Quality. However, certain parameters such as COD, BOD₅,
Ammonium, and Surfactants exceed the allowable limits under QCVN 08:2023/BTNMT.
Wastewater characteristics
The measurement results of wastewater parameters in the project area (from existing ports
subproject 1 and subproject 2, and aquaculture area) indicate that most industrial wastewater
parameters are within the permissible limits under QCVN 40:2011/BTNMT, Column B.
However, sample NT9 (wastewater sample at subproject 9) shows that the Total Suspended
Solids (TSS) parameter exceeds the standard by 1.52 times.
Current status of soil quality
The analysis results of soil environmental quality show that heavy metal parameters are all
within the permissible limits according to QCVN 03:2023/BTNMT (Type 2). During project
construction, the topsoil layer will be removed, and depending on the soil characteristics, a
decision will be made on whether to reuse or dispose of it. Therefore, the excavated soil from
construction areas may be reused for various purposes such as site leveling, or may be disposed
of as general waste.
Current status of sediments


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              8
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

The analysis results show that heavy metal concentrations in sediment within the project area
are significantly lower than the permissible limits specified in QCVN 43:2017/BTNMT –
National Technical Regulation on Sediment Quality. Therefore, the dredged sediment from the
subproject component is considered non-hazardous and not contaminated by heavy metals.
The construction areas of subprojects 1 and 2 are mainly located within existing fishing ports
and storm shelter anchorages. The remaining subprojects, from subproject 3 to subproject 9,
are all implemented along existing canals and ditches within the local aquaculture areas.
Sensitive receptors for each subproject are presented in Section 2.6, Chapter 2. No tangible
cultural heritage resources are affected by the project.
Alternative analysis
The report has compared the scenarios of “with project” and “without project,” as well as the
technical design options for each subproject, including auxiliary components such as dredging
methods and material storage locations.
Analysis of Dredging Method Selection: The selection of the dredging method was based on
the following conditions: (i) Dredging activities will be carried out in currently active
navigation canals; (ii) These canals are currently narrow and shallow, requiring careful
guidance during operations; (iii) The distance to designated dredged material disposal sites must
be taken into consideration. To ensure safe operating conditions for both dredging vessels and
commercial traffic, the dredging method must allow for efficient fleet organization and
maximum productivity. These factors have guided the technical analysis and gave the final
decision on the dredging approach.
Environmental and Social Impacts and Risks
The ESIA has identified potential negative impacts and risks of the subproject. Most of the
impacts are temporary, localized and reversible because the project components are in small to
medium scale. These impacts can be mitigated by applying appropriate technology and specific
mitigation measures, and the contractor must closely monitor and consult with local residents.
   a. In the preparation phase:
Impact of land acquisition: The total area of land permanently acquired for the construction of
project components is approximately 201,881 m2, affecting 269 households. Among these: 262
households are impacted by land acquisition of less than 30% (accounting for 78% of affected
households), the remaining affected from 30% to 70% (accounting for 22%). Additionally, 11
organizations are affected, primarily communal People’s Committees, now reduced to 07 due
to administrative commune mergers. Importantly, no households or organizations are subject
to relocation or resettlement within the project implementation area.
Safety risks associated with unexploded ordnance: The subprojects will be upgraded and
expanded within fishing ports and aquaculture zones, including mine and explosive ordnance
clearance activities on both land and water surfaces in areas such as Hoang Truong and Lach
Truong fishing ports, as well as existing canals. The total area designated for mine and
explosive ordnance clearance is 547,700 m² (54.77 ha), comprising 47.39 ha of land-based
clearance and 7.38 ha of underwater clearance.
   b. During construction phase:
         ✓ General impacts and risks
Construction activities and daily worker routines generate dust, emissions, noise, vibration,
wastewater, and solid waste. These impacts are considered low to moderate for each individual
component and can be mitigated. Overall, construction is expected to cause the following
environmental and social impacts: Air pollution due to dust and exhaust emissions; Noise and
vibration impacts; Wastewater generation; Solid waste generation; Decline in water quality;

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             9
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

Impact on aquatic ecosystems; Local erosion, sedimentation and flooding; Increased safety
risks and traffic disruption (water, road); Risk of damage to existing infrastructure due to
transportation of construction materials and machinery; Impact on workers’ health and safety;
Safety risks for the community; Social impacts due to labor influx; Impact on cultural resources
(pagodas, temple, churches, etc.).
         ✓ Specific impacts during the construction phase
Specific impacts during the construction phase at each subproject area are as follows:
   − Subproject 1: Disruption to the operation of the existing Hoang Truong fishing port;
     Impact on the water quality of Lach Truong river due to dredging activities; Impact on
     the operation of ship repair facilities within the existing port; Impact by the disposal site
     of dredged materials; Impact on sensitive receptors (temporary disruption of the
     operation of the existing shipbuilding workshop).
   − Subproject 2: Disruption to the operation of the existing Lach Truong fishing port;
     Affect the operation of some nearby seafood processing facilities; Disrupt the operation
     of the existing Lach Truong fishing port central market; Affect aquaculture activities on
     Lach Truong river; Impact by the disposal site of dredged materials; Temporarily impact
     on sensitive receptors (Churches, markets, temples/pagodas).
   − Subproject 3: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Sensitive receptors are temporarily
     affected (markets, schools).
   − Subproject 4: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site; Sensitive receptors are temporarily affected (pagodas,
     aquaculture areas).
   − Subproject 5: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site.
   − Subproject 6: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site; Sensitive receptors are temporarily affected (schools).
   − Subproject 7: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site; Sensitive receptors are temporarily affected
     (aquaculture area).
   − Subproject 8: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site; Sensitive receptors are temporarily affected
     (aquaculture area).
   − Subproject 9: Temporarily disruption to water intake and drainage serving aquaculture;
     Temporarily impacts on local people’s livelihoods; Impact due to disposal at the
     dredging material dumping site.
   c. During the operating phase:
For subproject 1 and 2:
The activities of vessels entering and leaving fishing ports/storm shelters will cause the
following impacts and risks: Air pollution due to emissions from vessels; Deterioration of
surface water quality due to increased oil and grease concentrations from ships; Impact on

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             10
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

aquatic ecosystems in the area.
Periodic dredging activities to maintain navigation channels/storm shelters will cause the
following impacts and risks: Decline in air quality due to the operation of dredging vehicles
and machinery; Decline in surface water quality due to dredging activities (increased turbidity,
TSS, grease, etc.); Impact on aquatic ecosystems in the area; Increased risk of saltwater
intrusion.
For Subproject 3,4,5,6,7,8,9:
Impacts and risks during the operation phase of canal/ditch works include: Subsidence and
landslides of canals after construction; Cracks on the surface of the management and operation
road due to overloading.
   d. Cumulative impacts:
Construction phase: (i) Increased pollution (dust, noise, construction wastewater): Total
emissions of dust, gas, noise, and construction wastewater may significantly increase across the
entire project scale, especially if dredging, material transport, and construction activities occur
simultaneously in multiple coastal areas, affecting public health and surface water quality in
the area. (ii) Decreased water and soil quality due to excavation and embankment, affecting the
ecosystem of rivers, seas, canals, and aquaculture areas; (iii) Increased risk of landslides,
collapses, and erosion, especially during construction during the rainy season; (iv) Disruption
of transportation—especially waterway traffic—as well as daily life and production of local
residents, and impacts on aquaculture activities due to production interruptions if construction
occurs near active farming areas, (v) Increased pressure on the supply chain for materials,
equipment, and technical labor, potentially affecting implementation progress and efficiency.
Operational phase: (i) Conflicts in the use of coastal resources between sectors such as fisheries,
tourism, industry and ecological conservation. (ii) Pressure on social infrastructure, increased
long-term operation and maintenance costs for systems such as transportation, water supply,
fishermen’s livelihoods, and waste management as infrastructure ages or is not properly
maintained, repair costs may rise significantly over time. (iii) In the context of climate change,
coastal subprojects will face increasing risks such as sea level rise, saline intrusion, and more
frequent severe storms, affecting storm shelter effectiveness, domestic water sources,
aquaculture areas, and fisheries activities. (iv) Increased environmental pressure from
expanding farming areas and long-term intensification of production may lead to water
pollution and biodiversity loss if not well managed. (v) Increased risk of aquatic disease
outbreaks due to high stocking density and movement between farming areas, which may
spread pathogens if not properly controlled. (vi) Land and water resource use conflicts may
arise due to competing demands among aquaculture, agriculture, domestic use, and
conservation. (vii) Dependence on a single production model, if farming areas rely on only one
or a few species or a specific market, may increase risks in the event of market fluctuations or
disease outbreaks.
Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP)
The Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) outlines detailed implementation,
monitoring, supervision and reporting procedures to ensure effective management of these
impacts. The plan also includes a capacity building program to enhance the skills and
knowledge of stakeholders, as well as a comprehensive cost estimate for all proposed measures.
Key components of the ESMP include: measures to mitigate environmental and social risks and
impacts; implementation agreements; project compliance framework; environmental
monitoring program; capacity building activities; cost estimates; and grievance redress
mechanism.
Mitigation measures

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              11
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

The project’s environmental and social (E&S) mitigation measures are designed to address risks
across all project phases – feasibility study, detailed design, site preparation, construction and
operation. These measures are developed and implemented according to the World Bank’s
mitigation hierarchy, which prioritizes measures to prevent, minimize, mitigate and compensate
or offset negative impacts.
Proposed mitigation measures for FS and detailed design
Mitigation measures for the detailed design phase of all subprojects are presented in Section
5.1.1. In summary, during the detailed design phase, mitigation measures will be implemented
to address environmental and social risks associated with dredging, fisheries logistics and port
infrastructure, as well as wastewater and solid waste management.
For dredging activities, stakeholder consultation will guide the selection of appropriate
locations, timing, and techniques, while also supporting the development of waterway safety
signage and channel routing plans to minimize disruptions to local communities and aquatic
livelihoods.
For port infrastructure, comprehensive geological and hydrological surveys will provide inputs
for resilient designs, taking into account sediment dynamics, sustainable materials, and
revetment techniques to counter subsidence and tidal surge. Port facilities will incorporate
wastewater collection, drainage systems, and green space, while minimizing vegetation
clearance and promoting energy-efficient building design.
Wastewater and solid waste treatment plants will be located away from vulnerable areas,
designed to operate efficiently and in compliance with national standards, and equipped with
appropriate treatment technologies and odor control measures. Qualified personnel will be
responsible for management of activities, with reinforced pits and robust emergency response
plans to address potential risks at fishing ports.
Mitigation measures in the preparation phase
Section 5.1.2 specifies the mitigation measures that need to be implemented during the
preparation phase.
To address the impacts due to land acquisition, during the preparation phase, the project
developed a Resettlement Plan (RP) outlining compensation, assistance and livelihood
restoration measures for project-affected people (PAP), based on the principles of full
replacement cost and comprehensive consultation. The RP also includes a grievance redress
mechanism (GRM), preliminary cost estimates, ensuring that all mitigation actions are
consistent with the ESS principles of the World Bank and the Government of Vietnam.
To address the risks from unexploded ordnance (UXO), the project will conduct UXO clearance
prior to ground leveling or dredging at six project sites. Unexploded ordnance clearance will be
carried out by authorized agencies, with public notification, and construction will not be
permitted until the sites are certified safe and officially handed over. The total budget is VND
2.3 billion.


Mitigation measures in construction phase
Environmental and social impact mitigation measures are detailed for each source of impact,
corresponding to each project construction phase, including: (i) general impact mitigation
measures (ESCOPs); (ii) site-specific impact mitigation measures.
Measures to mitigate the Environment and Social (E&S) risks and impacts during construction
phase are outlined in section 5.1.3. The Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP)
consists of two main parts. Since most of the major impacts will occur due to construction and
transportation of construction materials and waste, a range of potential negative impacts on the

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             12
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

physical, biological and social environment – such as noise, dust, water pollution, waste
generation and social disturbances, health and safety of workers and the community – can be
addressed through a set of general measures commonly applied to construction projects. In the
project ESMP, these general measures have been translated into standard environmental and
social specifications, namely the Environmental and Social Code of Practice (ESCOP). The
ESCOPs are designed to mitigate typical construction-related impacts in all subprojects and
will be included in all construction contracts. Details of the ESCOPs are provided in Section
5.1.3.1.
In addition to these general measures, each of the nine subprojects has site-specific impacts that
require site-specific impact mitigation measures during construction. Sections 5.1.3.2 to 5.1.3.3
describe the site-specific measures that need to be taken during construction for each subproject
1, 2 and subproject 3 to 9, which include additional actions beyond those prescribed in the
ESCOP. All site-specific construction measures will also be incorporated into the relevant
construction contracts.
Mitigation measures in the operating phase
Similarly, Section 5.1.4 details the mitigation measures to be implemented during the
operational phase. This section is divided into two subsections: Section 5.1.4.1 describes the
mitigation measures in the operation phase of subproject1 and 2, while Section 5.1.4.2 describes
the mitigation measures in the operation phase of subprojects 3 to 9.
General mitigation measures during the operational phase of the 9 subprojects include: dust and
emission control; noise management; wastewater treatment and discharge; solid waste
treatment; prevention of environmental risks and incidents; mitigation of impacts and risks
related to traffic (for both waterways and roads); protection against surface water quality
degradation; and mitigation of adverse impacts on aquatic life; Site-specific mitigation
measures in the operational phase, applicable to each subproject as described above.
Mitigation of cumulative impacts
To address the cumulative environmental and social impacts of the Thanh Hoa SFDP and
regional development, mitigation measures are taken at the subproject, whole-project and inter-
regional levels, tailored to each phase of the project. Integrated climate-adaptive and
environmentally protective design is achieved through enhanced regional planning, permeable
infrastructure, green materials, and efficient water management, while policy-level planning
ensures that climate risks are systematically addressed.
During construction and operation, coordinated planning, traffic regulation, and stakeholder
engagement help minimize emissions and social disruptions. Regional coordination,
independent monitoring, and digital tools support the management of overlapping activities and
risks. Investment in social infrastructure, livelihood support, and continuous community
feedback ensures inclusive, adaptive, and long-term sustainable governance across coastal
regions.
Community Engagement: Establish feedback systems to address public concerns, improve
access to public services, and provide trainings on environmental protection, waterway safety,
and disaster risk management.
ESMP implementation arrangement
The Thanh Hoa SFDP’s Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) includes roles
and responsibilities for ESMP implementation, monitoring staff, environmental compliance
framework, reporting organization, environmental monitoring program, capacity building
program, and ESMP implementation costs. The implementation cost is estimated at
USD124,491.
During the construction process, the ESMP requires the participation of a number of
Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             13
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

stakeholders and agencies: PMU, Contractor, Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC),
Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) and the local
community. Each will have distinct roles and responsibilities.
Public Consultation and Information Dissemination
Community consultation:
Direct consultation with the communal/ward People’s Committee, the community and
stakeholders was carried out in November 2024 with the following main contents: General
introduction about the project, project’s investment works, identification of residential
areas/residential groups in the project area; Introduction of basic requirements of the World
Bank’s Environmental - Social Framework (ESF); Current status of environmental sanitation
of works in the area and existing problems; Potential environmental - social impacts and risks
of the project on the local community; Solutions proposed by the community to limit
environmental impacts and problems during project implementation; Contributions to the
construction of project works.
Information dissemination:
The initial drafts of the ESIA, SEP, LMP, and ESCP in Vietnamese were disclosed locally in
the project communes and at the Project Management Unit (PMU) office in November 2024
for community consultation. Based on the ESIA content presented during consultation
meetings, local residents were informed about the Project and voiced their feedback/comments
on related environmental and social issues. The final draft ESIA and RP, SEP, LMP, and ESCP
in English were disclosed on the World Bank’s external website in September 2025 for public
consultation. The final ESIA in both Vietnamese and English is expected to be disclosed locally
at project sites and on the World Bank’s website in October 2025.




Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                          14
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

                                         OVERVIEW
I.1.      Project Background
I.1.1.    General Information
The Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province was approved for
investment policy by the Thanh Hoa Provincial People’s Council under Resolution No.
460/NQ-HĐND dated December 14, 2023, with the objective of building and upgrading
infrastructure and reorganizing production in order to develop the fisheries sector of Thanh Hoa
Province towards enhanced value, international integration, and climate change adaptation.
To establish an integrated system of marine fisheries infrastructure associated with supporting
industries and logistics services to improve the efficiency of marine exploitation, reduce post-
harvest losses; to develop brackish-water aquaculture infrastructure to improve shrimp
productivity and quality while protecting the ecological environment. The project includes 2
investment components, of which:
Component 1: Investment in developing fisheries and aquaculture infrastructure, including 09
subprojects: (1) Subproject 1 - Upgrading and expansion of Hoang Truong fishing port
subproject; Subproject 2-Upgrading and expansion of Lach Truong fishing port and storm
shelter anchorage in Hoa Loc commune, Hau Loc district; Subproject 3- Upgrading aquaculture
infrastructure in Nga Thuy, Nga Tan, and Nga Tien communes, Nga Son district; Subproject 4-
Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Yen commune, Hoang Hoa district; Subproject
5- Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Phong commune, Hoang Hoa district;
Subproject 6 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Luu commune, Hoang Hoa
district; Subproject 7 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Hoang Chau commune, Hoang
Hoa district; Subproject 8 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Da Loc commune, Hau Loc
district; Subproject 9 - Upgrading aquaculture infrastructure in Quang Trung commune, Quang
Xuong district.
Component 2: Project management.
I.1.2. Thanh Hoa SFDP’s Consistency with Other Plans and Development Projects
The project complies with the Construction Law, Decree No. 56/2020/ND-CP dated May 25,
2020 of the Government on the management and use of official development assistance (ODA)
capital and concessional loans from foreign donors, as well as Government’s policies on priority
areas for using official development assistance capital and concessional loans.
The project complies with Law on Public Investment No. 39/2019/QH14; Resolution No.
973/2020/UBTVQH14 July 8, 2020 of the National Assembly Standing Committee on
stipulating principles, criteria and norms for allocating public investment capital from state
budget capital in the 2021 period -2025;
In addition, the project complies with Directive No. 18/CT-TTg dated June 29, 2019 of the
Prime Minister on strengthening management and improving the efficiency of using ODA loans
and concessional foreign loans in the new context, strictly for development investment purposes
and not for recurrent expenditures.
         Consistency with National Fisheries Development Strategies
In Decision No. 339/QD TTg dated March 11, 2021 of the Prime Minister on approving the
fisheries industry development strategy to 2030 and vision to 2045, it was determined:
Investment project to upgrade infrastructure fisheries infrastructure, comprehensively
improving the fishery infrastructure and seafood logistics service systems to meet the seafood
supply chain requirements. The project includes: investment in major fishery centers, grade I
and II fishing ports, storm shelters for fishing vessels, infrastructure for seed production, shrimp
farming, marine seed farming, and centralized industrial aquaculture zones, offshore

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               15
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

aquaculture infrastructure, cold storage facilities, and the development of a fisheries database.
It is prioritized for implementation during 2021-2030 period.
       Consistency with Sectoral and Local Planning
The fishing ports and aquaculture infrastructure are consistent with the master plan to 2020,
with an orientation to 2030, approved by the Prime Minister under Decision No. 1976/QĐ-
TTg dated November 12, 2015.
In addition to the existing ports implemented in line with Thanh Hoa Province’s development
plan, the province is currently performing new investment projects according to the approved
detailed planning, specifically: (i) Hoang Truong Fishing Port: Approved under Decision No.
779/QĐ-UBND dated February 23, 2024, of the Thanh Hoa Provincial People’s Committee
regarding the “Approval of the 1/500 detailed planning project for Hoang Truong Fishing Port,
Hoang Hoa District, Thanh Hoa Province”; (ii) Hoa Loc Fishing Port and Lach Truong storm
shelter anchorage for fishing vessels: Approved under Decision No. 5017/QĐ-UBND dated
December 18, 2024, of the Thanh Hoa Provincial People’s Committee regarding the “Approval
of the 1/500 detailed planning project for Hoa Loc Fishing Port and Lach Truong storm shelter
anchorage for fishing vessels, Hau Loc District, Thanh Hoa Province.
The remaining fishing ports and storm shelter anchorages have been proposed by the locality
for implementation based on spatial arrangements in line with the province’s development plan.
       Consistency with the National Environmental Protection Strategy
Locations for the implementation of the Project’s works/investment items are in line with the
National Environmental Protection Strategy to 2030, with a vision to 2050 under Decision No.
450/QD-TTg dated April 13, 2022. The project’s investments that do not pose a potential risk
of environmental pollution, do not affect the health of the community, aim at the goal of
sustainable socio-economic development, climate change adaptation and resilience through
structural and non-structural works on sustainable fisheries development and response to with
local climate change to minimize damage caused by natural disasters and climate change for
fishermen in the project area.
       Consistency with the National Climate Change Adaptation Plan
The project is to build and upgrade infrastructure at 2 fishing ports and aquaculture areas in
Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh communes to develop the
fisheries sector of Thanh Hoa Province and response to climate change. The project is consistent
with Decision No. 1055/QĐ-TTg dated July 20, 2020, of the Prime Minister promulgating the
National Climate Change Adaptation Plan for the period 2021–2030, with a vision to 2050.
I.2. LEGAL GROUND
The proposed project will fully comply with the current environmental and social laws of
Vietnam and the World Bank's Environmental and Social Safety Framework (ESF).
Environmental and Social Assessments are conducted for the project in accordance with the
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) under the ESF and Vietnam’s requirements.

I.2.2. World Bank’s policies and guidelines on environmental and social safeguard
The project will apply the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) and eight
Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs), specifically from ESS1 to ESS10, excluding ESS7
and ESS9. The project will also comply with the World Bank’s operational policy on
international waterways (OP/BP 7.50).
The Project's E&S documents: Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), Labour Management Plan
(LMP) and Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP). The scope of application of
social support institutions is discussed below.
ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS1 requires borrowers to assess, manage and monitor relevant environmental and social risks
and impacts at each stage of subprojects/projects funded by WB in order to achieve
environmental and social outcomes in consistent with ESSs.
Overall, the proposed project offers several potential environmental benefits, such as positive
impacts on waste and wastewater management through investments, improved regulatory
compliance, reduced storm-related damage, and enhanced post-harvest loss control through
effective management. However, overall, the environmental and social risks and adverse
impacts caused by the project are considered significant due to the following reasons:
  • Socially, key risks and impacts will include: loss of land, on-land assets, livelihoods and
    other properties due to temporary and permanent land acquisition and resettlement;

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                          21
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

    disruption to livelihood activities of farmers and fishermen (due to upgrading/construction
    and operation of fisheries infrastructure and capacity enhancement); occupational safety
    and health concern; gender-based violence and abuse of sexual exploitation associated with
    migrant workers and risks to public health and safety; farmer’s lack of adaptability to new
    livelihood; risks from failed livelihood models negatively affecting the welfare of project
    beneficiaries; unequal access to project benefits among vulnerable groups such as the poor
    and female-headed households. Although these risks and impacts are considerable, they
    are manageable and can be mitigated through feasible environmental and social assessment
    and mitigation plans to be developed during the preparation and implementation of the
    project.
  • The main environmental risks and impacts during project implementation include (i) safety
    risks associated with unexploded ordnance; (ii) environmental pollution due to the
    generation of dust, waste, wastewater and dredged materials; (iii) degradation of water
    quality and impacts on aquatic ecosystems and aquatic species ; (iv) the risk of erosion and
    subsidence during construction; (v) impacts on sensitive areas such as natural habitats and
    altered habitats; (vi) the risk of erosion, subsidence and loss of protection forests during
    the construction of embankments, harbors and storm shelter anchorages. Major
    environmental risks and impacts during operation include: (i) soil erosion and embankment
    subsidence; (ii) Threats to aquatic biodiversity due to the potential introduction of artificial
    and exotic species into local water environment; (iii) Pollution of aquatic ecosystems and
    water sources may occur if treated wastewater from aquaculture activities is discharged
    directly into the environment; (iv) air pollution and noise caused by the operation of fishing
    vessels and road vehicles; (v) water pollution and disturbance to aquatic life due to
    improper waste management. These risks and impacts are predictable, mostly at moderate
    level and few are significant, local, and can be managed and mitigated.
  • Based on the type and location of the proposed investment project, its sensitivity and the
    capacity of the implementation and management agency, environemtal and social risks and
    negative impacts are assessed as Significant.
Although Vietnam has an advanced system of legal documents, certain differences still exist
between national regulations and international practices in environmental and social
assessment. The application of the Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs) for the project
is discussed below, while Table 0-3 presents the key differences between national
environmental and social assessment requirements and those under the World Bank’s
Environmental and Social Standards.
ESS2: Labor and Working Conditions
ESS2 recognizes the importance of job and income creation in the pursuit of poverty reduction
and inclusive economic development. The project can promote a healthy relationship between
workers and management and enhance the development of project interests by treating workers
in the project with equality and providing safe, secure and healthy working conditions.
With the initial project design, the project owner is expected to employ labor (mostly from local
sources) to support the project management and carry out the construction/renovation/upgrade
of existing fishing ports, storm shelter anchorages, hatcheries, and shrimp ponds/farms. most
fish port upgrade works are expected to be of medium scale, with a few of larger scale, involving
approximately 50 workers per port—most of whom will be locally recruited. Other construction
sites are expected to have around 20 workers each, also primarily recruited locally. Most risks
related to disease outbreaks and other construction-related risks, including occupational health
and safety (OHS), gender-based violence (GBV), and sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA)
associated with construction activities such as fishing port development are considered to be of
medium risk level.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               22
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

To comply with the ESS2, as part of the environmental assessment process, a Labor
Management Plan (LMP) has been developed. The LMP outlines the approach to managing
project workers in accordance with national labor laws and the provisions of ESS2.
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS3 acknowledges that economic activity, agricultural and fishery production and widespread
urbanization often cause air, water and soil pollution, and that consumption of finite resources
may pose threats to human health, ecosystem services, and habitats at the local, regional, and
global levels. The EES3 sets out requirements to address resource efficiency and pollution
prevention throughout the project lifecycle. Given the nature and scale of the proposed
investment, the project is expected to consume a substantial amount of resources and
construction materials, resulting in medium to significant risks and adverse impacts on human
health and the environment. Risks and impacts related to generated waste/wastewater, the
management of antibiotics and hazardous chemicals, potential effects on communities, and
resource use efficiency will be assessed. Corresponding management and mitigation measures
will be proposed during the preparation of subprojects as part of the Environmental and Social
Assessment (ESA).
Dredged materials from ports, channels, fishing ports, storm shelters and water supply canals
for shrimp farming areas may be contaminated with organic pollutants or naturally occurring
acidity (e.g., alum). The adverse impacts and risks associated with dredging, storage,
transportation and handling of dredged materials will be identified and addressed during
Environmental and social assessment (ESIA) of each subproject. The Dredged Material
Management Plan (DMMP) have been prepared as part of the Environmental and Social Impact
Assessment (ESIA) process for the project.
If the project is identified as potentially generating significant greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions
during its construction and operation phases, an estimation of the total GHG emissions from
the project will be required, provided that such estimation is financially and technically feasible.
The project will apply measures outlined in the World Bank Group Environmental, Health, and
Safety Guidelines (WBG EHSG) and Good International Industry Practice (GIIP) to ensure
efficient use of raw materials and to optimize energy consumption to the extent that is
technically and financially feasible..
ESS4: Community Health and Safety
ESS4 addressed the risks and impacts on health, safety and security to communities potentially
affected by the project and outlines the responsibilities of the project owners in avoiding or
mitigating such risks and impacts. This includes exposure and disease prevention, with
particular attention to individuals who may be vulnerable due to their specific circumstances.
With the existing nature and scale, the project is not expected to discharge a large amount of
hazardous substances and dredging materials without proper management that could affect
public health. Alsot, it is not expected to expose local communities to high or significant risks
related to waterway and road traffic accidents, electricity use, or social disruption due to migrant
labor influx. The project does not involve the construction or rehabilitation of dams, or depends
on water supplies from existing dams;
Therefore, the standard measures set forth the Environmental, Health and Safety Guidelines
(EHSG) are considered sufficient to ensure public health and community safety during the
construction and operation of the infrastructure financed by the project. The guidance covers
general measures for design and operation, communication and training, and measures to
address risks to physical and chemical hazards, personal protective equipment, special
hazardous environments, and monitoring.
ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               23
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

ESS5 recognizes the adverse impacts of the project on local people, especially those considered
vulnerable or disadvantaged. Involuntary resettlement will be avoided. In the event that
involuntary resettlement is unavoidable, mitigation measures and the implementation of
appropriate measures to minimize the negative impact on displaced persons (and for host
communities hosting displaced persons) should be taken.
Although most of the project’s infrastructure works will be carried out within the boundaries of
existing facilities (such as fish ports, storm shelter anchorages, and existing canals) for
renovation and upgrading, the construction/rehabilitation of infrastructure may require land
acquisition for project purposes. This could lead to involuntary resettlement and affect the
livelihoods and economic opportunities of local people. Land acquisition may also be required
for the disposal and treatment of dredged materials resulting from the upgrading/construction
of fish ports, storm shelter anchorages, and canals, potentially causing permanent loss of land
and associated assets. A mitigation hierarchy and alternative analysis will be applied during the
project design phase to avoid or minimize land acquisition and restrictions on land use, thereby
reducing adverse socio-economic impacts on local communities, especially vulnerable groups.
A Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) will be developed in compliance with the ESS5.
ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS6 recognizes that the protection and conservation of biodiversity and the sustainable
management of living natural resources are fundermental for sustainable development. It
emphasizes the importance of maintaining the core ecological functions of habitats, including
forests, riparian zones, coastal and marine areas and the biodiversity that these ecological
functions support. ESS6 also addresses the sustainable management of primary production and
harvesting of living natural resources, and acknowledges the need to consider the livelihoods
of parties affected by the project, including local people of ethnic minorities regarding their
access to and use of biodiversity use or living natural resources that may be affected by the
project.
Given the nature and scope, the project’s activities will be carried out on existing canals and
rivers [coastal areas and bays] that have been used as waterways for fishing, storm shelter
anchorage, water supply and drainage for aquaculture. These rivers and canals may be
considered aquatic ecosystems and habitats for aquatic species; however, they are classified as
modified habitats under ESS6. All project construction activities are expected to avoid adverse
impact on the protected areas/areas requiring conservation.
The relevance of ESS6 will be further discussed and assessed during preparation of
ESIA/ESMP for the project. Mitigation measures will be taken to protect or minimize adverse
impacts on aquatic ecosystems during construction activities (e.g., increased water turbidity,
pollution due to spillage of construction materials, and oil or grease leakage).
ESS7: Ethnic Minorities
ESS7 aims to ensure that the development process encourages full respect for the human rights,
dignity, aspirations, identities, cultures and natural resource-based livelihoods of ethnic
minorities (definition equivalent to Indigenous Peoples). ESS7 also means that in order to avoid
the adverse impacts of subprojects, or when it cannot be avoided, mitigation measures applied
to mitigate and/or offset those impacts should be implemented.
For the Sustainable Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province, no significant
adverse socio-economic or cultural impacts on ethnic minorities are anticipated, as there are no
ethnic minority populations residing scatteredly alongside the Kinh people in the coastal areas
where the project will be implemented. Therefore, this standard is not relevant.
ESS8: Cultural Heritage



Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            24
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

ESS8 recognizes cultural heritage as a continuum of tangible and intangible forms across past,
present, and future generations. ESS8 sets out measures designed to protect cultural heritage
throughout the project lifecycle. Given the nature, scale and location of the project, its
implementation will not have a significant negative impact on tangible and intangible cultural
heritage. However, due to potentially large scale of excavation/dredging accidents, chance finds
may occur during the project construction. A chance find procedure has been included in the
project’s ESMP during the ESA process. The project has been screened and assessed for
potential impacts on cultural heritage, and relevant mitigation measures has been incorporated
into the project’s ESMP and construction contracts.
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
ESS10 recognizes the importance of open and transparent engagement between project owners
and stakeholders as an essential element of Good International Practice. Effective stakeholder
engagement can improve the environmental and sustainability, increase project acceptance, and
contribute significantly to the successful project design and implementation. For the Thanh Hoa
SFDP Project, key stakeholders include beneficiary communities and households, project-
affected persons, national-level implementing agencies (Ministry of Finance and Ministry of
Agriculture and Rural Development), and local authorities in participating provinces (including
Provincial People’s Committees, Departments of Agriculture and Rural Development,
Departments of Finance, and relevant Commune People’s Committees).
During the preparation of environmental and social (E&S) documentation, project owners has
been required to prepare a Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) for the project. This is to ensure
transparency and meaningful consultation with affected parties and interested stakeholders.
Stakeholder engagement and consultation will be conducted throughout the project/subproject
lifecycle. This will include discussions on the subproject design and impacts with potential
beneficiary communities as well as consultation with provincial stakeholders on environmental
and social issues during the project preparation phase. Special attention should be paid to
identifying individuals or groups affected by the subproject who are “disadvantaged or
vulnerable” during stakeholder identification and analysis. The SEP, along with other
environmental and social instruments will be disclosed during the public consultation and
project information disclosure process as required by the ESS10 and will be considered as a
living document that needs to be updated regularly during the project implementation .
(b) Other applicable policies
This policy is triggered because certain construction activities in the project province belong to
the Thanh Hoa SFDP will take place within the basins of international waterways, such as the
Ma River. This international waterway are shared by countries including Laosand Vietnam.
Thanh Hoa is located in the Ma River Basin. The Ma River is an international waterway flowing
from Vietnam through Laos and return to Vietnam. Vietnam is lowest downstream country
among those sharing this international river. The determination of notification exemptions or
required notifications is currently under discussion, and will be appropriately addressed prior
to project appraisal.
The WB’s policy on access to information 1. The World Bank’s Access to Information Policy
aims to ensure that individuals and groups affected by the project are informed of the project’s
objectives and impacts, and are consulted throughout the project to ensure that their interests
are represented. Environmental and social documents re disclosed locally in project areas and
made publicly accessible through the World Bank’s internal and external websites, providing
public access to information about the Bank’s projects and programs.


1
  For detailed information on the World Bank’s Access to Information Policy, please visit the website.
http://www.worldbank.org/en/access-to-information

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                                 25
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

The World Bank’s policy requires that, during the ESA process, the Government must conduct
meaningful consultations with stakeholders such as project-affected groups and local non-
governmental organizations (NGOs) on the environmental and social aspects of the project, and
take their views into account in project design. All draft environmental and social instruments
must be disclosed locally in accessible locations and in formats and languages understandable
to key stakeholders, and in English on the World Bank’s internal and external websites prior to
project appraisal.
Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines 2. Projects funded by the World Bank (WB)
should also refer to the World Bank Group Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines
(WBG EHSG), hereafter called the “EHS Guidelines,” as well as the relevant World Bank
Group Environmental, Social, Health, and Safety (ESHS) guidelines related to aquaculture,
ports, and harbors3. The EHS Guidelines serve as both general technical references and sector-
specific guidance aligned with Good International Industry Practice (GIIP). The levels and
measures outlined in the Guidelines are generally accepted by the WBG and considered
achievable for new construction projects at reasonable cost using existing technologies. The
environmental assessment process may propose alternative implementation levels or measures
(higher or lower), subject to WBG approval, which will then become specific requirements at
designated project sites. he applicable EHS Guidelines for the project are reflected in the
Environmental Code of Practice (ECOPs), which will be integrated into construction contracts.

Vietnam has signed several international agreements and conventions related to environmental
management, community rights, and ethnic minorities. International conventions are not always
incorporated into national law. Some key agreements are listed below.

I.2.4. Analysis of the differences between the GoV’s regulations and WB’s ESF
The application of environmental assessment policies in Vietnam, as well as various efforts
directed to policy harmonization between GoV and donors, has gradually narrowed the gap
between the two systems. However, starting October 2018, the WB applies the Environment
and Social Framework (ESF) to all WB-financing projects. The ESF describes the WB E&S
policy to ensure that all WB’s financing investment projects will meet the 10 Environmental
and Social Standards (ESSs) which were designed to avoid, minimize, reduce or mitigate the
adverse E&S risks and impacts of projects. These ESSs are:
       •   ESS1 - Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and
           Impacts;
       •   ESS2 - Labor and Working Conditions;
       •   ESS3 - Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management;
       •   ESS4 - Community Health and Safety;
       •   ESS5 - Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement;
       •   ESS6 - Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural
           Resources;
       •   ESS7 - Indigenous Peoples;
       •   ESS8 - Cultural Heritage;
       •   ESS9 - Financial Intermediaries; and

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              28
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

       •   ESS10 - Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
In late 2018 - early 2019, the WB team conducted a comprehensive assessment of Vietnam’s
National System Framework Assessment to identify key differences between the national legal
system and the ESSs and priority needs to ensure full compliance of the ESSs. The assessment
concluded that Vietnam has a comprehensive legal framework that, in many cases, meets the
requirements of the 10 ESSs and key issues that are more associated with implementation and
varying capacity among the central and provincial project management units. There are
opportunities to accelerate implementation of recent improvements in the national legal system,
especially on labor, community safety and stakeholder engagement.
Initial screening of the proposed project suggested that the E&S risks and negative impacts of
the proposed activities can be classified as Moderate. In this context, it is expected that national
regulations and relevant requirements under the Environmental and Social Standards (ESSs)
will apply to most of the proposed investment works. Specific attention and capacity building
for contractors will be necessary to ensure they can adequately address issues related to contract
management, community safety, and worker protection. Table 0-3 below summarizes the key
differences between the World Bank’s Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) process
and the Government’s Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) requirements, along with the
solutions proposed by the project.

I.3. ESIA PREPARATION METHOD
During the preparation of the ESIA report, the consultant employed a combination of the
following research methods:
Listing method: This is a commonly used method to identify the study area, sampling locations,
and to determine the areas to be studied, sampling positions, identify the impacted as well as
sensitive areas that may be affected by project activities during the pre-construction and
construction phases.
Rapid assessment method: Experience-based emission factors and coefficients were applied to
calculate fugitive emissions and dust emissions (TSP); predict noise and vibration dispersion to
the nearest settlements; greenhouse gas emissions; solid and hazardous waste, etc. For air
emissions, the Sutton model was applied to model dust and air emissions as a rapid assessment
for project activities. In this report, pollution load coefficients were taken from World Bank
guidance documents (Volume II, Sector Guidelines, Environment, World Bank, Washington
DC, 8/1991) and the Emission Handbook, Non-industrial and Industrial Sources, Netherlands,
and were used for rapid assessments.
Expert method: This method is implemented with the knowledge and experience of experts,
related to the survey of the project implementation area, including: location, socio-economic
development, people’s lives, cultural and religious assets; natural environment (existing status
of basic air quality conditions, water and soil); regional and local climatic characteristics such
as hydrometeorology, geology, geography, linkage structures and landscapes; and ecology
(animal and plant characteristics).
Matrix method: This technique is applied to predict potential impacts caused by project
activities. The matrix is established based on the sources causing impacts and the potential
receptors affected by the project.
Comparative method: Survey results on the natural environment and laboratory analyses are
compared with national technical standards for physical environmental components to assess
the baseline environmental conditions in the project area.
GIS mapping method: GIS is used in combination with existing maps (such as topographic
maps) and specialized software (such as MapInfo, AutoCAD) to create comprehensive maps to
support the ESIA process, including: maps of sampling locations for vegetation surveys; maps
of sampling locations for air and surface water quality.
Field survey and investigation: Based on available environmental data and maps (topographic
maps, existing land-use maps of communes in the project area, etc.), on-site measurements,
sampling, investigations, and field surveys of natural environmental components (air, water,
soil, and flora and fauna) in the project area are conducted throughout the year during both the
dry and rainy seasons.
Sampling and analysis: All water, air, soil, and sediment samples collected in the field are
stored and taken to laboratories for analysis. The samples are analyzed at the laboratory of KGZ
Investment Joint Stock Company (certified by VIMCERTS 320), recognized by the Ministry
of Natural Resources and Environment as competent to perform environmental parameter
testing. Sample storage, preservation, transportation, and analysis are carried out in strict
compliance with regulations.
Public consultation: Public consultation is used to help identify opportunities and risks,
improve subproject design and implementation, and increase ownership and sustainability of
the subproject. During the preparation of the project’s ESIA report, 12 public consultations
were conducted in the communes of Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Hoa Loc, Van Loc, Tan Tien,
Nga Son, and Quang Chinh. Public consultation is specifically required by the World Bank’s
Environmental and Social Safeguard policies. Meaningful public feedback is considered. This

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             35
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
is a two-way process in which beneficiaries provide advice and input on how the proposed
subproject design may affect their lives and environment, promoting dialogue between the
government, community, and implementing agencies to discuss all aspects of the proposed
subproject. Feedback from consultations is incorporated into the ESIA design and subproject.
Affected persons include those to be resettled and those in nearby communities impacted by
subproject activities, intended subproject beneficiaries, primary and secondary stakeholder
groups, local mass organizations including women’s associations, local, provincial, and central
authorities, other donors and development agencies, and other relevant stakeholders. In
particular, stakeholder engagement was conducted during the ES assessment to meet ESS10
requirements.
Data processing and statistics: All data and statistical documents obtained from local levels
(commune level), as well as on-site measurements, are processed and presented in tables,
figures, and charts for interpretation. This data is systematized over time and adjusted to support
the identification of natural and socio-economic environmental conditions; analysis of
environmental change trends in the project area. These data are essential as a basis for assessing
and predicting environmental impacts during project implementation, as well as proposing
mitigation measures.

                             CHAPTER 1. PROJECT DESCRIPTION
1.1.       Project information
✓          Project name: Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
✓          Executive agency: People’s Committee of Thanh Hoa province
             + Address: 35 Le Loi Boulevard, Hac Thanh Ward, Thanh Hoa Province.
             + Phone: (0373) 857 290
✓          Project’s owner: Thanh Hoa Project Management Unit of Construction Investment
           Works for Agriculture and Rural Development
             + Address: 06 Hac Thanh, Hac Thanh Ward, Thanh Hoa Province.
             + Phone: (02373) 853 406
1.2.       Project objectives and components
Overall objective of the Project: To construct and upgrade infrastructure and reorganize
production system for the development of fisheries sector in Thanh Hoa Province, aiming to
enhance value, promote international integration, and strengthen climate change adaptation.
Specific objectives of the Project: To establish an integrated marine fisheries infrastructure
system, linked with supporting industries and logistics services to enhance fishing efficiency
and reduce post-harvest losses; to develop brackish water aquaculture infrastructure to increase
shrimp productivity and quality while protecting the ecological environment.
1.3. Project location
The project will be implemented in seven communes: Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan
Tien, Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh. Specifically:

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               56
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
✓ Sources of construction materials and main transportation routes
      -   Materials such as sand, soil, stone, and gravel will be purchased from suppliers in the
          local area, districts within the Province/City, or neighboring Provinces/Cities.
      -   Steel and cement: Steel will be purchased from suppliers within the project commune
          in the Province or from neighboring Provinces/Cities.
The project (all 9 subprojects) does not include any component involving large-scale extraction
of sand, stone, or soil; therefore, no new quarries are required. No quarry serves as an exclusive
source for the project. All materials will be quality-verified and priced according to offical
annoucements issued Department of Construction of each Province where the project is located.
This ensure that all materials are sourced rom legally licensed suppliers. A summary of the
material sources for the project is presented in the table below:

✓ Electricity, water, and fuel supply
    -   Water supply: water for construction and for the workers’ daily use will source from
        available clean water sources in each project area or from nearby households.
    -   Electricity demand: the electricity for the project will be connected from the 10kv
        medium-voltage line running across the site to the substation. The distance from the
        connection point to the project substation is approximately 100–200 meters.
    -   Diesel and gasoline: fuels for operating construction machinery will be supplied by local
        petroleum companies. Thanks to the existing distribution network, fuel supply for each
        subproject is relatively convenient and reliable.

      1.6.        Disposal sites
      The volume of debris from the demolition of existing structures will be collected and disposed
      of by a contracted local authority unit at disposal sites designated by the local government.
      The volume of excavated soil and surplus dredged material from the construction phase of the
      project must be transported to designated disposal sites to prevent environmental impacts and
      to allow future resource utilization. Most of the dredged material (mainly sand) can be reused
      as a sand resource (fill sand – provided that it meets the construction material standards). his
      reuse will be subject to future resource auctions in accordance with the provisions of the Mineral
      Law. The disposal sites for excavated soil and surplus material for each subproject are as

The volume of excavated soil and surplus dredged material will be transported to temporary
disposal sites by 10-ton trucks. Specifically: For subproject 1: dredged material will be pumped
onto 200T and 250T barges, transported along the Lach Truong River, and then pumped onto
the disposal site. For subproject 2: dredged material from the storm shelter mooring area will
be sucked by dredger vessels and pumped directly to the disposal site via a pipeline system,
with an average pipeline length of L = 0.5 km.
