1.7. Construction method
1.7.1. Construction method applied for Subproject 1 and Subproject 2
          Construction of vessels berth
-     Site preparation: clear the construction area, carry out unexploded ordnance (UXO)
      clearance, and remove obstacles. Establish control points for the construction zone.
-     Bored piles: conduct supplementary geological surveys; perform tests on the stability of the
      borehole walls; construct test piles and perform static load tests; inspect the integrity of
      concrete piles according to design specifications.
-     Assemble reinforcement and pour concrete for pile head connection details.
-     Install formwork and reinforcement, then pour concrete for the longitudinal and transverse
      beams of the wharf.
-     Install precast slab: install reinforcement, and pour concrete for the deck slabs.
-     Pour concrete wearing course and shape the slope of the wharf surface.
-     Install anchor plates, fenders, and access ladders. Apply anti-corrosion coating to steel
      components and paint wheel stops.
          Port yard leveling:
-     Position and install survey stakes at control points within the designated leveling area.
-     Materials used for leveling are those purchased or ultized from material dredged from the
      water area, providing it meets quality standards
-     The yard ground is leveled in combination with the construction of drainage system.
-     The construction will be carried out from the inner area outward, using equipment such as
      bulldozers, rollers, and compactors.
          Embankment:
-     Align the work.
-     Dredge the revetment foundation.
-     Place ripraps at the revetment toe by a crane mounted on a pontoon, with support of barges
      transporting stone.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                                69
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
-   Lay a geotextile using a combination of machinery and manual labor.
-   Place stone on the slope by a crane mounted on a pontoon, with support of barges barges
    transporting stone.
-   Install revetment surface layer by using a crane mounted on a pontoon, with support of
    divers.
-   Construct concrete road at the crest of the revetment.
       Dredging of navigation channels and water areas:
-   Preparation:
   Before dredging, tthe site, control points will be handed over for the construction, complete
procedures to obtain construction permits, and approve maritime safety measures.
-   Geodetic survey:
+ Re-survey the site, verify existing control points, and establish additional points if necessary.
+ Establish a construction control grid as the basis for all geodetic surveying work; on that
basis, perform longitudinal and vertical surveying to control vertical deviations and elevation
levels of structural components.
+ Position the project works according to the design, verify elevation deviations of structural
elements.
+ Positioning to support the execution of all construction components.
+ Monitor settlement and deformation of the structures.
+ Perform as-built drawings and measurements upon completion of construction; comply with
specific requirements set out in construction standards for surveying works.
-   Dredging method:
       + Subproject 1: use a grab bucket combined with barges to transport dredged material
to the disposal site, with transporation distance of L=3 km; the disposal method: dredged
materials are pumped from the barge to the disposal site at the inefficient salt fields in Van Loc
commune.
       + Subproject 2: for dredging the storm shelter area, suction vessels are used with a
pipeline system to spray dredged materials directly onto the disposal site, with an average
pipeline length of L=0.5 km. The dredged materials mainly consists of clayey mud, containing
organic matter, pumped directly into the storage area at the inefficient salt fields in Van Loc
commune, located along the shore of storm shelter anchorage zone. The disposal site is
surrounded by a dike system for containing sludge, equipped with filtration gates; the
discharged water flows into existing drainage canal before entering the river.
     + Construction measures at the dredged material storage site: the dredged material storage
site will be enclosed by sandbag embankment (sand may be sourced from the dredged materials,
provided it meet quality standards), with the top elevation following the approved planning
elevation (average height approximately 4 m). Construct primary settling pits and water
collection pits during dredged material deposition. Water drained from the dredged material
will flow through temporary drainage channel to the collection pits before being discharged
into the receiving source.
       Construction of architectural works
-   Construction of architectural works: administration building, specialized warehouses,
    transfer warehouses, seafood product sorting and collection buildings. The construction

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              70
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    sequence is carried out from foundation to walls, roof, plastering, painting, finishing walls,
    and installing doors.
-   Excavation of foundation pits: excavate soil to the elevation indicated in the drawings;
    measures must be taken to support the foundation pits to ensure safety for workers and
    structures.
-   After completing the foundation excavation, proceed to cast blinding concrete and
    foundation concrete or build the foundation masonry.
-   Construction of reinforced concrete components cast in place must ensure the integrity and
    monolithic quality of the concrete.
-   Construction of the beam frame system, roof frame system, and roof covering.
-   Finishing works: plastering, painting, door installation, basic interior works…
        Technical infrastructure system
-   The technical infrastructure system includes works for electricity supply & lighting, water
    supply and drainage, wastewater drainage, and wastewater treatment areas. Construction
    methods for these works are as follows:
        Electricity supply & lighting:
-   Installation of transformer stations: construct transformer stations to supply electricity for
    the port, including equipment such as transformers, distribution cabinets, circuit breakers,
    and protective devices.
-   Installation of electrical cables: dig trenches and install electrical cables from the
    transformer station to the areas requiring power, ensuring safety and durability.
-   Installation of distribution cabinets: the cabinets will distribute electricity to different areas
    of the port.
-   Installation of lighting: determine the positions and types of lights suitable for each area of
    the port (high-pressure lamps, LED lights, etc.). Calculate illumination and coverage to
    ensure safety in the area.
-   Wiring and connections: install the electrical wiring system for the lighting, connecting
    them to the power source and control cabinets.
-   Installation of grounding system: ensure electrical safety for the system by installing
    grounding, lightning protection, and devices to prevent electrical leakage.
-   System inspection: check all connections and electrical equipment to ensure the system
    operates effectively and safely.
-   Acceptance and commissioning: carry out inspection and acceptance of construction items,
    evaluate quality, and confirm the completion of the electrical and lighting system.
Stormwater drainage system:
-   Trenching: Excavate trenches according to the design to install the stormwater drainage
    system. Trenches must have appropriate slopes to ensure rapid and efficient water drainage.
-   Installation of stormwater culverts: Install pipe sections along the length of the trench,
    ensuring tight joints with no water leakage. Stormwater pipes are generally arranged with
    proper slopes to automatically direct water away from the area.
-   Installation of manholes and valves: Manholes allow inspection, maintenance, and clearing
    of the pipe system, while valves enable regulation and shutdown of water flow when
    necessary.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                                 71
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Construction of the wastewater drainage system and 200 m³/day treatment Plant:
-   Excavate trenches and pipe installation: the construction process is similar to the stormwater
    system but special attention about selection of pipe materials resistant to acids, alkalis, or
    chemicals present in the wastewater should be paid.
-   Installation of manholes and connection points: manholes facilitate monitoring and
    maintenance of the wastewater system. Ensure that pipe joints are sealed to prevent leakage.
-   Excavation of foundation pits: excavate foundation pits for treatment tanks, ensuring depth
    and area meet design specifications.
-   Construction of tank foundations: Pour concrete foundations for treatment tanks and other
    underground structures.
-   Construction of treatment tanks: build wastewater treatment tanks, including sedimentation
    tanks, biological tanks, and disinfection tanks (according to the selected technology).
-   Installation of piping and equipment: install wastewater pipelines, pumping systems, valves,
    distribution equipment, and other treatment devices such as mixers and aeration pumps.
-   Integration of wastewater into treatment tanks: connect the drainage system to the treatment
    tanks according to design requirements, including storage tanks, manholes, and
    connections.
-   Electrical system installation: install electrical systems for pumps, mixers, aerators, and
    lighting within the facility.
-   Control systems setup: set up control cabinets and monitoring systems to regulate the
    treatment process and control parameters such as ph, temperature, water flow, and other
    critical indicators.
-   System testing: conduct tests for pumps, treatment equipment, and control systems to ensure
    efficient and stable operation.
-   Completion and acceptance: after construction, carry out project acceptance following
    standards for quality, safety, and environmental compliance.
Water supply system:
-   Trench excavation and site preparation: Excavate trenches according to the designed length
    and depth. Clear trenches thoroughly to facilitate pipe placement.
-   Pipe installation: Install water supply pipes according to the design layout. Ensure joints are
    tight with no leaks. Pipes should be installed with proper slopes to maintain water flow and
    allow easy maintenance and repair.
-   Pressure testing: Perform pressure tests across the entire pipeline system, check for leaks,
    and flush the pipes.
-   Backfilling and site restoration: After installation and testing, backfill trenches, protect
    pipes, and reinstate the surface to its original condition.
1.7.2. Construction method applied for Subproject 3 to Subproject 9
       Construction diversion
      Period of channel diversion in construction:
      - Dry season: From November to June each year.
      - Rainy season: From July to October each year.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              72
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
      Design frequency for channel diversion: Based on TCVN 9160:2012 “Hydraulic
structures – Technical requirements for design of diversion channel in construction”, taking
into account construction duration, water level frequency, and maximum flow, the design
auxiliary structures such as cofferdams and diversion channels is carried out with P = 10%.
         Channel diversion during construction period:
         *For dredging of irrigation and drainage canals:
      - Use Lassen III sheet piles to enclose the construction area of the right bank embankment,
and divert the flow to allow construction of the remaining section of the drainage canal.
      - For Bao Trieu Canal (Ong Ty Canal): During construction, a pontoon is used to support
the excavator for dredging the canal according to the design cross-section, allowing flow
diversion through the existing drainage canal.
      - For Subprojects 4, 5, 6, 7 in Hoang Yen, Hoang Luu, Hoang Phong, and Hoang Chau
communes: During construction, a pontoon is used to support the excavator for dredging the
canal bed according to the design cross-section, so flow diversion is not required.
      - Main drainage canal, segment from Ong Ninh sluice to Quay Co sluice, section from
K0+429.14 – K0+488.90 m (right bank embankment, downstream of Ong Hong sluice): Use
bamboo piles and woven bamboo screens to form twin-pile cofferdams for constructing the
right bank embankment and divert the flow for the remaining section of the drainage canal.
     - Dredging in-field canal branch: During dredging, a pontoon is used to support the
excavator for dredging the canal according to the design cross-section, allowing flow diversion
through the existing drainage canal
         - Canal from Ong Thuoc sluice to Ong Vanh sluice: Flow diversion follows the existing
canal.
          Main construction methods:
      Due to the extended alignment of the main drainage components, construction activities
are carried out based on a segmented approach. Branch roads are ultilized for transporting soil,
materials, and equipment to the site.
          Weathered soil removal:
      - Before excavation, all roots of large and small trees must be manually removed. Grass,
weathered soil, and poor-quality soil as specified in the design must be cleared using
mechanical equipment. Excavated waste is to be transported to the designated disposal site
using self-dumping trucks with a capacity ≤ 10 tons. Excavated waste is to be transported to the
designated disposal site using self-dumping trucks with a capacity ≤ 10 tons. Excavated
embankment soil is reused for filling.
      - At positions where embankment slope excavation is carried out, the soil must be
loosened, finely broken down, and moisture-conditioned uniformly before being reused for
backfilling.
      - If the contractor discovers underground technical systems, archaeological sites, etc.
Within the construction area, the excavation must immediately halted and the area fenced off.
Within 24 hours, the contractor must notify the project owner and relevant authorities for
resolution.
         - During construction, the contractor must minimize impacts beyond the excavation area.
     - Excavation of foundation pits must be carried out in accordance with the specified
method, boundary lines, and slope angles as shown in the design drawings.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            73
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       Construction of Foundation pit:
      Preparation work:
      After being handed over, the site is divided into zones, transfer and mark reference points,
set boundary stakes, and clear the entire area.
      Install temporary camps and auxiliary areas, construct access roads, stormwater drainage
routes, and implement safety measures such as guard stations, project gates, signage, and
lighting systems.
      Site layout and positioning:
      Before construction, benchmark coordinates and control grid must be handed over
between the project owner and construction contractor. Reference stakes must be made of
concrete and placed in positions that do not interfere with the construction workers, and are
proected throughout the construction period.
      Based on the approved site planning, the structure is positioned. Use surveying equipment
locate centraline axes, foundation pit boundaries, cross-sections, and reference markers beyond
the construction area. Verify actual site dimensions before construction.
      Soil excavation:
     For excavation of foundation pit, both machinery and manual labor are used. Excavators,
dump trucks, and bulldozers are mobilized for collection, loading and transportation of
excavated materials.
      For deep excavation areas, apply the construction method of stepwise top-down
excavation according to the design cross-section, and transport the excavated material to the
designated disposal site.
       For soil/rock layers, excavation is performed directly with excavators, loading onto trucks
for transport. Construction equipment includes excavators, bulldozers, and trucks.
      After excavation, proceed with positioning reference points and determining the elevation
of the structure to prepare for the next phase of construction.
      Foundation pit drainage:
      The foundation pit of the spillway is located on a hillside higher than surrounding areas,
providing good drainage capacity. Therefore, it is sufficient to install surface and subsurface
drainage channels discharging into the river and nearby streams.
      During foundation excavation, create horizontal and longitudinal slopes of 2–3% toward
rivers and streams; combine with longitudinal and transverse canals to ensure effective surface
and groundwater drainage.
       Earthwork and Embankment Construction
   ❖ Dredging and Excavation:
       - Step 1: Use the old canal bed to divert the flow during construction. Remove the
weathered soil within the canal bank area. For sections on the existing canal bed, anti-bog mats
are used to allow the excavator to operate while standing on the mats during construction.
       - Dredging depth: Based on geological documents from the design phase, the average
sludge layer is 0.50 m. Equipment is mobilized at both ends of the canal, and proceeds the
dredging from the ends toward the center.
        - Step 2: Drive bamboo piles to reinforce the foundation within the construction area of
the toe beam for the riverbank revetment. Construct the toe beam, complete the cross-section

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             74
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
of the revetment and canal bank foundation, and simultaneously build the operation and
maintenance road according to the design cross-section.
        - Step 3: Dredging the main canal channel according to design. Excavators are
positioned on both sides of the canal, shape the canal slopes as designed. Excavated material is
loaded onto trucks and transported to designated disposal site.
        - Step 4: Complete embankment foundation according to design cross-section. Finish
the reinforcement of crushed stone layers and concrete on the canal bank.
   ❖ Soil embankment:
       The embankment construction must strictly follow the approved design, TCVN 9165-
2012, and other relevant regulations to ensure required quality.
        - Embankment construction generally includes the following tasks: surface preparation,
soil placement, leveling, watering, and compaction.
          - Soil must be laid in layers and the construction must not be carried out during rainy
season.
       - The embankment construction must be only carried out after the foundation treatment
has been finished and accpeted.
- For the sections with thin manual embankment adjacient to the existing dike body, after
clearning glass, steps must be proceeded. Each step should have a height equal to one layer of
fill soil, a width of 0.2 m to 0.3 m, slightly inclined toward the existing dike body, and the
surface should be roughened before placing the fill.
        - Soil layers are placed both mechanically manually, compacted to achieve design
density γ≥1.65 T/m³ with required compaction rate K≥0.95. Use 9–16T compactors to ensure
suitability for mechanical work on steep and narrow existing dike slopes, while avoiding
excessive excavation into the dike body. During embankment slope excavation and soil grading,
steps must be formed to meet the requirements for filling and compaction at the contact
interface.
        - If heavy rain is forecast during soil spreading, work must be stopped immediately. The
soil surface should be graded with a slope to allow rainwater drainage, then compacted to
prevent the formation of mud puddles that would require post-rain treatment.
         - After the rain, wait until the surface soil evaporates to reach the controlled moisture
level before continuing with soil placement. If any part of the dike surface becomes excessively
soft, it must be removed before further filling. For mud puddles, all mud must be excavated and
replaced with compacted soil to level the surface before proceeding with the next layer of fill.
       - On sunny days, if the surface dries before compaction, dditional water must be sprayed
to ensure controlled moisture content before proceeding with compaction. Watering must be
done evenly, avoiding excessive concentration in one spot to prevent soil softening and mud
formation.
          - Backfill soil must be compacted to the required density.
       - Compactors must operate in a direction parallel to the centerline of the dike and must
not compact in a direction perpendicular to the centerline.
       -When segmenting for compaction, the compacted strip at the boundary between two
adjacent segments must overlap by at least 0.5 meters.
       - Within the same segment, each subsequent compaction pass must overlap the previous
pass by 0.1 to 0.15 meters. The compaction passes of the upper and lower soil layers must not
coincide.
Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             75
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       - Mechanical compaction can be performed with two methods: forward-reverse
compaction and circular path compaction. For circular path compaction, the compactor’s speed
must be reduced in curved sections, and care must be taken to avoid missed spots and to
thoroughly inspect curved areas, as the soil is more prone to damage.
       - When using mechanical compactors, manual compaction is required to reinforce
corners and the edges of the dike where machines cannot reach.
          Pavement construction
   ❖ Formwork, Scaffolding, and Bracing:
   -      Formwork and bracing structure must follow the design and meet the following
          requirements:
            +   Ensure stability, structural integrity, and deformation within allowable limits
                under load.
            +   Ensure correct shape and dimensions as per the design drawings.
            +   Ensure tight sealing to prevent cement slurry leakage.
            +   Scaffolding and working platforms: Must be placed on firm ground to prevent
                slipping. If props are placed on soft soil, wooden pads must be used under the base
                to prevent settlement.
   -      Installation of formwork : Must be based on established survey benchmarks and follow
          the construction design drawings to ensure accurate dimensions, alignment between
          structural components, and correct spatial positioning of the structure.
            +   For critical components, additional control points must be established to facilitate
                inspection of formwork installation.
            +   After the formwork and bracing installation is complete, inspection and
                acceptance must be carried out based on the following criteria: :
            +   Accuracy of the formwork compared to the design.
            +   Accuracy of embedded components.
            +   Tightness between formwork panels and between the formwork and the
                foundation surface or previously poured concrete.
          Stability of the formwork and bracing, with special attention to padding and supports
points.
   ❖ Work Platforms:
       + Work platforms must be sturdy and level. Rotten or decayed wood must not be used.
During concrete transportation, vibrations should be minimized.
        + Work platforms must not be directly connected or braced to the formwork or
reinforcement to avoid displacement of formwork or steel and prevent concrete from being
disturbed during setting period.
   ❖ Rebar bending:
   -      Rebar bending:
      + Rebar must be bent cold; hot bending is strictly prohibited. For deformed bars or
welded steel mesh/frame, hook bending are not required.



Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               76
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       + The starting point of the bend must form a smooth, uniform curve. The bending radius
must be 15 times the diameter of the bar, and the angle and position of the bend must comply
with the design specifications.
        + The bent hooks at both ends of the rebar must face inward toward the structure. For
stirrup rebar with diameters from 6mm to 9mm, the straight segment at the hook end must be
no less than 60mm; for diameters from 10mm to 12mm, it must be no less than 80mm.
   -   Transportation and Installation of Reinforcement steel:
      + Rebar must be transported from the fabrication site to the installation site without
damage or deformation. If deformation occurs, it must be corrected before installation.
      + Positioning, spacing, protective layer thickness, and dimensions of all reinforcement
components must follow the pre-determined layout and comply with the design drawings.
   ❖ Concrete construction works:
       The masonry volume is relatively small, and the structural components are minor, so
the construction method is entirely manual. For mortar mixing, small mixers with capacity of
80–150 l should be used to ensure quality of masonry units. During construction, strict
adherence to the industry standard 14 TCN 12-2002: Hydraulic construction – Stone Masonry
and Paving – Technical Requirements for Construction and Acceptance is required.
   ❖ Installation of precast concrete slab:
       Slab casting:
   -   The slope paving slabs are precast at the casting yard, and their geometric dimensions
       must strictly conform to the design specifications, with a tolerance not exceeding 2mm.




                                       Formwork panel
   ❖ Installation:
   -   Installation of paving slabs is carried out manually. The construction direction proceeds
       from the toe upward to the crest, following a sequential method to prevent displacement
       or erosion of the filter gravel on the slope.
   -   The filter gravel layer must be constructed with the full design thickness; After
       installation, the concrete slabs must be level and conform to the designed slope
       coefficient. Slabs are installed in a staggered pattern as shown in the design drawings.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                           77
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       At gaps adjacent to longitudinal and transverse beams, or where full-size slabs cannot
       be placed, M200 concrete is used to fill in, matching the slab thickness.
   ❖ Embankment construction:
   -   Only after the precast concrete elements reach the required design strength and have
       passed inspection and acceptance may they be transported for slope reinforcement.
   -   Construction includes reinforced concrete toe beams, installation of the precast
       elements, longitudinal beams along the revetment, and the revetment crest.
   ❖ Revetment crest construction:
       Install formwork and pour concrete for the revetment crest road;
       After completion, proceed with dry stones to reinforce the crest slope on the inland side.
   ❖ Grass coverage for slope protection:
Grass for slope protection is sourced from areas near the project site. When planting, select
species with strong, well-developed, and durable roots. Moreover, they must be drought-
resistant and low-growing; tall-stemmed varieties are not permitted. Grass should be cut into
sods, transported to the planting location, and securely pinned to the slope. After planting,
regular watering is required, and any dead patches must be removed and replanted. The
location, dimensions, and layout of the grass planting must follow the procedures specified in
the design documents. Grass must be planted immediately after completing the finishing work
of the structure.
1.7.3. Fishing port operation technology (for Subproject 1 and Subproject 2)
   ❖ Fishing port operation process
The fishing port operation process is as follows: Fishing vessels, seafood collection vessels →
Service port → Market for various products; Supply market.
Fishing port operation equipment
Equipment used at the fishing port includes:
   •   Unloading/loading equipment from fishing vessels to the wharf and receiving/sorting
       facility, including:
           o   Mini cranes, rubber-tired cranes: transport boxed seefoods from vessels to the
               wharf.
           o   Conveyors: transport bagged seafood or torn packaging, etc.
   •   Equipment for transportation from the wharf to the receiving/sorting facilities and from
       the sorting facilities to processing plants: forklifts, conveyors, trucks, and refrigerated
       trucks.
   •   Equipment for transporting from processing plants to the consumption market: trucks,
       refrigerated trucks.
   •   Equipment for transporting from port services back to fishing vessels includes:
           o   Fuel (gasoline/diesel): supplied via direct pipeline pumping.
           o   Ice: conveyor systems.
           o   Fresh water: supplied via direct pipeline pumping.
Lach Truong storm shelter technology



Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             78
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
The main method of mooring vessels currently used at storm shelter areas nationwide involves
grouping fishing vessels side-by-side in clusters of 2 to 6 boats, then anchoring using the
following methods:
Method 1: Self- anchoring
Traditionally, in several localities, fishermen find areas with sufficient depth and shelter from
waves and wind, then drop their vessel anchors. In some cases of severe storms, all fishing gear
and other assets may need to be moved and the vessel intentionally sunk to avoid damage. This
method is only applied to small vessels when the mooring infrastructure is limited.




                Figure 1-2: Mooring boats using the self anchoring method
Method 2: Double-anchor mooring
   •   The group of vessels are moored with the bow tied to a shore-based anchor post and
       the stern anchored to a buoy or underwater anchor post.
   •   Advantages: Keeps the vesselgroup stable, allows more vessels to be moored within
       the same area.
   •   Disadvantages: Subject to lateral wind and wave forces, increasing the risk of
       capsizing.
   •   Suitable for: anchorage areas that require extensive dredging and appropriate for large
       vessels.




   Figure 1-3: Mooring vessels using a combination of mooring buoys and shore-based
                                      anchor post




Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            79
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
              Figure 1-4: Mooring boats using bow and stern mooring buoys
Method 3: Vessel mooring combined both shore-based anchor posts and traditional anchors
   •   The vessel cluster is moored by tying to shore anchor posts while also dropping anchors.
   •   Only applicable for areas sheltered from waves and wind, or for mooring during minor
       storms. During severe storms, vessels are highly prone to collide with the breakwater,
       posing safety risks.




Figure 1-5: Mooring vessels using a combination of shore-based anchor posts and anchors
Method 4: Vessels moored with a single mooring buoy
   •   Vessel clusters are moored to a single buoy, allowing them to rotate around the buoy
       as wind direction changes during a storm.
   •   Advantages: This is the safest mooring method; as vessels always align with the wind
       direction, so there is no lateral force.
   •   Disadvantages: Requires a large mooring area, resulting in significant dredging
       volume for the anchorage zone.




                  Figure 1-6: Vessel mooring with a single mooring buoy

Wastewater sources generated:
   •   Domestic wastewater
      At subproject 1: Wastewater generated from the fishing port logistics area and from
fishermen when vessels dock will be collected and preliminarily treated through two 3-
compartment septic tanks of 10 m³ each (one at the operation building and one at the public
restroom) before being conveyed to the centralized wastewater treatment system with a capacity
of 200 m³/day.
      At subproject 2: Domestic wastewater generated from the fishing port logistics area and
fishing vessels docked will be collected separately and conveyed to the centralized wastewater
treatment system with a capacity of 200 m³/day.
   •   Industrial wastewater
      At subproject 1 and subproject 2: Wastewater from the seafood receiving–sorting area
will be collected via pipes and conveyed to the centralized wastewater treatment system with a
capacity of 200 m³/day.

1.9. Project implementation arrangement
Project implementation arrangement:
   -   Thanh Hoa Provincial People’s Committee is the governing body; the Thanh Hoa
       Project Management Unit of Construction investment works for Agricultural and Rural
       Development is the project owner.
   -   Project management method: The project owner organizes the implementation of the
       construction investment project in accordance with the Land Law, the Law on
       Environmental Protection, and the Construction Law. The project is carried out under
       the form of direct management by the project owner. The project owner will establish a
       Project Management Unit to administer the project implementation.
   -   Project implementation method: The project owner will invest in and construct the
       project in compliance with current investment and construction management regulations
       and other relevant regulations. The content of investment implementation, the schedule
       of construction items, and economic and technical targets shall be carried out according
       to the investment approval resolution of the competent authority.
   -   Construction management:
           o   The project owner, Thanh Hoa Project Management Unit of Construction
               investment works for Agricultural and Rural Development, directly organizes
               and manages the project.
           o   Consultants: Responsible for providing the project owner with consultancy
               service on surveys, design, engineering, and other services during construction,
               as well as supervising and managing construction quality.
           o   Construction teams: Responsible for executing the construction under the
               management of the Project Management Unit and functional departments of the
               Company.

         CHAPTER 2. NATURAL, ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC
                               CONDITIONS
2.1.      NATURAL CONDITIONS
2.1.1. Geographical location
a.       Geographical conditions
Project “Sustainable Fisheries Development in Thanh Hoa Province” comprises 9 subprojects,
covering the territories of 7 communes: Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Hoa
Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh in Thanh Hoa Province. Thanh Hoa Province is located at the
northernmost part of the North Central Coast region of Vietnam, with geographical coordinates
ranging from 19°18'–20°40' North latitude and 104°22'–106°05' East longitude. The
administrative boundaries of Thanh Hoa are defined as follows:
     -   To the north: bordering Hoa Binh, Son La, and Ninh Binh provinces;
     -   To the south: bordering Nghe An province;
     -   To the west: bordering Houaphanh province of the Lao People’s Democratic Republic;
     -   To the east: bordering the East Sea.
The total natural area of Thanh Hoa is 1,113,193.81 hectares (accounting for 3.37% of
Vietnam’s total area), including Agricultural land: 860,843 ha, Forestry land: 600,061 ha,
Special-use land: 70,804.90 ha, Residential land: 52,004.32 ha, Unused land: 88,891 ha. Thanh
Hoa plays a vital role in socio-economic development as well as national defense and security
in both the North Central Coast region and nationwide.
Geographical features of the communes covered by the 9 subprojects are as follows:
     -   Hoang Tien and Hoang Chau communes (Eastern Thanh Hoa): Coastal communes
         characterized by low-lying coastal plains interspersed with estuaries and tidal flats,
         favorable for agriculture, aquaculture, and capture fisheries.
     -   Hoa Loc and Van Loc communes (Northeastern Thanh Hoa): Coastal communes with
         fishing ports and sandy beaches suitable for fishing and seafood processing. The flat
         terrain with wetland and low-lying areas, advantageous for agriculture and aquaculture
         development.
     -   Nga Son and Tan Tien communes (Northeastern Thanh Hoa): Lowland communes with
         extensive wetlands, swamps, and river mouths, favorable for aquaculture. These
         localities are also traditionally known for sedge mat weaving.
     -   Quang Chinh commune (Eastern Thanh Hoa):A coastal commune with flat terrain and
         fertile soil, suitable for agriculture and marine economic development. The coastal zone
         includes tidal flats, aquaculture areas, bathing beaches, and strong potential for marine
         tourism development.
2.1.2. Climate and meteorological conditions
Temperature: During the year, there are two distinct seasons: The cold season from November
to April, with an average temperature of 19.2°C (in 2023). The coldest temperature was
recorded in January 2023 (average 16.1°C); however, on some days the temperature dropped
as low as 7–8°C. The hot season lasts from May to October, with an average temperature of
28°C. The hottest month was June 2023; the average temperature in the month was 29.5°C;
however, peak daily temperatures reaching 38–39°C.
Air Humidity: Air humidity is one of the important factors affecting the process of dispersion
and transformation of pollutants. According to statistics in 2023, the annual average humidity
was 85.4%; with a maximum monthly average of 91%, and a minimum of 79%. Air humidity

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             86
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

varies by season, but is not significant: humidity is slightly lower in the dry season and
moderately higher in the rainy season.
Rainfall: Rainfall plays a key role in purifying pollutants in the air and diluting pollutants in
the water, therefore, the pollution level in the rainy season is often lower than in the dry season.
The rainy season lasts for six months from May to October. The month with the highest rainfall
was October (180.3 mm in 2022, 115.8 mm in 2023); the month with the lowest rainfall was
February (12.8 mm in 2022, 5.2 mm in 2023); the average number of rainy days in the year was
137 days. Rainfall in the year is concentrated from June to the end of October each year and
accounts for about 80% of the total annual rainfall.
Sunshine: On average, sunshine hours increase during the summer months (from May to
October), reaching between 111–197 hours (in 2022) and 136–253 hours (in 2023), with peak
in June, July, and August. Meanwhile, the winter months (from November to February of the
following year) records the lowest sunshine duration, with as little as 4 hours (for example, in
January 2022 there were only 4 sunshine hours).
Fog: Fog often occurs in winter and spring. The number of foggy days in the year is
concentrated in November and December, ranging from 6–8 days, contributing to higher air
and soil humidity.
2.1.3. Topographic and geological features
 2.1.3.1. Topographical features
a. Topographical characteristics of subproject 1
Hoang Tien Commune (formerly Hoang Truong Commune, Hoang Hoa District) is a coastal
alluvial plain commune; however, the terrain is highly dissected. Characteristically located on
the coastal sandy barrier, the foreshore area consists of undulating sand dunes forming a natural
barrier running parallel to the coastline, predominantly covered with perennial grasses and
Casuarina equisetifolia (casuarina trees) serving as windbreaks and sand-fixing vegetation.
Behind the dunes lies a low-lying, narrow depression, topographically lower than the dune belt,
suitable for cultivation of crops such as sweet potatoes, sesame, peanuts, mulberries, and
various vegetables. The area near the foothills of the Linh Truong mountain range has a
significant groundwater reserve, with lower-lying terrain favorable for wet-rice cultivation. The
pedogenesis in this area is primarily influenced by long-term marine sediment deposition
processes: sand accumulation by littoral currents, combined with fluvial alluvium transported
by the Red River and Ma River estuaries. Additionally, alluvium from the Lach Truong River
(historically a distributary of the Ma River), together with estuarine tidal dynamics, has
contributed to sediment accretion over thousands of years, forming the present
geomorphological landscape. The Lach Truong River traverses the northern boundary of the
commune with a length of approximately 2.1 km.
The results of the topographic survey of the project area show that the project site is located
along the southern bank of the Lach Truong River (Lach Truong Estuary), within Hoang Truong
Commune, and includes a portion of land currently being leveled. The terrain elevation across
the study boundary, from the shoreline towards the outer limit of the surveyed area, ranges from
+2.9m to -7.27m




Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                               87
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province




                     Figure 2-1: Topographic plan of the subproject area
b. Topographical characteristics of subproject 2
Hoa Loc Commune (formerly Hoa Loc Commune, Hau Loc District) was formed through long-
term fluvial–marine sediment accretion processes. The terrain is relatively flat, sloping
gradually from the northwest to the southeast, which is favorable for the development of the
fisheries and aquaculture economy.
The subproject area is located on the Lach Truong River sandbank and the Kenh De River
sandbank, approximately 1 km from the Lach Truong Estuary. The Lach Truong River at the
study area has a width ranging from 125 to 200 m, with a relatively steep gradient. At a distance
of about 20 m from the riverbank, the average riverbed elevation reaches -4.30 m.
The navigational channel entering from the Lach Truong Estuary belongs to the inland
waterway route managed by the Vietnam Inland Waterways Administration. The channel has a
width of 40 m, with a 15 m protection corridor. The total width of the channel and its protection
corridor is 70 m.
c. Topographical characteristics of subproject 3
The subproject area is located within the administrative boundaries of Nga Son Commune and
Tan Tien Commune (formerly Nga Thuy, Nga Tan, and Nga Tien Communes of Nga Son
District). The area exhibits the topographical characteristics of the coastal plain, with a
relatively flat terrain dissected by drainage canals. The elevation difference between the
riverbank and the riverbed ranges from 3 to 4 meters. Transportation along the riverbanks is
relatively difficult, especially during the rainy season.
d. Topographical characteristics of subproject 4, 5, 6, 7
he areas of Subprojects 4, 5, 6, and 7 are located within Hoang Tien Commune and Hoang Chau
Commune (formerly Hoang Yen, Hoang Phong, Hoang Luu, and Hoang Chau Communes of
the former Hoang Hoa District). These areas exhibit the typical topographical characteristics of
the coastal plain, with a relatively flat terrain dissected by drainage canals. The elevation
difference between the riverbank and the riverbed ranges from 3 to 4 meters. Some
topographical and geomorphological features in these areas are as follows:
   -   Elevation of dikes and internal roads: (+1.50) ÷ (+2.80).
   -   Elevation of river-side floodplain: (+1.30) ÷ (+1.50).


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            88
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

   -   Elevation of inland fields: (+0.01) ÷ (+0.60).
e. Topographical characteristics of subproject 9
The topography of Quang Chinh Commune (formerly Quang Trung Commune, Quang Xuong
District) belongs to the delta region, characterized by terrain fragmented by a network of rivers
and canals. The Yen River flows along the western and southern boundaries of the commune
with a length of approximately 9.0 km, while the Hoang River passes through the central area
of the commune with a length of about 2.0 km.
 2.1.3.2. Geological Characteristics
a. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 1
Based on the results of the geotechnical investigation for Subproject 1 – Upgrading and
Expanding Hoang Truong Port, conducted by the Consortium of Agricultural and Rural
Infrastructure Construction Consulting Co., Ltd., Minh Khai Holdings Co., Ltd., and
VINASENA Construction Consulting JSC in September 2024. At the construction site, 11
boreholes were drilled. Based on the results of field investigations and laboratory testing, the
stratigraphy of the surveyed area is divided into soil and rock layers from top to bottom as
follows:
Geological Conditions of the Berth and Operation House Area:
   -   Layer 1: Topsoil – Greyish green to dark grey silty mud containing organic matter.
       Flowing state. Average thickness: 0.45 m. To be removed.
   -   Layer 2: Greyish green to dark grey clayey silt containing organic matter. Plastic–
       flowing to flowing state. Thickness varies from 0.8–1.8 m. Average thickness: 1.18 m.
   -   Layer 3: Consists of sub-layers 3a and 3b.
           o   Layer 3a: Silty sand interbedded with thin layers of clayey silt containing
               organic matter, greyish green to dark grey in color. Plastic–flowing state.
               Thickness varies from 0.7–1.4 m. Average thickness: 0.98 m. Bearing capacity
               R₀ = 58 kPa; Deformation modulus E₀ = 3,333 kPa.
           o   Layer 3b: Fine to medium sand containing shell fragments (clams, oysters),
               greyish green to dark grey in color. Loose to medium-dense state, water-
               saturated. Thickness varies from 0.4–1.3 m. Average thickness: 0.85 m. Bearing
               capacity R₀ = 170 kPa; Deformation modulus E₀ = 12,000 kPa.
   -   Layer 4: Gravel mixed with small pebbles and clayey sand, yellowish grey, brownish
       yellow, greyish green to dark grey in color. Medium dense to dense state. Encountered
       only in borehole BCC3. Thickness: 1.70 m.
   -   Layer 5: Strongly weathered siltstone and sandstone, decomposed into sandy silt with
       fine gravel, fragments, few small pebbles, mottled yellowish grey, purplish brown,
       greyish green. Semi-hard state. Thickness varies from 1.5–4.5 m. Average thickness:
       2.50 m.
   -   Layer 6: Moderately to slightly weathered siltstone and sandstone, yellowish grey,
       greyish green, mottled purplish.
           o   Upper part: Fairly hard, brittle, producing fragmented core samples (slam
               structure).
           o   Lower part: Hard, brittle, producing blocky core samples.
               Thickness varies from 3.2–4.5 m. Average thickness: 3.72 m.
Geological Conditions of the Foreport Basin:


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            89
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

    -   Layer 1: Topsoil – Greyish green to dark grey silty mud containing organic matter.
        Flowing state. Average thickness: 0.60 m.
    -   Layer 2: Greyish green to dark grey clayey silt containing organic matter. Flowing to
        plastic–flowing state. Thickness varies from 1.5–2.3 m. Average thickness: 1.96 m.
    -   This is a very weak soil layer with large deformation potential but very low
        permeability. It can support only small structures, and proper ground improvement
        measures are required before placing foundations on this layer.
    -   Layer 3a: Silty sand interbedded with thin layers of clayey silt containing organic
        matter, greyish green to dark grey. Plastic–flowing state. Thickness varies from 2.0–2.4
        m. Average thickness: 2.20 m.
    -   Layer 4: Gravel mixed with small pebbles and clayey sand, yellowish grey, brownish
        yellow, greyish green to dark grey. Average thickness: 0.43 m.
    -   Layer 5: Strongly weathered siltstone and sandstone, decomposed into sandy silt with
        fine gravel, fragments, few small pebbles, mottled yellowish grey, purplish brown,
        greyish green. Semi-hard state. Encountered at the bottom of boreholes LT1 and LT5.
b. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 2
Based on the results of the geotechnical site investigation, the stratigraphy of the surveyed project
area is as follows:
Geology of the Port Construction Area:
    -   Layer 1: Clayey silt containing organic matter, brownish grey to dark grey in color.
        Flowing to plastic–flowing state. Encountered in two boreholes BCC1 and BCC2.
        Thickness varies from 1.2–2.7 m, with an average thickness of 1.95 m.
    -   Layer 2: Fine sand with a small amount of clam and oyster shells and organic matter,
        greyish green to dark grey in color. Loose, water-saturated state. Encountered in
        boreholes BCC1 and BCC2. Thickness is consistent at 2.3 m.
    -   Layer 3: Clayey silt containing organic matter with sand lenses, dark grey to greyish
        green in color. Plastic–flowing state. Encountered in boreholes BCC1 and BCC2.
        Thickness varies from 1.7–3.0 m, with an average thickness of 2.35 m.
    -   Layer 4: Silty clay containing organic matter, dark grey to greyish green in color.
        Plastic–flowing to soft plastic state. Encountered in boreholes BCC1 and BCC2.
        Thickness varies from 5.9–10.9 m, with an average thickness of 8.40 m.
    -   Layer 5: Silty clay with a small amount of gravel, mottled yellowish grey, whitish grey,
        and greyish green in color. Stiff plastic to soft plastic state. Encountered only in borehole
        BCC1. Thickness is 3.00 m.
    -   Layer 6: Strongly weathered shale and siltstone, decomposed into silty clay with gravel,
        fragments, and small blocks, mottled yellowish grey, brownish yellow, greyish green,
        and reddish brown in color. Semi-hard state. Encountered in boreholes BCC1 and
        BCC2. Thickness varies from 4.4–4.6 m, with an average thickness of 4.50 m.
    -   Layer 7 (coded as No. 7 on the geological cross-section): Moderately to slightly
        weathered shale and siltstone, mottled yellowish grey, brownish yellow, and reddish
        brown in color. Upper part: Fairly soft, friable rock, producing fragmented core samples
        (slam structure). Lower part: Fairly hard rock, brittle, producing blocky core samples.
        The top and bottom depths of this layer were not fully penetrated in either borehole
        BCC1 or BCC2. Thickness ranges from 3.0–5.0 m, with an average thickness of 4.0 m.
Geology of the Foreport Basin:

Geology of the Lach Truong Fishing Vessel Anchorage Area
Based on borehole exploration in the surveyed area with a maximum depth of 5.0 m, combined
with laboratory soil testing, the stratigraphy consists of 4 layers from top to bottom:
   -   Layer 1 (coded as No. 1 on the geological cross-section): Clayey silt containing organic
       matter, brownish grey, dark grey, and greyish green in color. Flowing to plastic–flowing
       state (locally interbedded with thin silty sand layers). Encountered in all boreholes of
       both components. Thickness varies from 2.3–5.0 m, with an average thickness of 3.81
       m.

       and organic matter, greyish green to dark grey in color. Loose, water-saturated state.
       Encountered in boreholes KND1 and KND2. Thickness varies from 0.9–1.0 m, with an
       average thickness of 0.95 m.
   -   Layer 2b (coded as No. 2b): Fine sand with a high content of organic matter and
       clam/oyster shells, greyish green to dark grey in color. Loose, water-saturated state.
       Encountered in boreholes KND4 and KND5. Thickness varies from 1.2–2.3 m, with an

       silty sand layers, dark grey to greyish green in color. Plastic–flowing state. Encountered
       in boreholes KND1 and KND2 of both components. Thickness varies from 1.1–1.8 m,
       with an average thickness of 1.45 m.
c. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 3
Engineering Geology of Phu Son Sluice
Based on the borehole exploration data in the surveyed area, with a maximum exploration depth
of 9.0m combined with soil sample testing results, the stratigraphy of Phu Son 2 Sluice consists

   -   First layer: In the geological profile designated as No. 1. Alluvial channel bed deposit:

       plastic (flow to soft plastic). Encountered in boreholes HKC2 and HKC3. Thickness
       varies from 0.3 – 0.4m, with an average thickness of 0.35m.
   -   Second layer: In the geological profile designated as Layer Đ. This layer is only found
       in borehole HKC1. Thickness: 4.2m.
   -   Third layer: In the geological profile designated as No. 2. Silty clay containing organic
       matter, dark gray to grayish green. State: very soft plastic. Encountered in all three
       boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3. Thickness ranges from 0.8 – 1.2m, with an average
       thickness of 0.93m.
   -   Fourth layer: In the geological profile designated as No. 3. Sandy clay (clayey silt) in
       brownish gray, pinkish brown, dark brown. State: very soft plastic. Encountered in all
       three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3. Thickness ranges from 3.6 – 5.9m, with an
       average thickness of 5.10m.

d. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 4
According to the results of borehole investigations and laboratory testing, the stratigraphic
profile of the area consists of the following layers:
   -   Layer B: Composition: Silty clay, gray-brown, grayish-green, grayish-black in color,
       containing organic humus. State: Soft plastic – fluid-plastic. Areal distribution: This
       layer occurs at the surface, mainly within the canal bed and adjacent paddy fields on
       both canal banks, with a thickness ranging from 0.3–0.4 m. Geotechnical characteristics:
       Due to its composition and unstable condition, being located immediately at the surface.
   -   Layer Đ1 – Road embankment fill: Composition: Clay to clayey silt with gravel
       fragments, yellow-gray to reddish-brown in color, semi-hard consistency. Origin: Man-


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                             92
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

       made embankment fill. Areal distribution: Distributed along the road embankment (left
       bank of the canal). Average thickness: ~1.5 m.
   -   Layer Đ – Canal embankment fill: Composition: Clay, yellow-brown in color, stiff-
       plastic, locally semi-hard. Origin: Man-made embankment fill. Areal distribution:
       Distributed along the canal embankment (right bank of the canal). Average thickness:
       ~2.2 m.
   -   Layer 1:Composition: Clay, grayish-green to grayish-black, containing organic
       inclusions. State: Soft-plastic – fluid-plastic. Origin: Fluvio-marine deposits (amQIV).
       Areal distribution: Extensively distributed throughout the surveyed canal alignment,
       underlying silt Layer B. Thickness: From K0 to K2, the average thickness is about 1.0
       m; from K2 to the end of the alignment, thickness is >2.0 m.
   -   Layer 2: Composition: Clay, grayish-green to grayish-gray in color, State: Stiff-plastic,
       Origin: Fluvio-marine deposits (amQIV). Areal distribution: Occurs beneath Layer 1,
       at the initial section of the alignment from K0 to K0+300. Thickness: Thin (<0.5 m);
       the maximum thickness recorded at borehole HK2 is 0.5 m.
   -   Layer C: Composition: Fine-grained sand, grayish-green in color, intercalated with silty
       clay, loose structure. Origin: Fluvio-marine deposits (amQIV). Areal distribution:
       Found beneath Layer 1, distributed at the downstream section of the canal alignment,
       from K3+500 to the end. Maximum thickness: 1.5 m (recorded at borehole HK15).
e. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 5, 6, and 7
   -   Layer B: Silty clay, grayish-brown to dark gray in color, containing plant humus.
       Thickness ranges from 0.3–0.7 m.
   -   Layer Đ (Embankment fill): Fill soil composed of clay and clayey silt, yellowish-brown
       in color. Consistency: soft-plastic to stiff-plastic. This layer is distributed along the
       alignment of the dyke embankment, paddy field margins, and internal rural roads.
       Thickness varies from 0.7–2.5 m.
   -   Layer 1: Clay, grayish-green to dark gray, containing organic matter, locally
       intercalated with sand lenses. Consistency: soft-plastic to fluid-plastic. This layer occurs
       extensively across the surveyed area. Average thickness is approximately 2.2–3.0 m.
   -   Layer 2: Fine-grained sand, grayish-green to grayish-brown in color, locally intercalated
       with clay and clayey silt, medium-dense to loose structure. This layer occurs extensively
       across the surveyed area, with an average thickness of about 3.50 m.
The stratigraphy within the project construction area consists of soil layers with relatively
uniform distribution; however, bearing capacity varies, particularly in Layer 1. Therefore, the
following considerations should be taken into account during design and construction:
   -   Appropriate ground improvement measures should be adopted to treat weak soil layers.
       Specific treatment depth should be determined through design analysis and selection.
       During construction, attention should be paid to groundwater seepage into excavation
       pits.
   -   When constructing structural components, stockpiling of materials should be avoided at
       canal banks, weak points, or vulnerable slopes to prevent bank sliding or collapse.
   -   Heavy vehicles exceeding the allowable load should not be permitted to travel or operate
       on the dyke, to ensure safety for local residents and structural stability of the dyke
       alignment.
f. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 8.
The stratigraphic structure of the surveyed area from top to bottom is as follows:

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              93
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

   -   First layer: Alluvial channel fill deposit: Grayish-black silt containing organic humus.
       Consistency: soft plastic to flowing plastic. This layer was encountered in boreholes
       HKC2 and HKC3. The thickness of the layer is constant at 0.50 m.
   -   Second layer: Concrete pavement of the culvert made of aggregate 1x2 with a thickness
       of 0.20 m. The embankment soil consists of excavated waste soil: silty clay mixed with
       various types of brick and stone fragments, grayish-brown to yellowish-brown in color.
       Consistency: dense to medium dense, soft plastic. This layer was encountered only in
       borehole HKC1. The thickness of the layer is 1.0 m.
   -   Third layer: This layer was encountered in all three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and
       HKC3. The thickness of the layer varies from 0.50 m to 1.50 m, with an average
       thickness of 0.87 m.
   -   Fourth layer: Silty clay containing organic humus and interbedded lenses of silty fine
       sand, grayish-black to bluish-gray in color. Consistency: flowing plastic. This layer was
       encountered in all three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3. The thickness of the layer
       is constant at 1.50 m.
   -   Fifth layer: Silty clay, grayish-brown to dark brown in color. Consistency: flowing
       plastic. This layer was encountered in all three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3.
       The thickness of the layer varies from 0.60 m to 0.80 m, with an average thickness of
       0.70 m.
   -   Sixth layer: Medium-grained sand containing organic humus, bluish-gray to grayish-
       black in color. State: loose, saturated. This layer was encountered in all three boreholes
       HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3. The thickness of the layer varies from 0.60 m to 0.70 m.
   -   Seventh layer: Silty clay, grayish-black to bluish-gray in color. Consistency: soft plastic
       to flowing plastic. This layer was encountered in all three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and
       HKC3. The thickness of the investigated layer varies from 1.00 m to 1.60 m, with an
       average investigated thickness of 1.23 m.
g. Geological Characteristics of Subproject 9
The stratigraphic structure of the survey area from top to bottom is as follows:
   -   First layer: Alluvial channel fill deposit: Bluish-gray to dark gray silt containing organic
       humus. Consistency: soft plastic to flowing plastic. This layer was encountered in
       boreholes HKC2 and HKC3. The thickness of the layer is constant at 0.30 m.
   -   Second layer: Embankment soil (dyke fill): Silty clay, grayish-brown, grayish, to
       yellowish-brown in color. Consistency: medium dense to dense, moist, soft plastic to
       stiff plastic (with desiccation cracks resembling bird-foot patterns, resulting in high
       drilling fluid loss). This layer was encountered only in borehole HKC1. The thickness
       of the layer is 1.80 m.
   -   Third layer: Silty clay containing organic humus, bluish-gray to dark gray in color.
       Consistency: flowing plastic. This layer was encountered in all three boreholes HKC1,
       HKC2, and HKC3. The thickness of the layer varies from 1.20 m to 2.80 m, with an
       average thickness of 1.77 m.
   -   Fourth layer: Sandy silt mixed with silt containing organic humus, bluish-gray to dark
       gray in color. Consistency: plastic to flowing plastic (intercalated with thin layers of
       silty clay containing organic humus, dark gray in color, consistency ranging from
       flowing plastic to soft plastic). This layer was encountered in all three boreholes HKC1,
       HKC2, and HKC3. The thickness of the layer is constant at 3.40 m.
   -   Fifth layer: Silty fine to very fine sand containing organic humus, bluish-gray to dark
       gray in color. Consistency: loose to medium dense, saturated. This layer was

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              94
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

         encountered in all three boreholes HKC1, HKC2, and HKC3. The thickness of the
         investigated layer varies from 1.00 m to 2.00 m, with an average investigated thickness
         of 1.37 m.
2.1.4.    Hydrological Conditions
The subprojects within the project scope are located in coastal and lowland areas across seven
communes of Thanh Hoa Province, including Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien,
Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh. These areas are influenced by major river basins such
as the Ma River, Yen River, Lach Truong River, Len River, and Can River. Specifically:
   -     Subproject (Subp) 1, Subp 2, and Subp 4, 5, 6, 7 are situated within the Lach Truong
         River basin (a branch of the Ma River).
   -     Subp 3 is influenced by the Len River (a branch of the Ma River).
   -     Subp 8 is located near the lower reaches of the Can River.
   -     Subp 9 lies within the Yên River basin, one of the major rivers of Thanh Hoa Province.
The hydrological characteristics of the main rivers in the project area are described as follows:
   ❖ Ma River
The Ma River is 512 km long, originating from the Vietnam–Laos border area, in the southern
part of Dien Bien Province, where it discharges into the sea at the Hoi estuary. Its drainage
basin covers approximately 28,400 km², of which over 10,000 km² is within Vietnam. The river
network density is 0.66 km/km², with 39 primary tributaries, among which the Chu River, Len
River, and Lach Truong River are the main distributaries. The Ma River exhibits a flood season
flow of approximately 650–750 m³/s and a dry season flow of around 50–90 m³/s. The river
experiences irregular tidal influences, which are particularly pronounced during the dry season,
contributing to droughts and saline intrusion in the lower reaches.
   ❖ Can River
The Can River is located along the border of Ninh Binh and Thanh Hoa provinces in Vietnam.
It essentially represents the lower reach formed by the confluence of four upstream rivers: Tong
River, Hoat River, Tam Diep River, and But River. The Can River serves as a flood drainage
channel and acts as a natural boundary of the North Central region. Its flood season flow is
approximately 100–120 m³/s, while the dry season flow is low, only about 10–25 m³/s, which
can lead to localized droughts.
   ❖ Yen River
The Yen River (also known as Muc River or Chuoi River, with a branch called Nha Le River)
is one of the rivers in Thanh Hoa Province. It originates in Binh Luong (Nhu Xuan), flows
through Nhu Thanh, Nong Cong, Quang Xuong, Nghi Son, and empties into the East Sea at the
Lach Ghep estuary. The river is 94.2 km long, with a drainage basin area of 1,996 km² (49.5%
lowlands and semi-mountainous areas, 45.2% forested and mountainous areas). The total runoff
of the Yen River is 961×10⁶ m³ during the flood season and 185×10⁶ m³ in the dry season. Its
main tributaries include the Nhom River, Hoang River, ThiLong River, and Ly River, which
help regulate floods and distribute water during the dry season.
   ❖ Len River
The Len River shares its water source with the Ma River at Bong and discharges into the sea at
the Lach Sung estuary. The river is 40 km long, with a flood season flow of approximately 100–
150 m³/s and a dry season flow of 30–50 m³/s derived from the Ma River to meet domestic and
agricultural demands. The river plays a significant role in supplying irrigation water and flood
drainage for coastal areas. Both banks are protected by dikes to safeguard local communities
and agricultural production.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            95
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

   ❖ Lach Truong River
The Lach Truong River branches off from the Ma River at Tuan Junction, flowing eastwards
into the sea at the Lach Truong estuary. The main river is 22 km long, with wide riverbanks.
Water from the Ma River only flows into Lach Truong during the flood season, while in the dry
season, Lach Truong is affected by tidal influences from both the Ma River and the sea. Lach
Truong serves as a key drainage channel for the Hoang Hoa and Hau Loc areas.
2.1.5.    Natural Resources
The subprojects are located in the communes of Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien,
Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh – which are coastal communes of Thanh Hoa Province.
The local economy is primarily based on agriculture and aquaculture. Specifically, the coastal
areas (Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Hoa Loc, Van Loc) have well-developed aquaculture models
including clam farming, tiger shrimp, and brackish water fish, while the inland lowland areas
mainly cultivate rice, short-cycle crops, and fruit trees. Overall, the project area is
predominantly an agricultural ecosystem, with no presence of rare or endangered species listed
in the Red Book.
Terrestrial Ecosystems:
The vegetation surrounding the project area is primarily composed of agricultural ecosystems,
including:
   -     Plants: Eucalyptus trees and various fruit trees such as jackfruit, longan, pomelo, and
         bananas; as well as wild grasses and single-season rice cultivated by local households.
   -     Animals: Terrestrial fauna include soil-dwelling species such as reptiles, amphibians
         (frogs), birds, and rodents.
Aquatic Ecosystems:
   -     Plants: The project area is associated with river and lake systems including Thach Luyen
         River, Thuong Hoa Lake, and Bang River. The dominant aquatic vegetation includes
         planktonic algae such as diatoms (Silic algae), cyanobacteria, green algae, and
         filamentous algae, as well as floating plants such as water hyacinth and cultivated
         single-season rice. Algal diversity is higher in natural water bodies compared to
         aquaculture ponds.
   -     Animals: The aquatic fauna in the project area includes groups such as Protozoa,
         Copepoda (copepods), Cladocera (water fleas), Rotatoria (rotifers), Ostracoda (seed
         shrimps), and insect larvae. Among these, rotifers are the most abundant group,
         followed by copepods. Additionally, the water bodies host a diversity of aquatic animals
         including shrimp, fish, snails, and freshwater mussels.
Mangrove Forests:
   -     Mangrove forests in Thanh Hoa Province play an important role in environmental
         protection, disaster prevention, and supporting livelihoods for coastal communities. The
         total mangrove forest area in Thanh Hoa is estimated at approximately 822 ha, mostly
         planted forests, distributed mainly along the coastal areas of Hoang Hoa, Hau Loc, and
         Nga Son. Mangrove forests mitigate the impacts of sea waves and tidal surges, protect
         dikes, and safeguard coastal settlements. Since 2017, Thanh Hoa Province has replanted
         and restored over 337 ha of mangroves, particularly in Da Loc Commune in Hau Loc
         District.
   -     Several clusters of mangrove forests located 2–5 km away from the project area, serving
         as wave barriers and protecting coastal dikes and residential areas. However, all
         subproject locations are situated more than 2 km away from these mangrove areas or
         their buffer zones.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            96
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

In conclusion, the project area is characterized solely by conventional agricultural and
aquaculture ecosystems; there are no rare or specially protected species, and the subprojects
do not have negative impacts on mangrove ecosystems.
2.1.6.   Natural Disasters and Climate Change
The coastal and lowland areas of Thanh Hoa Province frequently experience tropical storms,
which can significantly affect the coastal districts of the province from July to November each
year. Common natural disasters in the area include storms, storm surges, heavy rainfall causing
flooding, and flash floods, typically concentrated in September. Severe droughts and cold spells
usually occur from December to February. Additionally, tornadoes, whirlwinds, and hailstorms
may occasionally occur, though with low frequency.
According to the annual summary reports on natural disaster prevention and control in Thanh
Hoa Province, in recent years the province has experienced an average of 30 disaster events per
year, including 3 tropical storms, 1 tropical depression, 8 heavy rainfall events, 5 tornadoes,
thunderstorms with rain, 4 landslides, 6 heatwaves, and 3 cold spells. Typical events are
described as follows:
   + Heavy Rainfall: Occurred on 05–06/5, 06–10/6, 25/6, 07–10/7, 26–28/8, 07–09/9, 13–
     15/9, and 24–27/9, resulting in 1 death; 23 houses damaged; 202 ha of rice fields, 398
     ha of seasonal crops, 587 ha of annual plantations, and 13 ha of forest damaged; 9
     livestock and 55 poultry killed; 247 sites of road embankment erosion with a total
     volume of approximately 59,000 m³; and other property losses.
   + Thunderstorms, Tornadoes, and Lightning with Rain: Occurred on 03/5, 09/5, 13/5,
     29/7, and 05/9 in the districts of Nhu Xuan, Quan Hoa, Nhu Thanh, and Thuong Xuan,
     resulting in 2 deaths (due to lightning); damage included 5 houses, 43 ha of rice, 5 ha of
     forest, and 1 ha of seasonal crops, as well as other assets.
   + Heatwaves: From 20–22/4, 09–23/5, 26–31/5, 14–30/6, 01–04/7, and 25–31/7/2021,
     severe heatwaves occurred in many parts of the province with maximum temperatures
     ranging from 37–41°C and minimum relative humidity of 40–55%.
   + Storm No. 2 (June 2021): From the night of 11/6 to the morning of 13/6/2021, Thanh
     Hoa Province experienced rain, moderate to heavy in some areas, with total rainfall
     ranging from 30–80 mm, peaking at 108 mm at Ngoc Tra Telemetry Station, Quang
     Xuong. Impacts included 9 houses, 2 ha of maize, 63 ha of vegetables, road embankment
     erosion at 224 locations with a total volume of approximately 17,280 m³, and other
     property losses.
   + Storm No. 8 (October 2021): From 13–16/10/2021, the province experienced rainfall of
     80–140 mm due to the combined effects of Storm No. 8, cold air influx, and tropical
     convergence over the North Central region. Some lowland districts recorded rainfall up
     to 190 mm, causing 1 house to be urgently relocated, 7 houses affected by landslides, 3
     school sites impacted, 2 ha of rice, 90 ha of seasonal crops damaged, and road
     embankment erosion at 31 locations totaling approximately 6,530 m³, as well as other
     property losses.
Damage from Natural Disasters :
In 2021, natural disasters in Thanh Hoa Province caused 3 deaths and 1 injury; damaged 39
houses, required urgent relocation for 5 houses, 33 houses affected by landslides; destroyed 260
ha of rice, 2 ha of maize, 552 ha of seasonal crops and vegetables, 590 ha of annual plantations;
killed 9 livestock and 55 poultry; caused road embankment erosion and sediment deposition at
651 locations with a total volume of approximately 182,610 m³, undercut slope erosion at 44
locations totaling 295 m in length, and other losses. The estimated total damage value was
approximately 66.5 billion VND.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              97
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

In 2023, Thanh Hoa Province experienced 25 natural disaster events, including 5 tornadoes, 7
heavy rainfall events, 1 tropical storm, and 12 heatwaves. In 2024, the province experienced 27
natural disaster events, including storms, heavy rainfall, floods, and landslides, causing
significant human and property losses. Detailed damage reports have not yet been fully
released.
2.2.     CURRENT STATUS OF ENVIRONMENTAL COMPONENT QUALITY

coordinated with the environmental monitoring unit, KGZ Investment Joint Stock Company
(holding VIMCERTS Certificate No. 320), to conduct surveys, measurements, and
environmental sampling and analyses (including air, surface water, groundwater, wastewater,
soil, and sediment) at each subproject site. The environmental sampling and analysis were
conducted on October 23, 24, and 30, 2024. The information on the quality of environmental

2.3.     SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONDITIONS
The “Sustainable Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province” comprises nine
subprojects, spanning the administrative boundaries of seven communes: Hoang Tien, Hoang
Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh in Thanh Hoa Province. These
areas were formerly part of 11 communes across four districts, including: Hoang Trương,
Hoang Yen, Hoang Phong, Hoang Luu, and Hoang Chau in the former Hoang Hoa District;
Hoa Loc and Da Loc in the former Hau Loc District; Nga Thuy, Nga Tan, and Nga Tien in the
former Nga Son District; and Quang Trung in the former Quang Xuong District.
Currently, there are no socio-economic statistics available for the seven newly merged
communes following the administrative boundary reorganization effective from July 1, 2025.
Therefore, this report uses the 2024 statistical data of the 11 former communes as baseline
information for assessing the environmental and social impacts of the project.
2.3.1. Social conditions
Population: According to the 2024 statistical data provided by the People’s Committees of the
11 former communes (currently consolidated into 7 communes) within the project area, the total
population is 86,228 people, equivalent to 20,462 households, accounting for approximately
2.33% of Thanh Hoa Province’s total population (3.7 million people).
Regarding gender structure, the male population in the project area is 43,220, representing
50.1%, while the female population is 39,880, representing 49.9%. Similarly, the male-to-
female ratio for Thanh Hoa Province is comparable, with males accounting for approximately
Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              101
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

50.2% and females 49.8%. Thus, the gender distribution within the project area is relatively
balanced and consistent with the provincial average, although minor variations exist between
communes. For example, Nga Tien Commune has a notable male predominance (5,571 males
and 2,858 females), whereas Hoang Yen Commune has slightly more females than males (2,185
males and 2,257 females).
Population Density: The project area has a population density of 877 people/km², significantly
higher than the provincial average of 315 people/km², meaning the project area’s density is
approximately 2.78 times higher than that of the province. Certain communes have densities
above the average, such as Hoang Truong (1,921 people/km²), Hoa Loc (1,696 people/km²),
and Quang Trung (910.9 people/km²), while communes like Nga Tan (450 people/km²) and
Hoang Yen (448.2 people/km²) have lower densities.

Working-Age Population: The proportion of the working-age population varies significantly
among the communes within the project area. Across the total population of each commune,
the working-age group ranges from 18.3% to 75.7%. Hoang Yen Commune has the highest
proportion of working-age residents at 75.7%, reflecting a young population structure and
abundant human resources. Other communes with relatively high labor participation include
Nga Tan (63.1%), Quang Trung (62.1%), Da Loc (62.1%), and Hoang Phong (61.5%).
Conversely, Hoang Luu Commune has the lowest proportion of working-age population at only
18.3%, indicating a relatively limited labor force in this locality, which may be due to an aging
population or high out-migration of workers.
