Income: According to the per capita income data in 2024, the communes within the project
area have income levels higher than the provincial average of Thanh Hoa (VND 55.2
million/person/year). Most communes in the project area exceed this provincial average, except
for Da Loc Commune (VND 55.1 million/person/year), which is almost equivalent to the
provincial average. Quang Trung Commune has the highest income, reaching VND 76.5
million/person/year, approximately VND 21.3 million (38.6%) higher than the provincial
average. Other communes with relatively high income include Hoang Truong (VND 73.7
million), Hoang Luu (VND 68 million), Hoang Chau (VND 65.3 million), and Hoang Phong
(VND 65 million). Conversely, communes with average income levels include Nga Tan, Nga
Tien, Hoang Yen (all VND 59 million), Nga Thuy (VND 60 million), and Hoa Loc (VND 57
million). The income gap among communes is quite pronounced, with the highest-income
commune (Quang Trung) exceeding the lowest-income commune (Da Loc) by VND 21.4
million/person/year, equivalent to 38.9%. This reflects differences in economic conditions,
production and service activities, and the level of infrastructure investment among the
communes. Overall, the project area has a per capita income higher than the Thanh Hoa
provincial average, but significant disparities still exist between the communes.




Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                        103
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province




           Figure 2-3: Average income per capita in communes in the project area
                (Source: People's Committees of communes, November 2024))
Poverty Rate: Data from the People’s Committees of the communes in the project area indicate
that the multidimensional poverty rate in 2024 ranges from 0.46% to 3.19%, all of which are
lower than the provincial average of 6.52%. Some communes have higher poverty rates
compared to the regional average, including Nga Thuy (3.19%), Hoang Yen (2.98%), and Nga
Tien (2.67%). Conversely, the communes with the lowest poverty rates are Hoang Phong
(0.46%), Hoang Luu (0.48%), and Hoang Chau (0.62%). The gap between the commune with
the highest poverty rate (Nga Thuy) and the lowest (Hoang Phong) reaches 2.73%, reflecting
significant differences in economic conditions and living standards among the communes in
the project area.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                         104
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

2.3.2. Economic Structure
Economic Structure: The economic structure of the communes in the project area shows a clear
differentiation among three sectors: agriculture, forestry and fisheries; industry and
construction; and services and handicrafts. Some communes remain primarily agricultural,
notably Nga Tien (59.6%), Hoang Truong (54%), Hoa Loc (52%), and Da Loc (49.5%). These
communes have a high proportion of agricultural activities, reflecting dependence on crop
production and aquaculture, while industry and services remain underdeveloped—for instance,
Nga Tiến has only 19.4% in services and Hoang Truong only 21% in industry.
In contrast, some communes are experiencing a strong shift towards industry and construction,
highlighted by Nga Tan (42.5%), Hoang Yen (39%), Hoang Luu and Hoang Chau (40%).
Meanwhile, certain communes show a well-developed service sector, such as Quang Trung
(38.7%), Hoang Phong (36.9%), Hoang Luu (35%), and Hoang Chau (32%). These communes
may have advantages in trade, consumer services, or tourism. However, Da Loc has the lowest
service proportion (10.3%), indicating that its economy still relies predominantly on agriculture
and industry.
Table 2-3: Economic structure of communes in the project area (Unit: %)
                                       Agriculture, forestry     Industry and      Services and
    Subproject          Commune
                                          and fisheries          construction      handicrafts
    Subproject 1     Hoang Truong               54                    21                25
    Subproject 2     Hoa Loc                    52                    19                29
                     Nga Tan                   31,5                  42,5               26
    Subproject 3     Nga Tien                  59,6                   21               19,4
                     Nga Thuy                   40                    30                30
    Subproject 4     Hoang Yen                  33                    39                28
    Subproject 5     Hoang Phong               31,6                  31,5              36,9
    Subproject 6     Hoang Luu                  25                    40                35
    Subproject 7     Hoang Chau                 28                    40                32
    Subproject 8     Da Loc                    49,5                  40,2              10,3
    Subproject 9     Quang Trung               30,5                  30,8              38,7
                                 (Source: People's Committees of communes, November 2024)
   ❖ Fisheries Exploitation Conditions
Fishing activities are shifting from nearshore to offshore exploitation, accompanied by an
increase in the capacity of larger vessels and the adoption of modern equipment. The annual
catch has steadily increased, reaching approximately 130,200 tons in 2020, including: marine
capture fisheries: ~120,860 tons, inland capture: ~4,140 tons. The offshore fleet (vessels ≥15
m) comprises 1,337 vessels. The estimated fishery resource stock is around 165,000 tons, of
which offshore areas account for ~100,000 tons and nearshore & inshore areas ~65,000 tons.
The offshore catch in 2020 reached 86,212 tons.
   ❖ Aquaculture Conditions
Aquaculture in the region has developed steadily, with a trend toward implementing best
practice models (VietGAP), including certification and traceability systems. Both cultivated
area and production volume have increased consistently, especially in rice-fish systems and
freshwater and brackish water aquaculture. The primary farming methods are improved
extensive systems, gradually transitioning to semi-intensive, intensive, and integrated farm
operations. Productivity is approximately 2.2 tons/ha for improved extensive systems and 8–10
tons/ha for intensive systems.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                           105
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

2.4. CURRENT STATUS OF TECHNICAL INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE
SUBPROJECT AREA
2.4.1. Transportation System
    Current Transport Conditions – Subproject 1
   ❖ Road Transport:
The project site borders the existing external road connecting Hoang Yen – Hoang Truong and
links to Provincial Road D.510. The section passing through the project area has a length of
850 m, with a concrete pavement structure. The carriageway is 5.5 m wide, and the total width
including the pavement and shoulders averages 8 m. Along the southern side of the road is the
existing residential area, while the northern side borders the waters of Lạch Truong. There are
currently no designated parking lots in the area; vehicles are mostly parked at private
residences.
   ❖ Inland Waterway Channel
The inland waterway channel extends from the western side of Lach Truong River mouth to the
sea. The channel has a width of 40 m, with a protected corridor of 15 m on each side, resulting
in a total water surface width of 70 m (Source: Directorate for Inland Waterways). The edge of
the protected corridor of the inland waterway passing through the study area is located
approximately 15–55 m south of the existing embankment. There is currently no centralized
clean water supply in the project area.


    Current Transport Conditions – Subproject 2
   ❖ External Transport
The northern dike road of Hoa Loc Port and the western road of the Fishery Port Management
Board (Cross-section 1-1) connect to Xuan Loc Commune to the west and Diem Pho Urban
Area to the north. Within the planned area, the road length is approximately 436 m, with a
roadbed width of 7.5–10.0 m, carriageway width of 6.5–7.0 m, shoulder width of 0.5–1.5 m,
and a cement concrete pavement.
The western dike road along Kenh De (Cross-section 2-2) connects to Diem Pho Urban Area
to the north and Xuan Loc Commune to the west. The road length within the planned area is
approximately 1.3 km, with a roadbed width of 4.5–7.5 m, carriageway width of 3.0–4.0 m,
shoulder width of 0.5–2.0 m, and a cement concrete pavement.
The eastern dike road along Kenh De (Cross-section 3-3) connects to Minh Loc Commune to
the north and south, and Hai Loc Commune to the east. The road length within the planned area
is approximately 2.0 km, with a roadbed width of 6.5–10.5 m, carriageway width of 4.5–5.5 m,
shoulder width of 1.0–3.0 m, and a cement concrete pavement.
   ❖ Internal Transport:
Within the planned area, there is Vich ferry dock to the north and Lach Truong ferry dock to
the south, connecting Hoa Loc Commune in the west with Hai Loc Commune in the east.
At Hoa Loc Port, there is a pier with a width of 11.5–13.5 m and a port yard with dimensions
of 49.0–68.0 m. Both the pier and yard are constructed with cement concrete.
    Current Transport Conditions – Subproject 3
The transportation routes leading to the project area include the main roads as follows: Inter-
communal Road Nga Son – Tan Tien: Carriageway width of 4.5–5.5 m, shoulder width of 1.0–
3.0 m, with cement concrete pavement. Provincial Road 527B: Length of approximately 12 km,
asphalt concrete pavement, road width about 6.5–7.0 m. Provincial Road 524: Asphalt concrete

pavement, road width about 6.5–7.0 m. National Route 10: Length of 45 km, carriageway width

reinforced with concrete and connected to the main roads in the area. The regional transport

2.4.2. Electricity and Telecommunications
   -   Subproject 1: The national electricity grid passes through the area, ensuring power
       supply for local residents and the subproject.
   -   Subproject 2: There is a 10 kV medium-voltage power line and 3 transformer stations
       with a total capacity of 1,060 kVA, ensuring electricity supply for the port. The existing
       telecommunications infrastructure includes three operators (Vinaphone, Viettel,
       Mobifone), providing 3G, 4G, and 5G services.
   -   Subprojects 3 & 4: Electricity supply systems are already available for residential,
       production, and business purposes.
   -   Subprojects 5–9: The national electricity grid passes through these areas, ensuring
       power supply for local residents and the subprojects.
2.4.3. Water Supply
   -   Subproject 1: The existing water supply comes from the Hai Tien Tourist Area clean
       water system. Currently, water is supplied through a D160 pipeline along Đ.510 road
       leading to But Pagoda.
   -   Subproject 2 : The existing water supply comes from the Bai Trung clean water plant
       system. A D160 pipeline runs along the road to Hoa Loc Commune (the western road
       of Hoa Loc Fisheries Industrial Cluster).
   -   Subprojects 3–9: There is no centralized clean water supply for residential areas.
       Households near these subprojects currently rely on drilled wells. During the
       construction phase, arrangements will be made to connect to existing household water
       sources or purchase clean water (tank or container).
2.4.4. Drainage, Wastewater Treatment, and Environmental Sanitation
    Subprojects 1 and 2
      There is no separate drainage or wastewater treatment system. Domestic wastewater and
rainwater flow together through roadside ditches and are discharged directly into the Lach
Truong River, causing unsanitary conditions and environmental pollution.
       A complete waste collection and treatment system is not in place. Some waste is buried,
burned on-site, or dumped near riverbanks or the sea; the remainder is collected and transported
to centralized treatment facilities (for SP 2). Waste mainly originates from port activities,
residents, and seafood processing facilities. Wastewater discharge, oil, and grease from fishing
vessels into rivers and the sea are common, polluting water and affecting the coastal landscape.
    Subprojects 3–9: There is no wastewater drainage system or solid waste collection system.
2.4.5. Current Status of Fishing Ports, Fishing Vessels, and Aquatic Resource Exploitation
    Subproject 1

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                           108
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

Current Status of Fishing Ports
Hoang Truong Fishing Port was built in 2008 and put into operation in 2009. Classified as a
Class III fishing port, it can accommodate 50 vessels of 450 CV, with a seafood throughput of
5,500 tons/year.
Existing Infrastructure:
   -    The quay embankment for mooring spans approximately 400 m within the project
        boundary. The embankment is constructed of tightly packed rubble stone, with several
        degraded sections and sediment accumulation along the embankment. Illegal landfills
        by surrounding residents for spontaneous commercial activities over the years have
        reduced its operational efficiency.
   -    Private boat repair areas are scattered along the foot of Linh Truong Mountain, outside
        the project boundary, along with spontaneous fill areas and rail tracks for boat repairs
        within the project boundary.
   -    The navigation channel is shallow and not dredged, making it difficult for large-capacity
        vessels to load and unload.
   -    Port infrastructure (berths, access roads, lighting, canopies, etc.) has not yet been
        invested.
Currently, it is estimated that more than 300 fishing vessels from Hoang Truong Commune and
neighboring communes unload cargo here, with an annual throughput of approximately 2,000–
3,000 tons.
       Table 2-4: Statistics on the number of vessels managed by Hoang Hoa district
       No.                     Ship length                            Quantity (pieces)
        1                         <6m                                       770
        2                      6m ÷ <12m                                     15
        3                      12m ÷ <15m                                    23
        4                      15m ÷ <24m                                   121
        5                        > 24m                                       09
        6                         Total                                     938
                                                     (Source: Thanh Hoa Fisheries Department)
The volume of seafood processed at the port is approximately 2,000–3,000 tons per year,
including: fermented fish (chượp: fish, moi), frozen processing (fish, shrimp, squid), dried fish,
dried squid, dried moi, etc.
With the current fleet mostly composed of small vessels (length <6 m accounting for a large
proportion), the mooring and berthing arrangements are spontaneous and lack formal planning
based on vessel size. This leads to prolonged unloading times, significant reduction in fish
quality, and consequently unstable income for fishermen. The management system has not
addressed critical issues such as: Environmental pollution at the shoreline and port areas due to
low awareness among fishermen and local residents. Embankment erosion at multiple points,
with rubble stones displaced over time by tides, compromising riverbank safety. Navigation
channels not dredged, making it very difficult for large-capacity vessels to dock and unload.
    Subproject 2
Current Status of Fishing Port:
Hoa Locc Fishing Port is a Class II port, covering approximately 3.5 ha, with a storm shelter
area of 19.5 ha, capable of accommodating vessels up to 400 CV. The port is located in Hau
Loc District, Thanh Hoa Province, and is one of the major fishing hubs in the province.
Investment and Operation: The port was constructed and put into operation in 2007. It was

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            109
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

designed for 200–300 vessels with capacities ranging from 135–450 CV. Port Length:
Approximately 270 m, of which: 120 m for vessels >400 CV and 150 m for vessels 90–400
CV. Currently, Hoa Loc Port regularly handles approximately 700 vessels (90–800 CV), with
an annual cargo throughput of 30,000–40,000 tons, including 10,000–15,000 tons of seafood.
The port can simultaneously accommodate unloading for 10 vessels
Trường Current Status of Lạch Truong Storm Shelter
The Lạch Truong storm shelter was invested in and put into operation in 2012, designed to
accommodate 264 vessels.
   -   Area 1: Vessels 250–400 CV; from the entrance of De Canal along De Canal to 425 m
       from the canal entrance.
   -   Area 2: Vessels 90–250 CV; from the end of Area 1 to La La Temple, 725 m long.
       Vessels are moored on both sides.
   -   Area 3: Vessels <90 CV; from La La Temple along the De Canal dike northward past
       Vích ferry, 680 m long.
Areas 1 and 2, totaling approximately 1.1 km, were constructed and put into operation in 2014,
including dredging of navigation channels, embankment construction, and vessel mooring
systems. After nearly 10 years, sedimentation due to floods and lack of regular dredging has
reduced the usable area. At low tide, mooring buoys sit on mud, narrowing the area while vessel
traffic has increased. This makes navigation for storm sheltering difficult and dangerous.
Vessels may need to wait hours for tide rise, with some grounding and obstructing other vessels.
During storms or low tides, emergency response and vessel evacuation are delayed.
According to the current survey, two mooring areas have been severely silted, and at the
entrance of the basin, when the tide is low, the water level is approximately cos(-1), allowing
only small vessels under 50 CV to enter. The siltation of the two mooring areas is detailed as
follows:
   -   Area 1: 425 m long, accommodates vessels up to 400 CV. At low tide, the channel depth
       is only about 0.5 m, while at high tide, it reaches approximately 1.8 m.
   -   Area 2: 725 m long, accommodates vessels up to 250 CV. At low tide, the channel depth
       is about 0.2 m, and at high tide, it reaches 1.5 m.
Additionally, the Lạch Truong mooring area lacks many critical infrastructure items, including:
Basin operations building, Community house, Access roads around the basin, Lighting system,
Clean water supply system
During storms or low-pressure events, the access roads around the basin become muddy.
Without an operations building, staff must manage vessel arrangements outdoors in rain or
storm conditions, day and night, to ensure safe mooring and storm sheltering.
The right-side embankment of the mooring area serves both as flood protection and as a traffic
route for local residents. However, the embankment has not been reinforced. During storms,
road traffic is extremely difficult due to muddy surfaces, with water pooling in potholes and
ruts, making passage very challenging.


2.5. SPECIFIC ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL CONDITIONS IN THE
SUBPROJECT IMPLEMENTATION AREAS
In the area of Subproject 1
The SP 1 area is located adjacent to the Lach Truong estuary, approximately 1.5 km from the
sea. The estuary within the project area has a width of about 1–1.5 km. Water quality in the

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                          110
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

river is fairly good, and inland waterway traffic is relatively high, with many vessels seeking
storm shelter during storms. Along both sides of the river, there are numerous aquaculture ponds
owned by local residents, as well as some restaurants and hotels.
The residential area of Hoang Tien Commune is about 20 m from the port upgrade and
expansion area, with a dense population of approximately 100 households. Within and around
the Subproject implementation area (radius 300 m), there are no cultural or religious heritage
sites (temples/shrines/communal houses, graves, churches) or other sensitive points.
   ❖ Dredged material disposal sites for SP 1 and SP 2
The dredged material disposal sites for SP 1 and SP 2 are located in a low-efficiency salt field
area in Hai Loc Commune, Hau Loc District (currently Van Loc Commune), with an area of
approximately 8.15 ha and a storage capacity of up to 383,800 m³, sufficient to accommodate
all dredged materials from both subprojects.
The disposal site is located about 2.5 km northwest of the SP 1 dredging area and about 0.5 km
east of the SP 2 dredging area. This area is near the Lach Truong storm shelter, making it
convenient for transport by barge (200–250 tons) along the Lach Truong River, and then
pumping the material through pipelines to the disposal site.
Currently, the area has a dense population about 80 m from the disposal site; however, the
pumping pipelines are appropriately arranged and do not affect surrounding residential
activities. The disposal site location has been approved by the People’s Committee of Thanh
Hoa Province under Official Letter No. 9483/UBND-NNMT dated 20 June 2025.
The main transport route for dredged materials is as follows: SP area → Lach Truong River →
Disposal site (distance 0.5–2.5 km). The location and current status images of the dredged
material disposal site are shown in the figure below.




                                                          Current Status of disposal site
            Location of disposal site

The area of Subproject 2
The Lach Truong River in the SP 2 area has a width of approximately 150–200 m (within the
project area), located about 4 km from the sea. Near the river mouth, the river width increases
to about 1,000–1,500 m. Water quality in the river is fairly good, and inland waterway traffic
is relatively high, with many vessels seeking storm shelter during storms. Along both sides of
the river, there are numerous aquaculture ponds owned by local residents, as well as some
restaurants and hotels.
Current status of the Lach Truong storm shelter: Located approximately 500 m east of the
existing fishery port, separated from residential areas, with a designed capacity for about 264
vessels. However, the storm shelter has been severely silted up. At the estuary entrance, when

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                          111
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

the tide is low, the water level is approximately cos(-1), allowing only small vessels under 50
CV to enter. The current shelter does not meet the docking and storm-sheltering needs of vessels
from Thanh Hoa province and other provinces, limiting the operational efficiency of the shelter.
The right-side dike system of the shelter functions both for flood protection and as a
transportation route for surrounding residents. Currently, the dike surface is not reinforced,
making transportation very difficult, especially during rainy or stormy days. The road surface
is muddy and uneven, with puddles forming in potholes. The residential area of Hoa Loc
Commune is about 120 m from the port upgrade and expansion area, with a dense population
of approximately 40 households. Additionally, within 20–100 m from the existing port, there
are several seafood processing enterprises, including: Chung Huong Frozen and Fresh Seafood
Facility and Kich Hang Ice and Seafood Facility.
Within and around the SP implementation area (radius 400 m), there are cultural and religious
heritage objects (temples/shrines/communal houses, graves, churches), including Truong Xa
Parish Church and La Ha Palace. Details of PCR/sensitive sites will be presented in Section 2.6
below.
In the area of Subproject 3
Current status of Phu Son 2 sluice: The sluice is located under the right dike of the Can River
and serves to take water from the Can River for aquaculture, combined with flood drainage.
Currently, no sluice exists, and drainage still flows toward Xuan Mai 2 sluice. The sluice will
be located on a relatively flat and open section at the beginning of the canal.
Current status of Phu Son 2 canal: The canal width ranges from 3 to 33 m, being widest at the
head and narrowing toward the end. The canal’s end section is silted, with eroded banks. Its
function is to supply and drain water for aquaculture and to serve agricultural production
drainage. Currently, water is taken in and drained to the Can River through Phu Son 1 sluice.
There are four head sluices along the canal that have degraded and lack sufficient capacity; they
need to be upgraded or replaced. A few households along the canal conduct aquaculture and
fishing activities.
Current status of Bao Trieu canal: The canal width ranges from 8 to 15 m, widest at the head
and narrowing toward the end. There are no inlet sluices, leading to siltation and bank erosion.
The canal supplies and drains water for aquaculture and also supports local sedge cultivation,
but the system lacks regulating sluices. At the canal’s end, there is a transformer station and
households conducting aquaculture (shrimp farming) located adjacent to the canal bank.
Current status of local population: The population density is high along the head of Phu Son 2
canal. A few aquaculture households live near the canal ends: approximately 100 m from Phu
Son 2 canal and 20 m from Bao Trieu canal. Some households raise poultry, while others
conduct aquaculture (shrimp farming) 20–100 m from the canals. Within and around the SP
implementation area (radius 400 m), there are no cultural or religious heritage sites
(temples/shrines/communal houses, graves, churches). PCR details will be presented in Section
2.6 below.
   ❖ Dredged Material Storage Areas of Subproject 3
The dredged material storage for SP 3 is arranged in 2 storage areas with a total area of 60 ha
and a total capacity of 875,000 m³, sufficient to contain all dredged materials from the
subproject. Storage Area 1: 15 ha, capacity 225,000 m³, Storage Area 2: 45 ha, capacity 650,000
m³
Current status of Storage Area 1 (located about 0.5 km east of the SP area): Currently an empty
land owned by Mr. Mai Sy Tuan. There are no residential areas or buildings around the storage
area. The storage site has an agreement for material dumping and has been approved by Mr.
Mai Sy Tuan and the People’s Committee of the commune.

In the area of Subproject 4
Current Status of Son Trang 1 Culvert: Located on the small-capacity drainage embankment,
currently severely damaged. The culvert gate, hinges, and concrete body are broken and
collapsed, and the mouth of the culvert is buried, making it impossible to supply water for
aquaculture or drainage in the area.
Current Status of Son Trang 2 Culvert: Small-capacity culvert, severely damaged. There is no
control gate, and the concrete body is broken and collapsed. Water cannot be supplied for
aquaculture or drained from the area.
Current Status of the Embankment: The current embankment crest is lower than the design
level, averaging around +2.00 to +2.50 m. The embankment width is 2.5–4.0 m, and the slope
is 1.0–1.2 m. Many embankment slopes are severely eroded. The surface has not been
reinforced, making movement slippery and difficult during rainy and flood seasons, impacting
aquaculture production.
Current Status of Son Trang 1 Canal: The canal serves to supply water for aquaculture, rice
Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            113
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

cultivation, and irrigation in Hoang Tien commune through Son Trang 1 Culvert (BxH = 1.40
x 3.0 m) to the Lach Truong River. Currently, only a narrow section of the canal remains; the
rest has been completely buried, causing frequent flooding.
Current Status of Son Trang 2 Canal: The canal supplies water for aquaculture, rice cultivation,
and irrigation through Son Trang 2 Culvert (D80) into the earth canal and then to Lach Truong
River. The canal is narrow, silted, and several banks are eroded, causing slow drainage and
flooding.
Current Status of Hung Tien Canal: Supplies water for aquaculture, rice, and irrigation through
a culvert under the dike (BxH = 1.60 x 2.40 m) to the Lach Truong River. The canal is narrow,
silted, and banks are eroded, slowing drainage and reducing water supply for aquaculture.
Existing structures: 2 combined irrigation-drainage culverts (ø80–ø100) are damaged and lack
gates; 1 double D100 circular culvert is damaged.
Current Status of Nghia Thuc Canal: Supplies water for aquaculture, rice, and irrigation via a
culvert under the dike (BxH = 1.80 x 2.30 m) to the Lach Truong River. The canal is narrow,
silted, and banks are eroded, causing slow drainage and insufficient water for aquaculture.
Existing structures: 2 culverts on the canal are damaged without gates; 6 combined irrigation-
drainage culverts are damaged without gates.
Current Status of Residents: Hoang Tien commune residents are about 30 m from the
construction area, sparsely populated (~10 households), mainly engaged in aquaculture and
agriculture. PCR Objects: Within a 400 m radius, Thien Phuc Pagoda is nearby. Details will be
provided in Section 2.6.
   ❖ Dredged Material Storage for Subproject 4
Located on vacant land in Son Trang Hamlet, Hoang Yen Commune (now Hoang Tien) with
an area of 2 ha and a storage volume of 40,000 m³, sufficient for the entire dredged material of
SP4. The storage site is 3 km from SP4 dredging area. No residential or public structures nearby.
Approved by the local People's Committee. Disposal Route: SP area → Storage Area (0.5–3
km).
In the area of Subproject 5
Current Status of Dong Coi Canal: Supplies water for aquaculture, rice, and irrigation in Hoang
Chau commune. The canal is narrow, silted, and mostly buried, causing frequent flooding.
Current Status of Hoi Trieu Canal: The Hoi Trieu Canal functions to supply water for
aquaculture and rice cultivation in Hoang Chau commune through a culvert under the dike that
discharges into the Ma River and subsequently flows to the sea. At present, the canal bed is
silted, and many sections of the canal banks have eroded, resulting in slow drainage,
waterlogging, and insufficient water supply for aquaculture activities..
Current Status of Tay Canal: The Tay Canal serves to supply water for aquaculture and rice
cultivation in Hoang Chau commune through a water intake culvert under the dike that
discharges into the Ma River and then to the sea. Currently, 400 meters of the canal have been
reinforced with concrete; however, the culvert dimensions (30 x 40 cm) are too small to
adequately fulfill the irrigation and drainage functions of the canal. The remaining sections are
earthen canals with narrow, silted beds and eroded banks, resulting in slow drainage,
waterlogging, and insufficient water supply for aquaculture activities.
Current Status of Residents: The residential area of Hoang Chau commune is located
approximately 10 meters from the construction area, densely populated with around 100
households, mainly engaged in aquaculture and agricultural production. Within and around a
400-meter radius of the subproject area, there are no PCR (communal houses, temples, shrines,
graves, or churches).

   ❖ Dredged Material Disposal Sites of Subproject 5
The dredged material disposal sites of SP5 are arranged at two sites with a total area of 0.93

can be accommodated. (Site 1: area 0.33 ha, capacity 4,519 m³; Site 2: area 0.6 ha, capacity
12,000 m³).
Current Status of Sites 1 and 2 (located approximately 2 km north of the subproject area):
Currently, these areas are used as pond land managed by the commune People’s Committee
(PPC) and leased to local residents for production, although the lease agreements have expired.
There are no residential areas or houses surrounding the disposal sites. The sites already have
official agreements for dredged material disposal, which have been approved by the commune
People’s Committee.
Main Disposal Route: Subproject area → Disposal sites (approximately 2.0 km). The location
and current status of the dredged material disposal sites are shown in the figures below.




          Current Status of Disposal site                 Current Status of Disposal site

In the area of Subproject 6
Current Status of Project Canals: The project area covers an aquaculture area of 170 ha.
Currently, the main supply and drainage canals do not have intake and discharge structures at
the canal heads, resulting in siltation and bank erosion. Besides their function of supplying and
draining water for the aquaculture area, the main canals also serve to drain water for the local
sedge cultivation area; however, the system still lacks structures for water regulation.
Transportation to and from the project area for production, harvesting, and transporting
products is relatively convenient. However, transportation within the project area is very
difficult due to the use of eroded earthen canal banks as roads.
Current Status of Residential Areas: The residential area of Hoang Chau commune is located
about 15 m from the construction area, with a dense population of approximately 50 households.
At the end of the Subproject route, there are a few households of Hoang Chau commune
engaged in aquaculture and agricultural production (in the construction area of Route No. 02
from Ngo Hao Laneto Thach culvert).
Within and around the subproject area (radius of 400 m), there are no cultural, religious, or
sensitive sites (PCR), such as temples, pagodas, shrines, graves, or churches.
   ❖ Dredged Material Disposal Site of Subproject 6
The dredged material disposal site of Subproject 6 is located on pond land managed by the
commune People’s Committee in Phuong Ngo 1 Hamlet, Hoang Luu commune (now Hoang
Chau commune), with an area of approximately 1.2 ha and a capacity of 17,500 m³, ensuring
that the entire dredged material from SP6 can be accommodated.

In the area of Subproject 7
Current Status of Project Canals: The project area covers an aquaculture area of 60 ha. At
present, the main supply and drainage canals in the aquaculture zone do not have intake and
discharge structures at the canal heads, resulting in siltation and bank erosion. In addition to
their function of supplying and draining water for the aquaculture area, the main canals also
serve to drain water for the surrounding local area; however, the system still lacks structures
for water regulation. Transportation to and from the project area for production, harvesting, and
transporting goods is relatively convenient. However, transportation within the project area is
very difficult due to the use of eroded earthen canal banks as roads.
Current Status of Residential Areas: The residential area of Hoang Chau commune is located
approximately 30 meters from the construction area, with a dense population of around 60
households. These households mainly engage in aquaculture and agricultural production.
Within and around the subproject area (within a radius of 400 meters), there are no cultural,
religious, or sensitive sites (PCR), such as temples, pagodas, shrines, graves, or churches.
   ❖ Dredged Material Disposal Site of Subproject 7
The dredged material disposal site of Subproject 7 is located on land used for crop cultivation,
managed by the commune People’s Committee in Chau Trieu Hamlet, Hoang Chau commune,
Hoang Hoa district (currently Hoang Chau commune), with an area of approximately 1.5 ha
and a capacity of 18,000 m³, ensuring that the entire dredged material from Subproject 7 can be

The disposal site is situated about 1 km from the Subproject 7 dredging area. There are no
residential areas or houses around the site. The site has an official agreement for dredged
material disposal, which has been approved by the commune People’s Committee.

In the area of Subproject 8
Current Status of Ong Ninh Culvert Construction Area: The culvert is located beneath the
concrete road connecting the residential area of Van Loc commune with the rice cultivation
fields. The culvert serves aquaculture purposes while also functioning for flood drainage. It is
situated along a canal with a relatively flat and open terrain.
Current Status of Main Supply and Drainage Canal (Section from Ong Ninh Culvert to Quay
Co Culvert): The canal width ranges from 6 m to 22 m. At the downstream end near Quay Co
Culvert, the canal is at its widest and gradually narrows toward the upstream end. The narrowest
section is in the middle of the canal due to bank erosion. The canal serves to supply and drain
water for the aquaculture area and also drains water for local agricultural production. Water is
currently taken and discharged into the Len River through Quay Co Culvert. Along the canal,
there are several simple footbridges (“monkey bridges”) used by local residents to access rice
fields and aquaculture ponds from the residential areas.
Current Status of Operation Management Road Construction Area: The starting point connects
to the existing cement concrete road, which is approximately 5 m wide, and the end point
connects to the Len River dike, which is 5.5 m wide. The construction area is located on the
right side of the main drainage canal, currently an earthen canal bank. Along the route, a few
households are scattered near the new road alignment. The nearest household is approximately
5 m from the road. The road passes several utility poles and ends near a shrimp farming area,
about 5 m away.
Current Status of Two Internal Field Canals: These two canals have widths ranging from 0 to 4
m. At the upstream ends, the canals are widest and gradually narrow toward the downstream
ends. The downstream ends are silted, and the banks are eroded. These canals serve to supply
water for aquaculture and drain floodwater from the fields. Water is currently taken from and
discharged into the main drainage canal. Along the canal banks, a few households engage in
aquaculture.
Current Status of Two New Road Routes: The existing routes are earthen roads approximately
3 m wide, serving the transportation needs of Van Loc commune residents. A few households
are scattered along the route. The nearest household is approximately 5 m from the road.
Current Status of Residential Areas: The residential area of Van Loc commune is approximately
40 m from the construction area, with a medium population density of around 50 households
living along the canal. These households primarily engage in aquaculture and agricultural
production. Near the main drainage canal, there is one household raising poultry and one
household engaged in aquaculture (shrimp farming), located approximately 5–10 m from the
project area.
Within and around the subproject area (within a radius of 400 m), there are no cultural, religious,
or sensitive sites (PCR), such as temples, pagodas, shrines, graves, or churches. The

characteristics of PCRs will be detailed in Section 2.6 below.

In the area of Subproject 9
Current Status of Ong Vanh Culvert Construction Area: The culvert is located at the
downstream end of the canal and serves the function of preventing saltwater intrusion. At
present, the culvert has severely deteriorated and is no longer operational.
Current Status of Ong Thuoc Culvert Construction Area: The culvert is located beneath the
right dyke of the Hoang River and is designed to take water from the Hoang River to serve
aquaculture, combined with flood drainage. Currently, the culvert has severely deteriorated and
is no longer operational.
Current Status of Canal from Ong Thuoc Culvert to Ong Vanh Culvert: The canal width ranges
from 2 to 4 meters, with the narrowest section at the upstream end and widening toward the
downstream end. The upstream section is silted. The canal serves to supply and drain water for
the aquaculture area as well as to drain water for local agricultural production. Water is
currently taken from the Hoang River through Ong Thuoc Culvert and discharged into the Yen
River via Ong Vanh Culvert. Along the canal, there are two head drainage culverts that have
deteriorated and are no longer capable of providing sufficient flow; these need to be upgraded
or replaced. Several simple footbridges (“monkey bridges”) have been constructed by local
residents to access rice fields and aquaculture ponds from residential areas.
Current Status of Residential Areas: The residential area of Quang Chinh commune is
approximately 10–20 meters from the construction area, with a medium population density of
around 50 households living along the canal. A few households near the downstream end (near
Ong Vanh Culvert) engage in aquaculture and agricultural production.
Within and around the subproject area (within a 400 m radius), there are no cultural, religious,
or sensitive sites (PCR), such as temples, pagodas, shrines, graves, or churches.

                     CHAPTER 3. ANALYSIS OF ALTERNATIVES
As part of the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment, the analysis of alternatives is
essential in determining the proposed level of investment, defining the scope of works, or
considering design and construction options in order to avoid or minimize potential adverse
impacts while enhancing the positive impacts and benefits of the Project.
This analysis includes both the “with-project” and “without-project” scenarios. It describes,
identifies, and compares design options, which are crucial to support decision-making regarding
the selection of construction activities within the Project Area. The selected option is expected
to have the least and lowest adverse impacts while meeting technically feasible solutions and
cost–benefit considerations applicable to the Project. Furthermore, the practice of analyzing
alternatives helps minimize adverse impacts and maximize positive environmental and social
outcomes. The results of the analysis are presented below.
3.1.   “With-Project” and “Without-Project” Scenarios
The Project, once implemented, will generate multiple positive economic, social,
environmental, and climate-related benefits, as follows:
The proposed Project offers long-term benefits. If successful, these benefits will extend beyond
the direct number of capture fishers, aquaculture farmers, and service providers engaged in
fishing, seed supply, and aquaculture production. The Project will contribute significantly to
the sustainable development of the fisheries sector, specifically:
   •   The Project design, particularly the fish landing ports and storm shelter ports, has
       integrated climate change considerations and extreme weather events, thereby extending
       the lifespan of infrastructure and reducing operation and maintenance costs.
   •   The upgrading/construction of fish landing ports and storm shelter ports will facilitate
       safe anchorage for fishing vessels from Thanh Hoa province and neighboring provinces.
       This will reduce fishing costs, increase product value by shortening the distance between
       fishing grounds and ports, and consequently improve incomes while preserving the
       nutritional quality of post-harvest fisheries products.
   •   Fishers will have opportunities to work in fishing, aquaculture, and processing
       environments under improved sanitation and environmental conditions, thereby reducing
       occupational health and safety risks. At the same time, the quality and value of fisheries
       products will be enhanced, and post-harvest losses reduced through improved landing
       and preservation practices.
   •   Improved waste management at port areas as well as aquaculture farms will contribute
       to reducing water pollution and sedimentation in the catchment area. In the long term,
       the Project will help improve the quality of surface water resources within the Project
       area.
   •   The adoption of Good Aquaculture Practices (GAP), Integrated Pest and Pathogen
       Management (IPPM), and the introduction of biosecurity measures and disease-free seed
       production will create a more efficient production and farming environment that is
       unfavorable to pests and pathogens. This will reduce the use of hazardous chemicals and
       antibiotics, protect the environment, mitigate economic losses caused by disease
       outbreaks, and ensure food safety and occupational health and safety for the fisheries
       workforce.
   •   Improved quality of aquaculture seed and enhanced production in aquaculture zones will
       enable fishers/farmers to expand brackish-water shrimp/fish farming, better adapting to
       the increasing challenges of sea level rise caused by climate change.


Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                           125
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

   •   The Project will also generate environmental and social benefits through the application
       of safeguard policies, including: (i) raising awareness and attention on environmental
       and social issues among beneficiaries and local communities; (ii) strengthening the
       presence of environmental and social officers/experts through training programs
       organized by MAE and provincial DAEs, as well as their fisheries extension officers, to
       enhance capacity in environmental and social screening and management; and (iii)
       raising awareness among senior government officials regarding the application of
       environmental and social safeguard principles in sustainable project development,
       thereby mainstreaming E&S considerations into project planning and implementation.

       Remarks:      Key positive impacts of the Project
                     Key negative impacts of the Project
The investment in project construction will generate environmental and socio-economic
impacts within the Project area. However, these impacts are short-term and can be mitigated
through specific measures. In the absence of the Project, such impacts will not occur in the
immediate term; nevertheless, ensuring safe anchorage for fishing vessels during storm and
typhoon seasons will face significant difficulties, and long-term losses of lives and assets among
fishers will inevitably occur. By contrast, with the Project, the selected sites are highly feasible
due to favorable natural conditions and low investment costs. In particular, with scientifically
designed scale, technology, and operation, in addition to ensuring safety for fishers and fishing
vessels, the Project will generate other positive impacts such as improved local infrastructure,
enhanced community benefits from project facilities, reduced production costs, and the creation
of new employment opportunities, etc
Potential environmental impacts can be controlled through the application of mitigation
measures during the construction and operation phases. The Environmental Management and
Monitoring Program will provide supplementary information that forms the basis for better
forecasting of certain environmental issues, thereby enabling the application of appropriate
measures to manage adverse environmental and social impacts, as well as to prevent and
respond to environmental risks/incidents that may occur during the implementation of all nine
subprojects under the Project.
3.2.    Consideration of Alternativs
3.2.1. Analysis of Options on Scale and Technology
Regarding the scale, technology of fish port exploitation/operation, waste management
technology, and project implementation sites, only one option was identified. This option has
been proposed on the following bases: (i) Compliance with the approved Master Plan; (ii)
Consideration of technical factors ensuring operational requirements; (iii) Consideration of
cost, environmental and social impacts, and feasibility of project implementation; and (iv)
Assurance that environmental impacts remain within permissible limits.
3.2.2. Analysis of Dredging Alternatives
Within the Thanh Hoa Province SFDP, only Subproject 2 – Lach Truong Fishing Port involves
large-scale dredging works for the navigation channel, turning basin, and storm shelter
anchorage area, with a dredged volume of approximately 365,000 m³. Therefore, dredging
alternatives were analyzed and compared, as presented below.
The remaining subprojects (Subproject 1 and Subprojects 3 to 9) also include dredging
activities; however, these are mainly limited to small areas in front of the port basins or inland

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                              127
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

canals/ditches supplying water for aquaculture activities. These dredging volumes are relatively
small and dispersed across multiple locations; hence, only one dredging method was selected
in accordance with existing conditions of the fishing ports and canal networks. Specifically:
   -   For Subproject 1: The dredging method involves a cable-bucket excavator combined
       with barges transporting dredged materials to a designated disposal site, with a transport
       distance of approximately 3 km. The disposal method consists of pumping dredged
       material from barges to the disposal site located in the underutilized salt field area of
       Van Loc commune (shared disposal site with Subproject 2).
   -   For Subprojects 3 to 8: The dredging method involves placing an excavator on a pontoon
       to conduct dredging of canal beds according to the designed cross-section.
For Subproject 2, based on an analysis of current river/sea dredging practices in Viet Nam, three
main methods are commonly applied, including:
   -   Option 1: Use of a clamshell grab bucket mounted on a barge (bucket dredger).
       Dredging is carried out using a bucket dredger (long-arm backhoe or hydraulic grab
       dredger mounted on a 500T pontoon). Dredged materials are loaded onto 400T barges
       and transported an average distance of 12–30 km to the disposal site. At the disposal
       site, dredged sand is pumped ashore using a pump installed on the barge, with a pumping
       distance of less than 500 m.




   -   Option 2: Use of a trailing suction hopper dredger (TSHD). The vessel employs a cutter
       head to agitate the seabed and then sucks the slurry of soil and water into the hopper.
       The water is discharged from the vessel, while the sediment is retained on board.




   -   Option 3: Use of a cutter suction dredger (CSD). This method has the advantage of high
       dredging productivity. The vessel employs a cutter head to agitate the seabed, after



Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                           128
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

       which the sediment-water mixture is suctioned and pumped through a pipeline system
       directly to the designated disposal site.

                       dredger)                 dredger)              dredger) – Selected option

Based on conditions including: (1) dredging will be carried out within an actively used
navigation channel, (2) the existing channel is narrow and shallow, and (3) the distance to the
dredged-material disposal sites; it is necessary to ensure safe working conditions for dredging
vessels and to protect navigation safety for both commercial vessels and dredgers, and to
organize an appropriate fleet of dredgers in order to achieve the highest dredging productivity.
Therefore, Option 3 (cutter suction dredger) is the selected dredging method for Subproject 2.
3.2.3. Analysis of Wastewater Treatment Technology Selection
Each of Subproject 1 and Subproject 2 will invest in a single wastewater treatment plant with a
capacity of 200 m³/day, using aerobic technology and enclosed treatment tanks. This single-
option technology proposal is based on an analysis of the operational characteristics of the two
existing fish ports, taking into account: (i) their relatively small area; (ii) the need to meet
current sanitary safety distance requirements; (iii) the general aesthetics of the ports; and (iv)

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                                  129
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

the characteristics of influent wastewater and the required effluent quality. Consequently, the
feasibility study does not present multiple alternative wastewater treatment technologies but
proposes one most-appropriate treatment technology, as described below.
Process flow: Wastewater → Collection tank → Grease trap → Equalization tank → UASB
reactor → Anoxic tank → MBBR → Biological clarifier → Disinfection tank → Receiving
environment.
Treated effluent from the centralized wastewater treatment system, with a capacity of 200
m³/day, is designed to comply with national standards QCVN 11-MT:2015/BTNMT, Column
B (Kq = 0.9; Kf = 1.1) — National Technical Regulation on Wastewater from Fishery Product
Processing — and QCVN 14:2008/BTNMT, Column B (K = 1) — National Technical
Regulation on Domestic Wastewater — prior to discharge to the environment.
Designated effluent receiving bodies: For Subproject 1: roadside drainage canal along the
southern axis of Hoang Truong fishing port; For Subproject 2: De Canal.




Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                         130
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

       CHAPTER 4. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
The assessment on environmental and social impacts and risks for the Project is carried out in
three phases: pre-construction, construction and operation.
This chapter is presented in the following order: (1) Positive impacts; (2) Classification of
negative impacts and risks; (3) Negative impacts and risks in the pre-construction phase; (4)
Negative impacts and risks in the construction phase; (5) Negative impacts and risks in the
operational phase; (6) Indirect impacts; (7) Cumulative impacts and risks; (8) Impacts caused
by climate change.
The assessment considers the impacts and risks outlined in ESS1 of the World Bank
Environmental and Social Framework (WB ESF), including aspects listed in the
Environmental, Health, and Safety Guidelines (EHSG), as well as issues related to community
safety, climate change, and socio-economic impacts. Cumulative impacts and risks, as required
under ESS1, are also addressed. Impacts and risks related to workers and their working
conditions, as specified in ESS2, have been discussed. Aspects covered under ESS3, including
pollution risks and energy use, have been reviewed. Aquatic species, fish, aquaculture, and
other living natural resources, as referenced in ESS6, have been considered in the
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) and Environmental and Social
Management Plan (ESMP). The potential adverse environmental impacts and risks are
presented below.
4.1.   POSITIVE IMPACTS
The project aims to concretize the strategic orientations outlined in the National Development
Strategy for the 2021–2030 period; the Strategy for Sustainable Development of Vietnam’s
Marine Economy to 2030, with a vision to 2045; the National Strategy on Green Growth and
Sustainable Development; the National Socio-Economic Development Plan for the 2021–2025
period; the Strategy for the development of Vietnam’s Fisheries by 2030, with vision towards
2045; and the Master Plan for Fishery Port System and Storm Shelter Areas by 2020, with
orientation to 2030. The project ensures continuity, inheritance, stability, and orders within the
National Planning System.
The project comprises 09 subprojects located in 7 communes of Thanh Hoa Province. It belongs
to infrastructure investment projects which aim at constructing and upgrading facilities to
support marine fisheries and provide regional storm shelters for fishing vessels. The project’s
objectives are to: (i) Accelerate socio-economic development in the project communes in
particular and Thanh Hoa Province in general; concretizing the master plan for fishing port
system and storm shelter areas for fishing vessels along the coast and on islands; meeting the
demand for mooring and logistical services for fishing vessels; gradually advancing toward
industrialization and modernization; establishing key interprovincial exchange hubs to drive
the development of seafood industrial zones, coastal economic zones, and fisheries centers (at
Hoang Truong and Lach Truong seaports) linked to major fishing grounds; and creating
momentum for the fisheries sector to develop efficiently and sustainably. (ii) Contribute to the
reduction of water pollution and protection of aquatic ecosystems through the collection and
treatment of wastewater from seafood preliminary processing and domestic activities before
being discharged into receiving bodies (the sea areas of Hoang Truong and Hoa Loc
communes); improving the quality of receiving water sources; managing solid waste by
reducing the amount of waste released into rivers and seas; preventing marine litter pollution
and protecting marine fauna; reducing waterborne diseases; and creating favorable conditions
for tourism and aquaculture. (iii) Contribute to sustainable development along with the
promotion of cultural and human development; ensuring national defense, security, political
stability, and social order and safety; guaranteeing social welfare, narrowing the gap between
rich and poor, and improving living standards; protecting the ecological environment; and
enhancing climate change resilience.

Environmental and Social Impact Asssessment (ESIA)                                            131
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

4.2.   CLASSIFICATION OF NEGATIVE IMPACTS AND RISKS
There will be potential negative environmental and social impacts and risks in pre-construction,
construction and operation of the investments under the nine subprojects.
These potential negative impacts and risks are classified based on criteria as below:
Significant Impacts (S)
   -   Impacts on large land area, important areas, or changes in environmental conditions in
       a period of more than two years;
   -   Impacts exceeding permitted standards and regulations. Long-term and large-scale impacts;
   -   Changes in ecological systems, impacts on ecological systems of large areas, or medium
       impacts (lasting for more than two years) yet required recovery period of the affected
       ecological systems is ten years;
   -   Impacts on health of people;
   -   Economic losses and damages to the subproject nearby people and communities;
   -   Potential significant social and environmental impacts which can only be controlled and
       mitigated if proper mitigation measures are implemented.
Medium Impacts (M)
   -   Impacts on large areas for a period from six months to two years;
   -   Changes in ecological systems or ecological functions at the localities in a short time
       and recovery capacity is good. The impact levels are similar to current changes yet such
       impacts can have a cumulative effect;
   -   Impacts might (or might not) affect people’s health, causing impacts on persons in the
       surrounding areas;
   -   Impacts are medium, localized and temporary and mitigation measures should be carried out.
Low Impacts (L)
   -   Social and environmental impacts that cause significant changes in less than six months
       or medium changes for a period of less than two years;
   -   Impacts are within permitted standards and regulations, causing minor changes at
       present. Impacts are fully controlled;
   -   Impacts that might affect daily activities yet not cause any obstruction to communities;
   -   Insignificant impacts on health and living standards of people;
   -   Impacts are minor, localized, and can be neglected.
No Impacts (N)
   -   Impacts that are not observable or cannot be distinctly attributed may arise from daily
       operational activities;
   -   No social and environmental impacts.
Based on the analysis of baseline data, field survey and discussions with officials and
stakeholders, the project’s potential negative impacts on the physical, biological and economic
environments have been identified and assessed. Level of potential negative environmental
impacts and risks are classified as substantial and specified in Table 4-1.

Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
During the project preparation phase (9 subprojects), the primary impacts and risks stem from land
acquisition, livelihood activities, land use purpose conversion, and unexploded ordnance (UXO)
clearance within the subproject areas.
During the construction phase, activities with potential environmental impacts include: (i) Site
clearance: Demolition and vegetation removal for site preparation (ii) Earthworks, construction
of roads and project components (iii) Transportation and storage: Movement and stockpiling of
construction materials, machinery, equipment, and waste (iv) Activities workers involved in
construction tasks (v) Construction works: Construction of project infrastructure components
(vi) Installation of machinery and equipment for the operational phase (vii) Dredged material
handling: Transporting dredged materials to temporary disposal sites and restoring construction
areas, etc.
Construction activities are expected to cause general environmental impacts, including: (i)
Emissions of dust and gases, noise, and vibration (ii) Generation of wastewater (iii) Generation
of solid waste (iv) Degradation of water quality (v) Impacts on aquatic life (vi) Effects from
erosion, sedimentation, and local flooding (vii) Disturbance to traffic and increased safety risks
(viii) Potential damage to existing public infrastructure and interruption of related services (ix)
Community safety concerns (x) Occupational health and safety issues (xi) Social impacts due
to labor influx. In addition, the project’s construction activities may pose site-specific impacts
depending on the scale of infrastructure components and subproject location.
The project’s operation phase includes: (i) Activities of vessels entering and exiting fishing
ports/storm shelter areas (ii) Operations of fisheries-related services (including the operation of
domestic and seafood processing wastewater treatment plants) (iii) Periodic maintenance and
dredging of navigation channels (for Subproject 1 and 2); for the remaining seven subprojects (from
subproject 3 to subproject 9), periodic dredging of canals/drainage channels is expected to depend
on the local maintenance and repair plans.
Activities of vessels entering and exiting the project’s fishing ports/storm shelter areas will result in
the following impacts and risks:
       -   Impacts on air quality due to emission from vessels, ships;
       -   Degradation of surface water quality due to increased concentrations of oil and grease
           from vessels and boats;
       -   Impacts on the aquatic ecosystem in the area;
Activities of fisheries-related services (including the operation of wastewater treatment plants)
under the project will cause the following impacts and risks:
       -   Deterioration of air quality due to unpleasant odors, including fishy smells, from
           seafood caught and the operation of wastewater treatment plants.
       -   Increased water pollution caused by wastewater generated from fisheries-related
           services.
       -   Increased generation of solid waste from fisheries related services.
Periodic dredging activities for the maintenance of navigation channels/turning basins under
the project will lead to the following impacts and risks:
       -   Deterioration of air quality caused by the activities of vehicles and machinery
           involved in dredging activities.
       -   Degradation of water quality due to dredging activities (increased turbidity, total
           suspended solids - TSS, oil and grease, etc.).
       -   Impacts on the aquatic ecosystem in the area.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                    136
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       -    Increased salt intrusion.
In addition, site-specific impacts associated with construction activities at each individual
subproject will also be considered and assessed in this chapter.
4.3.       IMPACTS AND RISKS IN PREPARATION PHASE
4.3.1. Impacts due to land acquisition and livelihood
The total area of permanently acquired land for the construction of project components is
approximately 201,881 m², affecting 269 households. Among them, 262 households are impacted by
land acquisition with less than 30% (accounting for 78%) and between 30–70% (accounting for 22%).
In addition, 11 organizations (now reduced to 07 due to merging of communes) are affected, primarily
commune-level People’s Committees (PC). Across the entire project implementation area, no
households or organizations are subject to relocation or resettlement. Specifically:
