The project’s site clearance for construction of sustainable fisheries development permanently
acquires land of 7 organizations and production land of 262 households. Among them, annual crop
land – 171 households, aquaculture land -35 households, salt land – 56 households. No household
is ethnic minority; 07 organizations lose their annual crop land (03 organizations), irrigation land
(07 organizations), aquaculture land (04 organizations), transportation land (05 organizations),
specialized water surface land (2 organizations) and unused land (04 organizations).
Land acquisition for subproject implementation is inevitably, but the affected areas are not large
compared to the total existing area (78% of households are affected with less than 30% and 22%
of households are affected by 30-70% of the total area). However, the affected areas are located
along the existing canals/ditches, which do not significantly affect the remaining total area of
the land plots, thus, local people’s income from the remaining areas will not be affected.
Therefore, the impact from acquisition of this land is considered not large.
The project does not require land acquisition for temporary storage site for dredged materials or precast
component yards. The construction materials, wastes are gathered within the land acquired by
the project components and those of works along ditches/canals are stockpiled in these
ditch/canal areas. The administrative area and workers’ camps with a total area of about 9,000
m² are temporarily provided by local residents and the Commune People’s Committee during
the construction phase; These areas with about 1,000 m2/per subproject are stadium (subproject
3), vacant land, vegetation area with main grassland and wild shrub along cannel sides or at the
foot of dike (subproject 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9), football field in Yen Loc village (Subproject 8).
There are no residential houses and other structures surrounding these subprojects. The
gathering of construction material may affect the environment due to increased sedimentation
and dust emission due to loading/unloading and stockpiling. For the vacant land for subproject
9, it is near the market 1A, which currently is informal market where 3-4 households selling
products in about 1-2 hours in the morning/afternoon. The subproject activities will not
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                       138
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
significantly affect their business in this area. Therefore, the impact of material and waste
stockpiling in this area is considered not significant as no residents reside in, short-term during
gathering period, and can be mitigated through appropriate mitigation measures.
According to the inventory, about 420 trees belong to 81 households will be affected due to
land acquisition, including: 372 fruit trees (banana, coconut, mango, jackfruit, star apple trees,
fig trees, 35 timber trees (including white cedar and chinaberry), 13 ornamental plants (such as
bougainvillea, flamboyant, ficus, and almond trees), and 5,155.3 m2 of affected crops (including
rice and canna). These trees are primarily from household gardens and do not generate
economic profit, thus they do not significantly impact the livelihood of the families if being
removed. Specifically:
      • Fruit trees (including banana, coconut, mango, jackfruit, and star apple): 36 households
        affected, with a total of 372 trees impacted.
      • Crops: 5,170 plants, with 28 affected households.
      • Perennial and ornamental trees (including almond, banyan, flamboyant, fig, chinaberry, white
        cedar, and bougainvillea): 8 affected households.
The preliminary inventory results indicate that 8 out of 9 subproject areas (excluding Subproject
2) will affect residential structures or architectural assets within the project area. A total of 72
households will be affected due to impacts on structures. However, no households will be
affected in terms of their main residential house; only auxiliary structures will be impacted,
specifically: 110.5 m² of livestock shelters belonging to 7 households; 616.5 m² of temporary
houses belonging to 13 households (temporary structures built on pond land for aquaculture
production); 84.5 m² of temporary huts belonging to 4 households; and 6.25 m³ of unused
rainwater storage tanks belonging to 1 household. In addition, the project will also affect other
household assets such as marine railway systems, concrete yards, stone embankments, fences,
gate pillars, brick walls, and low-voltage electric poles serving aquaculture areas. The overall
impact on residential structures and architectural assets of households and organizations is
considered minimal, with no significant effect on main dwellings. The areas subject to land
acquisition mainly involve temporary houses, guard huts, livestock shelters, water tanks, yards,
walls, and electric poles of a limited number of households. These affected structures can be
repaired or adjusted to suit current conditions. Therefore, these impacts can be mitigated
through technical measures and compensation and support mechanisms outlined in the project’s
Resettlement Plan (RP).
Therefore, the level of impact from land acquisition on the subproject areas is considered Low
and unavoidable. The impact on household income is minor, resulting from the acquisition of
land, residential structures, and the removal of crops. However, the affected households are
currently only losing a small portion of their agricultural production land, and most are engaged
in other income-generating activities such as business, fishing, processing, service provision,
and transportation. As a result, the land acquisition and associated losses may cause only a
slight reduction in household income. This impact is manageable and can be mitigated through
income restoration support measures, as outlined in the project’s Resettlement Plan (RP).
* Impacts on livelihood, income and jobs of local residents:
The acquisition of agricultural land primarily affects the areas used for annual crop cultivation
and aquaculture, resulting in short-term impacts on employment and livelihoods. Residents may
experience a temporary reduction in income from agricultural and aquaculture products, with
minor effects on daily food consumption. However, most of the acquired land accounts for a
small proportion (less than 10%) of total cultivated land, so the reduction in income is
considered negligible. In addition, many households earn income from other sources such as
trading, coastal fishing, and wage labor, which may not be significantly affected by the land
acquisition. Some aquaculture ponds may be temporarily impacted during construction, but this

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               139
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
can be mitigated by adjusting the timing of stocking and harvesting. Overall, the impacts on
livelihoods are assessed to be short-term, localized, and manageable through measures such as
adjusting aquaculture schedules to avoid critical periods of stocking and harvesting.
4.3.2. Impacts due to changes of land use purposes
Land acquisition for construction site clearance has led to a change in land use purposes. All
households whose agricultural land is being acquired have less than 30% of their total
agricultural production area affected, except for 36 households whose affected area exceeds
30%. Specifically, 28 households are affected by less than 30%, including 4 households in
Hoang Yen commune – Subproject 4, 2 households in Hoang Phong – Subproject 5, 2
households in Hoang Luu – Subproject 6, 3 households in Hoang Chau – Subproject 7, and 15
households in Van Loc – Subproject 1,2. Additionally, 8 households in Van Loc – Subproject
1,2 are affected by 30–70%. The conversion of current land use into other purposes (such as
transportation land, and irrigation land) affects areas of annual crop cultivation, aquaculture,
irrigation, and transportation land. This impact is considered long-term. Regarding positive
aspects: (i) Improve irrigation and internal transportation infrastructure, enhancing water supply
and drainage efficiency, reducing natural disaster risks, and supporting more effective
agricultural production (ii) Enhance the accessibility and operational management of
infrastructure systems, ensuring regular maintenance of canals and reducing long-term
maintenance costs (iii) Contribute to sustainable agricultural economic development, increasing
crop productivity, facilitating the transportation of agricultural products, and reducing travel
time and costs.
The land acquisition and conversion of land use purposes under the project primarily generate
positive impacts, especially in the long term, by promoting infrastructure development and
supporting efficient, sustainable agricultural production. Negative impacts are mostly localized
and manageable, provided that appropriate livelihood support and fair compensation measures
are implemented.
4.3.3. Unexploded ordnance (UXO) and residual explosive materials
Vietnam in general, and the project area in particular, experienced a civil war that ended in
1975. Although the land has been in use for over four decades since the end of the war, there
remains a possibility that unexploded ordnance (UXO) may still be buried underground. UXO
poses potential risks to workers and may cause damage to project infrastructure and nearby
households if disturbed by construction activities such as site clearance, land leveling, and
excavation. Most project components require clearance by licensed UXO removal units, except
for certain areas that were already cleared by previous projects and are located within
designated safety corridors. New construction works are considered to have a higher risk of
UXO exposure than existing culverts, due to excavation activities that may reach depths of 0.3
to 3 meters on land and approximately 0.5 to 3 meters underwater in agricultural fields, canal
beds, embankments, dredging zones, and port areas.
The subprojects under the Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province
will be upgraded and expanded across fishing ports and aquaculture zones, with unexploded
ordnance (UXO) clearance activities conducted on both land and underwater areas at Hoang
Truong Fishing Port, Lach Truong Fishing Port, and existing canal systems. According to the
Circular No. 59/2022/TT-BQP dated August 30, 2022, for Class IV agricultural and rural
development works, the safety corridor is defined as follows: 10 meters outward from each side
of the embankment edge for dredging works under Subproject 2, where dredging depth does
not exceed 3 m, and 3 m outward from each side of the structure edge for all other works.
Requirements for UXO clearance depth are as follows: (i) Up to 3 m for dredging works and
their safety corridors under Subproject 2; (ii) Up to 0.5 meters for underwater safety corridor
areas; (iii) Up to 0.3 m for land-based safety corridor areas; And (iv) No UXO clearance is

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             140
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
required for existing concrete roads and residential buildings. UXO clearance activities will be
carried out over a total area of 547,700 m² (54.77 ha), including 47.39 ha of land-based
clearance and 7.38 ha of underwater clearance.
If unexploded ordnance (UXO) is not cleared, it may pose risks during construction, potentially
affecting workers, local residents, and nearby infrastructure. According to consultations with
the People’s Committees of the communes within the project area, no incidents involving
bombs, mines, or explosive materials or related casualties have been reported in the past ten
years. Therefore, the risk of UXO in the project’s construction area is considered low to medium
in the long term, except for renovation and upgrading activities within the safety corridor, which
has already undergone UXO clearance. However, this risk can be mitigated through UXO
clearance activities that will be completed prior to construction, and construction activities
should be confined to areas confirmed to be free of UXO.
4.4. ENVIRONMENTAL                   AND       SOCIAL      IMPACTS        AND      RISKS       IN
CONSTRUCTION PHASE
4.4.1. General impacts and risks
 4.4.1.1. Impacts on ambient air quality
The sources of impact on ambient air quality during the construction phase primarily stem from
dust and emissions generated by construction activities, including: (i) Demolition/clearing for
site preparation; (ii) Earthworks, and construction of project components; (iii) Dust generated
during the transportation of construction materials; (iv) Dust generated during waste
transportation; (v) Dust emitted from the gathering and loading/unloading of construction
materials; (vi) Emissions from vehicles transporting materials and waste; (vii) Emissions from
construction equipment and machinery. Assessment for each source of impact related to the
subprojects is detailed below.
    ❖ Dust generated from site preparation
The primary source of dust emissions during site preparation activities originates from the
demolition of existing structures and debris resulting from the dismantling of deteriorated
embankments, shoreline roads, and temporary buildings. These activities do not involve
vegetation clearance.
 Determine the volume of dust generated from demolishing existing houses and structures using
the following formula:
                                    W = EQd             [CT1]
        Where:          W: Average amount of dust generated (kg);
                        E: Pollution coefficient (kg dust/ton);
                        Q: Emission amount (m3);
                        d: Debris d = 1.8 tons/m3 (According to Official Dispatch No.
                        1784/BXD-VP of the Ministry of Construction on promulgating material
                        norms in construction).
The amount of diffused dust is calculated based on the pollution coefficient and the volume of
excavated and embanked soil. According to the World Health Organization's guidance
document, the pollution coefficient E is calculated using the following formula:

                                              M 
                                               
                                               2
Where:
                -   E: Pollution coefficient (kg dust/ton).
                -   k: Particle structure, with average value (k = 0.2 for dust with size <10µm -

                    of Air Pollutant Emission Factors, Volume 1: Stationary Point and Area

                    and Area Sources).

The concentration of dust dispersion from the demolition of existing structures ranges from
0.2019 to 0.2199 mg/m³. This dust concentration shows minimal variation between the lowest
and highest wind speeds, and remains below the permissible limits set by QCVN
05:2023/BTNMT and QCVN 02:2019/BYT. The impact level is assessed as Low due to the
following reasons: (i) The dust concentration generated is lower than the permissible threshold;
(ii) The demolition activities are short-term, lasting approximately 1 to 3 months; (iii) The
affected group is limited, primarily consisting of construction workers, nearby residents, and
other groups (as mentioned in Table 4-3); (iv) This impact can be mitigated.
    ❖ Dust from earthwork and ground leveling
The dust generated from earthworks and ground leveling activities is considered the most
significant source of dust emissions during the construction phase, which typically lasts
between 3 to 6 months. The extent of dust dispersion during excavation and ground leveling
depends on the volume of soil excavated, transported, and backfilled. The amount of diffused
dust is calculated based on the pollution coefficient and the volume of excavated and backfilled
soil. Similarly, using the formula [CT2] mentioned above, the dust pollution coefficient and
dust load from earthwork and ground leveling activities for construction projects can be
determined as follows:
          Table 4-5: Dust Load Generated by Excavation and Leveling Activities

In fact, the amount of dust dispersed from excavation and ground leveling activities is not as
significant as estimated, due to varying construction timelines of each component (mainly
concentrated in the first 1–2 months of the construction phase), relatively small volumes of
earthwork, and the fact that subprojects are located in different areas with construction activities
not occurring simultaneously or continuously. Dust dispersion from these activities is therefore
estimated to be low in concentration, localized, and short-term—below the threshold set by
QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT and significantly lower than the standard QCVN 02:2019-BYT.
Moreover, dust concentration decreases rapidly with distance from the source. Dust mitigation
measures will be implemented to minimize environmental impact and protect workers and
nearby residents around the subprojects.
    ❖ Dust generated from road surfaces during the transportation of construction materials
      and waste out of the construction site.
The transportation of raw materials, fuel, and construction supplies from supply areas to project
sites, as well as the transportation of excess solid waste from construction sites to temporary
storage areas, generates dust and emissions from vehicles (mainly engine exhaust gases such as
CO, SO₂, and NO₂). Dust and emissions directly affect transport workers and residents living
along the transportation routes in each project area. The impacts caused by the transportation
of materials and waste include:
    -   Dust and emissions generated can obstruct visibility and negatively affect the health of
        workers, commuters, and residents living along the transportation routes.
    -   The transportation process may cause traffic congestion and pose risks to traffic safety,
        affecting the daily travel activities of local residents.
    -   It may also disrupt commercial activities and daily activities of people living along the
        transportation routes.
    -   Uncovered or spilled materials during transportation can cause loss of urban beauty and
        negatively impact environmental sanitation conditions along the transportation routes.
Dust from road surfaces during the transportation of construction materials originates from the
transported materials themselves and is stirred up by vehicle tires. This occurs during transport
using diesel-powered vehicles (10-ton trucks) traveling on rural roads over an average distance
of 15 kilometers to the construction site at:

Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
The dust load generated during the transportation of construction materials is determined
according to the formula of Air Chief, U.S. Environmental Administration, 1995:

                                                                                    [CT3]


        Where: L - Dust emission coefficient (kg/km/Turnaround);
                         k - Particle size, k = 0.8 with dust with size less than 30µm;
                         s - the amount of soil on the road, s = 2%;
                         S - Average speed of the vehicle, S = 30km/h;
                         W - Loaded weight of the vehicle, W = 10 tons;
                         w - Number of wheels, w = 10 wheels.
                         p – The average number of rainy days in the year, p = 137 rainy days.
                                     → L = 0.255kg/km/turn.
Applying the SUTTON model, the average concentration of pollutants at any time with linear
sources is determined according to the following formula:

                                

In the [CT4], we have:
    -    C - Concentration of pollutants in the air (mg/m3)
    -    E - Load of pollutants from the waste source (mg/m.s)
    -    z - Height of the calculation point (m)
    -    h - Height of the road surface relative to the surrounding ground (m)
    -    u - Average wind speed in the area (m/s)
The vertical pollutant diffusion coefficient  z (z) with atmospheric stability class B is
calculated based on the following formula:
                                         z = 0.53 x 250.73 (m)

In this calculation, x represents the distance from the calculation point to the exhaust source in
the wind direction. The calculation method divides the point coordinates into horizontal axis
(x) and vertical axis (z). The atmospheric stability is classified as type B.
The diffusion coefficient depends on the level of atmospheric diffusion, and the vertical
coefficient  z is calculated according to Slade for atmospheric stability type B at a distance x
= 25 meters from the center of the road to the sides.
The volume of construction materials will be supplied from material storage yards and local
suppliers near the project area, with an average transportation distance of 15 kilometers, using
10-ton trucks.

groups within a 500m radius of the subproject implementation areas. The impact level is considered
Low because: (i) Dust concentration at a 5m distance is significantly lower than the regulatory
standard; (ii) The construction period for the affected works is short-term; (iii) The number of
affected individuals—workers and nearby residents—is relatively small (as presented in Table 4-
3); (iv) These impacts can be mitigated through appropriate technical and management measures.
    ❖ Dust from material gathering, loading/unloading
The primary source of dust emissions from material gathering and loading/unloading activities
originates mainly at the main material storage area serving the construction works of Subproject
1.
Based on the dust pollution coefficient generated during the loading and unloading of materials,
referred to in AP 42, Fifth Edition Compilation of Air Pollutant Emission Factors, Volume 1:
Stationary Point and Area Sources, for example:
        - For crushed stone: 0,0035 kg of dust/ton
        - For sand: 0,0011 kg of dust/ton
        - For cement: 0,36 kg of dust/ton, etc.
Calculation of dust concentration arising from the loading and unloading of construction
materials according to the method of Tran Ngoc Chan, 1999, Air pollution and treatment
emissions (Volume 1), Hanoi Science and Technology Publishing House.
Think of the air mass at the construction site area as a box with the dimensions of length L (m),
width W (m), and height H (m), one side of the bottom of the air box is parallel to the wind

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              146
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
direction. Assuming that the air blowing into the box is polluted air and the air at the
construction site at the time of construction is quite fresh, the average concentration of dust
generated in 1 hour is calculated according to the formula.

                                         Es . L
                                 C=             (1 − e -ut/L )     [CT5]
                                         u. H

Where:           C – Dust concentration generated on average one hour (mg/m3);
                 Es - Emission load calculated per unit area;
                 Es = M/(L  W)           (mg/m2.s)
                 M- Population load (mg/s);
                 u - Average wind speed in the wind direction at the top of the box (m/s);
                 H - Height of the diffusion box (m);
                 L, W - Length and width of the diffusion box (m).
The calculated concentration of dust generated from material loading and unloading activities,

The impact level of dust generated from construction material loading and unloading activities

low because: (i) The concentrations of dust and emissions are still significantly below the limits

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                       147
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
set by QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT; (ii) The impact is intermittent and short-term (approximately
12–24 non-continuous months); (iii) The number of affected individuals is limited, as
households are sparsely distributed along the transportation route; (iv) The impact from dust
emissions during material loading/unloading can be effectively mitigated through appropriate
technical and management measures.
    ❖ Dust and exhaust gases emitted from transportation of materials and waste
The transportation vehicles and construction machinery/equipment primarily use diesel oil
(DO), thereby emitting pollutants from internal combustion, such as NOx, SO₂, and CO. As
mobile sources, these emissions directly affect road users along the transportation routes and
disperse into the surrounding air, impacting workers and residents living near the subproject
areas. The affected receptors include: (i) workers and residents in the areas listed in Table 4-3;
and (ii) the material and waste transportation routes such as Provincial Road 510, Provincial
Road 6 (Subproject A1), National Highway 10 (Subproject A2– Subproject 9), as presented in
Table 1-7 above. The calculated concentrations of dust and exhaust gases generated by
transporting materials and waste are based on World Health Organization statistics (1993), with
emission factors/1 ton of diesel fuel for internal combustion engines as follows: 4.3 kg dust; 20
× S kg SO₂; 28 kg CO; 55 kg NO₂. Based on the operation frequency of machinery, pollutant
concentrations depend on each construction area and are calculated using formula [CT6]. The
construction period is estimated at approximately 8 hours per day.

                                                      g
                             Emission coefficient (     DO) x Fuel demand (kg DO/day)
                                                      kg
Pollution load (g/s) =                                                                  [CT6]

The impact level of dust and emissions from construction material and waste transportation is
assessed as Low because: (i) A comparison between the concentrations of exhaust gases from
transportation vehicles (under unfavorable conditions with low wind speed and maximum
pollutant concentrations) and the QCVN 05:2023/BTNMT shows that at a distance of ≥5m
from the emission source, most pollutant concentrations remain within permissible limits.
Therefore, the impact from dust and emissions is considered negligible. For Subproject
specifically, the dust concentration exceeds the standard by 1.03 times; however, the dust
concentration remains below the regulatory threshold at a wind speed of u = 0.9 m/s within a
distance of <25 m, and at u = 1.4 m/s within <20 m; (ii) The duration of impact is short-term
and intermittent (approximately 12–24 months); (iii) The affected group is limited because the
construction is carried out in a large open area. Along the transportation route to the project
site, some sensitive receptors are identified, including: Hoa Loc Commune Health Station
(located along the asphalt road with a base width of 8m), Hoa Loc Commune People’s
Committee (35m from the road), Hoa Loc Commune Stadium (83m from the road), and schools
such as Hoa Loc Primary School and Hoa Loc Secondary School (located on branch roads,
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                          149
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
approximately 218 m and 145 m from the transportation route, respectively). Additionally,
scattered households reside along both sides of the road at the intersection of Bui Dat and Pham
Banh streets (Muoi Ba village, Xuan Loc Commune) and along the inter-village road in Xuan
Tien, Hoa Loc Commune (with distances from the road to main houses ranging from 5 to 27m).
As a result, dust from transport vehicles may settle on residential buildings and public
structures, affecting urban beautiful landscape and potentially impacting the health of residents
and road users. (iv) This impact can be mitigated through appropriate technical and
management measures, which the project owner is required to implement and are detailed in
the following sections of the report.
    ❖ Dust and emissions due to operation of construction machinery
Emissions are generated from the operation of construction machinery and equipment using
diesel oil for internal combustion engines such as: excavators, graders, welding machines,
clamshell dredger, generators, pile drivers, concrete mixers, etc. Pollutants emitted contain
products from the fuel combustion process of internal combustion engines such as NOx, SO2,
CO.
Similarly, to calculate the amount of emissions (CO, NO2, SO2) from machinery and equipment
operating during construction such as ground leveling, mixers, pile drivers, etc. (using one ton
of diesel fuel for internal combustion), This Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report is
based on statistical data from the World Health Organization (WHO – 1993). The emission
factors for internal combustion engines using 1 ton of diesel fuel are as follows: dust – 4.3 kg;
SO₂ – 20 × S kg; CO – 28 kg; NO₂ – 55 kg.
The calculation process is estimated based on the operation frequency of machinery, with
pollutant concentrations depending on the specific construction area. The construction duration
is estimated at approximately 8 hours per day. The emission volume and pollutant concentration
load from diesel combustion are calculated using formula [CT6].

ranging from 0.8 to 1.6 m/s and a working shift duration of 8 hours, the concentration of exhaust
emissions generated from construction machinery and equipment is as follows:

the permissible limits at a distance of 25 meters from the source. (ii) The duration of impact is
short-term and intermittent, estimated at around 12–24 months. (iii) The affected group is
limited due to the sparse residential density along the main transportation route (as referenced
in Table 1-11). Some fishing households operating at the current port may be affected, but the
impact is minimal, since most fishing activities conclude around 11:00 AM or 3:00 PM - outside
the typical hours of material and waste transportation. (iv) This impact can be effectively
mitigated through appropriate technical and management measures.

    ❖ Dust and emissions due to welding activities at the site

welding fumes and hazardous emissions such as CO and NOx - particularly during the joining
of structural steel frames and steel formwork. During welding, the chemicals contained in
welding rod combust and release fumes that contain toxic substances, which may pollute the
environment and pose health risks to construction workers. The emission load of pollutants
generated from electric welding of structural components depends on the type of welding rod
used, as follows:

of subproject 1, 2, and 3 respectively (as detailed in Table 4-3). The subproject areas are
spacious, with no residential households located within the construction zones, and
construction activities are intermittent rather than continuous. However, to ensure
optimal working conditions and protect the health of workers, the project owner should
take appropriate mitigation measures to reduce the impact of welding fumes and toxic
emissions on affected individuals.

   4.4.1.2. Impacts due to noise and vibration
❖ Impact due to noise
   During the construction of the subproject components, noise arises mainly from: (i) the
   operation of trucks transporting construction materials and waste; (ii) the operation of
   construction machinery and equipment; (iii) dismantling/completing the construction items;
   and (iv) the activities of staffs and workers involved in the construction. Among them, noise is
   primarily generated from operation of construction machinery.
   The forecasted noise levels generated by key construction machinery and equipment are
   presented in the table below.
               Table 4-12: Levels of noise generated from construction machinery
     No.                    Machinery, equipment                         Noise (dBA) by 1.5m
      1     Bulldozer                                                             93
      2     Compactor (roller)                                                  72 – 74
      3     Excavator                                                           72 – 84
      4     Bucket excavator (Clamshell dredger)                                72 – 93
      5     Scraper and grader                                                  80 – 93
      6     Concrete mixer                                                      75 – 88
      7     Concrete pump                                                       80 – 83
      8     Concrete vibrator                                                     85
      9     Generator                                                           72 – 83

     11     Truck                                                                 85
   (Source: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Noise from construction equipment and construction
                                                              machinery NJID, 300.1, 31 – 12 – 197)
   However, noise levels will gradually decrease by distance and are determined using the formula
   presented by Pham Ngoc Dang in the book Ambient Air Environment, Science and Technics
   Publishing House, 2003, as follows:

                                  Li = L p − Ld − Lc (dBA )   [CT7]

           In which:
           Li - Noise level at the calculated area at a distance d (m) from the source
           Lp - Noise level measured at the source of noise (1.5 m away);
           ∆Ld - Noise level decrease by distance d at frequency i;
                                             r 1+a 
                                Ld = 20 lg  1        [CT8]
                                             r2  

           Where:
           r1- Distance to the noise source with Lp (m);
           r2- The distance to calculate the noise level reduction according to the distance
           corresponding to Li (m);
           a- The specific absorption coefficient of noise with the ground terrain (a = 0.1);
           ∆Lc- Reduction of noise level through obstructions. The construction area is surrounded
           by buildings and brick walls, so ∆Lc = 1.2;
   The maximum noise levels at various distances generated by construction equipment operations
   are presented in the table below:

According to the calculation results, the noise levels at a distance of 30m from construction
vehicles and equipment remain within the permissible limit (<70 dB). Noise levels decrease
further as the distance from the construction site to the affected area increases. Among the noise
sources mentioned above, the most significant is the mobile noise generated by trucks carrying
construction materials through residential areas.
The receptors affected by noise from each subproject experience no significant impact within a
30m radius from the construction site. Therefore, noise impacts primarily affect workers who
directly operate machinery and equipment within the 30m construction zone. For the
surrounding area, the impact on local residents is considered minimal. Specific affected groups
are detailed in Table 4-3.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             154
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
The level of noise impact at construction sites is considered low because: (i) The construction period
is relatively short (12–24 months) and not continuous. (ii) Equipment does not operate
simultaneously but rather in phases or shifts, depending on the progress and workload of each
construction item. Different types of machinery are used at different times, resulting in minimal
cumulative noise impact at any single location. (iii) All vehicles and equipment are subject to
regular maintenance, and noise emissions will cease once construction activities are completed. (iv)
Noise primarily affects workers operating machinery and working on-site; there is no significant
effect on surrounding residential areas or the broader space. (v) Noise impacts can be effectively
mitigated through appropriate technical and management measures.
      ❖ Impact due to vibration
Vibration is primarily generated by high-powered and frequently operated equipment on the
construction site, such as bulldozers, excavators, and air compressors—mainly during activities like
ground leveling and pile driving for embankment/port reinforcement in subprojects 1 and 2. The
remaining subprojects do not experience vibration impacts from construction machinery.
                   Table 4-15: Vibration of some typical construction equipment
 No. Type of means and equipment             Reference vibration level (in vertical direction, dB)
                 used
  1 Excavator                                                         80
  2 Bulldozer                                                         79
  3 Heavy Duty Trucks                                                 74
  4 Roller                                                            82
  5 Air compressor                                                    81
 Source: U.S. Environmental Protection Commission Vibration from Construction Equipment
                                    and Construction Machinery NJID, 300.1, 31 - 12 - 197
To forecast the vibration level that decreases with distance, use the formula:
                         L = L0 – 10lg (r/r0) – 8.7a (r – r0) (dB)       [CT9]
          Where:
      -   L is the vibration in dB at the distance "r" meters from the source;
      -    is the vibration in dB measured at a distance of "ro" meters from the source. Vibration
          at a distance of ro = 10 meters is generally acknowledged as the source vibration.
      -   a a is the intrinsic reduction coefficient of vibration for the surrounding environment,
          approximately 0.5.
By applying formula [CT9], the forecasted vibration decrease over distance at the construction
sites of subprojects 1 and 2 is calculated. The results are presented below.

According to the calculation results, the vibration level of construction vehicles and equipment
at a distance of 12m is within the permissible limit of QCVN 27:2010/BTNMT (<75dB) and
will decrease as the affected location is farther away from the source of vibration.
The vibration of construction equipment will directly affect the construction workers at the
construction site and construction works on the site of subproject 1, 2. However, vibration
impacts on the nearest residential areas are minimal: approximately 20m away in Hoang Tien
commune for Subproject 1; 20–120m away in Hoa Loc commune and the existing port for
Subproject 2; and 20–100m away near Phu Son 2 canal and Bao Trieu canal for Subproject 3.
The predicted vibration levels beyond 14 meters remain low for general areas (≤70 dBA), and
beyond 16 meters for areas requiring special quiet conditions (≤60 dBA). For subprojects 3
through 9, vibration levels are approximately equal to baseline levels measured prior to
construction. Therefore, vibration impacts on these remaining subprojects are considered
insignificant.
In general, direct vibration impacts are assessed as MINOR because: (i) Equipment operation
is intermittent rather than continuous. (ii) Vibration effects are localized to specific construction
areas. (iii) Workers operate machinery in shifts and are equipped with appropriate personal
protective equipment. Vibration impacts can be effectively mitigated through the
implementation of suitable construction methods and scheduling plans tailored to each
subproject site. Therefore, vibration is considered to have no significant impact.
Impact of vibration on existing structures during construction of fishing port and canal
embankments
Two out of nine subprojects involve port embankment construction activities, including:
Subproject 1 - Hoang Truong fishing port and Subproject 2 - Lach Truong fishing port. In
which, the structure consists of a soft-base pier on a D800mm bored pile foundation (does not
affect the sediment flow at the river mouth), the average length of the piles is expected to be
from 13.0m ÷ 15m, the pile tips embedded in a hard rock layer; Each transverse frame segment
includes two rows of bored piles with a diameter of D800mm. The transverse pile spacing for
one segment is 10m (1.7m + 6.8m + 1.5m). Each longitudinal frame segment includes six rows
of piles, with longitudinal spacing of 30m (1.0m + (5.6m × 5) + 1.0m). The use of bored pile
drilling method poses minimal risk of vibration impact and causes less disturbance to nearby
structures compared to traditional pile driving methods. Vibrations mainly arise during the
drilling through soil and rock layers and during casing installation. The impacts of vibration on
existing structures depend on various factors, including:
      •   Distance: is considered the most important factor. The vibration intensity decreases
          rapidly as the distance from the vibration source (drill bit) to the structure increases.
          Nearby structures (within 10-15m) will be more significantly affected. Within subproject
          1 scope, the existing structures of Hoang Truong fishing port are more than 20m away
          from the construction area, so they are not significantly affected by bored pile drilling
          activities. For the subproject 2, neighboring structures such as Truong Xa parish church,
          Hoa Loc fishing port center, and the aquaculture area are 50-250m away from the

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                156
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
        construction area, and are not affected by vibrations caused by this bored pile drilling
        activity; Some seafood processing, trading, and frozen export facilities are located
        relatively close to the construction site; The adjacent areas consist of open yard and inter-
        communal traffic roads, so the level of impact on the existing workshops (about 20-25 m
        away) is assessed to be minimal.
    •   Stiffness and types of soil: Soft, weak ground has the ability to amplify vibration
        waves, while hard or rock ground will transmit vibrations more effectively. The fact that
        the pile tip embedded in a hard rock layer can increase the localized vibration level
        during the rock drilling phase. For the subproject 1 - Sedimentary siltstone, and sandy
        siltstone are strongly weathered into: Clay mixed with fine sand particles, gravel,
        fragments, and a few small pebbles with mottled yellow-gray, purple-brown, blue-gray.
        The soil condition is semi-hard; Subproject 2 - The geological layer in the water area in
        front of the port has a single layer: silty clay mixed with organic humus, clam and oyster
        shells, interspersed with very thin layers of sandy silt in gray-brown, dark gray, and
        gray-green colors. The soil condition ranges from flowable to plastic-flowable. The
        surveyed layer thickness is 5.0m. Given the stiffness and soil types, the vibration will
        be greatly reduced when drilling through these geological layers.
    •   Structure and age of the works: The existing structure at Hoang Truong fishing port
        and the old Hoa Loc fishing port have weak construction, posing the risks of crack and
        damagea. These structure, built from reinforced concrete, were built long ago (between
        2008 and 2007), some sections of the embankment are made of tightly packed rubble
        stone, many of which have deteriorated. Sediment accumulation near the embankment,
        along with years of unauthorized landfilling by surrounding residents for spontaneous
        business activities, has reduced their functional efficiency. However, these
        constructions are basically 20 - 25 m away from the construction area, so the impact on
        existing constructions is considered insignificant.
Seven out of nine (subproject 3 -> subproject 9) involve pile driving during dredging and
construction of infrastructure components on the canal, including: (i) M300 reinforced concrete
piles with diameters of 30 cm x 30 cm, about 6 m long, and bamboo piles with diameters of ϕ6-
8 cm, driven at a density of 25 piles/m² with a driving depth of 2.5–3 m. The pile driving method
poses significant risks of cracking, damage, or collapse to nearby existing structures. During
execution, the pile driving equipment exerts a large force to drive the piles to the required depth.
The impact force generates wave-like ground vibrations. These vibrations can cause fractures
in geological bonds and in the connections between structures and their foundations, leading to
shaking, cracking, or even collapse of buildings. According to initial technical calculations, the
risk of infrastructure collapse lies within a radius of 30–50 m (for piles driven deeper than 10
m) and within a radius of approximately 5–30 m (for piles driven less than 10 m deep)
depending on geological characteristics and pile depth at each location as well as factors such
as distance, soil stiffness and type of ground and structure, and age of potentially affected
buildings. Specifically:
For the subproject 3, the pile driving construction area is located more than 50m away from
existing structures (aquaculture area, Nga Tien kindergarten, Nga Tien primary school which
are situated 180–350m away); Some poultry farming areas adjacent to the Phu Son canal consist
only of temporary shelters made from tarpaulin and thin corrugated metal sheets or Nga Tien
market area is 20m from the canal route, a secondary market and no permanent structures but
only 3-4 households who conduct small scale trading activities; Therefore, the potential risk of
cracking or subsidence affecting nearby road and structures is negligible. The level of impact
is assessed as low because the inter-village and inter-communal roads within the port are
reinforced and paved with solid concrete. Moreover, the pile driving process is intermittent and
localized at each pile point.

various locations. Additionally, the embankment generally located more than 50m away from
existing residential houses/works/structures, except for the section near the aquaculture area
which is about 10-20m away and therefore pose a potential risk. The impact level is assessed
as low because the internal port road is reinforced and paved with solid concrete. Besides, the

In general, the risk of cracking existing structures due to canal embankment pile driving is
considered low and may not occur if the contractor adheres to construction methods and
implements ground reinforcement measures, including driving piles for protecting nearby
structures prior to construction activities. In the event such risks occurring, the contractor must
commit to reconstruction or compensation in accordance with agreements made with the
homeowners, property owners, or management entities of the affected structures. This
commitment is clearly stated in the agreement between the contractor, project owner, local
government representatives, and residents prior to construction commencement in the area.
 4.4.1.3. Impacts arising from wastewater
Wastewater generated during the construction phase of the subprojects consists: (1) Domestic
wastewater from workers (2) Construction wastewater (3) Stormwater runoff. The estimated
volume of wastewater generated is assessed as follows:
    ❖ Domestic wastewater of workers
The number of workers is highest during the initial phase of the project and gradually decreases
as construction progresses toward completion. According to QCVN 01:2021/BXD – National
Technical Regulation on Construction Planning, the standard water supply is 100 liters per
person per day and night. Based on TCXDVN 33:2006 – Water Supply: Pipeline Network and
Design Standards, the domestic water demand for workers working in 8-hour shifts and not
residing on-site is estimated at 25 liters per person per day. The total volume of wastewater
generated in each construction area is calculated as follows:

 The impact level is assessed as Low because: (i) The volume of domestic wastewater generated
 at each worker camp is relatively small (3.3–5 m³/day). (ii) Wastewater is concentrated within
 construction site areas. (iii) Wastewater generation is not continuous throughout the day,
 occurring mainly during rest periods and after daily work hours. Local labor is prioritized
 (typically accounting for 30–50% of the workforce mobilized for each subproject), and housing
 is rented from nearby residents, resulting in actual wastewater volumes being significantly
 lower than estimated. (iv) Although the flow rate of receiving bodies is not high, pollutant
 concentrations are relatively large. However, these water bodies have continuous flow. The
 receiving sources for wastewater are irrigation and drainage canals serving aquaculture zones,
 including the De Canal, Lach Truong River, and Hoang Truong Port. These areas primarily
 support vessel movement and storm sheltering, and are not intended for domestic water supply.
 (v) The duration of impact is not long (12–24 months) and occurs intermittently, with
 fluctuations in workforce size depending on the construction phase. Appropriate mitigation
 measures can be implemented to reduce these risks.
      ❖ Construction wastewater
 The sources of onshore construction wastewater during the implementation of the subprojects
 include: (i) Wheel-wash wastewater before vehicles exit the construction site; (ii) Wastewater
 from small-capacity mortar and concrete mixers used to cast small precast components (with

 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                   161
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
the remaining concrete sourced as ready-mix); and (iii) Wastewater from washing construction
equipment and tools. This type of wastewater is generated at a volume equal to 100% of the
supplied water. Its composition mainly includes suspended solids, soil, stone, mortar, and
cement. The pollution characteristics include hindering oxygen diffusion into the water and
elevating pH, which adversely affects aquatic life in the area. Total volume of construction
wastewater generated at each subproject is detailed as follows:
Table 4-20. Wastewater load generated from construction activities

      According to the 2005 study conducted by the Center for Urban and Industrial
Environmental Engineering – Hanoi University of Civil Engineering, the concentrations of
pollutants in construction wastewater are as follows:
               Table 4-21. Concentration of pollutants in construction wastewater
                               Flow rate                    Concentration of pollutants

      This type of wastewater is characterized by the presence of floating oils and suspended
solids. Wastewater generated from washing machinery, equipment, and vehicles contains high
levels of suspended sediments, oils, grease, soil, and sand. If this wastewater is not collected
and directed to sedimentation pits for preliminary treatment, but instead discharged directly into
drainage ditches or surrounding areas, it may negatively affect the quality of receiving water
bodies at each subproject site. Such discharge can increase turbidity, cause sediment
accumulation, and disrupt aquatic ecosystems. It may also degrade the quality of irrigation
water used for crops and aquaculture in nearby communities. Without proper treatment
measures prior to discharge into rivers, seas, or irrigation canals associated with each
subproject, the following impacts may occur:
     -   High concentrations of suspended solids increase water turbidity and reduce the ability
         of oxygen to dissolve from the air into the water.
     -   Accelerated sedimentation in the receiving water bodies.
     -   Elevated concentrations of pollutants in the receiving sources.
The level of impact is assessed as low to moderate. The increase in suspended solids and water
turbidity negatively affects the survival of aquatic species. If vehicle wash water containing oil
and grease is discharged without treatment, it may directly impact the aquatic ecosystem. At
high concentrations, such pollutants can cause toxicity and reduce the abundance of certain
aquatic species. However, since the volume of construction wastewater is relatively small, the
construction period is not long (12–24 months) and occurs intermittently—mainly during the
final 1–2 hours of each workday—the potential impacts can be effectively mitigated through
appropriate management and technical measures.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 162
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
In addition to construction wastewater generated from onshore activities, attention must also be
paid to wastewater arising from underwater construction works. Specifically:
Dredging activities at Subproject 1 involve suction dredgers, clamshell dredgers, and self-
propelled barges. At the end of each workday, suction dredgers are cleaned to remove dredged
materials adhering to the hull, generating wastewater from equipment and vessel cleaning.
Based on actual data from similar dredging projects, the estimated volume of wastewater at
Subproject 1 is approximately 3 m³/day. This cleaning process causes soil and sand to be
washed into the dredging channel, potentially increasing turbidity in the surface water of the
Lach Truong River and De Canal. Affected receptors include aquatic organisms, fishery
resources, and nearshore fishermen. However, these impacts are considered minor due to the
small volume of wastewater and the nature of the suspended solids—mainly mud and sand—
which settle quickly at the discharge site. Appropriate technical solutions and mitigation
measures should be taken to minimize the environmental impact of this construction
wastewater. Further analysis of water quality degradation due to dredging will be presented in
in the site-specific impact assessments in the following section.
(ii) Wastewater leakage from the bottom-discharge barge during the transportation and
disposal process (Subproject 1)
The total dredged volume for Subproject 1 is approximately 18,800 m³. The dredged materials
are transported using bottom-discharge barges from the dredging area to the disposal sit in Hai
Loc commune (now Van Loc commune), over a distance of about 3.0 km, posing a risk of
leakage and overflow—especially during periods of heavy rainfall. During transportation,
dredged materials may spill into surrounding waters, affecting aquatic organisms and increasing
turbidity in the surface water near Hoang Truong Port. The extent of leakage is considered
minor and depends on factors such as the volume of dredged materials on the barge and rainfall
intensity. These impacts can be mitigated through appropriate technical measures. A
transportation plan for bottom-discharge barges should be developed to minimize
environmental risks during dredged material handling.
(iii) Wastewater from the dredged material suction process of cutter suction dredgers
discharged to the disposal site – Subproject 2:
During the dredging process at Subproject 2, a cutter suction dredger will be used to pump
dredged materials directly to the disposal site in Hai Loc commune (now Van Loc commune),
located opposite the storm shelter anchorage area, with an average transportation distance of
about 0.5 km. The disposal site is an abandoned salt production area, currently unused, and
surrounded by elevated embankments higher than the ground elevation. The total dredged
volume of the subproject 2 is approximately 365,000 m³. Based on typical dredging mixture
ratios, the water-to-material volume ratio is 40:60, meaning the water content in the dredged
slurry is estimated at 263,993 m³. The dredged material is transported by channel water, which
flows back through settlement gaps and discharge outlets after passing through sedimentation
pits at the disposal site. This wastewater contains silt and suspended solids, but does not contain
oil or grease, and therefore does not pose significant environmental harm. However, without
proper flow direction planning, sedimentation pits along the route, containment embankments,
and wastewater filtration outlets, the discharged water may overflow into existing drainage
canals, potentially causing erosion, sand collapse, and other physical disturbances. Affected
receptors include aquatic organisms, irrigation canals adjacent to the disposal site, nearby
aquaculture areas, and local residents around Van Loc commune. Overall, the environmental
impact of wastewater from the dredging process of cutter suction dredgers is considered
negligible, due to: (i) No significant intrusion of pollutants into local water sources, thus no
substantial environmental contamination; (ii) Minimal impact on affected receptors, with no
major ecological or socioeconomic disruption; (iii) Short and intermittent construction duration,


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              163
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
depending on actual progress and contractor’s schedules. Appropriate technical and
management measures can be implemented to effectively mitigate these potential impacts.
(iv) Wastewater leakage from dredged material during transportation and dumping (Subproject
3, Subproject 4, Subproject 5, Subproject 6, Subproject 7, Subproject 8, Subproject 9)
The construction solutions selected include: (i) Use Lassen III sheet piles to enclose the
construction area along the right bank embankment. Flow will be diverted to allow construction
of the remaining drainage channel section; (ii) For the main drainage and supply canal section
from Ong Ninh Sluice to Quay Co Sluice, and from Ong Thuoc Sluice to Ong Vanh Sluice,
including internal branch canals: Use Pontoons to support excavators for dredging the canal
bed according to the designed cross-section. Flow will be diverted through the existing drainage
channel during construction; (iii) For the canals under Subprojects 4, 5, 6, and 7 located in the
communes of Hoang Yen, Hoang Luu, Hoang Phong, and Hoang Chau, construction will be
carried out using Pontoons to support excavators for dredging the canal bed according to the
designed cross-section. Therefore, flow diversion is not required during construction. Dredged
material from these canal sections will be temporarily stockpiled at designated locations along
the canal, allowing water separation to reduce the volume and weight of the dredged slurry.
Wastewater leakage during the transportation of dredged material—containing organic matter
and soil/sand residues—may affect both air (road transportation by trucks) and water
environments when overflowing into canals/ditches along the transportation route to disposal
site (road transportation by 10-ton truck to dumping site 1, 2 in Nga Tien commune – subproject
3, Son Trang hamlet, Hoang Tien commune – Subproject 4, Bac Hoi Trieu village, Hoang Chau
commune – Subproject 5, Phuong Ngo 1 hamlet, Hoang Chau commune – subproject 6, Chau
Trieu hamlet, Hoang Chau commune – subproject 7, Hung Thanh village, Van Loc commune
– subproject 8, Dung hamlet, Quang Chinh commune – subproject 9, Wastewater leakage from
trucks (subproject 3 to subproject 9) carrying dredged material may leave behind dust and
sediment, contributing to air pollution and affecting public health along inter-village, inter-
commune roads and National Route 10. These impacts are detailed in Table 1-7 – Chapter 1 of
the report. In addition, the wastewater also affects the beautiful landscape and sanitation of the
entire transportation route. However, since the construction period is relatively short
(approximately 12 months) and the activities are intermittent, the overall impact is considered
minor. With the implementation of appropriate technical and management measures, these
wastewater-related effects can be effectively mitigated.
(iii) Tugboats and barges at Subproject 1
At the dredging site of Subproject 1, tugboats and barges are arranged to transport dredged material
from the dredging area to the designated disposal site in Hai Loc commune (now Van Loc commune).
At the end of each workday, the suction dredger is cleaned to remove residual dredged material
adhering to the hull. Based on actual data from similar dredging projects, the estimated volume of
wastewater generated from this cleaning process at the subproject 1 is approximately 2 m³/day. This
cleaning activity causes mud and soil to be washed into the receiving water body, specifically the
Lach Truong River—within the dredging channel area of subproject 1—potentially increasing surface
water turbidity. However, the impact is considered minor, due to the small volume of wastewater and
the nature of the suspended solids, which are primarily mud and sand that settle quickly at the
discharge site.
(iv) Bilge Water
During the dredging operations at Subprojects 1 and 2, cutter suction dredgers, tugboats, and
canoes are used. Given the 12-month construction period, bilge water is expected to be
generated. According to the International Maritime Organization (IMO), improper discharge of
bilge water from vessels can lead to oxygen depletion, negatively affecting marine life. It can
also cause visible pollution, especially in coastal areas. Based on actual data from similar

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               164
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
dredging projects in the region, bilge water is typically generated due to leakage from pipes,
faulty sea valves, or the propeller shaft assembly into the engine bilge compartment. These
issues are usually addressed and repaired promptly. Estimated bilge water volumes are:
approximately 0.05 m³ per construction cycle for subproject 1; approximately 0.1 m³ per
construction cycle for subproject 2. Although the volume is small, specific management
solutions are required to manage bilge water as well as minimize impacts on the sea.
      ❖ Stormwater Runoff
Stormwater runoff in the construction phase is calculated is based on the land-based
construction area only, excluding the surface water area. According to TCVN 7957:2008, the
volume of rainwater runoff across the construction site areas is calculated using the following
formula:
                                          Qm= q.C.F (l/s)           [CT13]
       Where:
          - Q: Max rainwater runoff flow rate (l/s)
          - q: Rainfall intensity (l/s.ha).
          - C: Runoff coefficient
          - F: Construction site area (ha)
Rainfall intensity is calculated using the following formula:

                                        A (1 + ClgP)
                                  q=                        [CT14]
                                                    n
                                              (t + b)

Where:
          - q: Rainfall intensity (l/s.ha).
          - P: Repeated cycle of rain (P = 02 years).
          - t: Time of rain flow in the project area (about 90 minutes);
        - A, C, b, n: Climatic constants are based on local rainfall conditions, with the following
values: A=9.150; C=0.53; b=28; n=0,97 depending on climate of each region. The calculated
rainfall intensity at the project construction site is: q= 103.75 (l/s.ha), converting this value to
m3 per day (m³/day) using k=10-3 x 24 x 3600. Therefore, rainwater runoff volume across the
land-based construction areas of each subproject is calculated and presented in the following
table:
           Table 4-22. The largest stormwater runoff volume in construction phase
                                 Runoff coefficient        Area        Stormwater flow
 No.        Subprojects
                                       (C)               (F – m2)      rate (Q – m3/day)
  1     Subproject 1                    0.3               80,117              7,211
  2     Subproject 2                    0.9               56,500             15,255
  3     Subproject 3                   0.15             37,202,30             1,674
  4     Subproject 4                   0.15             14,518,32              653
  5     Subproject 5                   0.15             19,198,45              864

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               165
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Rainwater runoff must be managed through appropriate collection and flow diversion systems
to ensure discharge into the local common drainage ditches, thereby preventing localized
flooding within the project area. The concentration of pollutants in rainwater depends on the
interval between rainfall events and the cleanliness of the surface area. Pollutant levels are
typically highest during the initial phase of rainfall (defined as the period from when rainwater
begins to flow across the surface until approximately 15 to 20 minutes later).
According to WHO data (1993), the concentration of pollutants in rainwater runoff is
approximately: 0.5 – 1.5 mg N/l, 0.004 – 0.03 mg P/l, 10 - 20 mg COD/l and 10 - 20 mg TSS/l,
much lower than the permissible limits set by QCVN 40:2025/BTNMT – National Technical
Regulation on Industrial Wastewater Quality. However, rainwater runoff across construction
site surfaces can carry loose soil, debris, surface minerals, and leaked oil and grease. This leads
to increased concentrations of suspended solids, organic matter, turbidity, and hydrocarbons in
nearby water bodies such as coastal waters, rivers, and canals within the project area (as
previously mentioned). Such degradation in water quality can have negative impacts on aquatic
ecosystems, particularly affecting fish and other aquatic species by impairing respiration and
photosynthesis. The ecosystem in the project area is relatively simple, making it more
vulnerable to significant impacts from pollution.
The direct impact level of rainwater runoff is assessed as minor because: (i) The volume of
rainwater runoff at the construction site is moderate; (ii) Runoff is concentrated within
designated construction zones and disposal areas; (iii) Rainwater runoff is not continuous
throughout the year—the rainy season typically occurs from July to October, while the
construction period spans approximately 12 to 24 months; (iv) The receiving water bodies (sea,
rivers, canals, and streams) have relatively large flow capacities; (v) The impact can be
effectively mitigated through appropriate technical and management measures.
 4.4.1.4. Impacts due to solid waste generated
Solid waste generated during the construction phase consists of three main categories: (1)
Construction solid waste; (2) Domestic solid waste; (3) Hazardous solid waste. The estimated
quantities of each type of waste are assessed as follows:
    ❖ Construction solid waste
This type of waste is generated during the site preparation phase and the construction process.
The solid waste includes the volume of: (i) Cleared vegetation cover and soil or rocks spilled
during the transportation of earth and sand; (ii) Dredged materials; (iii) Excess construction
materials such as scrap metal, cement bag wrappers, surplus steel, wood fragments, and broken
bricks.
Volume of construction solid waste of each subproject is as follows:
                        Table 4-23. Solid waste during construction phase
                                      Source of construction solid wastes
                                 Demolition of existing    Vegetation
                Excavated soil                                              Construction solid wastes

If not properly managed and treated, the entire volume of construction solid waste (CSW) may
negatively impact the surrounding environment of the project area, as well as affect construction
progress and the quality of the built structures. This direct impact is assessed as moderate
because: (i) The volume of waste generated is relatively large but localized within the
construction site; (ii) The construction period is relatively short, approximately 12–24 months;
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  167
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
(iii) Construction solid waste (including excess excavated soil and dredged materials) will be
collected and transported to approved disposal sites; (iv) A significant portion of the dredged
material (primarily sand) may be reused as a sand resource (e.g., for landfilling), provided it
meets construction material standards, and may later be auctioned in accordance with the
Mineral Law; (v) These impacts can be mitigated through appropriate mitigation measures.
    ❖ Domestic solid waste:
Source of domestic solid waste: originates from daily activities of construction workers living
in temporary camps, which are expected to be set up at the construction sites of the subprojects.
The waste composition includes: Organic waste (leftover food, excess processed food, and fruit
peels) and inorganic waste (plastic bags, food containers, beverage cans, and packaging
materials).
Estimated waste generation per worker in the project area is approximately 0.9 kg/day
(According to QCVN 01:2021/BXD – the National Technical Regulation on Construction
Planning). However, since cooking activities will not be carried out on-site for workers, the
waste generation per worker is adjusted to be 0.3 kg/day. The total volume of domestic solid
waste generated during the project’s operational phase is detailed as follows:

The impacted groups include construction staff and workers directly involved at the site,
residing in temporary camps, as well as local residents in surrounding areas. Environmental
components potentially affected include: Surface water quality at Hoang Truong coastline
(Subproject 1), Truong Giang River (Subproject 2), Irrigation canals and aquaculture areas
(Subproject 3 through Subproject 9), Len River (Subproject 8), Hoang and Yen Rivers
(Subproject 9). Soil quality in the subproject areas.
Domestic solid waste typically consists of 60–70% organic matter and 30–40% other materials, and
may contain a high concentration of bacteria and pathogens. If not properly planned for collection,
transportation, and treatment, the organic components—especially those carrying disease-causing
agents—can pose significant risks to water and air quality, and indirectly affect the health of
construction workers, nearby residents, and the operation of adjacent infrastructure.
The individuals directly impacted by construction solid waste include site staff and workers,
fishermen operating at the existing fishing port, and employees of the ship repair workshop at Hoang
Truong Port. This waste is a source of increased dust concentration, which may affect the health of
workers and fishermen. Improper stockpile of bulky waste materials can obstruct pathways, create
slippery surfaces, and pose accident risks for both construction personnel and seafood harvesters. If
the substantial volume of construction solid waste is not properly collected, stored, and treated, it may
be washed away by rainwater runoff into water bodies such as Lach Truong River, internal canals,
and irrigation ditches. This can lead to sedimentation and blockage of surface water flow at the port

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                    168
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
area, increasing the risk of localized flooding at the construction site and surrounding areas—
especially during the rainy season. Such conditions may pollute water sources, disrupt water usage,
and negatively impact the local aquatic ecosystem. The impact level is assessed as moderate, and with
the implementation of appropriate mitigation measures, these environmental and human health risks
can be effectively avoided or minimized.
      ❖ Hazardous waste
Hazardous waste generated during the construction phase includes: (i) Oil-contaminated
materials from machinery maintenance activities (code 18 02 01) (ii) Discarded asphalt from
road construction and asphalt containers (iii) Packaging waste and containers for lubricants,
asphalt, fuel, and paint (code 18 01 02) (iv) Welding rod stubs (code 07 04 01) (v) Batteries,
light bulbs, and toner cartridges from on-site office (code 16 01 06). The volume of hazardous
waste depends on the number of machines and equipment used, the workforce size, and the
quantity of construction materials. The specific amount of hazardous waste (both solid and
liquid) generated during the construction phase of each subproject is as follows:

The individuals directly impacted include staff and workers on-site and residing in temporary
camps, as well as local residents in surrounding areas. Environmental components potentially
affected are: Surface water quality along the Hoang Truong coastline (Subproject 1), Truong
