The level of impact is assessed as low because: (i) The volume of hazardous solid waste
generated on-site is relatively small (averaging 7.3 kg/month/subproject) and is localized to
specific construction stages; (ii) In the project area, there are designated units responsible for
the collection and treatment of hazardous waste (HW); equipment and machinery repairs are
expected to be carried out at local professional repair centers; (iii) The impact duration is not
continuous, occurring over an estimated 12–16 months of construction; (iv) The impact can be
effectively mitigated through the implementation of appropriate mitigation measures.
 4.4.1.5. Impact on Terrestrial and Aquatic Flora and Fauna
      ❖ For terrestrial animals

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                169
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Impacts on terrestrial organisms primarily arise during the construction phase, due to the
removal of the organic soil layer for foundation works at Hoang Truong and Lach Truong
fishing ports, leading to loss of existing vegetation cover. Additionally, noise and vibrations
from machinery may affect certain terrestrial species such as field birds, reptiles, livestock, and
domestic animals in the surrounding project area. However, based on current site surveys, the
project area does not contain any rare or endangered species, and the habitat is relatively simple
with low biodiversity. Therefore, the level of impact is assessed as minor. These effects can be
effectively mitigated through appropriate construction management measures, including
limiting the construction footprint, controlling noise levels, and restoring vegetation after
construction.
According to Chapter 2 of the report, the terrestrial biological resources in the project area are not
diverse, with a simple species composition and no rare or endangered species requiring
conservation. Therefore, the impacts on terrestrial animals are considered insignificant.
    ❖ For aquatic species
During the construction phase, the primary sources of impact on aquatic ecosystems include
domestic wastewater, rainwater runoff from worker camps and construction sites, and dredging
activities. These factors contribute to increased turbidity, organic matter, nitrogen, and oil
concentrations in receiving water bodies such as the Hoang Truong coastline, Truong Giang
River, internal irrigation canals, aquaculture zones, and major rivers including Len, Hoang, and
Yen. Dredging may also disrupt water flow and degrade water quality, thereby affecting aquatic
habitats. However, based on current site surveys, the project area exhibits low biodiversity, with
no rare or endangered species recorded. The aquatic ecosystem mainly consists of brackish
water fish, zooplankton, small shrimp and crabs, benthic algae, clams, mussels, and snails, all
with relatively low population density. Therefore, the level of impact is assessed as minor and
can be effectively managed through appropriate construction practices and water quality control
measures.
Subproject 1,2:
Impact on Phytoplankton: The impact on phytoplankton primarily occurs during dredging
activities, which increase water turbidity and total suspended solids (TSS). This reduces light
penetration through the water column, thereby limiting photosynthesis and growth of
phytoplankton, and affecting their productivity and role in nutrient cycling within the aquatic
ecosystem. Some phytoplankton may also be displaced or damaged by construction equipment.
However, these impacts are confined to a limited spatial range (approximately 500m in radius)
and a short duration (sediment settles within about 15 minutes after dredging stops). Once
dredging activities conclude, environmental conditions gradually stabilize, allowing
phytoplankton to recover quickly and reestablish ecological balance—especially when turbidity
and TSS return to acceptable levels. Therefore, the impact is assessed as minor.
Impact on Zooplankton and Benthic Organisms
Dredging activities disturb the bottom sediment layer, causing some species to relocate to
adjacent habitats. However, this process may make certain benthic organisms to be swept away
with the sediment and removed from their natural environment. Others may perish due to
habitat destruction. These impacts are largely confined to the dredging corridor, are
unavoidable, and localized in nature. Nevertheless, aquatic ecosystems typically possess strong
self-recovery capabilities. The benthic communities in the affected area may gradually
reestablish themselves within approximately 6 to 12 months, depending on environmental
conditions and water flow.
Potential impacts on fish, shrimp, and aquatic species in rivers and coastal waters: Increased
water turbidity and suspended solids can hinder the respiration of aquatic species in rivers and

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 170
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
sea. CH4 and H2S5 released from disturbed bottom sediments may adhere to the gills of aquatic
organisms, further obstructing their breathing. However, fish and shrimp tend to respond
quickly to habitat disturbances, often swimming away as soon as dredging begins. Therefore,
the risk of mortality due to habitat changes during dredging is considered very low. If oil or
grease spills into the water during construction, it may cause acute toxicity to aquatic organisms
and accumulate in the food chain—from lower-level organisms such as algae and
phytoplankton to higher-level species like shrimp and fish. Once dredging activities cease,
sediment layers are restored, and due to the short life cycles of many aquatic species (ranging
from 7 to 30 days), recovery is typically rapid following stabilization of the riverbed in areas
such as Lach Truong River and De Canal. Additionally, the project area experiences frequent
vessel traffic, resulting in low species density and limited species diversity. As such, the overall
impact of the project on the aquatic ecosystem is considered insignificant.
Impact on wetland vegetation: The impact on wetland vegetation primarily concerns species
such as mangrove apple (Sonneratia), mangrove (Avicennia), and scattered wild plants growing
along both sides of the Lach Truong estuary and De Canal, with heights ranging from 0.2 to 1.2
m and located approximately 25–80 m from the dredging area. Since dredging is carried out
using suction dredgers and barges, with a discharge system that transports sediment to disposal
sites located about 500m away, the direct impact on wetland vegetation is minimal. The
disturbance is limited in both spatial extent and duration, occurring mainly during the
construction of the port’s frontage and storm shelter area. These wetland plant species possess
natural regenerative capacity after construction activities. Therefore, the impact level is
considered low and controllable.
Subproject 3,4,5,6,7,8,9:
Disturbance of bottom sediments: Dredging activities stir up bottom sediments, increasing
suspended silt and sand in the water, which in turn increases the concentration of pollutants.
Elevated levels of total suspended solids (TSS) reduce light penetration to the seabed, thereby
hindering the photosynthesis of algae species within the project area. This disruption limits
metabolic processes and the growth of aquatic organisms, ultimately reducing the availability
of food for higher trophic levels in the ecosystem.
Flow changes due to dredging activities. Dredging activities can change natural water flow
patterns, creating conditions that allow aquatic organisms from surrounding areas to migrate
into the project zone. This may lead to the formation of new biological interactions with native
species. If these interactions are symbiotic or mutually beneficial, the aquatic ecosystem may
thrive and experience increased biodiversity. Conversely, competitive or predator–prey
relationships could shift species composition and abundance, potentially restructuring the local
ecosystem. However, due to the short life cycles of many aquatic species (typically 7–10 days)
and the restoration of sediment layers following construction, the recovery potential of the
aquatic ecosystem is considered high. Once the canal and riverbed stabilize, ecological balance
is expected to reestablish rapidly.
Potential impacts on aquatic ecosystems in subproject areas: The potential impacts on aquatic
ecosystems across the subprojects are assessed as low because: (i) There are no rare or
endangered aquatic species identified within the project area. However, wastewater and
rainwater runoff may increase water turbidity, which can negatively affect aquatic organisms.
(ii) The duration of impact is intermittent and short-term, estimated to last 12–24 months during
construction. (iii) These impacts can be effectively mitigated through the implementation of
appropriate environmental management measures.
 4.4.1.6. Impacts due to erosion, sedimentation, and local flooding

5 According to secondary study, the main gases are CH and H S
                                                     4     2



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               171
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
During the construction phase, activities such as dredging, excavation, and filling in the
subproject areas, combined with erosive forces of rainfall and tidal fluctuations at estuaries and
rivers, may lead to a significant increase in suspended sediment levels over an extended period.
This elevated sediment load contributes to enhanced deposition within the construction area.
Heavy rainfall can cause surface erosion, mobilizing suspended materials from the construction
site. These materials may be carried by runoff into receiving water bodies, degrading water
quality and contributing to sedimentation in those aquatic environments. However, based on
environmental assessments, these impacts are considered temporary, occurring only during the
construction period (estimated at 12–24 months, intermittently). Once the technical
infrastructure is completed and the area is concreted, these impacts are expected to cease
entirely, specifically:
Subproject 1:
Sediment buildup at the estuary poses significant challenges for waterway navigation and flood
discharge safety. Therefore, dredging the navigation channel in the frontage area is essential.
However, dredging activities may change the seabed morphology in the frontage area, which
can affect water flow patterns both within and around the dredging zone of Subproject Area 1.
This change is a contributing factor that may increase the risk of shoreline erosion, particularly
along the protective embankments and the revetment behind the wharf, throughout the 24-
month construction period.
During the dry season, it is difficult to divert water in the Ma river into the Lach Truong River,
causing the estuary to be heavily influenced by wave action and tidal forces, which lead to
significant sediment accumulation. (i) The Lach Truong estuary experiences a diurnal tidal
regime, with large tidal amplitudes reaching up to ΔH max of 3.0–3.5m. Tidal effects during
the dry season are particularly pronounced, influencing both the estuary and the frontage area
of the port. The forechannel area in front of the port significantly influence seabed morphology
and increased disturbance of bottom sediments during low-flow periods. (ii) The estuary
exhibits an asymmetric morphology, with weak hydrodynamic conditions and complex
interactions among river flow, tidal currents, and wave energy. As a result, the deep channel
alignment tends to shift northward, and the northern shoreline of the estuary experiences
frequent sediment deposition6. On average, the estuary accumulates approximately 850,000
cubic meters of sediment annually, with sediment thickness ranging from 0.2 to 1m. The highest
accumulation is observed at the head of the deep channel and the area in front of the estuary,
extending toward the southern rocky cape.
The subproject 1 site is the water surface area located along the southern bank of the Lach
Truong River. The terrain elevation varies from +2.9 m to –7.27 m from the shoreline outward.
Dredging of the navigation channel is expected to bring primarily positive impacts, facilitating
vessel movement and improving flood drainage capacity in the area. However, dredging
activities may slightly affect the seabed morphology in the frontage area and pose MINOR risks
to the structural integrity and safety of existing infrastructure. Measures on construction
methods and assessment of structures and connection to existing Linh Truong port area and
investment in new port are necessary to reduce to erosion risks as well as promote the efficiency
of this port.
In addition, the leveling of entire planned land plots for site preparation and technical
infrastructure in Subproject Area 1 will alter the area’s natural elevation. Previously unused flat
terrain will be replaced with compacted sand or concrete surfaces during construction. This
transformation reduces the area’s natural water retention capacity, causing rainwater to flow
more rapidly into the river during heavy rainfall events. As a result, localized flooding may

6
 Study on Hydrodynamic Regime and Erosion–Sedimentation Trends at Lach Truong Estuary, Thanh Hoa
Province, November 2023, conducted by the National Key Laboratory for River and Coastal Dynamics.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              172
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
occur within the watershed during the construction phase. The impact is assessed as MINOR,
occurring only during short-term periods of intense rainfall, and may affect construction
progress and quality. However, in most cases, construction is paused during heavy rain or
scheduled for the dry season, minimizing disruption. With the implementation of appropriate
mitigation measures, these impacts can be effectively avoided or reduced.
Subproject 2:
Similar to the subproject 1, the general trend at the Lach Truong estuary is predominantly
sediment accumulation, with a relatively large annual volume of silt and sand deposition. This
sediment buildup poses significant challenges for waterway navigation and flood discharge
safety. According to the hydrodynamic regime and erosion–sedimentation trend study for the
Lach Truong estuary, the area experiences consistent sedimentation, with an annual deposition
thickness ranging from 0.2 to 1 m, concentrated at the upper section of the estuary. The sediment
source originates from the Lach Truong River, carried downstream by river flow, with an
estimated volume of approximately 166,000 m3 per year. Dredging activities in the forewater
area and storm shelter area under the subproject 2—Lach Truong Port, Hoa Loc Commune—
may disturb the sediment layer, alter seabed morphology, and affect water flow patterns in and
around the dredging site. These changes may also increase the risk of shoreline erosion,
particularly along the protective embankments and the existing Hoa Loc wharf revetments,
throughout the 24-month construction period.
The subproject 2 is the water surface area located along the eastern bank of the Lach Truong
River and the alluvial ground of the Kenh De River. The elevation ranges from +1.5 to +2.5
meters. The filling of the designated land plots for site preparation and construction of technical
infrastructure, creating space for vessel turning and mooring—will change the area’s natural
elevation. As a result, during rainfall events, rainwater will flow more rapidly into the river due
to reduced natural retention capacity. This may affect watershed hydrology and cause local
flooding during short periods of heavy rain. The flooding impact is assessed as MINOR, short-
term, and localized within the construction area during heavy rainfall events. These effects can
be effectively mitigated by appropriate construction methods, engineering techniques, and
construction scheduling.
In addition, dredging activities may pose risks to the structural integrity and safety of existing
facilities. However, the level of impact is assessed as MINOR. Geological and topographic
surveys were conducted in July 2024 to determine the geotechnical properties of the foundation
soils and embankment materials, with boreholes drilled along the channel and road centerlines
to depths of 3–5 meters. The implementation of appropriate construction methods, along with
structural assessments and integration with the existing Hoa Loc Port and the newly planned
port facilities, is essential to reduce the risk of shoreline erosion and enhance the port’s
operational efficiency. This issue was also raised and acknowledged during the community
consultation process, with input from local authorities of Hoa Loc commune and representatives
of the local residents. By combining the results of geological surveys, laboratory testing, and
stakeholder feedback, it is concluded that the impacts related to sedimentation and erosion
during dredging on both the environment and existing infrastructure can be effectively
mitigated through suitable engineering measures.
Subproject 3 → Subproject 9: The terrain elevation of the project site will be raised, and the
surface conditions will be altered, resulting in faster water concentration during rainfall events.
This reduces the area’s natural drainage capacity, thereby increasing the risk of local flooding
and potential subsidence within the construction zone, specifically:
Subproject 3: The project area is the water surface area of a irrigation canal. Geological
conditions vary across construction zones, with elevations ranging from 0.08 m to +2.29m. The


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              173
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
terrain elevation also changes gradually from the canal banks toward the channel bed, covering
Phu Son 2 Canal, Bao Trieu Canal, and the T4 sluice area.
Subproject 4: The natural elevation of the area varies significantly, ranging from –2.0 m to
+3.86 m, with uneven topography. The existing canals including Son Trang 1, Son Trang 2,
Hung Tien, and Nghia Thuc are currently narrow and silted, while other sections have been
completely filled in. As a result, the area is frequently flooded. The surface layer consists mainly
of alluvial deposits and sediment accumulation, which reduces the natural water retention
capacity of the site. Consequently, rainfall runoff flows rapidly into the river, leading to
potential impacts such as erosion, sedimentation, and local flooding within the aquaculture
canal under the subproject 4 in the protective dikes of the aquaculture area, Son Trang 1 and 2
sluices, Son Trang 2 drainage canal, Hung Tien Canal, and Nghia Thuc Canal in Hoang Yen
commune.
The subproject 5 is located within the irrigation canal system serving the aquaculture area.
Terrain elevation varies from the canal banks to the canal beds as follows: Cau Dua canal
ranging from 0.15 m to +1.88 m, Tay canal: –0.11 m to +2.15 m, Hoi Trieu canal: –0.35 m to
+1.41 m, Dong Coi canal: –0.91 m to +1.74 m. The stockpiling of construction materials and
dredged sediments may affect the upgrading works of Dong Coi, Hoi Trieu, Tay, and Cau Dua
Canals, causing erosion of canal banks and local flooding within the construction area.
Subproject 6: The landfilling process will alter the natural elevation of the area, transforming
the terrain from uneven levels ranging between –0.12m and +1.75m into a more stable and
uniform elevation. Previously, the surface layer consisted mainly of alluvial deposits and
sediment accumulation, which provided limited natural water retention capacity. As a result,
rainfall runoff tended to flow rapidly into the river. Upon completion of construction, however,
the stabilized ground conditions will create favorable conditions for water irrigation and
drainage in the aquaculture area along both sides of the canal, as well as for the operation and
management of associated infrastructure such as the Thach sluice, Voi sluice, and management
roads.
The location of subproject 7 is the surface water area of the existing irrigation canal system
serving the aquaculture area of Hoang Chau commune. The terrain in this area is moderately
varied, which is favorable for site leveling and construction activities. The elevation differences
from the canal banks until the project boundary are as follows: Canal banks and internal roads
within the aquaculture area: +1.50 m to +2.80 m, riverbank area: +1.30 m to +1.50 m. Field
area: +0.01 m to +0.60 m. During construction, the ground elevation will be raised, and the
surface conditions will be modified, leading to faster water concentration during rainfall events.
This change is expected to enhance both drainage capacity and water storage functions of the
irrigation canal system in the project area.
Subproject 8: The project site is located within the water surface area of the irrigation canal
system in Da Loc commune. Terrain elevation varies from the canal banks to the project
boundary, ranging between +0.01m and +0.56m. Previously, the surface layer consisted mainly
of alluvial deposits and sediment accumulation, which provided limited natural water retention
capacity. As a result, rainfall runoff tended to flow rapidly into the river, reducing the area’s
ability to regulate water naturally and affecting local hydrology. Upon completion of
construction, however, the improved ground conditions will create favorable conditions for
water storage and regulation, ensuring adequate irrigation and drainage for the aquaculture area
of Da Loc commune and adjacent area along the main irrigation canals, including Ong Hong
sluice, Quay Co sluice, and Ong Ninh sluice.
Subproject 9: The irrigation canal system is located in Quảng Trung commune. Terrain
elevation from the canal banks and bed until the project boundary ranges from –1.39 m to +5.82
m. Previously, the surface consisted of alluvial deposits and sediment accumulation, which

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               174
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
reduced the area’s natural regulation capacity and caused rainwater to flow rapidly into the
rivers, affecting the hydrology of the Yen River and Hoang River. After completion, the project
will facilitate water storage and regulation for the aquaculture area and incorporate an operation
and management road along the canal from Ong Thuoc Sluice (right dike of the Hoang River)
to Ong Vanh Sluice (left dike of the Yen River).
However, during the construction period of Subproject 3 to Subproject 9, the stockpiling of
construction waste and dredged materials may affect the project sites, potentially causing
erosion, sedimentation, and local flooding along canal banks, canal beds, and sluice areas. The
level of impact is assessed as MINOR, localized, and intermittent, occurring mainly during
heavy rainfall events within the overall 12–24 month construction period. These impacts can
be effectively mitigated through the application of appropriate construction methods,
management practices, and technical measures.
 4.4.1.7. Impacts due to increased risks and traffic disturbance
    ❖ For road traffics
In the project area, the existing transportation system consists of National Highway 10,
Provincial Road 10, and inter-village/commune roads, with widths ranging from 4 to 20 m.
These roads are surfaced with concrete or asphalt and currently experience low traffic density.
Construction activities, however, are expected to significantly increase the number of vehicles
using these routes, including: Trucks transporting construction materials, dredged materials
and excess excavated soil, and machinery and equipment moving in and out of the subproject
areas. The transportation of heavy vehicle (e.g., excavators, cranes, hoists, rollers) may disrupt
existing traffic flows, particularly on narrower inter-village and commune roads, increase traffic
safety risks for road users, especially during peak hours and in areas near People’s Committee
offices and schools, where there is a high concentration of commuters, civil servants, and
students. Affected groups are identified in Table 4-3 above:

        5.5m and total width of both carriageway and roadsides is 8 meters. Along the southern
        side of the road is an existing residential area, while the northern side is the water surface
        along the Lach Truong shoreline. (ii) The inland waterway canal extends from the
        western part of the Lach Truong estuary to the sea, R = 40 m. The protection corridor
        on each side is 15 m, giving a total water surface width of 70 m. The edge of the inland
        waterway protection corridor is located approximately 15–55m to south of the existing
        embankment line.

Impacts on road traffic: (i) The construction process will significantly affect the mobility of
local residents and surrounding areas. During peak hours, when construction activities are
ongoing, traffic congestion will be unavoidable. This requires the project owner to coordinate
with contractors for proper management and supervision, and to take measures for traffic
guidance and diversion along the affected routes. (ii) The risk of traffic disruption and accidents
is particularly high in areas near commune People’s Committee offices and primary schools,
due to the presence of residents commuting to administrative offices and students traveling to
school. These risks are especially relevant during the construction phase, when road
connections and underpasses for local access have not yet been completed. (iii) In addition, the
quality of the main dyke roads used for transportation—such as Phu Son 2 Canal (subproject),
dyke roads 1 and 2, Son Trang 2 drainage canal (subproject 4), operation and management roads
and Hoi Trieu Canal (subproject 5), operation and management roads along the main supply
canal (subproject 6), Khe River Canal, branch canals 1, 2, 3 (subproject 7), and the main supply
canal from Ong Thuoc Sluice to Ong Vanh Sluice (subproject 9)—as well as existing irrigation
sluices along the dykes, may also be affected by the operation of heavy trucks and construction
machinery. These impacts may include the formation of potholes, uneven surfaces, and
waterlogged depressions during rainfall, thereby increasing traffic safety risks for both people
and vehicles.
Impacts on waterway (i) During the dredging process, the equipment used includes 1,200 CV
suction dredgers, barges of 200T, 250T, and 400T capacity, and cable-bucket excavators with
crane systems mounted on barges. These vessels are constantly anchored or moving near the
dredging area, which may obstruct the navigation of passing boats and ships. (ii) In addition,
the transportation of dredged materials on the river will increase traffic density along the

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                176
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
navigation channel, leading to obstruction and congestion of inland waterway traffic within the
navigation channel during dredging at subproject 1 – Hoang Truong Fishing Port and
Subproject 2 – Hoa Loc Fishing Port; Collisions and accidents as vessels enter and exit the
channel; Narrowing of the navigable channel available for vessel movement; Oil spills or
leakage incidents caused by vessel collisions, leading to water pollution and damage to ship
equipment; Delays in dredging progress and disruption of cargo transportation schedules along
the channel; Impacts on the ecosystem (ECS) in the port area; Negative effects on the economic
activities of vessels entering and leaving the port, as well as fishing boats operating outside the
transportation route; Increased vessel density, which raises the risk of marine accidents and
potential environmental incidents.
The level of impact is assessed as moderate due to the following reasons: (i) The number of
trucks involved in transportation is relatively low (2–36 trips per day), and construction
machinery is only mobilized during specific periods when required and will cease operation
once the works are completed. (ii) The impact duration is not continuous, but rather short-term,
within approximately 12–24 months. (iii) The number of affected stakeholders is not large,
since the density of existing traffic on the main transport routes is low. Moreover, construction-
related traffic can be scheduled to avoid peak hours when residents commute to work and
students go to school. (iv) These impacts can be effectively mitigated by taking appropriate
management and technical measures.
    ❖ For waterway traffic of Subproject 1, 2:
Impact: Waterway traffic may also be affected due to the increased number of construction
machines and equipment that must be transported by water to the construction sites. In
particular, during the dredging process, a variety of vessels and equipment will be mobilized,
including: Sand-suction dredger 126 CV, cranes 5–50 tons, large suction dredger 1,200 CV,
barges 200T – 400T capacity, tugboats 150 CV, boats of 40T capacity, canoes, pile-driving
vessels, etc.
Impacts and obstacles to inland waterway traffic: (i) Obstruction and congestion of inland
waterway traffic within the navigation channel during dredging activities at subproject area –
Hoang Truong Fishing Port and subproject 2 – Hoa Loc Fishing Port. (ii) Collisions and
accidents caused by vessels entering and exiting the navigation channel. (iii) Narrowing of the
navigation channel, reducing the available space for vessel movement. (iv) Oil spill or leakage
incidents resulting from vessel collisions, leading to water pollution and damage to ship
equipment. (v) Negative impacts on dredging progress and delays in the transportation of goods
entering and leaving the channel. (vi) Impacts on the ecosystem (ECS) in the port area. (vii)
Impacts on the overall economy of vessels entering and leaving the port, as well as fishing boats
operating outside the transport route:
Waterway traffic – Subproject 1
The inland waterway channel at the Lach Truong estuary has a width of 40m, with a 15-meter
protection corridor on each side, giving a total water surface width of 70m. The channel passes
through the project area, located approximately 15–55 m to the south of existing embankment.
During the 12-month construction phase, the transportation of materials and dredging activities
using vessels and barges may increase traffic density, causing temporary obstructions to inland
waterway traffic and posing a risk of collisions, particularly in the Linh Truong wharf area.
However, the impact is assessed as low and localized, since the cross-section of the wharf is
130 m wide, and the dredging area occupies only about one-third to one-fifth of the total water
surface, leaving sufficient space for vessels to navigate.
At present, Hoang Truong Port accommodates approximately 300 fishing vessels, with an
annual catch of 2,000–3,000 tons and a daily traffic volume of around 250 vessels. Of these,
about 225 trips per day are of 450 nearshore fishing vessels, and 32 trips/2-3 days of 32

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              177
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
medium-shore vessels. Since vessel arrivals and departures occur within specific timeframes
and the port has multiple berthing positions, construction activities can be scheduled and
regulated to minimize conflicts with fishing operations. In addition, attention must be given to
the control of floating waste (e.g., plastic bags, styrofoam boxes, damaged nets) to prevent
impacts on port equipment and vessels. The project owner should ensure appropriate
construction planning and coordinate traffic management to reduce risks throughout the project
implementation period.
Impacts on waterway traffic is assessed as low to medium and localized because: (i) The
port cross-section is wide (~130 m), and the dredging area occupies only one-third to one-fifth
of the water surface, thus ensuring sufficient passage for local vessels. (ii) Dredging equipment
operates only in place for a few hours at a time. However, during dredging operations, attention
must be paid to floating waste (plastic bags, styrofoam, damaged nets, etc.), which may affect
both equipment and vessels. Therefore, the project owner should implement appropriate
construction methods and traffic regulation measures during material transportation and
dredging activities to minimize impacts on inland waterway traffic.
Waterway traffic – Subproject 2
The inland waterway channel located in the west of the Lach Truong estuary to the sea has a
width of 40 m, with a 15 protection corridor on each side, giving a total water surface width of
70 m. During dredging, types of vessels will be in operation, including suction dredgers, barges,
tugboats, small boats, and supply vessels for fuel and water. At present, the Hoa Loc Port area
accommodates frequent vessel traffic, with more than 100 offshore fishing vessels operating
twice per month. On average, there are approximately 492 vessel departures and 364 arrivals
annually, with a total yield of about 1,500 tons. The dredging and port construction works are
expected to last about 12 months, which may cause temporary obstructions to inland waterway
traffic due to channel narrowing and an increased risk of collisions between dredging
equipment, supply vessels, and fishing boats. In addition, the anchoring of barges and the
transportation of dredged materials along the channel may disrupt local waterway traffic,
particularly in the storm shelter area, affecting the berthing of approximately 700 fishing vessels
during the storm season. Collision risks are higher during peak hours of vessel arrivals and
departures, especially along the route from the construction site to the disposal area in Hai Loc
commune (now Van Loc Commune), and may also increase accident risks for fishermen and
ferry passengers at Lach Truong and Hoa Loc ferries (now Hoa Loc Commune). However,
these impacts are assessed as minor and controllable, provided that appropriate traffic
coordination and management measures are implemented throughout the construction period.
Thus, impacts on inland waterway traffic are assessed as minor to Medium and localized at
each dredging and construction site because: (i) The cross-section of the project area is
relatively wide. (ii) Dredging equipment is usually stationed at one location and operates for
only a few hours at a time. (iii) The density of vessel traffic is low to moderate, depending on
the season/day (approximately 100–250 vessel movements per day). (iv) With the application
of proper waterway and road traffic safety management during the construction phase, potential
impacts including traffic accidents can be avoided, minimized, and mitigated. (v) Appropriate
scheduling and allocation of time and berthing positions for vessel owners during fishing period
will be arranged when dredging and construction are carried out at subproject 2 area.
 4.4.1.8. Risk of Damage to Existing Infrastructure due to Transportation of Construction
 Materials and Machinery
Most of the road networks surrounding and within the project site are constructed with cement
concrete or asphalt concrete pavement. The transportation of construction materials to the site
by trucks exceeding the allowable load capacity on these routes may cause damage to road
surfaces. Technically, repairing concrete roads can be difficult; therefore, such impacts should

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              178
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
be avoided by applying solutions such as: using alternative transportation routes (e.g.,
waterways), or restricting access only to trucks with appropriate load capacity. In addition, the
operation of cranes and heavy lifting equipment may affect existing technical infrastructure
such as power lines, telephone lines, water supply, and drainage systems (as mentioned in
Chapter 2), particularly along the routes for transportation of construction materials and waste.
Impacts on infrastructures
Infrastructure such as roads, power systems, canals, bridges, and embankments may be affected
during the construction of project works and components. This will have direct impacts on
residents and organizations currently residing in the area, as well as on the mobility and daily
activities of local people and related entities within the project implementation zone. These
impacts may cause inconvenience and disruption, both directly and indirectly, to production
activities, particularly in fishponds and aquaculture areas, and will increase pressure on
surrounding infrastructure due to the higher frequency and volume of transportation along
adjacent routes.

 4.4.1.9. Occupational health and safety (OHS) issues
For the construction of subprojects, around 130-200 workers of each subproject are expected to
participate in construction activities over an 12–24-month period. As such, there are potential
occupational health and safety risks as follows:
Workers may work in environments with increased dust and toxic gas pollution due to
demolition, excavation, and machinery activities, which can lead to dizziness or fainting while
working. Workers can have accidents due to exposure to electrical tools/equipment, dangerous
objects such as sharp iron, nails, screws, and the simultaneous operation of multiple machines
in a narrow construction site, especially when workers are not paying attention, not using full
personal protective equipment, and lacking awareness of safety regulations. Construction
activities can cause other occupational accidents for workers such as fires and explosions due
to welding-gas leaks, fuel leaks, welding activities, electric shocks, working at heights, falling
into the sea, construction collapses, and the operation of bulky machinery (barges, cranes,
rollers, steel cutters, welders, wheel loaders, trucks, etc.) and materials, as well as working in
extreme weather conditions such as large waves, strong winds, and poor construction site
organization.
Regarding working conditions, workers’ OHS problems can be caused by falling into water or
even drowning while working on water/sea due to illness or loss of balance. In addition, they
can also come into contact with materials containing toxic substances such as oils, etc. If direct
contact is made, short-term health effects can be skin or respiratory system irritation while some
substances have can penetrate through the skin and enter the body system. The accumulation
of toxic substances during work will lead to more serious long-term health effects, so it is
necessary to be concerned.
Other OHS issues related to working conditions would be inadequate lighting or inadequate
protection when working in specific conditions or locations.
Working with construction electric or diesel tools and equipment (e.g. 200T – 400T barges,
1200CV suction dredger, 150CV-360CV tugboat, cranes, hoists.), workers can also be
electrocuted or burned. Extreme weather conditions also cause OHS risks and issues. Strong
winds and very heavy rain during storms in coastal areas can cause unfinished/temporary
construction works, even construction factories to become unstable, and even cause some tools
and equipment to move. Outdoor construction equipment and materials lead to accidents for


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             181
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
people around, including workers. Working long hours on very hot days can also cause
exhaustion and even fainting for workers.
Regarding worker behavior, safety risks can come from workers’ awareness and attitude
towards the use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and personal behaviors at the
construction site. Regarding PPE, it is common for workers at larger and more experienced
contractors to be well-trained in EHS and provided with adequate PPE, so they are often subject
to more stringent safety rules on site. However, workers of smaller/local contractors using PPE
(e.g. used as life jackets in rivers/sea) on construction sites depends on the level of enforcement.
Decline in workers’ health
For dredging workers as well as other construction workers, inadequate living conditions in
workers’ housing also lead to health and safety problems. Illnesses related to water-borne
diseases can be caused by not having enough clean water for use, not having access to adequate
sanitation facilities, and poor sanitary conditions in and around the camp. Living at a
construction site, away from home for a long time, if workers do not follow a healthy lifestyle
and get into fights, drinking, or prostitution, they can also suffer long-term health effects from
these activities.
Risk of fire and explosion:
Fire and explosion incidents, as well as electrical short circuits, may occur during the
transportation and storage of fuel or due to the lack of safety in the temporary power supply
system, causing damage to people and property during subproject implementation. The specific
causes can be identified as follows: (i) Temporary storage facilities for raw materials serving
machinery and technical equipment during construction (paint, gasoline, etc.) are sources of
fire and explosion. When incidents occur, they can cause damage to people, property, and the
environment; (ii) Electrical incidents such as shocks, explosions, short circuits, etc., due to the
temporary power supply system for construction machinery and equipment; (iii) The use of
heating devices (welding, heating, etc.) can cause fires, explosions, or workplace accidents if
preventive control measures are not in place; (iv) Especially in the case of thunderstorms,
lightning strikes and workers’ carelessness in using electrical equipment are the main causes of
short circuits and explosions; (v) Careless behavior of workers can lead to accidents or even
danger in construction areas, including smoking near flammable materials such as fuel tanks,
careless handling of electrical wires, unauthorized operation of construction machinery.
Electrical short circuits and explosions can damage construction machinery and equipment, and
cause fires in camps and material gathering areas during the construction phase. Since such
incidents can occur at any time, the project owner actively takes effective preventive and control
measures to minimize and limit these negative impacts as much as possible.
In general, the risk of occupational accidents and traffic accidents at the construction site is
assessed as MODERATE, due to the following factors: (i) Such risks are inherently present and
may occur at any time during construction activities. (ii) The number of workers is moderate
(approximately 130–200 people). (iii) These risks may occur during the 12–24 month
construction period. (iv) The risks can be mitigated through appropriate measures, such as:
Conducting occupational safety training before and during construction, providing workers
with adequate personal protective equipment (PPE), strengthening supervision and monitoring
by the contractor’s environmental staff, the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC), and
the project owner.
 4.4.1.10. Issues related to community safety and health
Local residents living around the construction site, along transport routes, and near disposal
areas may be exposed to hazardous waste, unpleasant odors from dredging activities, temporary
sludge storage, and pathogens from poor sanitation. Furthermore, increased dust concentration,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               182
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
noise, and traffic density due to machinery operation and transportation, soil subsidence,
construction collapse, and unprotected deep holes on the roads could pose risks to public health
and safety.
Risk of spreading infectious diseases: Local people and workers are at risk of transmitting
infectious diseases such as sexually transmitted diseases (STDs) and HIV/AIDS. New workers
may be more susceptible to infections from diseases in the local community. The impact can be
minimized through training to equip workers with essential knowledge. The anticipated level
of risk is assessed as LOW, under the condition that national regulations7 and control measures
are strictly applied, in line with WHO guidelines, combined with the country’s established
experience in preventing communicable diseases and the lessons learned from the
implementation of World Bank–funded projects. However, during the environmental and social
assessment, surveys and social assessments should be conducted to identify and assess local
infectious disease risks. Prevention solutions will be included in the Subproject Labour
Management Plan (LMP). Such impacts and risks are assessed as MINOR and can be managed
through the application of the Code of Conduct and other measures outlined in the Labor
Management Protocol. In addition, the boundaries of each construction site/component/contract
will be clearly defined and agreed upon among contractors under the coordination of the
Construction Supervision Consultants (CSC) and the Project Management Unit (PMU) in each
province/city.
The risk to community health and safety is assessed as LOW because: (i) Construction activities
are carried out in separate, designated construction sites, not extending along existing traffic
routes or interspersed within residential areas; concentrated residential zones are located at a
distance of about 50 m and have relatively low density; (ii) The number of workers is not large
(approximately 130–200 people per subproject), concentrated within a single construction site,
which may have some influence on community safety; (iii) The construction period for
subproject components is relatively long (about 12–24 months); (iv) The project
owner/contractor gives priority to hiring local labor; (v) Workers receive training on
occupational safety and environmental hygiene before commencing construction activities; (vi)
The risks can be mitigated through appropriate construction methods and rational site layout
planning.
 4.4.1.11. Social impacts due to labor influx
✓ Labor influx
It is estimated that about 130-200 workers will be mobilized during the peak period of
subproject implementation. This number of workers may come from various locations of
project provinces/cities or other provinces.
Some potential social risks and impacts related to labour influx of this project are predicted as
follows: (i) Inadequate management of workers and labor camps may lead to competition for
jobs as well as issues concerning working conditions and social welfare, such as labor standards
and access to basic health care services. Conflicts between local residents and workers may also
arise due to differences in background, language, occupation, and behavior (particularly
between migrant workers and local communities) as well as disparities in income levels and
personal interests. (ii) The movement of workers can pose a risk to local security or social
stability if some workers engage in social evils (such as drinking, gambling, prostitution, etc.).
(iii) The mobilization of migrant workers can increase the risk of transmitting sexually
transmitted diseases, including HIV/AIDS, when workers from different localities interact with
each other and with local communities. In the case of an epidemic, this would place an

7Law No. 64/2006/QH11 dated June 29, 2006 on the prevention and control of the virus that causes human immunodeficiency

syndrome (HIV/AIDS). Decision No. 1246/QD-TTg dated August 14, 2020 by the Government approving the National
Strategy to End AIDS by 2030.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                                  183
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
additional burden on the local health system. (iv) Potential conflicts may arise between workers
and local communities due to differences in background, culture, customs, employment,
income, or minor disputes. (vi) Gender-based violence (GBV), exploitation, and sexual
abuse/sexual harassment (SEA/SH) may be associated with the concentration of male workers
in the project area. However, the risk of GBV and SEA/SH is considered low and controllable.
There are potential risks of child labor, forced labor, and sexual exploitation and abuse. In
addition, both the local community and female workers may face risks related to gender-based
violence (GBV) and child abuse, as well as sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA), particularly
associated with the influx of workers.
There are potential risks of illegal use of child labor at construction sites. Gender-based violence
(GBV) and sexual harassment may also occur due to the presence of migrant workers, leading to
negative impacts on the mental health and personal freedom of female workers. However, with
the enforcement of national regulations and the experience gained from the implementation of
World Bank–funded projects, the risks related to child labor, forced labor, violence against
children (VAC), and GBV are anticipated to be low.
✓ Community Disturbance
Construction activities may disrupt commercial operations and daily activities of households
and/or businesses located near the construction site. In addition, the proximity of fishing vessels
operating in the area, as well as livelihood activities that rely on the use of nearby water
resources (such as shrimp, crab, and fish farming), may also be affected.
During construction phase of the fishing port/storm shelter area, local communities would be
disturbed by construction activities and environmental impacts resulted from these activities.
The disturbance could be:
    •   Not being able to access by motorbikes to the road side houses, buildings, facilities
        including markets, schools, temples and grave yards, etc.... The transportation of fishing
        gear and harvested products from households to the sea may become more difficult
        when the new road intersects the access routes leading to the beach, fishing port, or
        anchorage area;
    •   Nuisance or even illness from waste and waste water from camps and construction sites.
    •   Hearing, recreation, watching TV, studying or sleeping may be disturbed by the noise
        from dredging, excavation, especially at lunch time or night time. It is necessary to have
        agreement and prior notification with the local residents or community, along with the
        establishment of specific sanctions for any violations.
    •   House, yard, garden land may flooded due to blockage of ditches after construction
        wastes fill it up
    •   Exposure to increased safety risks from construction activities, fuels, materials and
        wastes, and movements of trucks and other heavy construction plants on roadway and
        waterway.
    •   Not being able to access to water fronts, cannot take river water for production due to
        turbidity.
Exposure to environmental pollution or increased safety risks or being disturbed by construction
activities may also be the causes of conflicts between local communities and the workers. Such
disturbance and potential conflicts can be minimized through the implementation of RP,
informing local communities about construction schedule and the implementation of mitigation
measures during construction phase including ESCOP.
✓ Impacts on Women
The concentration of 130-200 workers in construction sites may cause social disturbance,
affecting women who directly work on the construction sites or those are living surrounding
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               184
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
the project area. Unmarried women may be disadvantaged in getting married or they may
become single mom. Meanwhile, married women can be affected the happiness of their
families. In addition, they may also suffer SEA/SH. However, all these potential risks and
impacts are MINOR and controllable by mitigation measures.
✓ Use of Child Labor and Forced Labor
To reduce construction costs, child labors may be used for some jobs that do not require high
qualifications (according to Vietnam’s regulations, child is under 15 years old). Due to limited
knowledge, children can suffer from labor abuse that affects their psychology, health and ability
to learn. According to the socio-economic survey results, no case of child labor or forced labor
was recorded in previous projects in the locality. Child labor and forced labor is prohibited in
this project and will be included in bidding document. However, some children may come to
project sites to sell food, cigarette or soft-drink for workers. Construction supervision consultant
will closely monitor to avoid SEA/SH risks to these children, especially female children.
✓ Labor and Working Conditions
The Project’s workforce will include: (i) direct workers, (ii) contracted workers (recruited by
third parties such as contractors or as consultants), and (iii) primary supply workers. The project
is not likely to engage community workers.
    -   Direct workers: are those who are directly employed by the PMU (PMU’s staff) or
        individual consultants that PMU hired to support project implementation. The PMU is
        the project owner, responsible for the assessment and management of E&S risk, detail
        design, procurement, disbursement, contracts management and internal monitoring.
    -   Official/contracted employees: The workforce consists of employees hired by
        consulting firms and contractors during the design, construction, and supervision
        phases. The demand for labor depends on the scale and timing of each activity.
    -   Consultants: Cover areas such as technical design, construction supervision,
        environmental and social management, resettlement, and technical assistance. Each
        consulting firm typically employs 5–10 staff, while construction supervision companies
        usually have around 10–30 staff.
    -   Construction contractor: It is estimated that approximately 130–200 workers will be
        required for the construction of the subproject. In addition, a technical team consisting
        of project managers, engineers, environmental officers, health and safety staff, and
        accountants will include about 10–20 personnel. Unskilled local labor will be recruited
        directly by the contractors and subcontractors.
    -   Primary supply workers: Are employees of key material suppliers, including those
        providing aggregates, bitumen, steel, and precast concrete. The number and type of
        suppliers will be determined during the implementation phase. Their work will be
        concentrated primarily during the construction stage.
Other stakeholders: This group includes officials and civil servants from communes and
provincial-level agencies such as the Department of Agriculture and Rural Development,
Finance, and Home Affairs. They are subject to the provisions of the current civil service labor
laws and must comply with Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) requirements when
participating in project-related activities, particularly when working at construction sites.
Key labour risksIn addition to the risks to the health and safety of the workers caused by working
conditions (e.g. expose to extreme weather conditions, not enough lighting, electrical shocks,
working in confined space, working underground or at height, lack of protection or non-
compliance to safety rules etc.) discussed in the previous assessment of each work item, there
are also other social issues related to the labor influx: (i) Possibility of child labor or forced

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               185
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
labor, with reference to the sector or locality; (ii) Gender Based Violence risks/Sexual
exploitation and abuse/Sexual Harassment risks related to the concentration of relative large
number of male workers to the project area. (iii) Infection during outbreaks of communicable
diseases.
✓ Impact assessment related to the labor force of the main suppliers
The main supplier: The project will purchase sand, stone, and other construction materials from
suppliers within the province or neighboring provinces. Since most of the work is mechanized,
the number of workers from the main suppliers is expected to be only about 5–10 people.
Similar to the contractor’s workforce, they will also generate domestic wastewater and solid
waste. The main risks include: (i) Traffic accidents arising from the transportation of materials
by road or waterway; (ii) Potential conflicts with contractor workers or the local community;
(iii) Occupational accidents at the construction site; (iv) Health and safety risks for seasonal
workers.
However, this indirect impacts and risks are forecasted “Low” level and can be mitigated due
to: (i) The number of employees is not much; (ii) The employment time is not long; (iii) The
main suppliers’ workforce is regularly trained and instructed on traffic safety; (iv) They are
usually local people; (v) Due diligence review on labor and working conditions of primary
suppliers has been completed and monitoring requirements will also cover primary supply
workers during the construction phase.
4.4.2. Site-specific impacts
Subproject 1: Upgrade of Hoang Hoa fishing port
With the location, investment scale and environmental-social characteristics in the location of
the subproject 1 as mentioned in the Chapter 1 and 2, site-specific impacts of the subproject
1’s construction activities include:
          -   Disruption to the operations of the existing Hoang Truong fishing port;
          -   Degradation of water quality in the Lach Truong River due to dredging activities;
          -   Impacts on the operations of the ship repair facility within the existing port;
          -   Impacts associated with the disposal site serving the subproject 1;
          -   Affected sensitive receptors – subproject 1.
Details about impact assessment are presented below:
(i) Disruption to the operations of the existing Hoang Truong fishing port
Hoang Truong Fishing Port is classified as a Class III fishing port, capable of accommodating
50 vessel trip/450 CV, an annual throughput of about 5,500 tons of aquatic products, with
around 200–300 fishing vessels entering and anchoring at the port.
During construction, approximately 520m of the existing wharf downstream of the Lach Truong
River will be retained for the operation of the existing fishing port. The project will invest in
the construction of a new 330m embankment upstream of the Lach Truong River.
Consequently, construction activities may cause adverse environmental impacts such as dust,
emissions, wastewater, solid waste, and noise, and may partially disrupt the operations of the
existing fishing port area, but will not interrupt the overall functioning of the port.
It is anticipated that the demolition, construction of the embankment and port quay will require
the use of various types of underwater construction equipment, such as canoes, 30T floating
cranes, barges, and pile-driving vessels. In addition, 46 pieces of construction machinery (as
presented in Chapter 1, Section 1.4) will be mobilized. During the construction process,


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               186
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
additional vessels such as fuel supply boats and water supply boats will also be deployed to
support these operations. These activities may give rise to the following specific impacts:
     -   The increase in the number of construction vessels moving on the water such as canoes,
         30T floating cranes, barges, and pile-driving vessels required for the subproject
         implementation will disrupt the entry and exit of fishing boats at the existing port.
         Approximately 450 offshore fishing households, 32 midshore fishing households, and
         103 nearshore fishing households may be also affected during the construction period.
     -   The risk of waterway traffic accidents also increases due to potential collisions
         between fishing vessels entering or leaving the port and the construction machinery
         deployed. At present, waterway traffic on the Lach Truong River in the Hoang Truong
         port area is of moderate density (approximately 20 vessel calls per port entry, with
         about 200–300 vessels regularly anchored).
     -   In-water construction activities (such as floating cranes, barges, pile-driving vessels,
         etc.) for the construction of the embankment and mooring quay may narrow or obstruct
         the existing berthing area at the embankmen section, potentially causing difficulties
         for fishing vessels entering and leaving the port. However, for the newly expanded
         section of the port, such impacts are not existed.
This impact is assessed as Moderate, localized, and intermittent during the 24‑month
construction period because: (i) At the subproject 1 area, the Lach Truong estuary has a width
of approximately 1–1.5 km (at the project section, about 1.5 km from the river mouth). The
remaining width of the Lach Truong River is still sufficient for local vessels to navigate; (ii)
Construction machinery on the water will be anchored at fixed sections, thereby limiting
interference with the entry and exit of fishing vessels at the port; (iii) The Hoang Truong
anchorage area, located adjacent to the existing fishing port, will be arranged as a temporary
berthing area during the demolition of the existing quay; and (iv) These impacts can be
mitigated by applying waterway traffic safety management and successive construction
method.




                     Seafood planning area at the port – Hoang Truong commune
(ii) Degradation of water quality in the Lach Truong River due to dredging activities:
Dredging of the riverbed and banks to create navigation channels, turning basins, and port
berths may cause significant adverse impacts on the quality of river and coastal waters. Water
quality may deteriorate due to increased turbidity (FTU) and Total Suspended Solids (TSS) as
the water column is disturbed during dredging. The loss of water clarity in coastal areas, caused
by the presence of suspended particles and colored impurities, may result in water discoloration
and pollution.
The dredging process is the main cause of increased turbidity in the water at the dredging site,
as sediment particles are disturbed, dispersed, and suspended in the water. Coastal sediments
of sand and silt are heterogeneous, both alongshore and cross-shore. As a result, the turbidity


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            187
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
of coastal waters in the project area may increase due to dredging activities, arising from the
following causes:
    -    Bottom sediments are disturbed and dispersed from seabed into water surface.
    -    Sediment spillage during the movement of dredging buckets from the river/sea bottom
         to the water surface.
    -    A small portion of dredged material may fall from the bucket during its transfer onto
         barges.
    -    Leachate from wet dredged material may flow back into the river/sea within the
         subproject area.
According to the Handbook on Dredging Assessment in Rivers/Seas 8 , when using trailing
suction hopper dredgers, grab dredgers, bucket dredgers, or barges, the dredging of 1 m³ of sand
may result in a loss of approximately 0.3–0.4 kg of silt and sand. This loss amount is dispersed
along the dredging area of the subprojects, thereby increasing water turbidity before eventually
settling back onto the riverbed or seabed.
Dispersion of dredged materials: The extent to which disturbed sediments from the seabed may
disperse (and thus affect turbidity) depends largely on the geological composition of the seabed
and the dredging method applied. For example, dredging sand layers generally results in lower
turbidity compared to dredging silt or sandy–silt layers. Similarly, the use of a grab dredger
tends to generate higher turbidity than a suction dredger.
Reference values of turbidity and Total Suspended Solids (TSS) measured from the Ky Ha Port
Dredging Project in Quảng Nam Province (a coastal port area in Central Vietnam) are as
follows.
Table 4-28. Measurement results of turbidity and suspended solid in the dredging area
                    Position                              Turbidity (FTU)        TSS (mg/l)
5m from the dredging point                                      115                 105
15m from the dredging point                                      33                 29
20m from the dredging point                                      15                  11
QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT (table 2, collum B)                         -                 100
TCVN 13656:2023: aquaculture water                                -                 100
Notes:
"-" Not applicable; QCVN 08-MT:2023/BTNMT (table 2, column B) – National technical
regulation on surface water quality - The water quality is assessed as moderate. The aquatic
ecosystem consumes a significant amount of dissolved oxygen due to the presence of a large
volume of pollutants. After the application of appropriate treatment measures, the water can
be used for industrial and agricultural production purposes; TCVN 13656:2023 – National
Regulation on water for aquaculture– water quality for intensive culture of black tiger shrimp,
white leg shrimp.
          Table 4-29. Turbidity measurement results by depth at the dredging area
                     Position                                  Turbidity (FTU)
                                               Surface point    Middle point     Bottom point
5m from the dredging point                          125             365              1,120
15m from the dredging point                         41              105               365
30m from the dredging point                         17               26               44


8 Japan Ministry of Transport 1982.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             188
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
(Source: Water quality monitoring during construction phase – Dredging of Ky Ha Port, Quang
Nam province)
The data indicate that turbidity and TSS contents decrease with increasing horizontal distance from
the suction dredger but increase with river depth. Compared with the standards for brackish-water
shrimp farming, within a 5m radius, TSS concentrations exceeded the National Technical
Regulation on Surface Water Quality by approximately 1.05 times and 2.1 times. At a distance of
15 meters or more, the parameters complied with the permissible limits. However, due to the
influence of currents, sediment transportation, and wind, turbidity and TSS are expected to spread
beyond the levels indicated above.
The dispersion of sediments in river and marine waters is the main cause of increased TSS. Sand
particles generally settle quickly to the riverbed or seabed under the influence of gravity. Therefore,
while sand and silt may temporarily increase turbidity, they do not significantly contribute to long-
term increases in TSS. Elevated TSS levels directly affect the ecosystem in the dredging area by
reducing light penetration, thereby limiting photosynthesis of aquatic plants (algae, seaweed),
restricting growth and reproduction, and altering habitats. However, ecosystems in the port
construction areas are typically of low biodiversity because vessel traffics frequently moving
through. As such, the overall impact is assessed as Moderate and can be mitigated through
appropriate construction methods.
(iii) Impacts on ship repair facilities
In the existing Hoang Truong fishing port area, there is one ship repair workshop located
approximately 20m from the construction site. This facility has an area of about 1,000 m² and
employs around 10 workers, operating from 7:00 AM to 9:00 PM when vessels are docked for
service. Construction and port upgrading activities may partially affect its operation.
During the construction phase, the existing fishing port will remain operational. However, the
upgrading and expansion of Hoang Truong port may disrupt the workshop’s production and
service activities due to factors such as dust, emissions, and obstruction of waterway access for
vessels entering and exiting the repair facility due to the gathering of equipment, machinery,
and construction materials in the port area.
This impact is assessed as low because: (i) the project is constructed based on successive
methods, so the workshop’s operations are only disrupted locally. (ii) The waterway within the
port is sufficiently wide to accommodate both construction activities and vessel repair
operations. (iii) The impact duration is short, estimated at 2–3 months for the nearest
construction area. This impact can be mitigated through appropriate construction and
management measures.
(iv) Impacts of the disposal site serving the subproject 1
Construction waste (excavated soil, debris, dredged materials, etc.) from Hoang Truong and
Lach Truong fishing ports (subproject 1 and 2) will be transported to disposal site No. 2 in Hai
Loc Commune, Hau Loc district (currently Van Loc commune). The site covers approximately
8.15 ha and has a capacity of 383,800 m³, sufficient to accommodate waste from the subproject
1 and 2. Potential impacts on local residents and the surrounding Lach Truong River and canal
areas are assessed as low because: (i) The site borders the areas with low and infrequent traffic,
adjacent to ponds and roads, and does not affect local salt-making livelihoods, as the land has
been unused for agriculture for over a decade. (ii) The site is elevated above surrounding ground
levels, reducing the risk of overflow. (iii) It is encircled by drainage ditches, facilitating the
management of the runoff of sedimentation to the outside. (iv) The disposal timeline is short
and intermittent.(v) Impacts on nearby communities and the environment can be effectively
mitigated through technical and management method.
(v) Affected sensitive receptors in Hoang Truong fishing port
