Subproject 2: Upgrading of Lach Truong fishing port (Existing Hoa Loc port)
Based on the location, investment scale, and environmental and social characteristics outlined
in Chapters 1 and 2, the subproject 2’s construction activities are expected to cause the
following site-specific impacts:
             -   Disruption to operations at the existing Lach Truong fishing port;
             -   Impacts on nearby seafood processing facilities;
             -   Disturbance to the center market at Lach Truong fishing port
             -   Impacts on aquaculture activities along the Lach Truong River
             -   Impacts from waste disposal at the regulated disposal site of the subproject 2
             -   Sensitive receptors.
Details about the impacts are presented below:
(i) Disturbance to the activities of Lach Truong fishing port (current Hoa Loc fishing port)
Lach Truong - Hoa Loc) fishing port is a Class II port, covering approximately 3.5 ha. The
storm shelter area in the De Canal has an area of 19.5 ha and is designed to accommodate around
300 vessels with capacity of 135 - 450 CV, including over 100 small-scale nearshore fishing
households using boats of 24–40 CV.
During construction phase, 270 m of the existing wharf in the upstream of Lach Truong river
will remain operational to support daily vessel traffics. The project will build a new 60m
embankment downstream. Thus, the construction activities may generate negative impacts on
the environment due to dust, emissions, wastewater, solid waste, and noise, causing minor
disruption to port operations but not halting them.
The demolition and construction of the embankment and docking area are expected to involve
various types of underwater construction equipment, including canoes, 30-ton floating cranes,
barges, and pile-driving vessels. In total, approximately 61 construction machines will be
mobilized for the implementation of the subproject’s infrastructure works (as outlined in
Chapter 1, Section 1.4). Additionally, support vessels such as fuel and water supply boats will
be deployed to service the construction fleet. These activities will have the site-specific impacts
as follows
      -     The increased number of construction vessels such as canoes, 30-ton floating cranes,
            barges, and pile-driving boats will need to navigate via waterways to the construction
            site. This will be expected to disrupt the regular docking activities of existing fishing
            vessels at the port. Moreover, the risk of maritime traffic accidents will rise due to
            potential collisions between fishing boats and construction equipment operating in the
            area. Currently, waterway traffic on the Lach Truong River near the port is of moderate
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                   190
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
         density, with approximately 50 vessel movements per day and around 300 vessels
         regularly moored.
     -   Construction activities on water (use of floating cranes, barges, and pile-driving
         vessels) for embankment and docking infrastructure may narrow or obstruct the
         existing docking area during the upgrading phase. This could hinder the movement of
         fishing vessels entering and exiting the port in the upgrading section but do not affect
         the expansion section.
This impact is assessed as moderate and localized, occurring intermittently over the 24-month
construction period because: (i) The Lach Truong River at the subproject 2 site is 150–200m
wide, approximately 4 km from the river mouth, and the remaining width is sufficient for local
vessel navigation. (ii) Construction equipment operating on water will be anchored at fixed
locations, and since subproject 2 involves building only a short 60m embankment, the impact
on fishing vessel movements will be limited. (iii) The Lach Truong mooring area, located
adjacent to the existing fishing port, can be used as a temporary docking area during demolition
of the current wharf. (iv) These impacts can be effectively mitigated through the
implementation of waterway traffic safety management and successive method.
(ii) Impacts on nearby seafood processing facilities
Within the existing Lach Truong fishing port area, approximately 20 to 100m from the port, there
are two seafood processing facilities: Chung Huong – specializing in frozen and fresh seafood;
Kich Hang – providing ice and seafood-related supplies; These businesses offer fishery services,
including seafood purchasing, freezing, and other support services for local fishersmen. They
operate throughout the day, serving customers as needed. As a result, construction and port
upgrading activities may partially disrupt the business operations of these service providers.
During the construction phase, operations at the existing Lach Truong fishing port will remain
active. However, the upgrading and expansion works are expected to disrupt production and
business activities of nearby facilities (seafood processing, trade, and export of frozen shrimp,
squid, and other aquatic products) due to dust and emissions generated by construction activities,
causing traffic congestion, affecting the movement of transport vehicles and daily commuting of
30–40 workers, landscape in front of the workshops/facilities due to gather of construction
materials, machinery and waste.
However, this impact is assessed as Moderate because: (i) the construction is carried out based
on the successive method, causing only localized and intermittent disruption to the operations of
seafood processing facilities and fishery service providers. Importantly, construction activities
will not coincide with peak fishing periods, such as harvest or vessel departure times. (ii) The
internal port roads are over 15m wide, ensuring sufficient space to for simultaneous construction
and business operations. (iii) The disruption will be short-term, lasting approximately 2–3 months
at the nearest construction areas. These impacts can be avoided or effectively mitigated through
appropriate construction and management measures.
(iii) Disruption to activities of center market in existing Lach Truong fishing port
The construction and upgrading of Lach Truong fishing port will disrupt operations at the center
market area of the existing fishing port (located 250m from the construction site) due to: dust,
emissions, noise, vibration, solid waste, traffic congestion, and traffic accidents; obstruction of
access and trading activities for local residents in the market area; risk of community conflict
between local residents and construction workers, affecting the trading and exchange of goods
of local people.
Business households at the market may experience temporary disruption (approximately 24
months) due to restricted access. However, this impact is assessed as Moderate because: (i)
construction components are implemented within the existing port; (ii) the impact occurs over

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              191
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
a short duration for construction activities near the market and for the transportation of materials
and waste passing through the road in front of the market gate; (iii) once operational, the project
will help promote the development of household businesses. This impact can be mitigated
through the measures proposed in Chapter 5.
(iv) Impacts on aquaculture activities along the Lach Truong river
Approximately 200 m upstream from the port area (Subproject 2), there are several aquaculture
cages of shrimp and clams located in the riverbed/aquaculture area on the opposite bank of the
Lach Truong River.
The waterborne construction equipment is mobilized from the Lach Truong estuary into the
subproject area, avoiding the river section where aquaculture activities are taking place.
Therefore, it will not impact aquaculture operations. Although subproject covers the dredging
of the navigation channel leading to the mooring area which is located in a creek adjacent to
the Lach Truong River section with the port’s docking area, this is a small creek separated from
the port and situated downstream from the aquaculture area. As such, the risk of pollution (TSS,
turbidity) spread from the dredging process to these areas is considered low.
In reality, there is a potential risk of pollutant dispersion (TSS, oil, etc.) from machinery and
in-water construction activities affecting the water quality of the Lach Truong River. However,
the aquaculture area is located upstream, not too close to the project site (approximately 200–
300m away as previously mentioned). The in-water construction area is localized (a short 60-
m embankment section, with a brief construction period of around 6 months) within the existing
port area, so the level of impact on the river’s water quality is considered low. Therefore, the
risk of pollutant dispersion from oil leakage incidents involving waterborne construction
equipment (canoes, barges, pile-driving vessels, etc.) is assessed as Low and can be controlled
through appropriate measures.




Aquaculture area in Lach Truong river, 200-300m from the existing port on the opposite rive
                                          bank
(v) Impacts of the disposal site serving the subproject 2
Waste from subproject 2 will be disposed of at Disposal Site No. 2, which is the same location
used for subproject 1. As outlined in Section (v) regarding the disposal site for subproject, the
impact level on local residents and surrounding areas is considered low, short-term, and
intermittent, and can be effectively mitigated through appropriate technical and management
measures.
(vi) Impacts on sensitive receptors

As stated in Table 2-6 (Chapter 2), sensitive receptors potentially affected by the construction
of Subproject 2 – the upgrading of Lach Truong fishing port – include: Truong Xa Parish
Church (200m from the construction site), seafood processing facilities within the port, Hoa
Loc Center market (250m away), Phu La La (50m away), Lach Truong ferry terminal, and the
aquaculture area (200–300m away). The main impacts during construction in areas with these
sensitive receptors are primarily related to dust, noise, and traffic obstruction caused by
construction activities and the gathering of materials. The level of impact is assessed as LOW
to MODERATE, as construction at each location lasts approximately 2–3 months and may
affect daily activities, production, transportation, and religious/spiritual practices (e.g., temple
ceremonies, festivals), especially on full moon and first lunar days (for Phu La La) or weekends
(for Truong Xa Parish Church). These impacts can be avoided or mitigated through appropriate
measures.
Site-specific of subproject 3
The site- specific impacts in construction site of Subproject 3 are temporary effects on
aquaculture areas of households within the investment scope of Phu Son 2 sluice, the upgraded
section of Phu Son 2 canal beneath the right bank of the Can River, Ong Ty canal, and the
dredging of the main drainage ditch that supplies water for aquaculture (serving both water
intake and drainage for this area). Details are as follows:
(i) Temporary disruption of water intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                    194
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Dredging activities along the water supply/drainage canals (Phu Son 2 canal, 1.43 km long, and
Ong Ty Canal, 2.58 km long), along with the reconstruction of two water intakes for these
canals, will be carried out using embankment and flow diversion construction methods (dry
season from November to June annually; rainy season from July to October annually). These
works will only partially obstruct the existing flow and will temporarily disrupt water intake
and drainage activities serving aquaculture in the area.
However, water is fetched according to the farming schedule of local households, and
successive construction method will be applied for each canal segment and flow diversion
(using sheet piles to isolate each segment, temporarily occupying only part of the canal surface)
will be carried out. Therefore, the impact is localized and short-term (approximately 1–3
months per segment), and the level of impact is considered low. It can be mitigated through
appropriate construction methods and combined with the aquaculture plans of local households.




            End of Phu Son 2 canal                                 Ong Ty canal
(ii) Livelihood of local residents
Approximately 70% of local residents practice extensive aquaculture, with the farming season
from February to October. The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction activities
under Subproject 3 will reduce water quality and supply in the canals serving aquaculture in the
area. Aquatic species such as shrimp, crab, and mullet are unable to relocate and may die due to
their sensitivity to increased TSS, dust, noise, and vibration, negatively affecting the quality and
growth of farmed seafood. This impacts the livelihoods of local residents.
Additionally, during the rainy and stormy season, drainage from the construction area may
accumulate TSS on exposed surfaces, material/waste gathering area, leading to rainwater runoff
into aquaculture ponds and causing damage to local farmers.
These impacts are considered moderate and short-term, occurring at specific times and locations
during the construction of sluices, canal surfaces, and other structures along Phu Son 2 Canal
and Ong Ty Canal. Construction methods, along with response plans and mitigation measures,
must be prepared to minimize impacts on the livelihoods of aquaculture households and local
trade, sales, and consumption activities.




                   Ong Ty canal                        Duck farming on Phu Son 2 canal

 (iii) Impacts of the disposal site serving the subproject 3
 Dredged materials of the subproject are disposed at: (i) Disposal Site No. 1 in Tan Tien
 commune – receiving dredged materials from Phu Son 2 canal, with a total area of 15 ha and a
 storage capacity of 225,000 m³. (ii) Disposal Site No. 2 in Tan Tien – receiving dredged
 materials from Bao Trieu canal, with a total area of around 45 ha and a storage capacity of
 650,000 m³. Affected parties include nearby residents and surrounding natural areas. The
 impact level is assessed as low because: (i) The disposal sites have been approved in official
 document by the Provincial People’s Committee or agreed upon between the local authorities
 and the Thanh Hoa PMU of Construction Investment Works for Agricultural and Rural
 Development; (ii) The surrounding area of the disposal sites consists of vacant land, with no
 residential buildings or households, short transportation distance of 0.5–1 km; (iii) The disposal
 transportation period is not long and not continuous; (iv) Impacts from disposal activities can
 be mitigated through appropriate management and technical measures.

 Affected sensitive receptors include aquaculture households (located 350m away), Nga Tien
 Market (20m), Nga Tien Kindergarten (200 meters), Nga Tien Primary School (180m), and the
 poultry farming area along the canal. Affected groups include aquaculture households, residents
 around Nga Tien Market, students and teachers at the schools, canal-side poultry farmers, and
 residents along the transportation route. Key impacts along the main material transportation
 route during the construction phase include: (i) Disruption to local traffics during daily activities,
 production hours, and school start/end times: Monday to Saturday, with peak hours of 6:30–
 7:30 AM, 11:00 AM–12:00 PM, 1:00–2:00 PM, and 4:30–5:30 PM; (ii) Increased levels of dust
 and noise, affecting the learning and teaching conditions of students and teachers; (iii) Elevated
 risk of traffic accidents during school arrival and end times due to increased vehicle density
 (approximately 5 trips/day for transporting construction materials and 2 trips/day for waste
 transportation, using 10-ton trucks). However, the level of impact is assessed as LOW due to
 the short-term, intermittent disruption, which can be avoided or mitigated through appropriate
 management measures.
 Site-specific impacts of the subproject 4:
 The site-specific impacts of the Subproject 4’s works in Hoang Yen commune, Hoang Hoa
 district include effects on aquaculture areas of households within the scope of dredging Son
 Trang 1 and 2 sluices, upgrading and dredging Son Trang 1 and 2 canals, upgrading and
 dredging the main drainage ditch that supplies water for aquaculture, combining water intake
 and drainage at the downstream left bank of Hung Tien Canal and the upstream left bank of
 Nghia Thuc canal. These impacts also affect the livelihoods of aquaculture households, the
 disposal site, and several sensitive receptors that may be affected by the project.


 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                       197
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
(i) Temporary disruption of water intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture
activities
Dredging activities along the water supply/drainage canals (Son Trang 1 canal, 0.52 km long,
serving 30 ha of aquaculture and 30 ha of drainage area, Son Trang 2 canal, 1.36 km long,
serving about 30 ha of aquaculture and 30 ha of drainage area, reconstruction of 3 new water
intakes for these two canals; Hung Tien canal, 0.87 km long, serving 45 ha of drainage and 30
ha of irrigation; and Nghia Thuc canal, 0.82 km long, serving 45 ha of drainage and 30 ha of
irrigation). The construction method involves embankment works and flow diversion dredging
(dry season from November to June annually; rainy season from July to October annually),
which will only partially obstruct the existing flow. However, it will temporarily disrupt water
intake and drainage for aquaculture.
However, water is fetched according to the farming schedule of local households, and
successive construction method will be applied for each canal segment and with flow diversion
(using sheet piles to isolate each segment, temporarily occupying only part of the canal surface).
Therefore, the impact is localized and short-term (approximately 1–3 months per segment), and
the level of impact is considered low. It can be mitigated through appropriate construction
methods and combined with the aquaculture plans of local households.
(ii) Livelihood of local people
Aquaculture households practice both extensive and intensive farming, with the farming season
from February to October. The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction
activities under Subproject 4 will affect the local ecosystem, impacting both the quality and
quantity of aquaculture production. Aquatic species such as shrimp, crab, and mullet cannot
relocate and may die due to their sensitivity to increased levels of total suspended solids (TSS),
dust, noise, and vibration, etc., and negatively affecting the health and growth of farmed
seafood, leading to reduced yields. Consequently, this poses a threat to the livelihoods of
aquaculture-dependent households.
Aquaculture farm: specializing in shrimp and fish farming using a modern, closed-loop
production system, provide to the market with the highest quality and freshest seafood products
to meet customer demand on all types of live seafood. The operations are self-contained within
the farm. The construction of drainage channels surrounding the aquaculture area and the Son
Trang 1 and 2 drainage canals, which encircle the farm on all sides, poses certain challenges in
receiving input materials and exporting products. Transportation and distribution of products
along the roads adjacent to the canals may be disrupted. Increased traffic volume and personnel
presence in the area may affect the farm, along with construction-related disturbances such as
noise, lighting, and vibration. These impacts are considered phased, short-term, localized, and
of low to moderate intensity, and can be mitigated through appropriate measures.




               Linh Trang seafood farm                       Linh Trang seafood farm

Additionally, during the rainy and stormy season, drainage from the construction area may
accumulate TSS on exposed surfaces, material/waste gathering area, leading to rainwater runoff
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             198
     Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
     into aquaculture ponds and causing damage to local farmers in rainy season (normally July to
     October annually).

     construction sections of Son Trang 1, 2 sluices, embankment surrounding the aquaculture area,
     Son Trang 1 and 2 drainage canals, Hung Tien canal, and Nghia Thuc Canal. Construction
     methods, along with response plans and mitigation measures, must be prepared to minimize
     impacts on the livelihoods of aquaculture households and local trade, sales, and consumption
     activities.

     surrounding natural areas. The impact level is assessed as low because: (i) The disposal sites

     vacant land, with no residential buildings or households, short transportation distance of 3km;

     disposal activities can be mitigated through appropriate management and technical measures.

Site-specific impacts of the Subproject 5
The site-specific impacts of the subproject 5 in Hoang Chau commune include effects on
aquaculture and crop areas due to temporary disruption to drainage activities, impacts on
household livelihoods, impacts from the disposal site, and affected sensitive receptors within
the scope of upgrading and completing canal surfaces and related structures on Dong Coi canal,
Cau Dua Canal, Tay Canal, Hoi Trieu Canal, as well as dredging of primary irrigation and
drainage canals and the canal operation and management road. These works aim to ensure water
supply for aquaculture and combined water intake and drainage for the area.
(i) Temporary disruption of water intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture
activities
Dredging activities along the water supply/drainage canals (Son Trang 1 canal, 0.52 km long,
serving 30 ha of aquaculture and 30 ha of drainage area, Son Trang 2 canal, 1.36 km long,
serving about 30 ha of aquaculture and 30 ha of drainage area, reconstruction of 3 new water
intakes for these two canals; Hung Tien canal, 0.87 km long, serving 45 ha of drainage and 30
ha of irrigation; and Nghia Thuc canal, 0.82 km long, serving 45 ha of drainage and 30 ha of
irrigation). The construction method involves embankment works and flow diversion dredging
(dry season from November to June annually; rainy season from July to October annually),
which will only partially obstruct the existing flow. However, it will temporarily disrupt water
intake and drainage for aquaculture.
Dredging activities along the irrigation and drainage canals will be carried out using
embankment construction and flow diversion methods (the dry season construction period
spans from November to June annually. Specifically: Dong Coi Canal is 1,310.20m long,
serving 25 ha of drainage and approximately 25 ha of aquaculture; Hoi Trieu Canal is 1,714.57
m long, with 4 new box culverts to be constructed for water intake, ensuring drainage for 105
ha and aquaculture for approximately 65 ha; Cau Dua Canal is 2,081m long, serving 30 ha of
drainage and 30 ha of aquaculture; Tay Canal is 535m long, serving 45 ha of drainage and
approximately 30 ha of aquaculture. The rainy season lasts from July to October annually (with
storms typically occurring in September and October). Construction activities will only
partially obstruct the existing flow and are expected to temporarily disrupt water intake and
drainage operations serving aquaculture in the area.
However, water is fetched according to the farming schedule of local households, and
successive construction method will be applied for each canal segment and with flow diversion
(using sheet piles to isolate each segment, temporarily occupying only part of the canal surface).
Therefore, the impact is localized and short-term (approximately 1–3 months per segment), and
the level of impact is considered low. It can be mitigated through appropriate construction
methods and combined with the aquaculture plans of local households.
(ii) Livelihood of local residents
In this area, about 60–70% of aquaculture households practice extensive farming, while around
30% operate industrial aquaculture under greenhouse systems, with the farming season from
February to October. The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction activities

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             200
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
under Subproject 5 will impact the local ecosystem, affecting both the quality and quantity of
aquaculture production. Aquatic species such as shrimp, crab, and mullet cannot relocate and
may die due to their sensitivity to increased levels of TSS, dust, noise, vibration, and light,
shrimp have been observed jumping out of ponds when exposed to nighttime lighting. These
factors negatively affect the health and growth of farmed seafood, leading to reduced
productivity and quality. Consequently, this poses a threat to the livelihoods of local residents.
Additionally, during the rainy and stormy season (typically from July to October annually),
drainage from the construction area may accumulate TSS on exposed surfaces, material/waste
gathering area, leading to rainwater runoff into aquaculture ponds and causing damage to local
farmers in rainy season.
These impacts are considered moderate, short-term, and localized, occurring at specific times
and segments during the construction of canals, canal surfaces, and related structures on Cau
Dua, Hoi Trieu, Dong Coi, and Tay canals. Construction methods, along with appropriate
solutions and response plans to address changes during the construction process, must be
prepared to ensure the minimization of impacts on the livelihoods of aquaculture households
and local businesses, trade, and consumption activities.
(iii) Impacts of disposal site serving subproject 5
Dredged materials of the subproject are disposed at the disposal site located in Bac Hoi Trieu
Village, Hoang Chau commune: (i) Zone 1 covers an area of approximately 0.33 hectares and
storage capacity of around 4,519 m³; (ii) Zone 2 covers an area of approximately 0.6 hectares
and storage capacity of around 12,000 m³. Affected parties include nearby residents and
surrounding natural areas, surrounding crop cultivation area. The impact level is assessed as
low because: (i) The disposal sites have been approved in official document by the Provincial
People’s Committee or agreed upon between the local authorities and the Thanh Hoa PMU of
Construction Investment Works for Agricultural and Rural Development; (ii) The surrounding
area of the disposal sites consists of pond land managed by Hoang Chau PPC and was
previously leased to local residents for production purposes, but the lease contract has expired.
Therefore, the disposal activities do not affect the income or livelihoods of the former land
users. The site contains no residential buildings or households, and the transport distance is
short, approximately 2 km, along sparsely trafficked inter-communal roads; (iii) The disposal
transportation period is not long and not continuous; (iv) Impacts from disposal activities can
be mitigated through appropriate management and technical measures.
(iv) Sensitive receptors – subproject 5
Within and around (a 400m radius) the area of Subproject implementation, there are no Physical
Cultural Resources (PCRs) such as temples, pagodas, shrines, graves, churches, nor any other
sensitive receptors.
Site-specific impacts of subproject 6
Site-specific impacts of the Subproject 6 in Hoang Luu commune include effects on aquaculture
areas and cultivated crops due to temporary disruption of drainage activities, impacts on
household livelihoods, effects from the disposal site, and sensitive receptors within the scope
of upgrading and completing canal surfaces and related structures. These works span from the
Tay dike, Cung River to Con Voi sluice, the road section from Ngo Hao to Thach sluice, and
involve dredging of primary irrigation canals and secondary drainage branches to ensure water
supply for aquaculture, combining water intake and drainage for the area.
(i) Temporary disruption of water intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture
operations
Dredging activities along the irrigation and drainage canals (Canal 1, with a length of 819.29m,
serving approximately 25 ha of drainage and 25 ha of aquaculture; Canal 2, with a length of
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             201
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
213.38m, serving approximately 25 ha of drainage and 35 ha of aquaculture. The construction
method involves embankment works and flow diversion dredging. The dry season is from
November to June annually, while the rainy season lasts from July to October. These activities
will only partially obstruct the existing flow and are expected to temporarily disrupt water
intake and drainage operations serving aquaculture in the area.
However, water is fetched according to the farming schedule of local households, and
successive construction method will be applied for each canal segment and with flow diversion
(using sheet piles to isolate each segment, temporarily occupying only part of the canal surface).
Therefore, the impact is localized and short-term (approximately 1–3 months per segment), and
the level of impact is considered low. It can be mitigated through appropriate construction
methods and combined with the aquaculture plans of local households.
(ii) Livelihood of local residents
The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction activities under Subproject 6 will
impact the local ecosystem, affecting both the quality and quantity of aquaculture production
and one season of rice cultivation (aquaculture are of 25ha with water used from canal 1 and
35ha from canal 2). Aquatic species in ponds such as shrimp, crab, and mullet cannot relocate
and may die due to their sensitivity to increased levels of TSS, dust, noise, vibration, and light.
These factors negatively affect the health and growth of farmed seafood, leading to reduced
productivity and quality. Consequently, this poses a threat to the livelihoods of local residents
Additionally, during the rainy and stormy season, drainage from the construction area, from the
Tay dike of the Cung River to the Con Voi sluice, and along the road from Ngo Hao to Thach
sluice, including dredging of primary and secondary canal branches, may lead to increased
accumulation of TSS on exposed surfaces, material/waste gathering areas. This can result in
rainwater runoff entering aquaculture ponds, causing damage to local farmers during rainy
season.
These impacts are considered moderate, short-term, and localized, occurring at specific times
and segments during the construction process. They are controllable and can be mitigated. Local
residents are highly supportive and in agreement with the implementation of Subproject 6 in
the area. Construction methods, along with appropriate solutions and response plans to address
changes during the construction process from the Tay dike of the Cung River to the Con Voi
sluice, and along the road from Ngo Hao to Thach sluice, including dredging of primary and
secondary canal branches must be prepared to ensure the minimization of impacts on the
livelihoods of aquaculture households and local businesses, trade, and consumption activities.
(iii) Impacts of disposal site serving the subproject 6
Dredged materials of the subproject are disposed at the disposal site located in Phuong Ngo
village, Hoang Chau commune – with an area of about 1.2ha and storage capacity of 17,500
m3. Affected parties include nearby residents and surrounding natural areas, surrounding crop
cultivation area. The impact level is assessed as low because: (i) The disposal sites have been
approved in official document by the Provincial People’s Committee or agreed upon between
the local authorities and the Thanh Hoa PMU of Construction Investment Works for
Agricultural and Rural Development; (ii) The surrounding area of the disposal sites consists of
pond land managed by Hoang Chau PPC. Therefore, the disposal activities do not affect the
income or livelihoods of the land tenant. The site contains no residential buildings or
households, and the transport distance is short, approximately 0.75 km, along sparsely
trafficked inter-communal roads; (iii) The disposal transportation period is not long and not
continuous; (iv) Impacts from disposal activities can be mitigated through appropriate
management and technical measures.

      materials and 2 trips/day for waste transportation, using 10-ton trucks).
      The level of impact is assessed as low to moderate because: (i) The number of truck trips is
      relatively low (7 trips/day), and machinery is mobilized only during specific periods and
      demobilized upon completion of works; (ii) The impact duration is non-continuous and short-
      term (approximately 24 months); (iii) There are many traffic participants and high vehicle
      density along the main transportation route, especially during peak hours coinciding with school

      Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                     203
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
and office schedules—typically from 7:00–8:00 AM and 4:00–6:00 PM daily.
Site-specific impacts of Subproject 7
The site-specific impacts of the Subproject 7 in Hoang Chau Commune include temporary
disruption of water intake and drainage activities, impacts on livelihoods, effects from the
disposal site, and sensitive receptors located within agricultural production areas (aquaculture
and crop cultivation) of local households. These impacts fall within the scope of investment in
the Song Khe sluice located beneath the left embankment of the Ma River, and dredging of the
Song Khe canal/channel system, including branch canals No. 1, 2, and 3, which serve
aquaculture operations and provide combined water intake and flood drainage for the area.
(i) Temporary disruption of water intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture
operations
Dredging activities along the irrigation and drainage canals include: Song Khe canal, with a
length of 1,336 me, serving irrigation with approximately 70 ha and aquaculture with around
60 ha; branch canal 1, 487m long, providing drainage with approximately 35 ha and aquaculture
with around 20 ha; branch canal 2, serving drainage with 20 ha and aquaculture for around with
ha; branch canal 3, 523m long, ensuring drainage with 15 ha and aquaculture with 15 ha. The
construction method involves embankment works and flow diversion dredging. The dry season
is from November to June, while the rainy season lasts from July to October annually. These
activities are expected to partially obstruct the existing flow, causing temporary disruption of
water intake and drainage operations serving aquaculture in the area.
However, water is fetched according to the farming schedule of local households, and
successive construction method will be applied for each canal segment and with flow diversion
(using sheet piles to isolate each segment, temporarily occupying only part of the canal surface).
Therefore, the impact is localized and short-term (approximately 1–3 months per segment), and
the level of impact is considered low. It can be mitigated through appropriate construction
methods and combined with the aquaculture plans of local households.
(ii) Livelihood of people in Hoang Chau commune – subproject 7
Over 80% of local agricultural households engage in aquaculture, with the farming season from
February to October, while the remaining 20% participate in crop cultivation. The temporary land
occupation for dredging and construction activities under Subproject 7 will impact the local
ecosystem of Song Khe canal, branch canals 1,2,2, Phong Chau canal, affecting both the quality
and quantity of aquaculture production. Aquatic species in ponds such as shrimp, crab, and
mullet, and crops including corn, legumes, and asparagus. As these species cannot relocate and
may die due to their sensitivity to increased levels of TSS, dust, noise, vibration, and light.
These factors negatively affect the health and growth of farmed seafood, leading to reduced
productivity and quality. Consequently, this poses a threat to the livelihoods of local residents.
Vegetables and other crops in the area are traditionally cultivated using freshwater. During the
construction process, there is a risk of saltwater intrusion, which may increase soil salinity and
adversely affect the health of the crops. This could impact the livelihoods of local residents.

(ii) Livelihood of local residents
In this area, 70% of aquaculture households practice extensive farming, with the farming season
from February to October. The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction
activities under Subproject 8 will impact the local ecosystem, affecting both the quality and
quantity of aquaculture production. Aquatic species such as shrimp, crab, and mullet cannot
relocate and may die due to their sensitivity to increased levels of TSS, dust, noise, vibration,

gathering area, leading to rainwater runoff into aquaculture ponds and causing damage to local
farmers in rainy season.

during the construction of sluices, canal surfaces, and related structures along the main drainage
canal from Ong Ninh sluice to Quay Co sluice, as well as branch canals 1 and No. 2 extending
toward the main route of the Len River. Construction methods, along with appropriate solutions
and response plans to address changes during the construction process, must be prepared to
ensure the minimization of impacts on the livelihoods of aquaculture households and local
business activities, including trade and consumption.

(ii) Livelihood of residents
Dredging of the main and branch irrigation and drainage canals, along with embankment,
upgrades and construction of operation and management roads will follow the alignment of
existing infrastructure. Therefore, no residential land or housing along the route will be affected.
However, most aquaculture pond areas are located along the canal route and within irrigation
land and buffer zones adjacent to the embankment, so the subproject’s land acquisition will
reduce the aquaculture area available to local households and increase impacts due to changes
in the living environment during the construction period, particularly affecting shrimp farming
cycles—either during stocking preparation or active cultivation in ponds.
In this area, aquaculture households practice extensive farming, with the farming season from
February to October. The temporary land occupation for dredging and construction activities
under Subproject 9 will impact the local ecosystem, affecting both the quality and quantity of
aquaculture production. Aquatic species such as shrimp cannot relocate and may die due to their
sensitivity to increased levels of TSS, dust, noise, vibration, and light. These factors negatively
affect the health and growth of farmed seafood, leading to reduced productivity and quality.
Consequently, this poses a threat to the livelihoods of local residents.
Regarding the current canal surfaces and in-field irrigation canals, the transportation routes
along the embankment, canal sections with concrete surfaces have deteriorated or remain as
narrow dirt roads. In particular, the transport route of the aquaculture have been eroded, with
small banks, making it difficulties for the transportation of aquatic products and daily travel of
local residents. The implementation of construction activities under the Subproject 9 may
further accelerate the degradation of these existing routes, thereby affecting trade, product
distribution, and especially the consumption of aquaculture products in the area. This indirectly
impacts the income and livelihoods of local residents. Although the construction period is short-
term and non-continuous, mitigation measures are necessary to minimize these impacts on the
community.
Additionally, during the rainy and stormy season (typically from July to October annually), the
drainage from the construction area may accumulate TSS on exposed surfaces, material/waste
gathering area, leading to rainwater runoff into aquaculture ponds and causing damage to local
farmers in rainy season. This issue was mentioned in the public consultation and in depth
interview with local residents in aquaculture area and local authorities.
These impacts are considered moderate and short-term, occurring at specific times and locations
during the construction of Ong Vanh sluice (Yen river) and Ong Thuoc sluice (Hoang river),
the canal surface as well as the main canal connecting these two sluices. Construction methods,
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               209
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
along with appropriate solutions and response plans to address changes during the construction
process, must be prepared to ensure the minimization of impacts on the livelihoods of
aquaculture households and local business activities, including trade and consumption.

Dredged materials of the subproject are disposed at the disposal site located in Dung village,

4.5.    ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL RISKS DURING OPERATION PHASE
Due to the specific characteristics of the subprojects, in this stage the assessment of
environmental and social (E&S) impacts and risks will be conducted by group of subprojects
as follows: (i) subproject 1 and 2; and (ii) subproject 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9, as presented below:
4.5.1. Impacts and risks during operation phase of subproject 1, 2
The Subprojects 1 and 2 are to upgrade and expand Hoang Hoa fishing port and Lach Truong
fishing Port. The sources of environmental and social impacts and risks during the operation
phase of these two ports arise from: (i) Operation of transportation vehicles such as ships, boats,
and cargo trucks; fish and seafood loading and unloading activities; (ii) Activities of Port
operators, fishermen at the port, boat/ship drivers, and port trader; (iii) Operations of fishing
boats entering and existing the port and vessels sheltering in the port ; (iv) Operation of
functional areas within the port (seafood receiving area, sorting area, storage area, etc.); (v)
Operation of technical infrastructure systems such as the wastewater treatment plant, waste
storage facilities, public toilets, etc.
The general impacts and risks during the operation phase of subproject 1, 2, including:
    -   Dust and emissions generated
    -   Noise
    -   Wastewater generated
    -   Solid waste generated
    -   Environmental risk incidents
    -   Traffic impacts and risks (waterway and roadway)
    -   Impacts on aquatic life and deterioration of surface water quality due to the
        concentration of vessels in the port area.
Detailed Impact Assessment is presented below:
 4.5.1.1. Dust and Emissions Generated
Sources of dust and emissions from the operation of 02 fishing ports, especially in the fishery
service areas, include:
    -   Dust and emissions from transportation vehicles such as ships/boats/trucks, and
        fish/seafood loading and unloading activities.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               210
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    -   Fishy odors generated from fishing vessels upon return to the port and from cold storage
        facilities used for seafood preservation.
    -   Odors and emissions (NH₃, H₂S, etc.) generated from the wastewater treatment system,
        solid waste storage facilities, etc.
Details of impacts from dust and emissions during the operation phase:
    ❖ Dust and emissions from transportation ships/boats/trucks, fish/seafood loading and
      unloading activities
Dust and emissions of ships/boats/trucks are primarily generated from the operation of diesel-
powered generators and vessel exhaust systems. The pollutants mainly include dust, COx, SOx,
NOx, Hydrocarbons (HC), etc. The emission load from vessels entering and leaving the port is
difficult to estimate and depends on the daily ships going in and out of the port, with an
estimated number of about 100 vessels/day (subproject 1) and 70 vessels/day (subproject 2).
The average berthing and departure time is approximately 2 hours. The fuel consumption rates
of vessels under subproject 1 and 2 are 40.88 kg/h and 28.62 kg/h, respectively, equivalent to
81.76 kg/2h for subproject 1’s vessels and 57.23 kg/2h for subproject 2’s vessels.
According to statistics of the World of Health Organization (WHO - 1993), the emission
coefficient per tone of diesel fuel (for internal combustion engine as follows: dust 4.3 kg; SO2
20S kg (0.05% S); CO 28 kg; NO2 55 kg. Applying the formula for calculating the pollutant
load in exhaust gases arising from the operation of ships/boats:
Pollutant load (mg/m·s) = Pollution coefficient (kg/day) × number of ships in a day × 1000
                               (average distance × 86,400).
Where: 1000 is the conversion factor from “km” to “m”; 86,400 is the conversion factor from
“day” to “seconds”; the sulfur (S) content in DO oil is 0.05%; average travel distance: 1.56 km
(Subproject 1) and 1.15 Km (Subproject 2). Based on the fuel consumption rates and the
emission factors for diesel combustion in internal combustion engines, the pollutant loads are
calculated as follows. The Sutton model was applied to calculate the diffusion of air pollutants
from a continuous emission source. The calculation results of dust concentrations at several
random points along the route, at a distance of 5m from the emission source, show that:
                      Table 4-37. Pollutant load from movement of vessels
No. Pollutants     Fuel consumption        Volume of          Pollution load         Pollution
                         (ton)            emission (kg)         (mg/m.s)       concentration (mg/m3)
Subproject 1
 1      CO                0.082                     0.00230      0.0170              0.00073
 2      SO2               0.082                     0.00008      0.0006              0.00003
 3     NO2                0.082                     0.00451      0.0335              0.00144
 4     Dust               0.082                     0.00035      0.0026              0.00011
Subproject 2
 1      CO                0.057                     0.00160       0.0119             0.00055
 2     SO2                0.057                     0.00006       0.0004            0.0000198
 3     NO2                0.057                     0.00315       0.0234             0.00109
 4     Dust               0.057                     0.00025       0.0018             0.00075
According to the forecast results of dust and exhaust gas dispersion from vessel movements, all
parameters remain within the permissible limits.
The affected group at each of subproject 1 and subproject 2 is estimated at approximately 450–
500 people, consisting mainly of about 350 fishermen engaged in port activities and operating
fishing vessels entering and leaving the port; around 100–135 local residents engaging business
and trading at kiosks within the port area; and approximately 15 port operation staff.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                211
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
The impact level of this emission source is assessed as Low because: (i) the concentration is
only greatest when all vessels gather at the port at once and only affect the area for a short time
(according to general practice and procedures at fishing ports, the docking period can last from
30 minutes to 2 hours within 9-11 am, 15-15pm); (ii) this source of emissions will gradually
decrease when ships cease operating; and (iii) this source of emissions gradually dilutes into
the air with fairly ventilated conditions on a large port with strong wind blows.
    ❖ Fishy odor generated from fishing vessels upon arrival at the port
The source of fishy odors mainly appears when fishing vessels carrying seafood that is caught
and frozen onboard. The fishy odor composition is mainly from NH3, Hydrocarbons (HC),
generated from the decomposition of organic matters and refrigeration equipment. The load of
NH3 emissions arising from refrigeration equipment and fishy odors from fishing boats is
considered low, as the aquatic products (shrimp, crab, seafood, etc.) are quickly loaded and
unloaded, sorted, and delivered to the market for consumption or immediately put into cold
storage. The main gases generated from the decomposition of organic matter include CO₂, NH₃,
H₂S, CO, CH₄, and Mercaptans. In which, the main odor-causing gases are NH3, H2S and
Mercaptan, discomforting fishermen, traders, and sellers/buyers in the port area.
This impact is long-term and continuous, and the impact level assessed as Medium, because:
(i) the volume of aquatic products arrived at the port is localized, depending fishing vessels
(hourly - early morning (from 4 a.m. to 6 a.m.) and afternoon (from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m.), seasonally
- the peak fishing season is normally from June to September every year); (ii) The seafood
decomposition contributes to an increase in fishy odor, adversely affecting the environment in
the port area (accounting for about 5%–10%). Nevertheless, this impact can be mitigated
through appropriate measures.
    ❖ Odors and emissions (NH₃, H₂S, etc.) from the wastewater treatment plant
Each of subproject 1 and 2 will invest in the construction of a wastewater treatment plant with
a capacity of 200 m³/day, applying aerobic treatment technology and designed with closed
treatment tanks. However, odors and gaseous emissions (such as NH₃, H₂S, CH₄, etc.) are
expected to be generated from the wastewater treatment facilities of the subprojects.
WWTP technology process: Wastewater from sources → Collection tank → with grease
separation tank → Equalization tank → UASB tank → ANOXIC tank → MBBR tank →
Biological settling tank → Disinfection tank → Treated wastewater meeting QCVN
40:2011/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on industrial wastewater (Column B) →
Receiving source (Lach Truong river).
According to this process, odors from the WWTP mainly arise from the collection tank, grease
separation tank, equalization tank, reaction tank (UASB), Anoxic tank. Aerobic decomposition
also produces odors but at a lower level. Gases generated primarily from anaerobic
decomposition include H₂S, Mercaptans, CO₂, and CH₄, among which H₂S and Mercaptans are
the main odor-causing substances.
Based on the aerobic treatment technology and the design of enclosed treatment tanks, the 200
m³/day wastewater treatment plant will significantly reduce odors generated from substances
such as H₂S, CH₄, and NH₃. However, odor concentrations may still remain at low levels in
cases where complete elimination is not possible. Typically, the concentration ranges of odor-
causing substances (H₂S, CH₄, NH₃) in aerobic wastewater treatment systems with enclosed
tanks fall within the following thresholds:
    •   H₂S (Hydrogen Sulfide): The concentration of H₂S in enclosed aerobic treatment tanks
        is typical maintained below 0.1 ppm, and under ideal conditions, it can be even lower.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              212
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    •   CH₄ (Methane): The concentration of CH4 in aerobic treatment tanks can range from
        0.5 to 5 ppm, however, this figure depends on the level of ventilation and emission
        control in the tank.
    •   NH₃ (Ammonia): The concentration of NH₃ in enclosed treatment tanks can be controlled
        within the range of 1–3 ppm, depending on the nitrification process and aeration system.
The groups directly affected by odors from the wastewater treatment plant and solid waste
storage facility are plant operators and fishermen at the port (approximately 450-500 people in
each fishing port of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong). Since the wastewater treatment plant use
aerobic technology with closed treatment tanks, the odor is minimized. The decomposition
process generates malodor but at a low level. In addition, the wastewater treatment plant of each
Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing port will be built closely, (closed treatment blocks), so
the odor generated will be minimized. Therefore, the impact level is assessed as Low and can
be mitigated by appropriate technological and technical measures.
 4.5.1.2. Noise
The source of noise arises mainly from vessels when docking, vehicles entering to purchase
and transport seafood for consumption and from the service area.
The forecast of maximum noise levels (taking into account cumulative noise when multiple
vessels arrive and depart simultaneously) for subproject 1 (Hoang Truong Fishing Port) and
Subproject 2 (Lach Truong Fishing Port)—with an average of 100 and 70 vessels per day, and
up to 500 vessels during storm events) is as follows: noise from vessels is expected to range
between 80–85 dBA; noise from road traffic between 85–95 dBA; and noise generated from
service areas between 75–79 dBA. Most motorized vehicles are equipped with mufflers;
however, as these are often self-fabricated, their effectiveness in noise reduction is not fully
ensured.
Noise mainly affects traders, workers working at Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports,
and fishermen (about 450 - 500 people each). The affected area is typically within a radius of
10–20 meters around the receiving, sorting, and unloading areas. (According to standard
practices and procedures at fishing ports, docking times last from 30 minutes to 2 hours within
9-11 am and 15-16 pm).
However, field surveys at local existing fishing ports show that vessel docking,
loading/unloading, and sorting activities do not always take place all day, usually within a
month, the peak time of full-day operation is from the 8th to the 15th day of the lunar calendar.
On other days, these activities take place in the early morning (4:00–6:00 a.m.) and afternoon
(2:00–4:00 p.m.). Additionally, upon ship arrival, the engine will be turned off, the port space
is wide towards the river, so the noise is also diffused quickly. Therefore, the impact level is
assessed as Low and can be mitigated through appropriate measure.
 4.5.1.3. Wastewater generated
For the subproject 1 (Hoang Truong fishing port) and subproject 2 (Lach Truong fishing port),
the sources of wastewater generated from the operation include: domestic wastewater,
wastewater from onshore service areas, and wastewater from vessels operating in the waters in
front of the port. The wastewater volume is calculated based on the water supply volume
(according to the wastewater discharge standard, taken as 100% of the water supply standard).
The estimated wastewater flow for Hoang Hoa and Lach Truong Fishing Ports is 161.4 m³/day
and 339.24 m3/day. Details are shown below:

The characteristics of each type of wastewater arising from the operation of Hoang Truong
Fishing port – subproject 1 and Lach Truong fishing port – subproject 2 are as follows:
a. Domestic wastewater
Domestic wastewater arises from the working areas of staffs and employees in the service area
and fishermen/traders at the port, the volume and pollution level of this waste source mainly
depends on the number of workers at a port (about 450 - 500 people) and the amount of water
used daily at the operational facilities and public toilets, garbage storage facilities, wastewater
treatment plant. The domestic wastewater volume is estimated at 12.5 m3/day. The waste
generated from this source is highly dirty, its composition mainly contains organic matters,
nutrients, residues, suspended substances and pathogenic bacteria. Besides, it contains high
organic content (BOD, COD), high solid content and many pathogenic bacteria. The above
amount of wastewater, if not collected and treated appropriately, can cause the spread of
pathogens, affecting coastal seawater sources in Lach Truong river, damaging the beautiful
landscape in the surrounding areas.
The operation of subproject 1 – Hoang Truong fishing port, especially from fisheries service

Based on the above table, it can be seen that the concentration of organic substances in domestic
wastewater after treatment with septic tanks is approximately and exceeds the permissible level
by 1.0 to 2.0 times. TSS ranges from lower than to 1.8 times higher than that of permissible level.
The content of animal and plant fats and oil is 2.1 times higher than permissible level. Therefore,
wastewater treated by septic tanks needs further treatment at centralized water treatment facilities
before being discharged into the receiving environment.
b. Production wastewater at each fishing port of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong
Wastewater of onshore service areas
When the fishery logistics service area comes into operation, it will be accompanied by a system
of service and commercial facilities. The operation of these facilities will generate a significant
volume of domestic wastewater. According to the standards for water supply for fishery
services, they include:
       Qservice= 2 l/m2 x supply area of 10,700 m2 =21,400 l = 21,4 m3/day.night.
       Qwashing=10% x Qservice =10% x 21.4 = 2.14 m3/day.night.
       Qfirefighting= 54 m3 (1 fire within 3h). This volume of water is supplied together with the
       domestic water supply (service water, washing water, and fire-fighting water) through the
       main water meter, stored in an underground tank, and pumped to the respective facilities
       at the port according to the design.
       Qberthing- Demand of fresh water when vessels berth at the port is about 70 m3/day.
Total water volume for domestic use and firefighting is 140 m3/day.night (calculate max
coefficient k=1.25).
- The domestic wastewater generated from the logistics area and from vessels berthing at the
fishing port when the subproject 1 comes into operation is approximately 21.4 + 2.14 + 70 =
93.54 m³/day, equivalent to a total wastewater flow of about 94 m³/day at Hoang Truong port.
After being collected in the treatment tank located on the western side of the project site, the
wastewater will be treated to meet discharge standards before being conveyed to the Hai Tien
urban wastewater treatment plant for complete treatment prior to discharge into the
environment. This impact on the water supply for services is considered insignificant.
The domestic wastewater generated from the port logistics area and from vessels berthing at
subproject 2 is approximately 15.36 + 2.29 + 70 = 87.65 m³/day, equivalent to a total wastewater
flow of about 88 m³/day at Hoang Truong port.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               215
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Production wastewater from seafood receiving facilities, seafood storage warehouse
The waste stream here mainly contains organic matter, animal fats and oils, and by-products
(such as fish scales, discarded small fish, shrimp shells, etc.). If not properly collected and
treated, these substances can easily decompose and cause foul odors in the surrounding area.
Ice: It is estimated that about 60% of the total seafood passing through the port requires ice for
preservation, with an average of 2 tons of ice needed per 1 ton of seafood. With an annual
seafood catch of 15,000 tons, the corresponding freshwater demand for ice production is about
15,000 m³/year. The total freshwater demand for activities involving direct contact with seafood
is approximately 124.423 m³/day, including:
         Qice water supply = 15,000 tons x 2 tons of water for ice production = 83.33 (m3/day). The
         utilization rate is usually around 80%, corresponding to approximately 65.75 m³/day
         (subproject 1) and about 138.24 m³/day at (subproject 2). This portion is used for ice
         production to preserve aquatic products at the port and to supply fishing vessels engaged
         in catching and consuming seafood.
         Q freshwater= 41.09 m3/day. This wastewater mainly consists of seafood washing water
         (49.3 m³/day). After the removal of by-products such as fish scales, shrimp shells, etc., it
         is collected through branch and main pipelines into the wastewater treatment tank located
         on the western side of the project site, where it is treated to meet the required standards
         before being discharged into the receiving source as previously described.
Water from ship, mechanical, and vessel equipment repair workshops: The water supply
for the maintenance and repair workshop is 2 m³/day-night. The main pollutants in the
wastewater from the mechanical and equipment repair workshop are primarily oil and grease,
and suspended solids. If this wastewater is not collected and treated before being discharged
into the receiving source, it will increase turbidity, reduce the penetration of sunlight into
seawater, and thereby decrease the photosynthetic capacity of aquatic plants, leading to a
reduction in total biomass production. Oil and grease will also reduce the ability of oxygen to
dissolve in water, thus affecting the respiration of aquatic organisms and lowering the oxidation
capacity of organic and inorganic substances in the water.
Ballast water is the water pumped into the ship’s specialized tanks (seawater). During the
process of ballast water refilling, various species such as plankton, microorganisms, and larvae
can be sucked into the water tank and move along the ship to other water bodies. The pumping
of water into the ballast tanks of vessels under anaerobic conditions causes some
microorganisms in the water to die and decompose, thereby affecting water quality. When
vessels operate without cargo, it is mandatory to pump water into the dedicated ballast tanks,
and when loading cargo, the ballast water is discharged. In the case, all vessels entering the port
for import and export activities are required to discharge ballast water. The ballast water
discharge volumes at subproject 1 and subproject 2 are specified as follows:
Subproject 1: The ballast water volume accounts for about 30% of the vessel’s cargo capacity.
With an estimated 15,000 tons of cargo passing through the port, this corresponds to 15,000
tons × 30% = 4,500 tons ≈ 4,500 m³/year of ballast water, or approximately 14.4 m³/day of
ballast water. Ballast water contains numerous microorganisms, bacteria, eggs, small
invertebrates, cysts, and larvae of various aquatic species. It is estimated that around 7,000
marine species are transported globally through ballast water. The discharge of ballast water
can therefore be toxic to coastal marine ecosystem.
- Subproject 2: The ballast water volume accounts9 for about 30% of the vessel’s cargo capacity.
With an estimated 18,000 tons of cargo passing through the port, this corresponds to 18,000
tons × 30% = 5,400 tons ≈ 5,400 m³/year of ballast water, or approximately 17.3 m³/day of

9
    Research, design and manufacture ballast water treatment system

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                216
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
ballast water. Ballast water contains numerous microorganisms, bacteria, eggs, small
invertebrates, cysts, and larvae of various aquatic species. It is estimated that around 7,000
marine species10 are transported globally through ballast water. The discharge of ballast water
can therefore be toxic to coastal marine ecosystems. A plan for the collection and treatment of
ballast water at Lach Truong port is required in order to reduce the risk of toxicity to coastal
ecosystems.
For small and medium-sized fishing vessels, the ballast water volume may fluctuate on average at
about 14.4 m³/day (subproject 1) and approximately 17.3 m³/day (subproject 2). Fishing vessels may
use ballast water to ensure stability when going offshore and during seafood catching, and it is often
reused. Under normal operating procedures, ballast water does not need to be changed too frequently.
Ballast water will be inspected periodically every 6 months to 1 year to ensure that no accumulation
of impurities or invasive organisms (such as bacteria or marine species) occurs in the ballast water.
Therefore, the amount of ballast water discharged is considered low.
Bilge Water: is the wastewater generated from cleaning and maintenance of equipment and
machinery on board; leakages from fuel pipes and engine rooms; overflow from tanks; and
incidents during vessel operation. Bilge water is collected in bilge wells on each vessel. The
depth and number of bilge wells depend on the vessel’s size, capacity, and the volume of bilge
water generated.
Based on statistical data from the Thanh Hoa Maritime Administration from 2010–2018; and
on the operational processes of several terminals in the vicinity of the Project such as the
petrochemical refinery port area, specialized terminals, and the general cargo terminals of Nghi
Son Port, the estimated bilge water generated by each type of vessel is as follows: (i) For vessels
with a capacity of ≤ 10,000 DWT: about 0.01 – 0.05 m³/day/vessel; (ii) For vessels with a
capacity of 10,000 – 20,000 DWT: about 1 – 1.3 m³/day/vessel. (ii) For vessels with a capacity
of < 30,000 DWT: about 1 – 1.5 m³/day/vessel. Accordingly, vessels entering the ports under
subproject 1 and subproject 2, with a capacity of ≤ 10,000 DWT, generate about 0.05
m³/day/vessel of bilge water.
The composition of this type of wastewater may contain oil, grease, and other pollutants. If this
wastewater is not treated as hazardous waste before being discharged into the marine
environment, it will cause water pollution, directly affecting the aquatic ecosystem along the
coastal areas of Hoang Truong commune, Hoa Loc commune, and neighboring localities, and
reducing the local food chain. The impact level is assessed as Moderate, with long-term effects
if untreated, but it can be avoided or mitigated through appropriate vessel management
measures.
Wastewater from vessel cleaning activities: Vessel cleaning water includes cabin washing water,
deck washing water, and cargo hold washing water. For cabin and deck washing, river water is
used; this type of wastewater cannot be collected and does not contain harmful substances, therefore
it is permitted to be discharged directly into the receiving source, the Lach Truong River. In the
case of cleaning cargo hold, the wastewater mainly contains sand, soil, and organic matter.
However, due to the specific nature of the port handling only one type of cargo, this type of
wastewater is generated infrequently. Oily wastewater is a liquid waste containing oil, originating
from the bilge system in the engine room, cargo hold washing water from oil tankers, fuel tank
cleaning water, waste oil, oil sludge, oil leaks, ballast water mixed with oil, and oily bilge water
from cargo holds. The volume of this wastewater is not large, estimated at about 3 m³/day. This
wastewater contains oil, grease, and other pollutants, and if not properly treated, it will cause
adverse impacts on the receiving environment.
In general, the impact level from wastewater sources generated during the operation of the two

10
     Louis Vuitton 4/21-2009

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 217
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
fishing ports, Hoang Truong and Lach Truong, is assessed as long-term, continuous, and at
Moderate level, because the fishing ports have been renovated and upgraded with integrated
functional subdivisions that take into account operational efficiency in terms of environmental
sanitation; moreover, the receiving source, the Lach Truong River, has a relatively large flow
capacity. This impact can be mitigated by appropriate measures (the fishing port will be
equipped with a separate wastewater collection system and one centralized wastewater
treatment station with a capacity of 200 m³/day-night, ensuring collection and treatment of all
wastewater generated at the port as calculated above).
c. Stormwater runoff
In addition, there is also a volume of stormwater runoff from the anchorage areas, carrying
pollutants directly into the Hoang Truong sea without passing through a treatment system. The
total stormwater runoff generated, calculated according to formulas [CT13] and [CT14], is
approximately 7,211 m³/day at subproject 1; and the total stormwater runoff generated is about
15,255 m³/day at subproject 2.
The composition of stormwater contains a high level of suspended solids, while also carrying
substances washed off from the port surface and materials spilled during operations. During
heavy and prolonged rainfall, widespread or localized flooding may occur; accumulated
pollutants may decompose, generating foul odors, creating favorable conditions for the rapid
growth of microorganisms and bacteria, and consequently leading to the proliferation of flies
and mosquitoes, which can easily result in the spread of infectious diseases. However, the
project area is enclosed by boundary walls and equipped with drainage channels around the site,
allowing water to flow from higher areas through these channels and follow the natural terrain
slope. The stormwater drainage system has been fully invested in, ensuring the collection into
the drainage network and discharge to the outside, thereby guaranteeing adequate collection
and drainage capacity for the area and preventing localized flooding. Therefore, the impact of
stormwater runoff on the Hoang Truong fishing port and the Lach Truong fishing port is
considered insignificant and controllable.
    4.5.1.4. Waste generated
The sources of solid waste generated during operation include: (i) Domestic waste from
approximately 15 staff members working at the service area and administration building, and
from 70–100 fishing vessels docking daily; (ii) Waste from the sorting and trading of seafood;
(iii) Hazardous waste. The forecasted waste generation load for the subproject 1 – Hoang
Truong fishing port and subproject 2 – Truong fishing port is as follows:
•     For domestic waste:
The waste generation rate is estimated at 0.9 kg/person/day (based on QCVN 01:2021/BXD –
National Technical Regulation on Construction Planning). However, at the project site, staff
work 8 hours/day and have one meal per shift. Therefore, the solid waste generation rate for
staff at the project is 0.3 kg/day/person. With approximately 15 staff members, the amount of
solid waste generated is 0.3 kg/person × 15 = 4.5 kg/day from the port logistics area, and from
vessels docking at the port (70–100 vessels/day, equivalent to 35–50 kg/day). Thus, the total
solid waste generated per day is about 54.5 kg/day at subproject 1 and 39.5 kg/day at subproject
2. This waste is collected daily by the local municipal solid waste collection unit. Therefore,
the impact of domestic solid waste from staff at Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports
is considered insignificant.
•     For waste from seafood classification, purchase and selling:
The seafood products spilled or discarded after the washing and freezing process for the
subproject 1 are estimated at about 0.1% (15,000 tons of seafood × 0.2% = 3 tons/year = 8.3
kg/day). This type of solid waste is rich in nutrients, therefore it decomposes easily, produces

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                           218
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
odors, and can cause serious environmental pollution. Currently, this solid waste is collected in
a designated area of the port and is locally reused by residents, such as composting for animal
feed or processing into feed for nearby aquaculture farms. Therefore, this type of waste is
considered to have no significant impact at the Hoang Truong fishing port area.
The seafood products spilled or discarded after the washing and freezing process for the
subproject 2 are estimated at about 0.1% (18,000 tons of seafood × 0.2% = 3.6 tons/year = 10
kg/day). This type of solid waste is rich in nutrients, therefore it decomposes easily, produces
odors, and can cause serious environmental pollution. Currently, this solid waste is collected in
a designated area of the port and is locally reused by residents, such as composting for animal
feed or processing into feed for nearby aquaculture farms. Therefore, this type of waste is
considered to have no significant impact at the Lach Truong fishing port area.

operation of the two fishing ports, as summarized in the following table:

ensure no environmental pollution occurs. Solid waste will be collected and transported to the
regional waste treatment plant in Hau Loc by a licensed unit with the required capacity for
transportation and treatment in accordance with regulations. Therefore, the impact of the
generated solid waste is considered insignificant.
