Impact level:
- Liquid hazardous waste: These wastes are toxic upon skin contact and harmful to the health
of staff and local residents who are directly exposed. The project’s liquid waste mainly consists
of used oil from machinery lubrication processes. These substances are highly flammable and
may easily cause fire and explosion incidents. At the same time, this type of hazardous waste
has rapid impacts on the environment through bioaccumulation, potentially hindering
photosynthesis and respiration of organisms, and even causing the death of aquatic species and
damage to the local ecosystem.
- Solid hazardous waste: These wastes have a strong impact on the environment if burned.
Unless being properly recovered, they will be released into the environment, causing soil and
water pollution.
If not collected and treated, these types of solid waste will cause air pollution (due to foul odors),
surface water pollution along the river near the port area, and negatively affecting the urban
landscape of the port area. However, since investments are being integrated in technical
infrastructure (waste collection, domestic wastewater collection and treatment, etc.), and the
locality already contracted with a sanitation team for collection, transportation, and treatment
of both general and hazardous waste, the impact from solid waste generated by the fishing port
is assessed as Low and controllable. Moreover, before the Thanh Hoa subproject comes into
operation, the Project Management Unit (PMU) will be required to obtain an environmental
permit, ensuring this impact is controllable.
 4.5.1.5. Environmental risks, incidents
       Fire and explosion incidents:
Causes: As being analyzed and forecasted above, the environment in the area contains fuel
vapors, so the potential of fire incidents is high when exposed to high heat sources or electrical
short circuits.
The power supply system for machinery, technical equipment, and electrical systems in
auxiliary facilities, cargo yards, and the administration building may experience short circuits,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                  220
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
fires, or explosions, causing damage to people and property. Additionally, the area containing
fuel vapors used for refueling vessels is prone to considerable risk of fire incidents when
exposed to high heat sources or electrical short circuits. A fire or explosion at Hoang Truong
port may cause human life and property loss. Such incidents may lead to fire spread to
warehouses, causing significant economic damage and threatening the safety of the port staff.
Affected group: People and property within the project area may be affected. However, as the
neighboring residential area is located over 150m away and separated from the port, it is less
likely to be affected.
Impact level: Assessed as Low because (i) functional areas are all designed with fire protection
measures and on-site fire-fighting equipment, such as fire extinguishers; (ii) The arrangement
of products and materials for the fishery service is allocated to specific zones with instructional
signage, and dedicated personnel responsible for management and compliance with operational
procedures; (iii) No incidents of electrical short circuits or fires have been recorded in the port
area or surrounding residential zones; (iv) Such incidents are considered controllable, as the
port management board undergoes regular trainings on fire prevention and response; and (v)
The port area is landscaped with greenery, and water supply infrastructure has been designed
with a fully equipped fire protection system. Therefore, the impact level is assessed as Medium
and controllable.
      Waterway traffic accidents:
Cause: During vessel operations, serious accidents such as shipwrecks, vessel collisions, or
fires may occur.
On average, the number of vessels entering and leaving Hoang Truong fishing port is relatively
high, with a maximum frequency of about 70–80 vessels/day; the number of vessels entering
and leaving Lach Truong fishing port is even higher, with a maximum frequency of about 100
vessels/day. When a storm hits the mainland, each port has recorded the presence of fishing
vessels operating nearshore (450 vessels), midshore (32 vessels), and offshore (103 vessels).
During the operation of ships, shipwrecks, ship collisions, or severe fire incidents may occur.
The transportation of products through the port will increase the waterway traffics in the
channel in front of the port, greatly affecting the load-bearing capacity of the channel,
obstructing visibility. Such increase in waterway traffic density is expected to lead to a higher
risk of maritime accidents and oil spills, significantly affecting surface water quality and the
ecosystem in the sea waters in Hoang Truong commune, Hau Loc commune and vicinity. In
addition, dirty water and domestic solid waste, ballast water from vessels navigating the
channels, in cases of collision where waste is not properly collected or treated may leak and
overflow into the sea and port areas. This will increase pollutant levels and negatively impact
the surrounding ecosystems along the navigation routes.
Impact scope: When there is a waterway traffic accident, depending on severities, oil spills may
occur, affecting local soil and surface water quality, causing damages to property of fishermen
and the port management authority, and economic damages to local communities who bear the
costs of recovery after such incidents.
Affected receptors: Depending on the severity, oil spills may occur, impacting the marine
environment in the Hoang Truong and Lach Truong port areas and surrounding waters;
affecting the health of people involved in waterway traffic; and causing damage to the assets of
fishermen and local communities who must deal with the consequences of such waterway
traffic accidents.
The impact level is assessed as Moderate because: (i) The design for the upgrading and
expansion of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports has been calculated based on actual
operational needs, port planning requirements, and future development trends in order to

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              221
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
consider the growth rate of offshore, midshore, and nearshore fishing vessels; (ii) port
management staff already have prior experience in port operations, therefore when operating
on an expanded and upgraded scale, there will have effective coordination and procedures in
port exploitation; (iii) there is always macro-level management and coordination from the
authorities and relevant agencies such as the Thanh Hoa Maritime Administration, ensuring that
the port can respond promptly, efficiently, and safely throughout its operation; (iv) appropriate
management measures and mitigation measures can minimize the risk of waterway accidents.
      Oil spill incidents:
For subproject 1 – Hoang Truong fishing port and subproject 2 – Lach Truong subproject, an
oil spill incident may occur due to: (i) Vessel capsizing; (ii) waterway traffic accidents; (iii)
spillage or leakage of oil and grease from fishing vessels entering or leaving the port; and (iv)
storms, winds, low pressures and other extreme weather events.
Once an oil spill occurs, an oil-water mixture is quickly formed and spread after about 3-6
hours. The oil gradually dissipates through weathering related to various of physical and
chemical processes, such as natural dispersion by wind and currents, sedimentation,
evaporation, or the formation of oil suspensions floating on the water surface. This spill will
harm the natural environment and economic activities in the area. Specifically:
      • Part of the oil products will sink and decompose on the seabed, polluting the water and
        sediment, while another part may float with air bubbles from the seabed. Oil residue
        on the seabed is a source of water pollution, affecting benthic organisms that are a food
        source of fish.
      • When the water source is contaminated with oil, the decomposition oil products reduce
        the water source’s ability to clean itself because the plankton and benthic organisms
        involved in the process die or decrease in number.
      • When oil is spilled into water sources, the level of dissolved oxygen decreases because
        oxygen is consumed during the oxidation of oil products; Oil slicks hinder the aeration
        process of the water surface.
      • In addition to affecting the aquatic environment, oil spill incidents also pose a serious
        risk of soil pollution. Even a very thin layer of spilled oil on the ground can cause the
        soil substrate and soil organisms to die.
Vessels passing through Hoang Truong fishing port have engine capacities ranging from 20 –
250 CV, with fuel storage volumes ranging between 0.964 – 12.045 tons of DO. Vessels passing
through Lach Truong fishing port have engine capacities ranging from 90 – 400 CV, with fuel
storage volumes fluctuating between 0.152 – 16.188 tons of DO.
The impact is assessed as Medium because: (i) the port area is arranged with a docking station
in the form of anchorage, suitable for each type of ship with different capacity, length as well
as purposes as required by vessel owner. The length and width of the berths are configured to
support cargo handling operations and ensure convenient access to services such as loading,
unloading, and other port-related activities for vessels arriving the port; (ii) The navigation
channel area and the turning area are designed to ensure appropriate width, channel depth,
turning radius, and maneuvering space, thereby reducing the risk of congestion, collisions, and
inconvenience during vessel berthing and service use at the port. (iii) Coordination is based on
years of experience in managing and operating the existing port, along with strict regulations
governing vessel entry and exit (iv) Shipowners who drive ship to enter and exist the port
regularly, have long practical experience, so there are insignificant risks of collision,
overturning, leakage of materials as well as adapting to changes in weather and climate in the
Hoang Truong and Lach Truong sea areas; (v) Strictly comply with port regulations and vessel
operation procedures, and equip the port with standby oil containment booms/absorbent socks
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            222
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
to enable prompt response to oil spills at the port… these measures can minimize the risks of
oil spill incidents..
To minimize and manage potential oil spill incidents, the project owner must develop a vessel
operation management plan and establish an emergency response plan for oil spills.
    ❖ Occupational accident incidents
       During the operation phase, there will be 15 operators in Hoang Truong and Lach
Truong fishing ports. Some risks and occupational accidents may occur, particularly in the
complex working environment with multiple activities such as seafood unloading,
transportation, and operation of mechanical equipment. Occupational accidents may occur
when operating Hoang Truong and Lach Truong 2 fishing ports, due to:
Accidents due to collisions and traffic accidents:
   - Risk of collision between vehicles: With the involvement of a variety of vehicles (trucks,
     boats, forklifts, cranes, etc.) in the port area, collision accidents may occur, especially
     when vessels operate under limited visibility conditions, or lack of proper separation and
     routing.
   - Road traffic accidents: Vehicles transporting seafood between ports and the mainland can
     cause traffic accidents due to poorly prepared roads or careless driving behavior.
Accidents involving mechanical equipment (cranes, forklifts, etc.):
   - Risks from lifting equipment, cranes: Using cranes and forklifts to load and unload
     seafood from ships ashore or from shore into storage warehouse can cause accidents if
     the equipment is not adequately maintained or operated incorrectly. Accidents may
     include falling heavy objects, snapped cables, or equipment malfunction.
   - Risks from electrical equipment and machinery: The operation of heavy machinery such
     as pumps, generators, or other motorized equipment may lead to electric shock, fire and
     explosion accidents if safety regulations are not observed.
Accidents due to unsafe working conditions:
   - Falls, trips, slips: Due to continuous movement on wet and slippery surfaces (caused by
     grease, seawater, or seafood waste), workers engaging in unloading and storage areas are
     at risk of slipping, falling.
Fire and explosion accidents:
   - Risk of fire and explosion from flammable materials: Substances such as oils, fuels,
     chemicals used in seafood processing or storage may lead to fire and explosion unless
     being handled and stored properly. Additionally, the use of electrical and mechanical
     appliances may result in electrical shorts or sparks, increasing the risk of fire.
   - Fire due to improper discharge: Flammable wastes such as oil, grease from boats, cranes,
     or from seafood processing may cause fires if not collected and disposed of properly.
Health-related accidents and illnesses:
   - Risk of infectious diseases: Working in humid environments and exposure to seawater
     can lead to infectious diseases, especially those related to the respiratory tract, skin, or
     waterborne diseases.
   - Respiratory diseases: Long-term exposure to dust from seafood or emissions from ships
     can cause respiratory diseases such as pneumonia, bronchitis, or other chronic diseases.
   - Diseases caused by marine life: Exposure to marine life can lead to injuries, infections,
     or poisoning due to toxins from seafood or other marine life.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            223
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Accidents caused by environmental factors:
   - Risks due to extreme weather conditions: Weather conditions such as heavy rain, storms,
     and strong winds can increase the risk of accidents for workers working outdoors,
     especially those working on docks.
   - Risks from sea waves and surge: Working in conditions of high seawater or strong waves
     may expose workers to risks of being swept away or encountering accidents while
     operating on vessels or at the dock or piers.
       In addition, other risks related to occupational accidents may occur due to working
environment at the port:
   - Fish holds containing miscellaneous fish which are sorted for long period at sea will
     generate toxic gases. The deeper the fish holds and the longer the voyage, the higher the
     risk of oxygen deprivation for fishermen working in the fish holds, leading to acute
     poisoning, fainting, or even death.
   - In the receiving and sorting areas where washing activities are frequently carried out and
     seafood is handled, the floor is always wet, increasing the risk of slips and falls during
     the lifting and transporting of seafood containers and trolleys.
   - Slip-and-fall accidents during the loading and unloading of seafood from vessels to the
     port, including incidents where seafood containers fall onto workers.
The risk level of occupational accidents at the port is assessed as Medium because: (i) these
impacts are always potential to occur on the port at any time; (ii) The number of operation
workers is relatively small (15 people/port); (iii) these risks may persist in extended period
during the fishing port operation (iv) They can be mitigated through appropriate measures, such
as occupational safety training before and during port operations and providing sufficient
personal protective equipment (PPE) for all workers
    ❖ Incidents of vessels docking beyond the port’s design capacity
        When the Class II fishing port, Class III facility, comes into operation, incidents may
occur in which vessels docking exceed the designed capacity, leading to the following potential
impacts:
   - Vessels waiting for their turn to unload may experience long delay, potentially resulting
     in disputes over docking priority and causing disorder.
   - Although, conventionally, docking is organized in sequence, some ship owners may
     attempt to push ahead, or maneuver around others to dock or leave the port, leading to
     traffic safety issues and negatively affecting the port’s loading and unloading capacity.
   - Wastewater and solid waste generated in excess of the subproject’s treatment capacity of
     the designed waste treatment plant (wastewater treatment plant, waste storage facilities).
Impact Assessment: This impact is considered to be Low, as the port facilities have been
designed and calculated in accordance with technical standards to ensure operational
sustainability However, if such incidents persist over an extended period, they may pose risks
of port infrastructure degradation, potentially leading to damage to docking facilities and
resulting in certain losses. This impact can be mitigated by appropriate port operation
management measures.
    ❖ Incidents of water supply and drainage pipelines broken and damage to wastewater
      treatment systems
During the operation, water supply and drainage pipeline and wastewater treatment systems
may encounter into the following incidents:

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                          224
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    - Breakage or cracking of water supply pipelines due to unsafe construction materials.
    - Water pipeline broken due to external force.
    - Failures of the wastewater treatment system, such as malfunctioning treatment equipment,
      leading to untreated or inadequately treated wastewater being discharged into the
      environment.
Such incidents, when occurring, will lead to water wastage (for clean water supply) and discharge
of environmental pollutants (for wastewater). However, the likelihood of these impacts is
considered low, as the water supply network is installed in technical compartment, and water
supply areas are equipped with a pressurized valve system to detect leaks in the pipeline system.
When the wastewater treatment system is broken, the level of air and water pollution will
increase as assessed in the above sections.
In addition, the materials used for pipelines are fire-resistant, corrosion-resistant, and highly
durable, resulting in a very low risk of breakage or leakage. Wastewater treatment plant are
designed with independent operating modules, with backup system, and equalization tanks that
can be stored for up to 24 hours when all modules fail. Therefore, the impact from these incidents
is minor and can be controlled through appropriate measures.
✓ Risks and incidents due to improper operation of wastewater treatment plant
In addition to risks from vehicles and equipment operating wastewater treatment plant, there
are also risks stemming from humans and funding sources in the operation and maintenance of
wastewater treatment plant. These risks include but are not limited to:
-   The wastewater treatment plant operators’ failure to comply with the operating process
    instructions that have been developed for the wastewater treatment plant.
-   The operators lack sufficient knowledge and qualifications to operate the WWTP.
-   Poor capacity of human resource management during the operation of the WWTP.
-   Infrequently periodic maintenance, replacement or repair of damaged machinery and
    vehicles.
-   Improper frequency and volume of sludge collection, which may result in reduced treatment
    efficiency of the WWTP and failure to meet current national technical standards.
The above risks, if occurring, will reduce the wastewater treatment efficiency of the wastewater
treatment and affect the quality of receiving water sources and the aquatic ecosystem in the port
area. However, the risk is assessed as “Low” and can be minimized: (i) The operation of the
WWTP is carried out by engineers who are knowledgeable and qualified in wastewater
treatment technology; (ii) The operation will be arranged in shifts and always have staff on
duty, operating in accordance with the regulated instructions; (iii) The WWTP is periodically
inspected and maintained daily or immediately when an incident occurs; (iv) Replacement of
machinery and means are always available in case of damages. (v) At the WWTP, an
emergency pond is provided as a backup in case the WWTP malfunctions or becomes
inoperable
Furthermore, before the wastewater treatment plant is officially put into operation, the
subproject must register for trial operation and apply for an environmental license.
✓ Risk of falling into the river and drowning
These cases are warned when going up or down the docks or when colliding with ships, people
on board often lose their balance and may fall/fall/slide into the river. Although berths are often
designed with barriers and warning signs, fishermen/boat drivers are at risk of accidents falling
into the river due to slippery and drowning or in the rainy and flood season, traveling on

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                              225
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
boats/ships that are prone to slipping and slipping into the river. This affects the health and even
the lives of participants, especially fishermen on the anchorage area. The design needs to be
done to prevent threats to fishermen when using works at the port. Therefore, this impact is
Low and can be minimized
✓ Quality of supply water in the Subproject 2 – Lach Truong fishing port
Upgrading, expanding the Bai Trung clean water supply plant at Lach Truong fishing port to a
capacity of 1,000 m³/day-night. The plant sources raw water from the upgraded raw water
supply facility located west of the De canal, drawing water from the De canal in Phu Loc
commune, approximately 500 m upstream of the salinity control sluice and about 4.25 km from
the water treatment site. The intake pipeline conveys water to the elevated water tower at Hoa
Loc fishing port.
Risk of raw water contamination: During the extraction process, the raw water source from the
De canal may be contaminated by external factors. Therefore, raw water should be monitored
and tested on a shift/daily basis to ensure that the quality of the intake water meets the
requirements of the designed treatment technology.
The lamella settling and filtration technology with an automatic backwash filter is applied.
Treated water will be supplied as domestic water for Lach Truong fishing port. A plan for
regular and periodic monitoring must be implemented to adjust chemical dosing and treatment
processes when necessary, ensuring that the supplied water meets the standards for domestic
water quality in accordance with QCVN 01-1:2024/BYT – National Technical Regulation on
clean water quality for domestic use.
Affected receptors: Staff and workers employed at the port, as well as customers, vessel owners,
and crew members docking and unloading at the Lach Truong fishing port.
The level of impact is assessed as moderate with long-term effects; there are risks of
contamination of the raw water source as well as risks that treated water may not meet the
requirements of QCVN 01-1:2024/BYT – National Technical Regulation on Clean Water
Quality for Domestic Use. However, regular inspection and protection of the raw water source
in the De canal, combined with water quality monitoring and adjustment of treatment processes,
can effectively mitigate these impacts.
 4.5.1.6. Disturbance to road traffics
At subproject 1– Hoang Truong fishing port:
When the project comes into operation, it will handle a large volume of aquatic products
through the port, which will affect road traffic activities. The transportation of seafood from the
port to consumption areas within the province, especially during peak hours, may cause
localized congestion along major transport routes such as NH1A, PR510, and the Hoang Truong
– Hoang Yen inter-communal road. This situation could disrupt the daily traffic of local
residents in Hoang Truong commune and surrounding areas, and may also lead to traffic
accidents, posing risks to people living along the routes as well as road users.
Affected receptors: Road transport vehicle owners (cars, motorcycles, bicycles, handcarts, etc.),
residents living along the transportation routes, and road users traveling on these routes.
The level of impact is assessed as low because: (i) fishing vessels usually return in the early
morning or early afternoon, so the transportation, sorting, and distribution of products do not
coincide with local commuting hours; traffic volume only increases during off-peak times, (ii)
the sorting is carried out directly at the fishing port and organized before vehicles arrive, thereby
reducing congestion and waiting time for cargo loading, (iii) coastal residents, who have long
been engaged in traditional fishing, possess extensive experience in coordinating product
handling and preservation at appropriate times for transportation, (iv) the transportation routes

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                226
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
are clear and convenient, allowing smooth circulation to markets, collection centers, and
consumption areas both within and outside the province. Therefore, the impacts on traffic
congestion or accidents are considered low and can be minimized through appropriate
management measures.
At subproject 2–Lach Truong fishing port:
When the project comes into operation, it will handle a large volume of products through the
port, which will affect road traffic activities. The demand for parking spaces specifically for
seafood loading vehicles from the port or fish market is calculated based on 2-5 ton trucks,
transporting 100% of the seafood volume through the port. The estimated demand for vehicles
passing through Lach Truong fishing port is approximately 18 trucks/day.
The transportation of seafood from the port to consumption areas within the province, especially
during peak hours, may cause localized congestion along transport routes leading to Hoa Loc
fish market, Hoa Loc industrial cluster, Minh Loc fish market, and local seafood restaurants.
This situation could disrupt the traffic activities of residents in Hoa Loc commune and
surrounding areas, and may also result in traffic accidents, posing risks to people living along
the transportation routes as well as road users.
Affected receptors: Road transport vehicle owners (cars, motorcycles, bicycles, handcarts, etc.),
residents living along the transportation routes, and road users traveling on these routes.
The level of impact is assessed as low because: (i) fishing vessels usually return in the early
morning or early afternoon, so the transportation, sorting, and distribution of products do not
coincide with local commuting hours; traffic volume only increases during off-peak times, (ii)
the sorting is carried out directly at the fishing port and organized before vehicles arrive, thereby
reducing congestion and waiting time for cargo loading, (iii) coastal residents, who have long
been engaged in traditional fishing, possess extensive experience in coordinating product
handling and preservation at appropriate times for transportation, (iv) the transportation routes
are clear and convenient, allowing smooth circulation to markets, collection centers such as
Hoa Loc fish market, Hoa Loc industrial cluster, local restaurants both within and outside the
province. Therefore, the impacts on traffic congestion or accidents are considered low and can
be minimized through appropriate management measures.
 4.5.1.7. Disturbance, impacts on aquatic species and reduction of surface water of river,
 canal due to concentration of vessels at the port
The quality of surface water in the Lach Truong River and De canal may deteriorate due to the
operation of vessels entering and leaving the port and the storm shelter area during the
operational phase (approximately 70–100 vessels/day and about 400 vessels sheltering in the
De canal) due to: (i) leakage of oil and grease caused by accidents such as vessel collisions or
grounding, or by the discharge of oily residues; (ii) ballast water mixed with oil and engine
cooling water containing oil and grease, along with domestic wastewater, solid waste, and
hazardous/toxic waste (as previously forecasted and assessed in the sections on “wastewater”
and “solid waste” generation).
The surface water quality of Lach Truong River and De canal is degraded due to increased
turbidity, higher concentrations of suspended solids, and traces of oil and grease, leading to a
reduction in dissolved oxygen levels and a decrease in the photosynthetic capacity of algae and
aquatic plants.
In general, the impact on the surface water quality of the Lach Truong River and De canal from
the operation of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports is long-term and continuous but
at a low level because: (i) Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports have been upgraded
and modernized, and managed by the existing port management board, which has prior
experience, ensuring that the activities of vessels entering and leaving the ports are

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                227
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
environmentally controlled; (ii) the receiving capacity of the river/canal is relatively large. This
impact can be mitigated through appropriate measures (the fishing ports are equipped with a
separate wastewater collection system and a WWTP with a capacity of 200 m³/day-night,
ensuring that all generated wastewater is collected and treated before being discharged into the
receiving sources — the Lach Truong River and De canal).
The impact on the aquatic ecosystem of the Lach Truong River and De canal from the operation
of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing ports is long-term and continuous but assessed at a
Low level because: (i) the port operation area is localized only to the waters in front of the
berths (subproject 1: 10 m wide, with a total berth length of about 600 m; and subproject: 12 m
wide, with a total berth length of about 200 m); (ii) there are no rare, endangered, or threatened
aquatic species in the Lach Truong River and De canal area: plankton in the area mainly consist
of copepods, cladocerans, and rotifers; benthic organisms mainly include snails, mussels, clams
and; aquatic plants are mainly water hyacinth and algae. At the same time, the project area is
not a habitat for any protected or high-value species. This impact can be mitigated through
appropriate measures.
4.5.2. Impacts and risks during operation phase of subprojects 3,4,5,6,7,8,9
Impacts in operation phase of these 7 subprojects are mainly caused by:
    -   Subsidence and erosion of canal/irrigation routes after construction: Landslide incidents
        along canal routes are unavoidable; however, the severity depends on external impacts
        as well as the maintenance and repair process. Some impacts may also occur due to
        malicious acts, intentionally damaging public property for personal purposes, though
        such cases are not common. Regular inspection and monitoring of the canals, together
        with proper implementation of periodic maintenance plans and installation of warning
        signs at hydraulic works, will enhance the effectiveness and sustainability of these
        structures.
    -   Cracking and damages to surface of the operation management road may occur due to
        overloading during transportation, or fractures and cracks caused by geological changes,
        climate change, or other factors. These issues can affect the quality of the road surface
        and pose risks to users. Records of current conditions, geological and topographical data
        series, as well as consultations with local authorities and residents, have been
        incorporated into the approved design options. Regular monitoring and coordination
        with villages, hamlets, and local authorities are required to promptly address any
        incidents, ensuring the quality of the infrastructure in the aquaculture area to support
        socio-economic development of the local community. Therefore, these impacts are
        assessed as MINOR; if they occur, they may affect residents using the operation
        management road and services supporting the aquaculture area inside the canal, but the
        level of impact is not significant. Such impacts can be avoided through measures applied
        from the design stage, during management and operation monitoring of the dike road,
        and can be mitigated by technical solutions such as repairing and upgrading sections
        where fractures or cracks occur during the operational phase.
4.6.    INDUCED IMPACTS
Induced impacts are environmental changes that do not occur directly from the project itself,
but are the result of changes triggered by the project. In other words, these are indirect impacts,
not the immediate outcome of project activities, but the consequence of subsequent changes,
often in the form of chain reactions, that the project generates.
Overall, the project is expected to generate primarily positive induced impacts. (i) The
investment in the “Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province” is highly
necessary to effectively utilize the region’s natural resources, climate, and water conditions to

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               228
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
support agricultural production and broader socio-economic development. (ii) The project
contributes to improving both the material and spiritual well-being of local residents, fulfilling
the aspirations of the people and local authorities. (iii) The investment ensures alignment with
the district’s long-term socio-economic development plans, while delivering high economic
efficiency and positive social outcomes for the beneficiary communities. (iv) The
implementation of the “Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province” will
enhance crop productivity, increase farmers’ income, improve water drainage during the rainy
season, and contribute to poverty reduction, rural development, regional economic growth, and
national defense and security. Therefore, there will be no negative induced impacts, so they are
not mentioned in this report.
4.7.    CUMMULATIVE IMPACTS
Cumulative impacts are the incremental impacts of the project when combined with other
related past, present, and reasonably foreseeable future development activities, including
unplanned but predictable activities that may occur later or in different locations. This
assessment aims to identify Environmental and Social (E&S) risks arising from overlaps,
synergies, or interactions between subprojects under the SFDP and other ongoing projects in
the region.
4.7.1. Ground and scope of the cumulative impact assessment
Spatial and temporal boundaries of the cumulative impact assessment
Spatial boundary. The study area of these cumulative covers 8 implementation area under 9
subprojects, because the 8 implementation areas are located in 07 communes — Hoang Tien,
Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang Chinh — in Thanh Hoa
Province.
Temporal boundary.
The temporal boundaries defined for this Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) include: (i)
Construction phase – lasting 24 months, starting in 2026 and continuing through 2028. This
phase temporarily covers all construction-related activities, including site preparation,
excavation and dredging, construction of port infrastructure, and transportation of materials and
waste to designated storage/disposal areas; and (ii) Operational phase, commencing in 2028,
when the fishing port components, storm shelter area, canal systems, and operation
management roads are put into use.
The project’s pre-construction phase is not considered in the CIA, as no impacts are anticipated.
4.7.2. Identification of Valued Environmental Components (VECs), related projects, and
natural factors affecting these components
1.7.2. Identification of Valued Environmental Components (VECs)
This ESIA considers whether the construction and operation of the project may contribute to
cumulative impacts on valued environmental and social components (VECs) that may also be
adversely affected by other current or future developments in or near the project area.
The identification of VECs in the CIA is based on the technical characteristics of the
subprojects, the ecological and social sensitivity of the area, and the potential for interaction
with other development activities. VECs are categorized by phase to ensure a comprehensive
assessment of potential cumulative risks.
These VECs are also used as the basis for analyzing specific cumulative impacts in later
sections of the report.
+ Construction phase:


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                             229
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    (i) Air quality (Total Suspended Particulates – TSP): Dust and emissions from construction
        may combine with other emission sources in the area, affecting the environment and
        public health;
    (ii) Road and waterway traffic safety: Transporting materials by road and water increases
        vehicle and vessel density, creating a risk of collisions if not well coordinated.
    (iii) Social impacts in construction areas: Temporary labor and pressure on social
        infrastructure may accumulate if construction occurs near residential areas or other
        projects.
+ Operation phase: The main VECs assessed in the operation phase include:
    (i) Surface water quality in rivers/seas at the construction areas of fishing ports and storm
        shelter anchorage under 09 subprojects: Surface water quality in rivers/seas at fishing
        ports and anchorages, and aquatic life: Wastewater, solid waste, and oil leaks from
        vessels may affect estuaries, coral reefs, seagrass beds, and aquaculture zones;
    (ii) Waterway traffic safety – Subproject 1,2: Vessel density increases during peak fishing
        seasons or storms, posing risks of collisions and congestion in waterways;
    (iii) Coastal livelihoods and social infrastructure: Changes in production models, labor
        migration, and resource-use conflicts may put pressure on coastal communities.
Factors affecting VECs
During the construction and development process, the project’s fishing port areas are
undergoing rapid urbanization. This has revealed several limitations of the existing ports, such
as a lack of integration and inadequate management mechanisms for fisheries-related
infrastructure, supply services, and processing facilities. These ports are currently overloaded,
posing risks of environmental pollution and making it difficult to meet the standards required
for sustainable fishery development, especially in response to export demands and international
integration.
The investment in infrastructure projects is being implemented based on the spatial urban
development planning of the project areas. Among the plans, projects, and development activities
of Thanh Hoa Province, several will be carried out within the project zones — specifically in the
seven communes of Hoang Tien, Hoang Chau, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Hoa Loc, Van Loc, and Quang
Chinh — and are scheduled to be implemented or operated within the temporal boundaries
defined for this Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA). These projects share overlapping areas of
influence with the current project and therefore will contribute to cumulative impacts. It is
anticipated that during the construction phase, no cumulative impacts will occur; cumulative
impacts will be assessed during the operational phase.
Each subproject within the project listed above includes a number of investment items. This
EIA only considers those factors that contribute to cumulative impacts within the spatial and
temporal boundaries defined for this EIA. Below is a brief description of the relevant factors of
each project involved.
Factors affecting VECs: including (i) natural factors and (ii) development factors.
(i) Natural factors: Strong winds, heavy rainfall during storms, and tidal surges are identified
as natural factors that may affect the previously identified Valued Environmental Components
(VECs).
   - Wind. Prevailing wind directions in the project area vary seasonally. The average wind
     speed ranges from 1.0 to 1.5 m/s. Strong winds can promote the dispersion of dust in the
     air, expanding the areas affected by dust from construction sites and active roads. This


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            230
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
      potential impact mainly occurs during the dry season, from December to March of the
      following year, and affects coastal and riverside areas.
   - Storm These events typically occur from May to October of the following year, with the
     heaviest rainfall in September. Heavy rain and strong winds during storms can
     significantly increase the risk of unsafe waterway transportation due to reduced visibility,
     slippery surfaces, and the potential for large objects to be blown away.
   - On the other hand, abnormal heavy rainfall may increase the risk of erosion in coastal and
     estuarine areas, leading to elevated water turbidity, which negatively affects both natural
     aquatic organisms and aquaculture activities in rivers and canal systems. The respiratory
     capacity of certain aquatic species may be reduced due to increased turbidity caused by
     surface runoff. Aquatic organisms in the water bodies within the project area are expected
     to be most affected by natural stressors occurring in the region.
(ii) Development factors: Other planning schemes, development projects, and activities in the
region — such as urbanization, coastal tourism, industrial development, and aquaculture —
may generate synergistic or conflicting impacts with the subprojects.
4.7.3. Current status of Valued Environmental Components
Air quality. The ESIA for the Thanh Hoa SFDP project reports that current air quality in the
project area is relatively good, with no signs of pollution. All measured indicators are below
the limits set in the National technical regulation on ambient air quality QCVN
05:2023/BTNMT and the WHO Ambient Air Quality Guidelines (which are stricter than
QCVN 05).
Waterway traffic safety: Although consultations have been conducted with relevant
stakeholders such as Hoang Truong Border Guard Station — the unit responsible for managing
and supervising fishing vessel activities in the area — the existing fishing port management
board, and local fisheries communities, the information on traffic safety is limited in the existing
documents of the above-mentioned projects. Therefore, water traffic density and vessel types
are considered as additional indicators to assess risks related to waterway traffic safety.
Relevant plannings and projects
The related projects within the same geographical region with the subprojects have been
considered for cumulative impact assessment and are presented in the table below. Among the
eight subprojects, three have associated projects, specifically: (i) subproject 2 – Transportation
Road connecting Bim Son Industrial Zone to the coastal road segment from Nga Son to Hoang
Hoa, Thanh Hoa Province; (ii) Project 8 – Industrial cluster project at Hoa Loc fishing port; (iii)
Subproject 9 – High-tech whiteleg shrimp farming project.
These associated projects have either been completed and are in operation, or is under
construction prior to 2026 — the scheduled start of construction for the AoIs. Therefore, these
related projects are not expected to generate synergistic, conflicting, or cumulative impacts
during the construction and operational phases of the current project.
4.7.4. Assessment of cumulative impact during operation phase
The simultaneous implementation of subprojects during the 2026–2028 period brings about
significant positive cumulative impacts at both the provincial and regional levels. The
development of modern and well-integrated fishing ports and storm shelter areas, the dredging
of canal systems, and the completion of operation management roads will enhance the
efficiency of marine resource exploitation, reduce post-harvest losses, support offshore fishing
activities, and promote the stable development of aquaculture zones. In addition, the project
will strengthen fisheries inspection capacity and establish technical infrastructure to protect
aquatic resources, thereby stimulating economic growth, creating employment opportunities,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               231
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
and improving fisheries management capacity in coastal and aquaculture-based localities. The
coordinated investment in fishing port areas will contribute to the formation of an inter-regional
fishing port network, supporting value chain-based production and enhancing climate change
resilience.
In addition to the aforementioned benefits, the simultaneous implementation of subprojects also
presents potential cumulative impacts that must be identified and managed. During the
construction phase, (i) the total emissions of dust, gases, and noise may increase significantly at
the project-wide scale, especially if dredging, material transportation, and construction activities
are conducted concurrently across multiple coastal areas, affecting health of local people and
water environmental. (ii) Degradation of water and soil quality due to excavation and
earthworks, affecting the ecosystems of rivers, coastal areas, canal systems, and aquaculture
zones; (iii) Increased risk of landslides, subsidence, and erosion, especially during construction
in the rainy and flood season; (iv) Obstruction of transportation- particularly waterway traffic -
as well as disruption to local residents’ daily activities and production, including impacts on
aquaculture operations in active farming zones due to construction near these areas; (v) Pressure
on the supply chain for materials, equipment, and technical personnel may also increase,
potentially affecting the project’s implementation schedule and overall efficiency.
However, due to the geographical separation of subprojects, with no direct hydrological
connections or shared transportation routes, the spatial extent of cumulative impacts is limited.
These impacts are primarily localized and can be effectively managed through decentralized
local governance. The overall cumulative impact level is assessed as low, but it should be
monitored and coordinated appropriately to ensure the long-term sustainability of the project.
Infrastructure investments under each subproject have been developed in accordance with the
respective locality’s urban spatial and sectoral development plans. Within the scope of this
Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA), several other local projects, plans, and development
activities have already been, are currently being, or will be implemented within the same area
and timeframe as the SFDP subprojects.
These projects may overlap in area of influence or have interactive impacts with the subprojects
and therefore must be considered in the cumulative impact assessment. A list of related projects
in the seven provinces/cities where the subprojects are being implemented is presented in the
Table below, together with a brief assessment of the potential cumulative impacts.

These impacts are forecasted and not precisely defined at the time of assessment but should be
considered in environmental management and construction coordination. The main potential
impact categories include:
Impacts on air quality
Air pollution in the subproject areas is expected to be cumulatively affected by various other
development activities in the region, including tourism, urban expansion, and industrial projects
— whether already constructed or planned for the coming years. Regardless of their specific
locations within the project area, dust and emissions generated from earthworks, machinery
operation, and vehicle movement associated with these projects may contribute to elevated
levels of particulate matter and toxic gases in the subproject zones, especially if dust and
emission control measures are not effectively implemented in accordance with standards. As a
result, air quality across the nine subproject areas (during the 2026–2028 construction phase) is
likely to be impacted by dust and emissions from concurrent construction activities of other
projects in the vicinity.
However, baseline air quality data in the subproject areas indicate that air quality is currently
good, and monitored parameters are well below regulatory limits. Therefore, cumulative
impacts on air quality are assessed as low and manageable through the application of mitigation
measures in the ESMP.
Cumulative impacts on waterway traffic safety
Cumulative impacts may arise when waterway transportation activities from other regional
projects — such as seaports, industrial zones, riverside tourism developments, or technical
infrastructure works — occur simultaneously or close to the construction period of the
Subprojects. These risks are particularly heightened during peak periods, such as the fishing
season, storm events, or when vessels gather for shelter anchorage, increasing the likelihood of
cumulative effects. The transportation of construction materials will lead to a rise in vessel

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                 233
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
traffic, resulting in greater environmental and social impacts, including dust and gas emissions,
noise and vibration, and an increased risk of accidents and congestion on waterways. These
factors may negatively affect fishermen’s health, surface water quality, and aquatic life in the
Lach Truong River, De Canal, and Hoang Truong coastal waters, which are used for
transportation. In the subproject 1, 2 areas, there are from 70 to 100 boats per day, with port
capacity accommodating vessels of 24–30 meters in length. The annual seafood throughput at
these ports reaches 15,000–18,000 tons, and vessel density is particularly high during shore-
landing periods, storm sheltering, and the fishing season (from late April to September), which
accounts for up to 60% of the annual catch volume. Therefore, the implementation of marine
safety measures for vessels involved in construction and transportation, along with coordinated
waterway traffic management and strict adherence to construction safety protocols, will help
minimize cumulative impacts.
Social impacts:
Some subprojects may face labor pressure, social infrastructure constraints, or land-use
conflicts if religious, cultural, or technical infrastructure projects are implemented nearby.
These factors should be monitored to ensure community safety and welfare.
Overall, cumulative impacts during construction at each subproject are expected to be low to
moderate, localized, and manageable through schedule management, local coordination, and
mitigation measures under the ESMP.
4.7.5. Assessment of cumulative impacts during the operation phase
(1) Upgrade fishing ports and establish an integrated marine infrastructure system associated
with supporting industries and logistics services to enhance the efficiency of seafood
exploitation, reduce post-harvest losses, and develop modern, regional-scale storm shelter areas
for fishing vessels. These improvements will support offshore fishing, increase harvesting
efficiency, reduce post-planning losses, contribute to maritime sovereignty protection, and help
lift the European Commission’s IUU yellow card. (2) Develop infrastructure and equipment for
the fisheries inspection sector for strengthen effective and sustainable exploitation and
protection of marine resources. (3) Boost management capacity for production organization to
combat Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) fishing, and promote sustainable fishery
resource management. (4) Reduce post-harvest losses, minimizing disease outbreaks,
improving productivity, quality, and value of farmed shrimp products, while protect the
environment and enhance climate change resilience. (5) Enhance fisheries sector governance
capacity. (6) Promote regional and international cooperation and development in the fisheries
sector.
In operation phase, Once the fishing ports and storm shelter areas become operational, the
number of fishing vessels, logistics services, and fisheries inspection activities will increase in
each locality. During peak periods, such as the fishing season or storm events, vessels from
neighboring regions may converge at shelter points, leading to higher vessel density, increased
risk of waterway congestion, vessel collisions, and deterioration of water quality due to oil spills
and waste discharge.
Besides technical related impacts: (i) Conflicts in coastal resource use may arise between
sectors such as fisheries, tourism, industry, and ecological conservation. (ii) Pressure on social
infrastructure, with rising long-term operational and maintenance costs for transportation, water
supply, fishermen’s livelihoods and waste treatment, especially if infrastructure becomes
outdated or is not properly maintained, leading to sharply increased repair costs over time. (iii)
In the context of climate change, coastal Subprojects will face growing risks such as sea level
rise, saltwater intrusion, and increased frequency of severe storms, which may affect storm
shelter effectiveness, freshwater availability, aquaculture zones, and fishing activities. (iv)
Environmental pressure from expanded farming areas and intensified long-term production

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               234
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
may lead to water pollution and biodiversity loss if not properly managed. (v) Higher risk of
aquatic disease outbreaks due to high stocking densities and inter-regional movement, which
can facilitate the spread of pathogens if not tightly controlled. (vi) Land and water resource
conflicts may emerge from competing demands among aquaculture, agriculture, domestic use,
and conservation. (vii) Dependence on a single production model, where farming areas rely on
one or a few species or a specific market, increases vulnerability to market fluctuations or
disease outbreaks.
The Subprojects are distributed across various communes in Thanh Hoa Province, which share
similar ecological, hydrological, and waterway transport characteristics. As a result, while
spatial cumulative impacts among the Subprojects are dispersed, they collectively form a clear
inter-regional synergy, generating notable positive cumulative effects, including: (i) Improved
and more stable aquaculture productivity and output, supported by enhanced infrastructure and
effective environmental controls, leading to sustained and improved yields across production
cycles. (ii) Local economic development, through job creation, increased household income,
and stimulation of supporting services such as logistics, processing, and trade. (iii) Enhanced
living conditions and social welfare, with improved transportation, water supply and drainage
systems, electricity, and telecommunications infrastructure, contributing to better quality of life
for local communities. (iv) Increased investment potential, as reliable infrastructure attracts
interest from domestic and international enterprises, boosting capital inflows and facilitating
technology transfer. (v) Strengthened climate change resilience, through infrastructure designed
to adapt to extreme weather events and sea level rise.
However, the upgrading of fishing ports and storm shelter areas will lead to an increase in
waterway traffic density, potentially introducing risks to waterway safety and declines in water
quality. Aquatic species may be affected if surface water quality in rivers and coastal areas
deteriorates due to oil leaks and waste discharge from vessels operating along navigation routes
during the operational phase. These impacts are assessed as low and can be mitigated, with
specific measures outlined below:
Increased waterway traffic density and marine unsafety risks in subproject 1, 2 areas
During the operational phase, once the fishing ports and storm shelter areas are completed and
put into use, they are expected to stimulate the development of fisheries, urban areas, tourism,
and industry, leading to a gradual increase in waterway traffic volume. During peak periods,
such as the fishing season or storm events, vessels from neighboring regions may converge at
the ports, resulting in higher vessel density along navigation channels. The peak cargo
throughput period at the ports is forecasted to occur annually between May and September,
accounting for approximately 12% per month over five months. Waterway traffic density in the
Subproject areas during peak days and months has been forecasted as part of the SFDP
feasibility study, with detailed statistical timelines to be supplemented as data becomes
available.
This cumulative impact may lead to congestion, vessel collisions, and an increased risk of
maritime accidents if an effective waterway traffic coordination system is not in place. The
impact is assessed as low to moderate and can be mitigated through appropriate monitoring,
guidance, and traffic regulation measures, especially during periods of high docking demand,
such as storm events when vessels seek shelter anchorage.
Increased pressure on road traffics and coastal technical infrastructure – subproject 1, 2
areas
The transportation of fish, seafood, and logistics supplies from the ports to processing facilities,
wholesale markets, or distribution centers will lead to increased road traffic density in port areas.
The continuous operation of trucks, refrigerated vehicles, and personal vehicle may cause
localized congestion, air pollution, and noise disturbances, particularly in Subproject 1, 2 areas,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                235
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
when passing near residential areas. This impact may become cumulative over time if seafood
throughput at the ports increases rapidly without corresponding infrastructure upgrades.
However, the impact is assessed as minor, given that seafood arrivals at the ports are seasonal
rather than year-round, and can be effectively managed through the implementation of
mitigation measures outlined in the ESMP.
Increased amount of wastewater and coastal waste in subproject 1, 2 areas
The operation of fishing ports will generate wastewater from activities such as fish washing,
ice production, preliminary processing, and daily activities by workers at Hoang Truong Fishing
Port – subprojec1 and Lach Truong Fishing Port – subproject 2. Without an effective
wastewater treatment system, these discharges may cause localized pollution, affecting surface
water quality and aquatic organisms. In addition, solid waste such as plastic packaging, foam
boxes, damaged nets, and discarded fishing gear may accumulate in port areas and drift into the
sea, leading to degradation of coastal habitats. These impacts tend to be cumulative over time
if not regularly managed. However, the impact is assessed as minor, as wastewater generated
during port operations will be collected and treated through the Subproject’s wastewater
treatment systems, which comply with permitted environmental standards prior to discharge.
Once the fishing ports are fully operational and properly managed, they will ensure the
collection and treatment of solid waste, maintaining port beautiful landscape and space to
support vessel docking and departure activities.
Conflicts of coastal resources use and social impacts
The operation of Hoang Truong and Lach Truong fishing port at subproject 1, 2 areas may lead
to increased spatial competition among sectors such as fisheries, coastal tourism, industrial
development, and ecological conservation. Certain areas may face conflicts between fishing
activities and coastal tourism development. Additionally, there may be heightened pressure on
social infrastructure, including transportation, water supply, waste management, and maritime
safety, particularly during peak seasons. The modernization of fishing ports and enhanced IUU
(Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated fishing) control may also affect the livelihoods of small-
scale fishermen, potentially leading to shifts in traditional production models, labor migration,
or changes in community structures. Overall, resource-use conflicts and associated social
impacts related to port operations in subproject 1, 2 are expected to be localized, temporary,
and limited in scope. These impacts can be effectively managed and mitigated through: Rational
coastal spatial planning; intersectoral coordination mechanisms, upgrades to social
infrastructure, livelihood support policies for fishing communities. Therefore, the impact level
is assessed as minor, with no significant negative effects on regional socioeconomic
development or coastal environmental conservation.
Risks of climate changes on the operation of fishing port and aquaculture area
Coastal Subprojects 1, 2, along with aquaculture areas (subproject 3 through 9) will face
increasing risks from climate change, including: (i) Sea level rise, which may inundate port
areas or reduce the effectiveness of storm shelter infrastructure. (ii) Saltwater intrusion,
affecting both domestic water supplies and aquaculture water sources in subproject 3 through
9. (iii) Higher frequency and intensity of storms, disrupting fishing operations and port activities
at Hoang Truong (subproject 1) and Lach Truong (subproject 2). Without adaptive design
features and flexible operational planning, these impacts may accumulate over time, affecting
the long-term sustainability of each subproject. Although climate change poses significant risks
to the operation of coastal fishing ports and aquaculture zones, these impacts are predictable
and can be integrated into infrastructure planning and design, and mitigated through appropriate
technical and management solutions. Therefore, the impact level is assessed as minor, with no
significant threat to the overall sustainability of the project, provided that necessary adaptation
measures are implemented.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               236
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Degradation of water quality and impacts on aquatic life
The ESIA for the SFDP Thanh Hoa estimates that, following dredging activities, it will take
approximately 12 to 24 months for river and seabed sediments to recover. However, dredging
is not expected to reduce aquatic biodiversity in riverine or marine environments. On the
contrary, it is anticipated that species composition may increase, as connectivity with estuarine
and coastal systems is enhanced through improved navigational channels.
During the operation phase, fishing port and shelter area are the places where seafood
exploitation, primarily processing, storage and transactions occur. These activities generate
wastewater with high content of organic matter, grease, oil and other pollutants from fish
washing, ice production, vessel cleaning and domestic activities of workers. Aquatic species
may be affected due to worsened river/sea water quality and wastewater discharged from
vessels along the navigation in operation phase. If not being effective treated, these waste
sources may accumulate at the port, reducing coastal surface water quality. However, this
impact can be controlled by the compliance of regulations in operation of vessels, ships entering
and existing the port, helping to reduce cumulative impacts of oil leakage, wastes on water
quality and local aquatic species.
This impact may directly affect aquatic life, particularly in sensitive ecosystems such as
estuaries, seagrass beds, coral reefs, or nearby aquaculture areas. Water quality degradation can
reduce reproduction, increase disease and mortality in aquatic species, affecting fishery and
aquaculture productivity. Without control and regular monitoring, these impacts could
accumulate, causing localized marine environmental degradation and affecting coastal
livelihoods. However, the impact is assessed as minor and not significantly harmful to marine
environments or local community livelihoods if managed with appropriate ESMP measures.


4.8.       CLIMATE CHANGE ISSUES
4.8.1. Impact of climate change on the project
The project will be negatively impacted by climate change. As Chapter 2 has shown, the strong
impacts of climate change will affect each subproject area. According to meteorological data
and climate forecasts in Thanh Hoa sea areas11:
       •   There is a fairly high frequency of storms and tropical depressions in September and
           October.
       •   Rainfall is concentrated in the rainy season from May to October (the highest rainfall
           measured is 300 mm/day with the average 137 rainy days/year measured at Nga Son
           hydrometeorological station, mainly from September to October).
       •   The average wind speed in the project area is 1.0 - 1.5 m/s. The highest wind speed
           measured during the storm was 40 m/s in the coastal area.
       •   This area is affected by sea level rise at an average level from 51cm to 106cm, with the
           RCP 8.5 scenario, the level is forecasted about 77cm (according to the Climate Change
           Scenario of the Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment).
       •   There is a possibility of prolonged heatwave and strong winds, affecting the safety of
           outdoor construction.
The trend of increasingly complicated climate change negatively affects living conditions of
people in the subproject affected area. Specifically, the impacts on fishing ports and aquaculture
areas are presented in the table below.

The level of impact during the construction and operation phases is assessed as low because:
(i) The project area has a very low frequency of storms and floods (about 1%, corresponding to
historical flood events estimated to occur once every 100 years); (ii) Since 2018, rainfall in the
project area has remained relatively stable; (iii) Technical measures and operational
management and maintenance strategies have been incorporated into the design to control these
risks; (iv) The embankments and port infrastructure have been designed with standardized
foundations to ensure structural sustainability. Aquaculture areas have been planned with
appropriate timing and location for farming activities, and have adopted adaptive design

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                      239
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
features, materials, and facility characteristics to withstand changing weather and climate
conditions, including potential extreme events.
In general, climate change impacts are unavoidable during the implementation and operation of
the project. This requires preventive and adaptive measures, along with specific response plans
to respond effectively to changing and extreme weather conditions, in order to proactively
minimize potential damages and risks
4.8.2. Project impact on climate change
a. Construction phase:
During construction of the project, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions will be emitted due to the use of
fossil fuels. The most common fossil fuels used for construction equipment at the project sites are
Diesel Oil (DO) and electricity. GHG emissions from diesel combustion on construction equipment
are calculated based on the estimated diesel fuel demand table (Chapter 3 – Impacts from construction
machinery emissions) and the 2014 emission factors for GHG inventories published by the United
States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). 12 Furthermore, GHG emissions are typically
expressed in terms of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO₂e). To convert individual gases into CO₂e, their
respective global warming potentials (GWP) must be multiplied by the quantity of each gas. The
emission factors calculated for the project have not yet been converted into CO₂e. To perform this
conversion, the emission quantities must be multiplied by the corresponding GWP values listed in the
tables below.
The results of greenhouse gas emission calculations for the use of fuels such as DO oil and electricity
are shown in the tables below:

Overall, GHG emissions during the construction period for the project are estimated to be small,
equivalent to 4,258 CO2e (ton/day). Of which, the largest amount of greenhouse gas emissions are
