Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                                          246
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Overall, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions during the operational phase of the project are
estimated to be relatively low, totaling approximately 32,213.67 tons of CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)
per day. The majority of emissions are concentrated at subproject 1 – Hoang Truong fishing
port (around 16,540 tons/day) and subproject 2 – Lach Truong fishing port (approximately
15,763 tons/day). Subprojects 3 through 9 are considered to have no significant GHG emissions.
This level of emission is considered small compared to medium- and large-scale fishing ports.
However, the volume of emissions may increase if: (i) a increase in production output in the
future, or (ii) Lack of technological improvements. During the operational phase, the
management boards of the fishing ports will carry out detailed GHG inventory services (if
required to compliance or in preparation for port expansion in the future).
Therefore, the project will inevitably contribute a modest amount of greenhouse gas (GHG)
emissions, stemming from activities such as fishing, transportation, cold storage, primary
processing, and preservation of aquatic and seafood products. Without effective control and
adaptive measures, these fishing ports may gradually evolve into sources of adverse impacts on
both regional and national climate change efforts.
Additionally, during the project’s operational phase, a certain amount of greenhouse gas (GHG)
emissions will be generated from the combustion of fossil fuels, such as gasoline, diesel oil,
and biodiesel, used by vehicles entering and exiting the port area. Due to the variety of fuel
types and the diversity and number of vehicles traveling along access roads to the port,
calculating the exact volume of GHG emissions is typically difficult. However, these emissions
are considered minimal, as the access roads are existing infrastructure designed for motorcycles,
small vehicles, and trucks. These roads have already been upgraded and currently accommodate
a relatively low volume of traffic. Therefore, GHG emissions from this source is considered to
have no significant impact on climate change or greenhoue effect.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                            247
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

        CHAPTER 5. ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL MANAGEMENT PLAN
5.1. Measures to minimize environmental and social impacts
5.1.1. Proposed mitigation measures for FS and detailed design
5.1.1.1. For construction items involving dredging canals/rivers/seas
    -    Conduct consultations with communities and stakeholders (such as the provincial
         Departments of Agriculture and Environment, the management boards of existing
         fishing ports/storm shelters, Lach Truong ferry operators, local residents, etc.) regarding
         the locations and peak periods when dredging activities should be minimized or
         avoided;
    -    Design of waterway traffic sign system to ensure waterway traffic safety during
         operation phase.
    -    Design the dredged channel properly to maintain the local people's access to river/sea
         water, especially to ensure aquaculture and fishing activities on rivers/sea.
5.1.1.2. For construction items of fishing port/anchoring wharf embankment
    -    Design railing-equipped ladders along the embankment to maintain safe access to and
         use of the water for the local community while still ensuring access for boats entering
         and exiting the harbor/storm shelter;
    -    Design of wastewater collection and drainage systems for works at fishing
         ports/anchoring berths;
    -    Design slope protection measures using hard structures, engineering, ecological
         measures or a combination of measures as appropriate.
    -    Toilets, sinks, and showers will be provided in the operation building for staff to use
         after work. The administration building is designed to maximize natural lighting, and
         the water and electrical equipment will be of energy-saving types.
5.1.1.3. For auxiliary works to collect and treat wastewater and solid waste for the port
    -    The design of wastewater collection and drainage systems serving works at fishing
         ports/anchoring berths must ensure aesthetics, minimize odors and be easy to operate;
    -    The wastewater treatment facilities (WWTFs) in the fishing ports shall be arranged in
         compliance with the Vietnamese National Technical Regulation (QCVN
         01:2021/BXD). This regulation requires that the WWTFs invested under Subprojects 1
         and 2 (with a capacity of 200 m³/day) must include an ecological buffer zone of at least
         15 meters. The wastewater treatment works applying mechanical, physico-chemical,
         and biological methods must be constructed as closed systems, equipped with odor
         collection and treatment systems. Specifically:
          Table 5-1. Environmental safety distance for wastewater treatment plant
        Work item                                   Minimum distance (m) from the wastewater
                                                    treatment plant with a capacity of 200 – 5.000
                                                    m3/day
        Wastewater pumping stations                                     20 m
        Wastewater         Treatment        Plant                       15 m
        (A treatment facility using mechanical,
        physico-chemical, and biological methods,
        constructed as an enclosed system with an


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               248
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

        Work item                                   Minimum distance (m) from the wastewater
                                                    treatment plant with a capacity of 200 – 5.000
                                                    m3/day
        integrated odor collection and treatment
        system).

    -    For wastewater pumping stations using submersible pumps installed in closed wells, no
         environmental safety distance (ATMT) is required; however, a ventilation pipe for odor
         release must be installed at an elevation ≥ 3 m above ground level, in accordance with
         the approved site planning. A green buffer zone with a minimum width of ≥ 10 m must
         be arranged around newly planned WWTPs and wastewater pumping stations.
    -    Within the environmental safety distance, only traffic roads, parking lots, power supply
         works, solid waste transfer stations, and other facilities of the wastewater pumping
         station or WWTP are allowed to be planned; no other civil structures shall be arranged.
    -    Subproject 1 and subproject 2 have selected biological treatment technology and
         designed closed treatment tanks to minimize odor generation and better control sludge
         from the WWTP. In addition, the subprojects have also proposed that the operation
         personnel at the Port Management Board/typhoon shelter area must have appropriate
         expertise/experience to meet the operational requirements of the waste treatment
         facilities of each subproject.
    -    Use Larsen sheet piles to reinforce and protect the slopes of excavation pits/trenches to
         prevent landslides and erosion when digging to depths of 3 meters or more for WWTPs
         and pumping stations if constructed underground.
    -    Prepare an Operations Manual and an Emergency Response Plan to address potential
         incidents/risks that may occur in the wastewater collection and treatment system of the
         fishing ports.
5.1.2. Mitigation measures in pre-construction phase
5.1.2.1. Mitigation measures due to land acquisition activities
A Resettlement Plan (RP) has been prepared for the project based on the preliminary design
and ESIA report to provide specific measures for compensation, assistance and resettlement of
affected persons. The RP also establishes mechanisms for project information disclosure, public
consultation and participation, and a grievance redress mechanism (GRM). Compensation and
assistance to affected households is based on the following principles:
    -    All affected persons who have assets on or residing within the project land area prior to
         the cut-off date will be compensated for their losses. Those who have lost income and/or
         livelihood will be assisted to restore their livelihoods based on eligibility criteria
         determined by the project in consultation with the project affected persons. If by the end
         of the project it is demonstrated that livelihoods have not been restored to pre-project
         levels, additional measures will be provided.
    -    The methods used to assess losses in WB-financed projects are based on full
         replacement costs. For this project, losses include losses to land, structures and other
         assets and these replacement costs will be assessed as follows: Full replacement cost of
         land includes the value of land determined at prevailing market prices plus management
         fees. For affected houses and other structures, the valuation is based on the market price
         of construction materials and labor costs to construct replacement houses of equal or
         better quality and area to the affected houses.
    -    Mitigate the unavoidable economic and social impacts of land acquisition or restriction
         of land use by: (a) providing timely compensation for loss of assets at replacement cost

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA)                                               249
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
        and (b) assisting displaced persons in their efforts to improve, or at least restore, their
        livelihoods and standards of living, in real terms, to pre-displacement levels or levels
        prevailing prior to the commencement of project implementation, whichever is higher.
    -   To ensure that all complaints and grievances of affected persons on any aspect of land
        acquisition, compensation and resettlement are promptly and satisfactorily addressed,
        and that all possible avenues are available for affected persons to address their
        grievances, a clearly defined grievance redress mechanism should be established. All
        PAPs can submit any queries to the implementing agencies relating to their rights. PAPs
        are not required to pay any fees in connection with seeking redress of grievances.
Preliminary estimates of compensation and support costs for PAPs have been prepared and are
included in the RP based on (a) current provincial regulations regarding compensation,
assistance, and resettlement, and (b) a rapid replacement cost assessment conducted to support
the process.
5.1.2.2. Mitigation measures for impacts from unexploded ordnance (UXO)
The measures to mitigate the impact of unexploded ordnance (UXO) on the project are as
follows:
    -   Land clearance: Manual clearing of areas that are not minefields; machine surveying to
        depths of -0.3 m and -0.3 m to -3 m with a signal density of type 1.
    -   Underwater clearance: (i) Surveying for underwater mines and explosives up to a depth
        of 0.5 m, in water depths of 0.5 to 12 m; (ii) Surveying for underwater mines and
        explosives from 0.5 m to 3 m or up to 5 m, in water depths of 0.5 to 12 m; (iii) Marking
        underwater signals up to 0.5 m, 3 m, and 5 m from the bottom of the water, in water
        depths of 0.5 to 3 m; (iv) Diving to handle signals at depths of 0.5 m, 0.5 m to 1 m, and
        1 m to 3 m, in water depths of 0.5 to 12 m.
The total cost for UXO clearance, both on land and underwater, is 2,308,750,237 VND,
including approximately 48,394,466 VND for materials, 1,244,739,591 VND for labor, and
1,015,616,180 VND for machinery.
5.1.3. Measures to minimize impacts during construction phase
The proposed mitigation measures for the Construction phase include: (i) Environmental and
Social Code of Practice (ESCOP), (ii) Workers’ Code of Conduct, and (iii) Labor Management
Procedures (LMP). Specific impact mitigation measures will be presented for each subproject
site and in the supplementary plans (in the annex).
5.1.3.1. General Impact Mitigation Measures – appliable to 9 subprojects
The ESCOP will be applicable to all civil works packages and will therefore be included in the
relevant part of the construction contract and tender documents. Note that in addition to the
ESCOP, the Construction Bidding Documents for each specific package will also include
specific mitigation measures and sub-planning relevant to the nature and location of the civil
works within that Package.

               maintenance; waste must be collected and disposed of according to hazardous waste
               regulations.
               • Wash water from vehicles entering and leaving the site must be collected into
               sedimentation tanks, then passed through sand and oil separation filters before discharge.

               iii) Rainwater Runoff
               Avoidance
               • Do not locate temporary material storage near rivers or seas.
               • Cover easily washed-away materials with geotextile fabric or soil/sand bags.
               Mitigation
               • Reinforce construction areas and internal roads with crushed stone or a proper
               drainage system.
               • Arrange drainage ditches and sedimentation pits on-site; regularly dredge pits (once a
               week or as needed) to prevent soil and waste from entering drainage systems and causing
               blockage or localized flooding.
               • Clean up spilled materials, oils, and grease to prevent runoff into the environment.
               • Limit or avoid excavation and earthwork on rainy days to prevent pollution runoff.
               Remediation
               • If rainwater runoff causes flooding or water pollution, the contractor must carry out
               remediation according to regulations.

                    For domestic solid waste:                                                                     Environmental Protection Law,        Supervision

                    For construction solid waste:
                    • Reuse broken bricks, soil, and rocks as fill materials.
                    • Sort steel and iron for scrap sale or recycling.
                    • Stockpile in designated areas with hard surfaces and roofs to prevent dust dispersion
                    or runoff.
                    • Do not illegally dump construction waste; install warning signs and surveillance
                    cameras if necessary.
                    For demolition and clearing waste:
                    • Allow local communities to collect valuable plants before clearing.
                    • Collect and reuse inert waste (rocks, soil, broken bricks) for filling.
                    • Do not bury or burn plant biomass on-site.
                    For dredged material and excess excavated soil:
                    • Precisely determine excavation areas to avoid removing topsoil in green areas.

                    • Use excavated soil for site leveling or planting; do not transport off-site unless
                    necessary.
                    • Store excavated soil in designated planting areas, with a maximum height of 1.5 m.

                    the Appendix – Dredged Material Management Plan.
                    Additional mitigation measures:
                    • Provide large-capacity (250 L) covered, water-tight bins with labeled waste
                    classification on-site.
                    • Sort waste at the source and concentrate it in temporary storage areas.

                    with regulations.

                    rain dispersion.

                    100–200 m², impermeable surface, fencing, and signage.
                    • Inspect and maintain equipment and machinery to ensure good condition and renew
                    registration before expiry.
                    • Major maintenance or repair of excavators is not permitted on-site; such activities
                    must be conducted at specialized maintenance workshops.

                    • If solid waste causes environmental or community impacts, the contractor is
                    responsible for remediation and compensation according to regulations.

                    • Control of hazardous materials: Do not use unapproved materials such as lead-

                    performed by trained and certified personnel.
                    Hazardous waste storage and classification:
                    • Hazardous waste must be classified, clearly labeled, and stored in dedicated containers
                    with lids (capacity 120 L).
                    • Construct a hazardous waste storage facility of at least 10 m², with impermeable
                    flooring, roofing, walls, and drainage channels.
                    Management of used oils and grease:
                    • Collect and store used oil and grease separately in sealed containers under cover, away
                    from ignition sources.
                    • Oil changes and filtration must only occur in designated areas.
                    • Waste oil may be sold to licensed recycling units or returned to the manufacturer.
                    Mitigation Measures for Spills
                    Emergency response:
                    • The person detecting the spill must ensure safety and immediately notify the
                    supervising engineer and PMU.
                    • Use sawdust, sandbags, or shovels depending on the scale of the spill.

                Avoidance
                • Do not hunt, trap, poison, feed, or harm any animals (birds, frogs, snakes, etc.).
                • Do not buy or sell wildlife for food.
                • Do not keep birds or animals in worker camps.
                • Do not burn waste in ways that damage or affect habitats.
                Minimization
                • Regularly monitor construction activities to detect and prevent risks to wildlife.
                • Identify and protect undisturbed areas throughout the construction process.
                Temporary protective fencing will be installed where necessary to effectively protect the
                project area's biological resources.
                Mitigation Measures
                • If wildlife is found within the construction area, coordinate with competent authorities
                for appropriate protection.
                • Organize training for workers on wildlife identification and resource protection.
                Remediation (if any)
                • If construction causes ecosystem damage or loss of biological resources, the
                contractor must coordinate with the owner and relevant authorities to assess the damage
                and implement compensation according to the Biodiversity Law and Environmental
                Protection Law.

               Mitigation
               • Develop a Service Disruption Plan for each type of service if shut-off is unavoidable,
               including: start–end time; affected areas; temporary or alternative supply measures (clean
               water, temporary Wi-Fi, generators, etc.); and contact points for incident handling.
               • Post public notices at community points (health stations, commune/ward People’s
               Committee offices, households, schools, etc.) at least 5 days in advance, in addition to
               direct notifications.
               • Provide alternative water supply to affected residents if service interruption lasts more
               than one day.
               • Assess the level of impact (number of households, healthcare facilities, schools, etc.)
               to prioritize support.
               • Clearly specify forms of notification (paper-based, electronic, loudspeaker, etc.).
               Remediation (if any)
               • Sign a tripartite agreement (project owner – contractor – infrastructure management
               unit) on responsibilities for compensation, repair, and restoration in case of damage to
               existing systems.
               • Maintain an incident logbook recording all damages to infrastructure, including: date
               and time; description of damage; cause; corrective measures; completion time of repair;
               confirmation minutes signed by the supervising engineer and infrastructure management
               unit.

                    • Keep camps, kitchens, bathing/washing areas, and toilets clean regularly; provide
                    waste bins and ensure daily waste collection. Specify cleaning frequency (e.g., daily
                    cleaning, weekly inspections).
                    • Keep drainage ditches around camps cleared periodically.
                    Mitigation
                    • Suspend all construction activities in the event of storms, accidents, or incidents.
                    • In case of chemical or fuel leaks: check injured persons, provide first aid and transfer
                    to medical facilities; do not flush chemicals into the drainage system; contain/absorb spills
                    using sawdust or sand, or remove contaminated topsoil if necessary.
                    • Provide emergency-response training for workers, especially for fire/explosion,
                    chemical incidents, and natural hazards.
                    • After any incident, prepare a detailed report and submit it to the supervising engineer,
                    the Project Management Unit (PMU), and relevant authorities if required.
                    Remediation (if any)
                    • If occupational accidents or incidents affect workers’ health, the contractor is
                    responsible for treatment, medical support, and compensation in accordance with law and
                    labor contracts.

B. Code of conduct for workers
The Workers’ Code of Conduct shall address, but not be limited to, the following measures:
Compliance with laws and regulations
   •   All workers must comply with the laws and regulations of the Socialist Republic of Viet
       Nam.
   •   The list of workers must be registered with the local authorities in accordance with
       current regulations.
   •   Children under 18 years old are not permitted to work under the Project.
Civilized behavior and respect for the community
   •   Behave properly when interacting with the local community, respecting local customs,
       practices, and traditions.
   •   Do not cause disturbance, disorder, or engage in fighting (physical or verbal) within or
       near the community.
   •   Workers residing in temporary camps, when visiting local communities, must conduct
       themselves in a manner consistent with the Code of Conduct.
Prohibited behaviors and restricted items
   •   Prohibit the use or possession of illegal substances, weapons, and firearms (except for
       authorized security personnel).
   •   Prohibit pornography, gambling, and alcohol consumption during working hours.
   •   Prohibit hunting, fishing with explosives/chemicals, trading wildlife, or keeping
       animals (particularly birds).
   •   Prohibit cutting trees, collecting firewood, or harvesting plants beyond approved areas.
   •   Prohibit disturbing architectural/historical sites, burning waste, or clearing vegetation
       without authorization.
Hygiene, safety, and environmental protection
   •   Maintain personal hygiene and living areas to appropriate standards.
   •   Smoking is only permitted in designated areas.
   •   Do not wash clothes, clean vehicles, or maintain equipment in rivers/streams or
       unauthorized locations.
   •   Do not dispose of garbage or construction waste indiscriminately; prevent the spread of
       pollutants such as oil or grease.
   •   Toilets must be used only in designated places.
   •   Do not work without personal protective equipment such as boots and helmets.
Disciplinary measures
   •   Any violation of the Code shall be subject to disciplinary action, ranging from a warning
       to termination of the labor contract, depending on the severity of the violation.
   •   Any construction worker, office staff, contractor’s employee, or any other person
       involved in the project found violating prohibitions shall be subject to disciplinary
       measures, which may range from a simple reprimand to dismissal, depending on the
       severity of the violation.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                               Page 271
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
C. Labor management process (LMP)
A Labor Management Procedure (LMP) was developed as a stand-alone document for
addressing the impacts on and of labor and working conditions of the project. The LMP
identifies the main labor requirements and risks associated with the project and help the PMU
and Contractor to determine the resources necessary to address project labor issues. The LMP
will enable different project-related parties, for example, staff of the project management unit,
contractors and sub-contractors and project workers, to have a clear understanding of what is
required on a specific labor issue. The LMP is a living document, which is initiated early in
project preparation, and is reviewed and updated throughout development and implementation
of the project. The LMP has 12 chapters. Chapter 1 served as Introduction. An overview of
labor use in the project is presented in Chapter 2. Key potential labor risks are listed in Chapter
3. Legislative Framework governing labor employment in Vietnam 2 is discussed in Chapters
4 and 5. Implementation Arrangements, Age Requirement, Policies and Procedures and Timing
of labor requirements follows in the subsequent chapters. Grievance Redressal Mechanism and
Contractor Management are presented in the last two chapters 10 and 11 respectively, while
Chapter 12 relates to primary supply workers. Details are presented in the Project’s LMP.
5.1.3.2. Specific mitigation measures for construction impacts of Subproject 1 and
Subproject 2
The specific construction impacts of the subprojects include: (i) Disturbance to the operations
of the existing Hoang Truong fishing port; (ii) Deterioration of water quality in the Lach Truong
River due to dredging activities; (iii) Impacts on aquaculture activities in the Lach Truong
River; (iv) Impacts from the disposal/stockpile sites serving the subprojects; (v) Impacts on
sensitive receptors. The mitigation measures for these specific impacts are presented below:
(i) Mitigation measures for impacts disrupting the operation of the existing Hoang Truong,
    Lach Truong fishing port
Upgrading the fishing port may affect the operation of the existing port. However, consultations
with stakeholders indicate that the locality has experience in arranging upgrading and dredging
activities simultaneously with maintaining the safe operation of fishing vessels and port
facilities. The key mitigation measures include:
   -   Consultation and inter-agency coordination prior to construction. The project
       owner/contractor will consult with stakeholders such as the waterway police, port
       management board, sub-department of fisheries, and local communities to fully
       understand port operations and coordinate suitable construction activities.
   -   Public disclosure, grievance mechanism, and risk communication. Information on
       construction scope, schedule, and grievance mechanism will be posted at easily
       accessible points within the fishing port to ensure transparency and timely resolution of
       emerging environmental and social issues. Weather updates and disaster warnings will
       be broadcast regularly through the port’s public address system to help fishermen
       proactively respond.
   -   Access management and port security. Control entry into the construction area,
       particularly where it adjoins berthing and cargo handling activities of fishing vessels.
       Vessels or organizations that do not comply with port regulations will not be allowed to
       access the construction area.
   -   Phased construction and spatial separation. Construction will be implemented in
       phases, with clear barriers separating construction zones from fishing port operation
       areas both onshore and offshore.



Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 272
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Signage and identification signals. Install signs, flags, and lighting in movement and
       mooring areas so that fishermen, traders, and residents can recognize construction
       boundaries, especially at night.
   -   Post-construction restoration. Conduct appropriate restoration of completed areas
       before moving on to subsequent construction phases to minimize prolonged impacts on
       the environment and surrounding communities.
   -   Maintenance of waterway traffic: Coordinate with maritime authorities to provide
       updates on channels, buoys, and wharves to ensure safe vessel access to the port
       throughout the construction period.
   -   Environmental incident monitoring and response. Maintain on-duty staff, hoist warning
       signals in the event of storms or tropical depressions; respond immediately to accidents
       or environmental pollution incidents occurring within the port area.
(ii) Mitigation measures for impacts on sensitive receptors
For areas with PCRs:
Avoidance
   -   Early consultation and notification: the contractor must inform local communities at least
       2 weeks before commencing construction near pagoda sites.
   -   Awareness raising and worker training: implement compliance with the Code of Conduct
       (COC) for workers, with particular emphasis on appropriate behavior and language when
       working near spiritual or religious sites.
   -   Appropriate site planning and scheduling:
   -   Do not stockpile materials, waste, or construction equipment within a 100m radius of
       memorial sites.
   -   Avoid construction activities on the 1st and 15th days of the lunar month and during
       holidays/festivals to prevent disruption of spiritual or community events.
   -   Minimization
       Control of dust, noise, and waste:
   +   Spray water to reduce dust during hot and dry conditions.
   +   Restrict noisy activities and construction during ceremonies or religious service hours.
   +   Minimize the volume of materials and waste temporarily stored near the pagoda.
        Traffic management and access:
   -   Install warning signs and assign traffic controllers at the entrance to memorial sites
       when necessary
   -   Provide safe access routes for people visiting the pagoda.
  Remediation
   -   Reinstate, clear, and clean the site after construction is completed.
For schools along the main transportation routes:
Avoidance
   -   Appropriate construction scheduling: arrange construction activities outside of school
       hours to minimize noise, vibration, and traffic disturbance. Adjust construction and
       transport schedules to avoid peak school hours (7:00–8:00, 11:00–11:30, 13:00–13:30,

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                Page 273
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       16:30–17:30).
   -   Adjustment of construction schedule: for special school events such as examinations,
       opening ceremonies, or school activities, the contractor must reschedule construction
       and transport to avoid interference.
Minimization
   -   Limiting truck traffic during peak hours: reduce the number of trucks departing
       construction sites during times when students are arriving or leaving school.
   -   Information sharing with schools: regularly update schools on construction progress,
       transport changes, and emergency response measures.
   -   Driver training: organize training for drivers on traffic safety around schools,
       including limiting horn use, reducing speed, and exercising caution when passing school
       gates.
   -   Traffic management and speed control: deploy personnel to guide traffic at
       intersections near schools during peak hours (7:00–7:30, 11:00–11:30, 13:00–13:30,
       16:30–17:30), and install speed limit signs and reflective barriers along school areas.
Mitigation
   -   Strengthened communication: promptly inform schools of emergency response
       measures and schedule changes.
   -   Support for students and schools: in case of student health issues or traffic accidents,
       provide adequate medical and financial support mechanisms.
Compensation
   -   Repairing school facilities: if construction damages school property (fences, gates, trees,
       etc.), the contractor is responsible for repair or compensation as agreed with the school.
   -   Supporting affected students: in cases where students are injured or experience health
       impacts due to traffic, the contractor must provide medical and financial support..
For the ship repair workshop near the port
  Avoidance
   -   Early notification and coordination: The contractor shall notify the ship repair
       workshop at least 2 weeks prior to the commencement of construction, allowing them
       time to adjust their production and operations accordingly.
  Minimization
   -   Worker awareness raising: Conduct training sessions for workers on proper conduct
       regulations, particularly emphasizing the avoidance of disturbances or conflicts with
       workers at the ship repair workshop near the construction site.
   -   Traffic coordination and port safety:
             o Arrange a system of warning signs, direction signs, and traffic guides within the
               existing fishing port premises to ensure safety for both construction activities and
               the operation of the workshops.
             o Assign workers to clean up spilled materials daily on internal roads within the
               port to avoid obstructing the transportation of materials to the ship repair
               workshop.
   -   Noise and dust control: install barriers to reduce noise and dust; schedule construction

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 274
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
        activities to avoid peak production and goods-handling hours of enterprises.
  Mitigation
    -   Worker behavior monitoring: Train and closely supervise the behavior of construction
        workers to avoid disturbances and impacts on the ship repair workshop workers.
  Compensation
        In case construction activities affect production operations or cause damage to the ship
        repair workshop, the contractor shall be responsible for repair or compensation in
        accordance with agreements with the workshop owner.
For nearby seafood processing facilities
  Avoidance
    -   Early notification and coordination: The contractor shall notify nearby
        facilities/enterprises at least 2 weeks prior to the commencement of construction, allowing
        them time to adjust their production and operations accordingly.
    Mitigation
    -   Awareness raising and worker training: Conduct training sessions for workers on
        proper conduct regulations, particularly emphasizing the avoidance of disturbances or
        conflicts with workers at nearby processing facilities.
    -   Traffic coordination and safety within the port:
    -   o Arrange a system of warning signs, direction signs, and traffic guides within the
        existing fishing port premises to ensure safety for both construction activities and
        enterprise                                                                   operations.
        o Assign workers to clean up spilled materials daily on internal roads within the port to
        avoid obstructing the transportation of raw materials and products of the processing
        facilities.
    -   Noise and dust control: Install noise and dust barriers; arrange reasonable construction
        schedules to avoid peak hours of production or cargo handling of the enterprises.
    Mitigation
    -   Worker behavior monitoring: Train and closely supervise the behavior of
        construction workers to avoid disturbances and impacts on workers of nearby
        facilities/enterprises.
    Compensation
    -   Support to processing facilities: In case construction activities affect production
        operations or cause damage to seafood processing facilities, the contractor shall be
        responsible for repair or compensation in accordance with agreements with the
        enterprises.
(iii) Mitigation measures for water quality degradation in the Lach Truong River caused by
     dredging activities
  Avoidance
    -   Dredging activities must be carried out sequentially in small sections within the port.
        Soft barriers using buoys and warning signs must be installed around the dredging area.
    -   Suspend dredging operations at night and during adverse weather conditions. When
        dredging is suspended, dredging equipment such as suction dredgers, barges, etc., must


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 275
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       be moved to a designated nearshore area of the port to avoid obstructing vessel
       navigation (this area must also be equipped with warning signs and nighttime lighting).
   -   Conduct monitoring and supervision of river water quality, dredged material, and
       biological characteristics within and outside the port area to track the growth and
       development of aquatic species, thereby enabling appropriate preventive measures.
  Minimization
   -   Use appropriate dredging equipment to avoid excessive rock fragmentation and bottom
       sediment disturbance.
   -   Immediately load dredged materials onto barges after lifting, avoiding long storage in
       the basin
   -   Do not overload barges or transport vessels, ensuring safety and avoiding spillage.
   -   Do not store oil/lubricants on barges or dredging equipment; oil-contaminated materials
       must be collected and handled by licensed entities.
   -   Dredging operators must be trained and certified, under supervision of the Construction
       Supervision Consultant (CSC).
   -   Provide full PPE to workers, especially life jackets for water-based activities; assign
       supervisors per shift for emergency response.
Emergency Preparedness
   -   Establish emergency response plans for waterway incidents, including collisions, engine
       failures, capsizing, or oil spills. These plans must be approved by the competent
       authorities.
   -   Suspend construction and move equipment to designated safe nearshore areas during
       adverse weather; these areas must be equipped with warning signs and nighttime
       lighting.
   -   Conduct regular training for crew members on rescue, evacuation, and incident response
       procedures on the river.
   -   Notify and coordinate with border guards, fisheries surveillance, police, waterway
       traffic, and port authorities for timely support in case of incidents.
   -   Establish an emergency communication channel between transport vessels, project
       owner, contractor, and competent authorities to quickly handle abnormal situations.
  Restoration
   -   Clean up and collect spilled materials and waste after each construction phase to ensure
       no residues remain that could affect water quality.
   -   Restore water quality and bottom environment by monitoring and adjusting construction
       progress if prolonged degradation is detected.
   -   Submit periodic environmental monitoring reports to the competent authorities.
(iv) Mitigation measures for impacts on aquaculture activities in the Lach Truong River
  Avoidance
   -   Avoid passing through aquaculture areas: Transport routes for machinery must avoid
       river sections with shrimp and fish cages.
   -   Localized construction: Underwater works should only be carried out within the existing
       port area to limit wider impacts.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                              Page 276
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    -   Avoid gathering construction machinery near aquaculture areas.
  Minimization
    -   Control oil and grease leakage; regularly maintain watercraft and equipment (barges,
        canoes).
    -   Equip oil spill response materials (containment booms, absorbent materials).
    -   Conduct construction in favorable weather conditions, avoiding heavy rains that may
        wash pollutants into the river.
    -   Utilize existing embankment/dike systems: Current embankments help reduce waves,
        erosion, and the spread of pollution to aquaculture areas.
    Restoration
    -   Clean up and collect waste after construction, ensuring no residual materials or oil
        remain on the water surface.
(v) Mitigation measures for environmental pollution risks at dredged material disposal
    sites
    -   Disposal sites must be pre-identified in compliance with approved construction
        specifications. Sensitive receiving water bodies or areas near water sources must be
        avoided. Drainage ditches shall be constructed around the storage area to block
        wastewater.
    -   Retaining walls shall be installed around disposal sites where necessary.
    -   Retaining walls must be completed before dumping begins and regularly inspected
        during the construction process. The height of the retaining walls must be at least equal
        to the dumping elevation to prevent overflow, erosion, and to ensure the stability of
        dredged materials.
    -   Conduct regular monitoring at disposal sites to ensure materials are not washed away.
    -   Strictly comply with the approved Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP),
        including requirements for transportation, storage, pollution control, and safety at
        disposal sites.
5.1.3.3. Specific mitigation measures for construction impacts of Subprojects 3 → 9
The specific construction impacts of the subprojects include: (i) Temporary disturbance to water
intake and drainage activities serving aquaculture; (ii) Impacts on local livelihoods; (iii) Impacts
from disposal/stockpile sites serving the subprojects; (iv) Impacts on sensitive receptors. The
mitigation measures for these specific impacts are presented below:
 (i) Mitigation measures for temporary disturbance of water intake and drainage activities
     serving aquaculture
    -   Consultation and coordination with local authorities and communities: The project
        owner/contractor shall conduct consultations with stakeholders such as the Commune
        People’s Committee, the Commune Fatherland Front, and local communities to
        understand water intake and drainage activities for aquaculture and to coordinate
        construction accordingly.
    -   Public disclosure, grievance mechanism, and risk communication: Information on
        construction scope, schedule, and grievance mechanisms shall be posted at accessible
        locations to ensure transparency and timely resolution of emerging environmental and
        social issues. Weather forecasts and disaster warnings shall be regularly broadcast
        through the port’s loudspeaker system to help local people respond proactively.
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                   Page 277
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Phased and spatially separated construction: Construction shall be implemented in
       stages, with clear separation barriers between construction areas and local aquaculture
       activities.
   -   Signage and visual signals: Install warning signs, flags, and lighting in movement areas
       to help residents recognize construction zones, especially at night.
   -   Post-construction restoration: Properly restore completed sections before moving to the
       next construction phase, minimizing prolonged impacts on the environment and
       community.
   -   Environmental incident monitoring and response: Establish on-duty teams, display
       warning signals during storms or tropical depressions, and handle on-site any accidents
       or environmental pollution incidents occurring within the port area
(ii) Mitigation measures for impacts on sensitive receptors
For areas with PCRs:

   -   Early consultation and notification: the contractor must inform local communities at least

        Traffic management and access:
   -   Install warning signs and assign traffic controllers at the entrance to memorial sites
       when necessary
   -   Provide safe access routes for people visiting the pagoda.
  Remediation
   -   Reinstate, clear, and clean the site after construction is completed.
For schools along the main transportation routes:
Avoidance
   -   Appropriate construction scheduling: arrange construction activities outside of school
       hours to minimize noise, vibration, and traffic disturbance. Adjust construction and
       transport schedules to avoid peak school hours (7:00–8:00, 11:00–11:30, 13:00–13:30,
       16:30–17:30).

Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Adjustment of construction schedule: for special school events such as examinations,

       construction sites during times when students are arriving or leaving school.
   -   Information sharing with schools: regularly update schools on construction progress,
