(vi) Mitigation measures for impacts on aquaculture activities
  Avoidance
   -   Notify local residents/aquaculture farm owners at least 2 weeks in advance of
       construction activities.
   -   The Project Management Unit (PMU) will closely coordinate with contractors, local
       authorities, and potentially affected households to optimize dredging operations —
       balancing technical dredging requirements while minimizing adverse impacts on local
       aquaculture activities
   -   Agree with local residents on the timing for allowing water intake into their aquaculture
       farms/areas, as well as the timing for stocking shrimp ponds. Farming seasons will be
       arranged with local households to ensure that construction activities do not negatively
       affect shrimp farming operations — for example, ensuring that river water used for
       aquaculture is not turbid due to dredging. The National Technical Regulation on
       brackish water shrimp farming (QCVN 02-19:2014/BNNPTNT on veterinary hygiene,
       environmental protection, and food safety conditions) will be applied to monitor this
       arrangement.
   -   Avoid gathering construction machinery near aquaculture areas.
  Minimization
   -   Control oil and grease leakage; regularly maintain construction equipment.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 279
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Equip oil spill response materials (containment booms, absorbent materials).
   -   Conduct construction in favorable weather conditions, avoiding heavy rains that may
       wash pollutants into the water.
  Restoration
   -   Clean up and collect waste after construction, ensuring no residual materials, oil, or
       grease remain on the water surface
(vii)   Mitigation measures for environmental pollution risks at dredged material
    disposal sites
   -   Disposal sites must be pre-identified in compliance with approved construction
       specifications. Sensitive receiving water bodies or areas near water sources must be
       avoided. Drainage ditches shall be constructed around the storage area to block
       wastewater.
   -   Retaining walls shall be installed around disposal sites where necessary.
   -   Retaining walls must be completed before dumping begins and regularly inspected
       during the construction process. The height of the retaining walls must be at least equal
       to the dumping elevation to prevent overflow, erosion, and to ensure the stability of
       dredged materials.
   -   Conduct regular monitoring at disposal sites to ensure materials are not washed away.
   -   Strictly comply with the approved Dredged Material Management Plan (DMMP),
       including requirements for transportation, storage, pollution control, and safety at
       disposal sites.
5.1.4. Mitigation measures during the operational phase
5.1.4.1. Mitigation measures for impacts of Subprojects 1 - 2 during the operation of fishing
ports
Measures to reduce odors, emissions and noise:
 a) Dust and Air Emission Mitigation Measures
Control of dust and air emissions from vessels, trucks, and other vehicles transporting and
handling aquatic products:
Avoidance
   -   Design parking lots and access roads to be wide and well-organized to avoid traffic
       congestion during peak hours.
   -   Install clear traffic instructions and regulations at the port, requiring drivers to
       disembark, switch off engines, and park only in designated areas.
   -   Vessels must travel at regulated speeds and dock properly to avoid disorder and negative
       impacts on water quality.
  Minimize
   -   Fully pave all internal roads and arrange regular cleaning staff to minimize dust
       generation.
   -   Sweep and spray roads twice daily to control dust within the port area.
   -   All vehicles must comply with speed, traffic safety, and environmental sanitation
       regulations to reduce emissions and prevent congestion or disorder.
   -   Vessels and vehicles must comply with national technical standards on exhaust
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                               Page 280
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       emissions (NOx, SOx, etc.).
   -   Encourage the use of low-sulfur fuels, alternative fuels, or less-polluting fuel blends.
   -   Conduct regular maintenance of vessels and vehicles to ensure operational efficiency
       and emission reduction.
   -   Minimize engine idling during loading/unloading operations.
   -   Reduce VOC emissions from fuel storage and transport activities by using suitable
       equipment.
   -   Install sprinkler systems in grass areas and gardens for irrigation, maintaining humidity
       and improving the local microclimate.
In addition, there are additional management measures for the 06 subprojects during
operation:
   -   Ensure green space accounts for approximately 20% of the subproject area.
   -   Install sprinkler systems in green spaces to maintain humidity and improve the
       microclimate.
Control of Odors Generated from Fishing Vessels upon Arrival and from Cold Storage
Facilities for Aquatic Products
  Avoid
   -   Design and arrange new facilities rationally to minimize the transport distance from
       vessels to storage areas.
   -   Handle and unload aquatic products promptly upon docking to avoid prolonged storage
       that may cause fishy odors and emissions from decomposition.
   -   Use sealed containers or specialized covered vehicles for transporting aquatic products
       and waste to prevent odor dispersion into the environment.
  Minimize
   -   Clean cold storage facilities regularly (1–2 times per day) to reduce odors generated
       from organic compounds and animal fats.
   -   Ensure proper ventilation of cold storage facilities to avoid odor accumulation from
       decomposition.
   -   Do not dispose of wastes such as fish scales, fish guts, shrimp shells, etc. into drains,
       rivers, or service areas, to prevent air and water pollution.
   -   Train staff to handle products quickly and properly in accordance with procedures to
       limit odor generation during operation.
  Offset/Compensation
   -   Plant green trees to create biological barriers around the port area to absorb odors and
       improve the microclimate.
Odor Control from Public Toilets and Wastewater Treatment Plants:
  Avoid
   -   Prohibit the disposal of spoiled aquatic products into drains, rivers, or the sea, as this
       causes pollution and degrades the port’s landscape.
   -   Wastewater treatment plants must be constructed as closed systems with odor collection
       and treatment systems, and surrounded by a minimum 15-meter ecological buffer zone

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                Page 281
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       in compliance with QCVN 01:2021/BXD for mechanical, physicochemical, and
       biological treatment facilities.
   -   Design solid waste storage areas separately from main operational areas to limit odor
       dispersion.
   -   Wastewater pumping stations must be underground reinforced concrete structures with
       submersible pumps to minimize odors and maintain aesthetics.
  Minimize
   -   Regularly collect generated solid waste, ensuring no accumulation at the port.
   -   Waste containers awaiting collection must have tight lids and be cleaned after each use.
   -   Regularly clean and spray deodorizing agents at waste collection areas.
   -   Apply biological agents to minimize odors and pests.
   -   Plant greenery to improve landscapes and limit odor dispersion to residential areas.
   -   Port management authorities must contract licensed companies for waste transport in
       accordance with regulations.
   -   Install tightly sealed manhole covers and regularly dredge sediment, maintaining related
       equipment.
   Mitigate
   -   Immediately address incidents of wastewater leakage or waste overflow causing odors,
       combined with deodorization using chemicals or biological agents.
   -   Rehabilitate the collection and drainage system if prolonged odor generation is detected.
   -   Construct sludge treatment systems and equipment in accordance with approved designs
       to ensure effective operation.
   -   Conduct periodic air quality monitoring every three months to assess and control odor
       emissions.
   -   Install and operate two odor collection and treatment systems at the two WWTPs,
       specifically: Odor generated in the wastewater treatment system of subproject 1 and
       subproject 2 will be extracted by exhaust fans and directed to an activated carbon
       adsorption tower and a primary absorption tower containing Ca(OH)₂ absorbing
       solution
 b) Noise Impact Mitigation Measures
Technical measures for equipment and vehicles
   -   All vehicles operating within the fishing port must undergo registration as required by
       regulations; conduct periodic inspections and maintenance of equipment, vehicles, and
       machinery to ensure that their operation does not generate noise levels exceeding the
       permissible standards.
   -   Encourage ship owners and transport units to equip vessels and trucks with silencers,
       especially for large-capacity vehicles frequently operating at the port.
   -   Require that engines be switched off when vehicles are parked or vessels are moored at
       the port.
   -   Prioritize the use of low-noise equipment, install noise barriers, or situate noisy facilities
       far from sensitive areas.
Operational management measures
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                   Page 282
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    -   Restrict operations during rest hours (midday and night).
    -   Require engines to be turned off when vessels dock and during cargo handling waiting
        times.
    -   Install traffic signs in the port to limit speed to 10 km/h and provide noise-reduction
        operational guidance.
Infrastructure design measures
    -   Locate reception, sorting, and service facilities away from existing residential areas to
        minimize noise impacts on local communities.
    -   Establish green buffer zones around port areas to absorb and reduce noise transmission
        to residential areas.
    -   Minimize intersections between transport routes and residential areas when vessels and
        vehicles enter or leave the fishing port.
Training and awareness raising
    -   Organize regular training sessions for fishermen, drivers, and port staff on noise impacts
        and mitigation measures.
 c) Wastewater Impact Mitigation Measures
Control of domestic wastewater, wastewater from onshore service areas, and wastewater from
vessels operating in the water area in front of the port
Stormwater runoff: At each subproject 1, 2:
    + The stormwater collection and drainage system is designed separately from the
        wastewater system.
    + The stormwater drainage system uses reinforced concrete pipes BTCT D800/BTCT
        D400, with inspection manholes and direct catch basins arranged along the route, at
        intervals of about 20–40 m/manhole.
    + The internal yards and roads are concreted, and project staff are assigned to clean the
        project premises to prevent waste from being washed away by rainwater.
    + Regular dredging and maintenance of the stormwater collection and drainage system
        are conducted.
-   Domestic wastewater at each subproject 1 and subproject 2 (port logistics areas, docked
    vessels) → 3-compartment septic tank → centralized wastewater treatment system with a
    capacity of 200 m³/day-night → environment.
-   Wastewater from the seafood receiving and sorting area is conveyed to the centralized
    wastewater treatment system (200 m³/day-night) for further treatment.
-   All wastewater generated during the operation phase of subprojects subproject 1 and
    subproject 2 is collected at the wastewater treatment plant of each subproject (200 m³/day-
    night capacity, with the option of 2 modules, each 100 m³/day-night). Treated wastewater
    must meet QCVN 11-MT:2015/BTNMT – National Technical Regulation on Seafood
    Processing Wastewater (Column B, Kq = 1.2, Kf = 1.1) before being discharged into the
    Lach Truong river at one discharge point with the following coordinates under VN2000
    coordinate system, central meridian 107°45’, projection zone 30: X(m) = 2187536.629;
    Y(m) = 594373.529.
Technological process:
Wastewater from sources → Collection tank → Oil and sand separator → Equalization tank

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 283
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
→ UASB tank → Anoxic tank → MBBR tank → Biological settling tank → Disinfection
tank → Environment → Treated wastewater meets QCVN 11-MT:2015/BTNMT (Column B,
Kq = 1.2, Kf = 1.1) → Receiving water bodies: the drainage canal along the southern road of
Hoang Truong fishing port → Lach Truong River (subproject 1) and De Canal (subproject 2).
Ballast water mitigation measures (applied to all 2 subprojects)
-    Require fishing vessels to install oil–sludge separation equipment before ballast water
     discharge, ensuring residues are collected and treated at the port.
-    Establish ballast water reception facilities at the port and contract licensed companies for
     collection and treatment in accordance with Decree 58/2017/ND-CP and Circular
     41/2017/TT-BGTVT.
-    Conduct periodic ballast water quality checks on vessels (every 6–12 months) to detect and
     address pollutants and invasive species.
-    Provide communication and training for fishermen on ballast water handling procedures,
     raising awareness of marine environmental protection.
-    Encourage application of the Ballast Water Management (BWM) Convention requirements
     for large-capacity or inter-provincial fishing vessels.
 d) Solid Waste Mitigation Measures
Management of ordinary solid waste:
Avoidance
On vessels
-    Establish a system for collecting and handling solid waste generated from fishing vessels
     during operation, in accordance with national regulations.
-    Food-related waste transported to the port must be managed under local regulations to
     protect human and animal health18.
-    Strictly prohibit the discharge of solid waste from vessels while anchored at the port, in
     compliance with MARPOL and national regulations.
On land
-    The Management Boards of the six fishing ports must strictly manage and minimize waste
     generated during sorting and receiving of fish, improving the environment at sorting houses
     and across the port.
-    Port operators must provide adequate facilities for wastewater and solid waste reception
     and management to meet the needs of the port and the vessels it serves.
-    The provision of waste reception facilities must be coordinated with local authorities, in
     line with commitments under the MARPOL Convention by port states.
-    Port operators must prepare environmental incident prevention, control, and response
     plans. Fuel suppliers must develop emergency response plans for oil spills to prevent large-
     scale dispersion that could cause significant harm to the natural environment and human
     health.
Mitigation (Minimization)
-    Contract with licensed units with full competence to collect, transport, and treat all daily
18
  Countries have specific regulatory requirements for the handling of food waste originating from international
voyages. The goal of most of these regulations is to prevent the cross-border spread of infectious diseases.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                             Page 284
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    domestic solid waste at the 02 ports, in compliance with the Law on Environmental
    Protection and relevant guiding documents.
-   Each subproject must designate a solid waste collection area with at least one day’s storage
    capacity, ensuring compliance with technical requirements for waste storage facilities.
-   Domestic solid waste at fishing ports (including from staff at the operation area and from
    fishermen/traders/visitors in public areas) shall be collected into 12 covered bins of 120
    liters each, with mandatory segregation: food waste, recyclable/reusable waste, and other
    domestic waste.
-   Sludge from wastewater treatment processes at the 05 wastewater treatment plants must be
    collected, transported, and treated by licensed contractors in compliance with regulations.
-   Sludge from the drainage and rainwater systems at the 02 ports must be dredged, collected,
    and treated by licensed contractors every 6 months.
-   Recyclable and reusable solid waste must be collected at designated places and handled
    once per week in accordance with regulations.
-   Fishery by-products from the sorting process must be collected into 02 bins of 500 liters
    each. Traders must collect them daily and transfer them to processing units for animal feed
    production.
Hazardous waste management:
-   The Port Management Boards must collect hazardous waste and periodically transfer it to
    licensed, competent units for collection, transportation, and treatment in compliance with
    regulations.
-   Hazardous waste must be collected and stored in a designated hazardous waste storage
    warehouse within each subproject’s waste collection facility. The warehouse must comply
    with current standards, with specific requirements as follows:
      + Concrete floor, partitioned compartments, roof cover, and warning signs for
        hazardous waste areas.
      + Raised curbs (10 cm) and a containment sump (40 × 40 × 40 cm) to prevent liquid
        spills.
      + Equipped with full emergency response and fire prevention/firefighting equipment.
Collection and storage plan:
-   Waste such as oily rags, broken neon lamps, used batteries, accumulators, and
    bottles/containers must be segregated in accordance with hazardous waste management
    regulations. They must be stored in specialized containers within the hazardous waste
    warehouse.
       Packaging waste from green space maintenance activities (fertilizers, pesticides) must
       be collected after use and stored in the hazardous waste warehouse.
e) Measures to Mitigate Impacts on Water Quality and Aquatic Life
Mitigating impacts on water quality:
-   Implement ballast water control measures as described in section (c) above.
-   Implement solid waste and hazardous waste control measures as described in section (d)
    above, to prevent their release into water bodies.
-   Effectively operate wastewater treatment plants at each subproject, ensuring treated
    wastewater meets standards before discharge into receiving rivers/seas.
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                               Page 285
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
-   Ensure seafood sorting and receiving areas are equipped with oil–grease traps and trash
    screens to prevent organic waste from entering water bodies.
-   Particularly, rainwater runoff from areas with potential oil leakage or hazardous materials
    (fueling or fuel transfer points) must pass through oil separators for sedimentation and oil
    removal before discharge into receiving waters.
-   Fishing vessel owners must comply with MARPOL and national regulations on oil, sludge,
    and wastewater management during operations. For example: oily waste and wastewater
    should be collected in barges, trucks, or centralized collection systems and tanks;
    wastewater with toxic chemicals from large-scale tank cleaning must be collected for onsite
    or appropriate treatment before discharge; incompatible wastes must not be mixed in the
    collection system; treatment methods must be based on wastewater characteristics.
Water quality monitoring:
-   Establish a periodic surface water monitoring program in front of the port.
-   Track indicators such as turbidity, suspended solids, oil and grease, dissolved oxygen, etc.,
    for timely management adjustments.
Mitigating impacts on aquatic life:
-   Limit fishing port operations to the designated water area in front of the wharf (36 m wide,
    400 m long), avoiding widespread impacts on the river/sea ecosystems of each subproject.
-   Prohibit direct wastewater discharge into rivers; all wastewater must be treated to meet
    standards before release into receiving waters.
-   Control chemical use in preservation and cleaning activities to avoid impacts on aquatic
    organisms.
-   Conduct awareness campaigns for fishermen and port staff on protecting water quality and
    aquatic ecosystems.
-   Collect packaging and food leftovers from operations; prohibit indiscriminate littering into
    surrounding areas or rivers/seas.
-   Raise awareness among local residents on vessel maintenance to prevent oil leakage into
    rivers/seas.
-   Comply with fishery development planning and sustainable aquatic resource management
    of the project provinces.
e) Measures to Mitigate Impacts on Traffic at the Port Area
Ensuring road traffic safety
Avoidance
-   Design port internal transport infrastructure rationally from the outset, including separate
    lanes for trucks, motorcycles, and pedestrians to avoid conflicts.
-   Schedule operating hours for different vehicle types to avoid peak-hour congestion.
-   Enforce vehicle weight checks for inbound and outbound cargo to prevent overloading,
    road damage, and ensure safety.
-   License and strictly control cargo transport vehicles, ensuring they meet technical and
    safety standards.
Mitigation/Minimization
-   Deploy traffic controllers at critical points (port gates, intersections of internal roads)

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                Page 286
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    during peak hours when fishing vessels land their catch (4:00–6:00 am).
-   Organize awareness campaigns on road and waterway traffic laws for residents and vehicle
    operators.
-   Require drivers to be trained in safe driving skills, especially those transporting cargo from
    the port.
-   Provide organized parking and waiting areas to reduce disorderly parking that obstructs
    traffic.
-   Enforce speed limits and install clear signage within the port area to minimize accident
    risks.
-   Install CCTV surveillance at critical points (port gates, intersections of internal roads) to
    detect violations in real time.
-   Conduct periodic inspections to assess traffic safety and promptly adjust management
    measures.
-   Relevant authorities such as the Port Authority, Transport Inspectors, Police, and local
    government must closely coordinate in managing and ensuring traffic safety at the port
    area.
Ensuring waterway traffic safety
Avoidance
-   Install appropriate waterway traffic signs at high-density traffic areas.
Mitigate/Minimize
-   Raise public awareness of vessel maintenance. Raise awareness of safe seafood transport:
    strict compliance with waterway traffic safety, no overloading, etc.
-   Clearly regulate waterway routes and operating schedules for different vessel groups to
    reduce conflicts.
-   Conduct regular technical inspections of vessels operating at the port.
-   Deploy waterway traffic control forces at complex intersections during peak hours.
f) Measures to Mitigate and Prevent Risks and Incidents
 Possible incidents during fishing port operation include: traffic accidents, occupational
 accidents, environmental incidents, and natural disasters/extreme weather events. Mitigation
 measures for these incidents are presented below:
 f.1. Traffic Accident Incidents
 Measures to Mitigate Waterway Traffic Accidents
Avoidance
    Measures to prevent traffic accidents from the outset:
    -   Provide formal training and issue professional certificates for all crew members and
        vessel operators, including rescue, emergency response, and safe navigation skills.
    -   Provide safety instructions for crew members on emergency situations such as fire,
        explosion, sinking, or storms.
    -   Install waterway traffic signs and signals in areas with high vessel density or complex
        hydrological conditions


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 287
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Establish coordination and traffic routing procedures at fishing ports and storm shelter
       anchorages to avoid congestion and collisions.
   -   Regularly maintain and inspect watercraft (fishing vessels, canoes, boats), especially
       engines, electrical systems, and safety equipment such as life buoys, life jackets, and
       firefighting tools.
   -   Equip vessels with modern communication systems such as VHF, radar, and GPS
       devices to monitor movement and avoid collisions at sea.
When a waterway traffic accident occurs at fishing ports or storm shelter anchorages, prompt
and accurate response is essential to ensure safety and minimize damage. The following steps
should be taken to mitigate and address such accidents:
Mitigation
   -   Check the condition of people and vessels: determine the number of injured, missing,
       or drowning-risk persons. Inspect the accident vessel to assess its condition and damage.
   -   Secure the accident area: establish a safety zone around the accident site to prevent
       additional hazards from passing vessels.
   -   Organize rescue: use rescue boats or canoes to bring injured persons safely ashore.
       Ensure sufficient life buoys, life jackets, and rescue equipment are available.
   -   Provide first aid on site: apply immediate measures such as artificial respiration,
       bleeding control, wound bandaging, and transfer to medical facilities if necessary.
   -   Notify relevant authorities immediately: contact rescue agencies, Port Authority, border
       guard forces, and other related bodies for timely assistance.
Remediation
   -   Record the situation: provide detailed accident information to investigators, including
       time, location, vessel, initial cause, and number of people affected.
   -   Identify accident causes: authorities investigate whether accidents stemmed from
       technical failures, human error, or adverse weather.
   -   Correct the incident: if the vessel is damaged (engine failure, hull damage), take
       temporary measures to tow it to a repair site.
   -   Evaluate the accident and improve procedures: review operational processes, safety
       systems, and crew training to prevent recurrence.
Measures to Mitigate Road Traffic Accidents
Avoidance
   -   Ensure access roads to fishing ports and storm shelter anchorages are built and
       maintained in good condition. Roads must be upgraded and regularly maintained to
       prevent deterioration and reduce accident risk
   -   Install clear, visible, and adequate traffic signs, especially for speed limits and high-risk
       areas (slopes, sharp curves)
   -   Set appropriate speed limits for each area, particularly around ports and storm shelters.
       Conduct regular inspections and strictly manage vehicles entering the area to ensure
       safety
   -   Provide safe driving training and road safety awareness programs for cargo drivers and
       workers.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 288
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Design transport infrastructure with adequate visibility and turning radius, meeting
       technical standards to eliminate accident-prone spots.
Minimization
   -   Maintain designated parking areas for vehicles to prevent illegal parking, congestion, or
       traffic obstruction.
   -   Establish a traffic coordination system to prevent congestion, especially during peak
       periods when vessels dock or storms occur.
   -   Ensure risk mitigation measures during adverse weather, particularly storms or heaVi
       rain, such as warning lights, anti-skid systems, and requiring vehicles to reduce speed.
   -   Develop emergency response plans for road traffic accidents, including rescue and relief
       procedures, to enable quick handling and damage reduction.
   -   Install traffic surveillance cameras at intersections and critical points to detect early
       violations and incidents.
   -   Conduct regular inspections of vehicle technical conditions, especially brakes, lights,
       and tires, for all vehicles entering the port.
   -   When a road traffic accident occurs near fishing ports or storm shelter anchorages,
       prompt and accurate handling is essential to ensure safety and minimize damage. The
       following steps should be taken for each case:
Mitigation
   -   Deploy rapid response teams at the port area to handle traffic accidents immediately.
   -   Stop vehicles at once: drivers must stop safely and activate hazard warning lights.
   -   Protect the accident scene: for serious accidents, use warning signs and hazard lights to
       halt traffic and secure the site, avoiding secondary accidents.
   -   Assess injury status: do not move victims unnecessarily. If spinal, head, or serious
       injuries are suspected, do not move them until professional rescue arrives—unless the
       site is unsafe.
   -   Provide first aid: basic measures such as bleeding control, keeping victims warm, or
       artificial respiration if needed.
   -   Call an ambulance and medical rescue team to transport victims to hospital promptly.
   -   Notify authorities: immediately call traffic police, port security, or other relevant bodies
       to handle the incident.
   -   Provide detailed information: report accurate location, time, vehicle conditions, and
       victim status to assist responders.
Remediation
Measures to address post-accident consequences:
   -   Investigate accident causes: authorities determine whether caused by driver error,
       technical failure, or road conditions.
   -   Record the accident: prepare reports with participation from relevant parties as a basis
       for further action.
   -   Clear the site: after investigation, remove damaged vehicles, repair or clean roads if
       necessary.
   -   Restore traffic: implement traffic flow control and guide vehicles safely.
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 289
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Evaluate the accident: reassess road safety measures, operations, and infrastructure to
       improve prevention.
   -   Update lessons learned: integrate findings into traffic management plans and regular
       driver/port staff training.
Risk of falling overboard and drowning for vessel operators/fishermen
Avoidance
   -   Vessels must have high stability: ensure vessels are designed for stability, especially
       when sailing in rough seas or high waves. Anti-capsize systems must be regularly
       inspected and maintained.
   -   Design vessels with clear and accessible emergency exits.
   -   Install handrails and guardrails: provide sturdy handrails or guardrails in hazardous
       areas to help fishermen move safely and prevent slips.
   -   Ensure dry, non-slippery decks by using anti-slip coating or rough surfaces to reduce
       fall risks during rain or waves.
   -   Provide spacious, unobstructed working areas on vessels, docks, and storm shelters for
       safe, easy movement.
   -   Establish pre-departure safety checks, including lifesaving equipment, communications,
       weather conditions, and crew health.
   -   Require voyage and safety logs to monitor unusual situations and improve operations
       after each trip.
   -   Assign tasks clearly and supervise closely, especially during bad weather or offshore
       trips.
   -   Update and monitor weather conditions, particularly storm seasons, to prevent sailing
       in dangerous situations.
   -   Provide regular training for fishermen and vessel operators on safety skills, vessel
       stability, and basic rescue techniques. Raise awareness on drowning risks and personal
       protective equipment.
   -   Equip vessels with early-warning systems for instability, such as horns, warning lights,
       or electronic devices suited to vessel type.
Minimization
   -   Life jackets: all crew, vessel operators, and fishermen must wear life jackets at all times
       while working on board, especially near ports or in bad weather.
   -   Safety harnesses: provide harnesses for crew working in hazardous conditions with
       strong waves or winds.
   -   Safety shoes and gloves: provide anti-slip footwear and gloves for safe movement and
       reduced fall risk.
   -   Adequate lighting: ensure full lighting in work areas on vessels and around ports at night
       to reduce slip and collision risks.
   -   Emergency communications: radios or satellite phones for contacting rescue agencies
       in emergencies.
   -   First aid/rescue stations near ports: maintain nearby stations or teams at fishing ports
       and storm shelters for quick response.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 290
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Emergency transport: provide ambulances, lifeboats, and necessary medical supplies for
       injuries or drowning cases.
   -   Personal locator beacons (PLBs): supply for crew working alone or in dangerous
       conditions, especially offshore vessels.
   -   Individual first-aid kits: equip each crew member, especially offshore, with basic
       supplies for small injuries.
   -   Encourage installation of man-overboard alarms on large fishing vessels or enclosed
       cabins if technically and financially feasible.
Mitigation
Emergency measures to reduce harm when falling overboard/drowning occurs:
   -   Conduct regular rescue drills for fishermen and vessel operators, including rescue
       techniques, lifesaving equipment use, and basic first aid.
   -   Rescue victims: if someone falls overboard, use rescue boats/canoes or throw flotation
       devices for support.
   -   Establish rapid response procedures, including assigning rescuers, deploying flotation
       equipment, and contacting rescue forces.
Remediation
   -   Record and report incidents promptly for investigation and procedural improvement.
   -   Hold debriefings with crew after each incident to update safety protocols.
   -   Conduct periodic risk assessments and update safety management and protective
       equipment accordingly.
   -   Provide psychological support and health recovery for crew after serious accidents to
       restore working capacity and reduce long-term mental impacts.
Incident: Fishing vessels docking beyond the design capacity
Avoidance
   -   The technical infrastructure and environmental treatment facilities of the fishing port
       shall be designed to accommodate the maximum number of vessels on peak days in
       accordance with the approved design.
   -   The breakwaters shall be designed in segments based on the vessel capacity, ensuring
       minimization of vessel congestion when berthing.
   -   Develop a vessel traffic management plan based on fishing seasons and catch volumes,
       in order to forecast peak periods and adjust port operations accordingly.
   -   Designate additional temporary mooring areas or buffer zones outside the port to
       regulate vessels exceeding the design capacity.
Minimization
   -   The port management authority shall assign staff to guide vessels in and out during peak
       days, ensuring order and limiting delays or security issues.
   -   Establish a pre-registration system for vessels before docking, particularly during peak
       seasons, to control traffic flow and allocate berthing positions appropriately.
   -   Apply information technology (e.g., port management software) to monitor vessel
       arrivals in real time, supporting efficient coordination.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                              Page 291
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Enhance communication and early warnings for fishermen through loudspeakers, SMS,
       or electronic boards at the port to prevent congestion.
   -   Organize vessel traffic by size, type of activity, and berthing time to optimize port space
       and usage.
Mitigation
   -   Deploy a rapid response team at the port, including security staff, coordinators, and
       technical support teams to handle emerging situations.
   -   Arrange for vessel rotation or temporary mooring outside the port, combined with
       announcements of estimated docking times to reduce immediate pressure.
Remediation
   -   Review and periodically update the vessel traffic management plan based on actual
       operations and catch growth trends.
   -   Collect and analyze data after each peak period to adjust designs, operational
       procedures, and vessel reception capacity in the future
 f.2. Measures for Prevention and Mitigation of Occupational Accidents
   Avoidance
   -   Develop a fishing port management regulation that enhances capacity on occupational
       health, safety, and community protection for the port authority, fishermen, service
       providers, and traders.
   -   The port’s operation and maintenance system must include functions to respond to
       potential impacts on occupational health and community safety caused by air pollution,
       noise, fire, explosion, traffic accidents, port security, structural collapse, landslides, or
       subsidence due to extreme weather.
   -   Design a reasonable port layout, with clear separation between mechanized equipment
       operation zones and pedestrian zones to avoid collisions
   -   Establish a pre-shift safety inspection system, including checking equipment, work
       areas, and worker health conditions.
   -   Provide clearly marked emergency exits, adequate safety signage, and arrange first-aid
       stations at strategic points within the port.
   -   Conduct regular occupational safety training, first-aid, and emergency response drills
       for all workers and port staff.
   Minimization
   Minimization measures for accidents caused by mechanized equipment such as cranes,
   forklifts, etc.)
   -   Ensure all workers operating mechanized equipment are properly trained and certified
       for safe operation.
   -   Conduct periodic inspection and maintenance of mechanized equipment.
   -   Use personal protective equipment (PPE): Operators must wear helmets, safety shoes,
       gloves, etc.
   -   Apply standard operating procedures (SOP): Workers must follow prescribed steps to
       avoid mistakes during operations
   -   Install warning systems when mechanized equipment operates near pedestrian areas.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 292
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Minimization measures for accidents caused due to unsafe working conditions
   -   Ensure safe working conditions: Work areas must be designed, constructed, and
       maintained safely, with adequate lighting, non-slippery floors, and no hazardous
       obstructions.
   -   Conduct periodic risk assessments to promptly detect and address potential hazards.
   -   Provide safety information and guidance: All workers must receive clear instructions on
       safe working procedures and safety signage at work sites.
   -   Establish a rapid feedback system when unsafe conditions are detected.
Minimization (for occupational health and disease risks)
   -   Conduct regular health check-ups for all workers to detect early occupational diseases
       and ensure workers are fit for duty.
   -   Provide adequate PPE suited to the port environment, including dust masks, gloves,
       anti-slip boots, waterproof and chemical-resistant clothing to protect workers from
       hazards such as dust, humidity, chemicals, and pathogens, thereby preventing diseases
       like pneumonia, poisoning, or dermatitis.
   -   Health education: Organize training sessions on health protection, disease prevention,
       and proper nutrition for workers.
Minimization (for environmental accident risks at the port)
   -   Maintain a clean, well-ventilated work environment, free of dust and harmful
       substances.
   -   Provide fire and explosion prevention equipment: Ensure that areas with risks (such as
       fuel or chemical storage) are equipped with adequate firefighting systems and are
       inspected regularly.
   -   Disaster prediction and response: Establish measures such as drainage systems,
       equipment protection, and evacuation plans in case of storms, high waves, or other
       disasters.
   -   Establish and implement a hazardous chemical and material management procedure at
       the fishing port, including classification, storage, transportation, and safe use of
       common substances such as:
       +   Diesel, FO/DO fuel oils, and lubricants for engines and mechanized equipment;
       +   Industrial detergents and disinfectants such as chlorine and formalin for cleaning ship
           holds and processing areas;
       +   Refrigerants such as ammonia or glycol used in cold storage systems;
       +   Industrial solvents such as acetone and xylene from ship repair activities;
       +   Flammable/explosive chemicals such as LPG and gasoline stored at fuel depots.
The procedure must ensure clear warning signs, safe isolation zones, adequate firefighting
equipment, and regular staff training on chemical spill response and emergency handling.
Remediation
   -   Conduct regular rescue drills, including rescue techniques, use of life-saving equipment,
       and on-site first aid.
   -   Protect victims: use rescue boats, flotation devices, and mobilize local forces.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 293
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Establish a rapid response team at the port to handle occupational accidents.
   -   Provide an internal emergency communication system for quick incident notification.
f.3. Measures for Environmental Incident Response
f.3.1. Oil Spill Response Measures
Oil spill response in fishing vessel operations must comply with Decision No. 12/2021/QD-
TTg dated March 24, 2021 of the Prime Minister, supplemented with practical measures and
international guidelines.
Avoidance
   -   Prohibit discharge of oily wastewater or oil-contaminated materials into rivers or
       receiving waters.
   -   Train fishermen in safe vessel operation to prevent oil leaks from engines and fuel
       systems.
   -   Conduct periodic inspection of fuel systems, engines, and oil tanks on fishing vessels to
       detect leakage risks.
   -   Prohibit use of old vessels that fail to meet fuel safety and environmental standards.
Minimization
   -   Develop, submit, and obtain approval of an oil spill response plan, and implement it
       effectively once issued
   -    Invest in equipment and materials to gradually improve self-response capacity of the
       port and waterborne transport.
   -   Organize annual training and at least biannual drills for staff and response forces.
   -   Sign agreements/contracts with competent agencies/units for oil spill response support.
   -   Equip each fishing vessel with a basic oil spill response kit (booms, absorbent materials,
       buckets, etc.). Equipment should be adapted by vessel type (e.g., offshore vessels with
       absorbents; nearshore vessels sharing equipment via fishing teams or the port).
   -   Organize oil spill response training specifically for fishermen, suited to their vessel
       conditions.
   -   Map coastal ecological sensitive areas for priority protection during oil spills.
Mitigation
   -   Proactively deploy response activities, mobilizing resources promptly and effectively
       when spills occur.
   -   Immediately notify authorities of spill location, scale, and cause, and begin on-site
       response:
       +   Ensure site safety; mobilize all available resources to contain oil; deploy booms to
           prevent spread.
       +   Use available means such as absorbents, buckets, etc., to collect oil.
       +   Mobilize local communities to assist in response.
       +   Pump remaining fuel in the affected vessel to another storage vessel to ensure safety
           during the response process. During the transfer, measures must be implemented to
           prevent any oil spillage
Compensation
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                   Page 294
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   If it is determined that the port response is ineffective or the oil spill incident may cause
       more serious consequences, promptly report to the provincial/city People’s Committee
       for direct response guidance.
   -   Coordinate with relevant local authorities to monitor and assess the effectiveness of oil
       spill remediation efforts.
   -   Temporarily store and safeguard the collected oil for reuse or to ensure compensation
       for damages caused by the spill. The handover of collected oil should be based on the
       agreed compensation amount with the vessel owner.
   -   Upon completion of remediation activities, report to the competent authorities on the
       situation and results of the incident response.
   -   Assess environmental damage after the incident in accordance with the guidelines of
       the Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment.
   -   Implement compensation for damages in accordance with the provisions of the Law on
       Environmental Protection.
f.3.2. Fire and explosion prevention and response
Prevention and response to fire/explosion incidents in fishing port and vessel operations must
comply with current laws, particularly the Law on Fire Prevention and Fighting, Decree
136/2020/ND-CP, and national technical standards.
Avoidance
   -   Train fishermen and port staff in fire prevention and firefighting skills.
   -   Comply with national fire safety standards at fishing ports, landing sites, and vessels.
   -   Equip port warehouses, offices, and logistics facilities with fire/explosion prevention
       systems in compliance with national standards (fire extinguishers, fire alarms, hydrants,
       firefighting beats/canoes, etc.).
   -   Provide portable fire extinguishers suitable for fishing vessels (e.g., ABC powder, CO₂),
       ensuring capacity to handle initial incidents.
   -   Clearly designate no-fire, no-smoking, no-welding areas at the port and on vessels.
   -   Conduct periodic inspections of electrical, fuel, and flammable systems on vessels and
       at the port.
Minimization
   -   The fire water supply system includes both indoor and outdoor firefighting water
       systems (at offices and storage warehouses).
   -   For buildings within the fish port, fish wharf, or mooring areas with a volume > 5,000
       m³, two fire hydrants must be installed in easily visible and accessible locations, with a
       water flow rate of 2.5 liters/second per hydrant. Additionally, warehouses should be
       equipped with foam fire extinguishers.
   -   Establish emergency response procedures in case of fire or explosion on vessels or at
       the port.
   -   Mobilize on-site personnel and coordinate with the local fire prevention and firefighting
       police
Compensation
   -   Assess environmental damage after fire/explosion.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 295
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Compensate damages as required by the Law on Environmental Protection.
   -   Restore sites and manage waste generated after the incident.
   f.3.3. Response measures for water supply, drainage, and wastewater treatment failures
   Avoidance
   -   Develop a maintenance plan: There should be a plan for regular maintenance and
       inspection of the water supply, drainage, and wastewater treatment systems to promptly
       detect signs of damage or system degradation.
   -   Inspect the quality of pipeline materials: Use high-quality materials for the piping
       system, such as stainless steel or corrosion-resistant plastic that can withstand pressure
       and harsh environments.
   -   Inspect system structure: Ensure that joints, valves, and other system components are
       not loose, damaged, or corroded.
   -   Comply with all design, construction, operation, maintenance, and servicing
       requirements of the wastewater treatment system.
   -   Design the water supply and drainage systems clearly separate from the wastewater
       treatment system to prevent cross-contamination in case of incidents.
   -   Choose the locations for wastewater treatment stations and technical pipelines to avoid
       areas prone to erosion, flooding, or weak soil foundations.
   -   Train wastewater treatment operators on the system’s operation and maintenance
       program.
   -   Design wastewater treatment plants for continuous operation; include shut-off valves at
       component tanks to increase storage capacity, ensuring maximum retention time in case
       of incidents.
Minimization
   -   Regularly monitor the operation of machinery and the condition of treatment tanks;
       prepare backup equipment for machinery prone to failure, such as wastewater pumps,
       blowers, sludge pumps, etc.
   -   Arrange motors/pumps in all modules with one active unit and one standby unit
       (operating alternately) to ensure the system remains functional during incidents.
   -   Provide a backup power supply for the centralized wastewater treatment station.
   -   Assign personnel to operate and supervise the wastewater treatment system according
       to the trained procedures.
   -   Install leak or abnormal pressure warning systems on main pipeline sections.
   -   Establish rapid inspection procedures following heaVi rainfall, high tides, or nearby
       construction activities.
Mitigation
   -   Design and arrange emergency tanks at each wastewater treatment station to temporarily
       store wastewater in case of emergency.
   -   In the event of equipment or machinery failure at the wastewater treatment plant,
       immediately close the discharge valve; wastewater shall be diverted to the emergency
       tank for cause and location identification. Only treated wastewater meeting standards
       may be discharged into the environment.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                Page 296
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   When an incident occurs, the wastewater treatment station operator must notify the
       management unit, isolate the faulty module, and ensure that wastewater from the faulty
       module is not discharged into the environment. Wastewater generated from project
       activities shall be stored in the emergency tank, while treatment continues normally with
       the remaining modules.
   -   After the issue is resolved, all wastewater from tanks within the faulty module shall be
       pumped back to the equalization tank for further treatment until it meets standards
       before discharge.
   -   Prepare emergency supplies in advance, such as shut-off valves, replacement pipes, and
       water pumping equipment, to enable rapid on-site response.
   -   Establish a rapid response team at the fishing port to handle environmental incidents
       related to wastewater
Remediation
   -   Conduct rapid environmental impact assessments after incidents and report to
       authorities.
   -   Restore affected systems and areas, including sludge removal, disinfection, and water
       quality testing..
5.1.4.2. Mitigation measures for operational phase impacts of Subprojects 3-> 9
 Incidents of canal/ditch bank erosion in Subprojects 3 -> 9
   -   Regularly inspect the drainage system (culverts) and embankment slopes along the
       routes to address erosion incidents that may cause soil and surface water pollution in
       the area.
   -   To minimize impacts from tidal surges, sea level rise, and seawater intrusion into inland
       areas, the management unit shall prepare a plan for periodic inspection of the entire
       embankment before the rainy and storm season. Any damaged sections will be
       immediately repaired; in urgent cases, sandbags, gabions, and tarpaulins shall be used
       to ensure the highest possible flood and tidal surge resistance.
   -
   -   The works are designed to withstand level-12 storms. In the event of storms exceeding
       this level or tsunamis causing damage, after the storm the management unit shall
       mobilize forces to inspect the entire route, then develop a repair plan to submit to higher
       authorities for timely implementation to maintain the designed storm resistance
       capacity.
   -   Localized flooding management: Construct drainage culverts as designed to ensure
       optimal water discharge capacity, and inspect/dredge the creek system before the rainy
       and storm season.
 Mitigation measures for dust and emissions at Subprojects 3 -> 9
   -   When the operation and maintenance (O&M) road is put into operation, vehicle traffic
       will become a source of air quality impacts. The following measures will be applied to
       mitigate these impacts:
   -   Prevent architectural structures from encroaching on the road traffic corridor.
   -   Vehicles must comply with load limits and maintain regulated speeds when traveling
       on the route.
   -   The road management authority must install traffic signs in necessary locations.
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 297
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
        -    Establish speed limits for vehicles traveling on the road.
        -    Vehicles must undergo regular inspections to ensure compliance with quality standards;
             non-compliant vehicles shall be subject to corrective action.
        -    Assign sanitation workers to sweep and clean the planned road once a week to minimize
             accumulated solid waste such as soil, gravel, and dust.
        -    Establish a road maintenance and environmental monitoring team to regularly inspect
             road quality and promptly address issues such as potholes, surface peeling, or
             subsidence that could damage the pavement structure.
        -    Construct speed bumps along the route, especially near intersections, road junctions,
             and sections transitioning from agricultural production areas to residential zones.
5.1.5. Minimize cumulative impact
•       Integrate climate change and sea level rise resilience into each content in the technical
        design phase for the Thanh Hoa province SFDP project.
    -       Increase concentrated and local permeable green space along canal/river/sea
            embankments, maintain and strengthen coastal protection forest systems or mangrove
            forests (if any), specific works suitable for the specific terrain of the natural drainage
            protection area; maintain water surface space, storm-resistant design, design of water
            retention basins for drought resistance and irrigation, flood-proof materials, permeable
            materials for water retention basins.
    -       Technical preparation: Calculation of elevation and hydraulics must take into account
            climate change and natural drainage factors.
    -       Proposing highly permeable materials and structures for construction of traffic works
            such as perforated concrete; Planting grass in parking lots on fishing wharves/ports, etc.
    -       Improve the capacity of rainwater and wastewater drainage by calculating the elevation
            of drainage foundations, conditions, locations of key works, drainage directions, and
            highly reliable materials that can withstand climate change and harsh weather.
•       For project level construction phase:
Coordinate with local specialized management units to:
    -       Raise fishermen's awareness of water/land use regulations and practices
    -       Monitor and comply with speed and driving/vehicle behavior,…
    -       Raise fishermen's awareness of socio-economic development opportunities and social
            issues related to risks through project programs and training in the area.
    -       Assign officers to regulate traffic in construction areas of ports/storm shelters.
    -       Raise public awareness of regulations and practices on the protection of aquatic life and
            aquaculture and fishing activities.
    -       Ensure that emissions from each individual project are minimal
    -       Regional/industry planning taking into account emissions, river/sea carrying capacity and
            pollution control requirements
•       For the operational phase:
Comply with the strategic planning for environmental protection of the project area,
including:
    -       Environmental protection area, riverside/seascape, port/anchoring area construction area.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                         Page 298
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
  -   Prohibited construction areas: Coastal protection corridors, inland waterway protection
      corridors in river/sea areas.
       + Coastal protection corridor: According to the provisions of Clause 1, Article 79 of the
       Law on Marine and Island Resources and Environment No. 82/2015/QH13, since the
       promulgation of this Law, the status quo must be maintained, and no new investment or
       construction activities are allowed within 100 meters from the average tide of many
       years to the mainland or islands as determined by the People's Committee of the
       province or centrally-run city with sea according to the guidance of the Ministry of
       Natural Resources and Environment until the coastal protection corridor is established
       according to the provisions of this Decree, except for some enclosures as prescribed by
       law.
       + Inland waterway protection corridor: The scope of inland waterway protection is
       specified in Clause 4, Clause 16. The scope of inland waterway protection corridor is
       specified in Clause 4, Article 16 of the Law on Inland Waterway Traffic, in particular:
       The scope of inland waterway protection corridor in case the waterway is not close to
       the shore level IV is 10m; The scope of inland waterway protection corridor in case the
       waterway is adjacent to the natural shore is at least 5m; in case the waterway is located
       in the city or town, the scope of the waterway protection corridor is the red line boundary
       in accordance with the planning approved by the competent authority.
  -   Area polluted by traffic and technical infrastructure activities.
5.1.6. Measures to address climate change issues
According to the Guidelines, the vulnerability of local communities to climate change may be
due to the following causes, taking into account the probability of occurrence:
  -   Increased flooding in the area due to reduced permeable surface area
  -   Increased access to ecologically sensitive locations leads to environmental degradation
      and threats to buffer zones that provide flood or drought mitigation functions
  -   Degradation of domestic water supply and environment, ecosystems related to population
      growth along the proposed route alignment.
  -   Microclimate change
To set adaptation targets, the following aspects can be considered:
  -   Temperature changes: increase in ambient air temperature in hot season and decrease in
      cold season, maximum temperature or excessively hot time can affect construction
      progress, reduce the durability and life of road and bridge structures (deformation due to
      expansion of materials / connections under the influence of heat);
  -   Changes in rainfall (depending on the rainy season) lead to increased flow intensity,
      which can cause deeper and longer flooding, slow down construction progress, and
      increase the risk of erosion and landslides;
  -   Increased intensity and frequency of extreme weather events including flooding will
      affect drainage systems, damage embankments/wharfs, and disrupt waterways;
  -   Rising sea levels can increase the risk of flooding and inundation in coastal/riverside
      areas, reduce drainage capacity and increase saltwater intrusion. These affect coastal/river
      embankment structures, corrode steel structures and steel types in reinforced concrete
      structures. As a result, roads will soon deteriorate or be damaged.
  -   Stronger and faster currents may affect the abutments and piles of
      embankments/structures on harbours/wharfs, increasing the risk of river/seaside erosion.

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 299
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province

         measures, and the costs that will be included in the service contract with CSC.
