5.3. Environmental Compliance Framework

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                   Page 302
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
5.3.1. Environmental and social responsibility of PMU/Detailed design consultant
During the preparation of Terms of Reference (TOR) for consulting services and bidding
documents for construction, the PMU will also work closely with consultants to ensure that: i)
contract package division and cost estimates include the implementation of the ESMP,
including independent safety supervision services, environmental sampling/monitoring and
compliance monitoring, reporting, etc.; ii) ESCOP and relevant general as well as site-specific
mitigation measures are incorporated into the bidding documents; iii) environmental
monitoring and training are included in the scope of work assigned to the construction
supervision consultant.
At the feasibility study / detailed design stage, the PMU will coordinate closely with feasibility
study consultants and detailed design engineers to ensure that green/landscape, environmentally
friendly solutions, and relevant mitigation measures proposed in the ESIA/ESMP are reviewed
and integrated into the technical design where appropriate.
During construction, the PMU will work closely with the supervision consultant to monitor
contractors’ compliance and report to relevant authorities. The PMU will also instruct the
supervision consultant and contractors on actions to be taken in case of issues, incidents, or
accidents, etc.
The PMU will assign at least one qualified staff member as an Environmental Officer (EO) and
at least one qualified staff member as a Social Officer (SO) throughout project implementation.
   -   The EO will monitor environmental issues and oversee the subproject’s safety
       compliance. The EO will be supported by the independent environmental supervision
       consultant, dedicated ES officers at each subproject, environmental officers of the
       construction supervision team, as well as the contractors.
   -   The SO will monitor and coordinate the social/resettlement aspects of the Project. The
       SO will be supported by the independent resettlement supervision consultant, social
       officers of the construction supervision team, as well as the contractors.
5.3.2. Environmental Responsibility of Contractors
The contractor is responsible for implementing measures to control and mitigate adverse
impacts arising from construction activities. The mitigation measures specified in the ESMP
must be fully applied to prevent impacts on the local community and the environment
throughout the construction phase.
When remedial measures cannot be effectively implemented during construction, the contractor
must carry them out during the completion stage (and prior to issuing the completion acceptance
certificate).
The contractor’s responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following tasks:
   -   Comply with relevant legislative requirements on environmental management,
       community health, and safety;
   -   Perform the work within the scope of the contract requirements and other bidding
       conditions;
   -   Assign a representative of the construction team to participate in site supervision
       organized by the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC;
   -   Implement remedial actions under the instructions of the Environmental Officer (EO),
       Social Officer (SO) of the PMU, and the Environmental Supervisor (ES) of the CSC;
   -   In case of non-compliance or non-conformance, conduct investigation and propose
       mitigation measures, as well as implement corrective actions to minimize

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 303
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       environmental/social impacts;
   -   Suspend construction activities when they generate adverse impacts beyond the
       guidance provided by the EO and ES. Propose and implement corrective activities or
       apply alternative construction methods, if necessary, to mitigate environmental impacts.
       Failure to comply may result in suspension of construction or other penalties until the
       issue is satisfactorily resolved as required by the EO and ES;
   -   If the contractor proposes to use raw material sources not mentioned in the subproject
       ESIA, the contractor shall report to the CSC and PMU and coordinate with them to
       conduct an environmental assessment of these sources to evaluate their compliance with
       national environmental requirements. Only compliant sources may be used under this
       project;
   -   The contractor shall be responsible for implementing remedial measures at its own cost.
       The contractor will also be liable for paying damages arising from non-compliance with
       the ESMP and/or applicable environmental regulations.
5.3.3. Contractor's Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)
After signing the contract, the contractor will be required to prepare and submit a Contractor's
Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP) for each contract package and submit
it to the CSC and PMU for review and approval.
The objective of the C-ESMP is to provide guidance for environmental and social management
of proposed works/activities at the Project construction sites. This is to ensure that contractors
– as well as subcontractors (if any) – carry out their work with the minimum possible
environmental impacts. The C-ESMP will detail how the contractor will mitigate impacts from
construction activities and record the contractor’s responses for inspection, supervision,
verification, internal review, and corrective actions or improvements of environmental
compliance conditions. The C-ESMP must be site-specific and include details of control
measures to be implemented on-site to minimize any potential environmental impacts from
proposed works/activities. If proposed works/activities in the C-ESMP are modified in the
Contract, the contractor shall revise the C-ESMP to reflect such changes or modifications.
The contents of the C-ESMP must include the following:
(i) Applicable policies/regulations, providing a definition of the contractor’s environmental
policy and a statement of commitment to implementing the C-ESMP.
(ii) A brief description of the document; Date of issuance; Revision status; Distribution list; and
details of staffing and access.
(iii) Applicable laws and regulations related to the requirements in the Project ESMP.
Identification of contractor permits, licenses, and approvals relevant to the C-ESMP.
(iv) Details of how the environmental and social impacts identified in the subproject ESIA will
be managed on-site, including: 1) specific measures to mitigate impacts during construction; 2)
ESCOP; 3) the Contractor’s ESMP to be developed after contractor selection and prior to
construction commencement; and 4) the Contractor’s Dredging Management Plan, which the
contractor is required to prepare.
(v) Detailed environmental and social training that all contractor staff (including
subcontractors) must undertake. At a minimum, all contractor personnel working at project sites
must: i) be familiar with and understand the C-ESMP for the works; ii) be aware of their
environmental responsibilities and legal obligations on-site; iii) comply with the workers’ Code
of Conduct; and iv) participate in health, safety, and emergency response training.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                  Page 304
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
(vi) Specific capacities, support mechanisms, and resources necessary for adequate
implementation of the C-ESMP. The detailed environmental responsibilities of all contractor
staff, including subcontractors working on-site, with appropriate knowledge, skills, and training
for specific tasks, shall be defined.
(vii) The contractor shall be responsible for preparing monthly environmental–social reports,
as part of the Progress Report required in the bidding documents, including ad-hoc reports if
any, for submission to the project owner. The contents of these reports may include the
following details:
   -   Implementation of the Contractor’s C-ESMP in compliance with the agreed program;
   -   Difficulties encountered in implementing the C-ESMP and recommendations for future
       improvement;
   -   Identification of non-compliances and non-conformances, with proposed corrective
       actions;
   -   Reports from subcontractors related to C-ESMP implementation, including minutes of
       meetings and discussions organized by the contractor.
5.3.4. Responsibilities of the Contractor's Safety and Environmental Officer (C-SE)
The contractor is required to appoint a qualified individual as the Contractor’s Environmental
and Safety Officer (C-ES) at the construction site. The C-ES must be trained in environmental
management and possess the necessary skills to communicate environmental management
knowledge to all relevant staff involved in the contract. The C-ES shall be responsible for
monitoring compliance with the contractor’s ESMP and environmental technical specifications.
The responsibilities of the C-ES include:
   -   Conducting on-site environmental monitoring to assess the contractor’s ESHS
       performance, equipment, and construction methods related to pollution control, as well
       as the adequacy of mitigation measures implementation.
   -   Monitoring compliance with environmental protection solutions, pollution prevention
       methods, control measures, and contractual requirements.
   -   Supervising the implementation of environmental impact mitigation measures.
   -   Preparing audit reports on environmental monitoring data and site environmental
       conditions.
   -   Investigating complaints and recommending corrective actions as required.
   -   Advising the contractor on environmental improvement, awareness, and proactive
       implementation of pollution prevention solutions.
   -   Proposing appropriate mitigation measures to the contractor in cases of non-compliance,
       and conducting additional monitoring as instructed by the PMU’s ES/ESO and the CSC.
   -   Reporting environmental issues to the contractor, ESO and ES (from PMU and CSC),
       submitting the contractor’s ESMP implementation reports to the PMU’s ESO, CSC’s
       ES, and other relevant agencies as required.
   -   Maintaining detailed records of on-site activities related to environmental matters.
   -   Utilizing or appointing qualified staff to implement required actions and measures to
       ensure labor-related issues are addressed, such as occupational health and safety (OHS)
       specialists to manage OHS matters.
   -   Preparing the Contractor’s Labor Management Procedures (LMP) and Contractor’s
       ESMP (C-ESMP), including OHS provisions, applicable to contracted workers under
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                Page 305
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
       the project.
   -   Submitting the contractor’s LMP and C-ESMP to the PMU/CSC for review and
       approval before mobilizing staff to the project site.
   -   Supervising subcontractors’ works, including their compliance with the contractor’s
       LMP and C-ESMP.
   -   Keeping employment records for contracted workers (including subcontractors),
       including documentation verifying minimum working age as stipulated in the LMP.
   -   Providing regular training for contracted workers on topics including, but not limited
       to, occupational health and safety, social risks such as GBV, SEA/SH, and the Code of
       Conduct to maintain good relations with local communities.
   -   Requiring primary suppliers to identify and address risks related to child labor, forced
       labor, GBV, SEA/SH, and OHS for their workers.
   -   Establishing and implementing a grievance mechanism for contracted workers based on
       the GRM outlined in this LMP, ensuring that worker complaints are addressed promptly,
       and reporting the status of grievances and resolutions to the CSC and PMU. This
       grievance mechanism shall be part of the contractor’s LMP.
   -   Ensuring that all contractor and subcontractor workers understand and sign the Code of
       Conduct before commencing work.
   -   Taking all necessary measures to address risks of sexual exploitation and abuse (SEA)
       and sexual harassment (SH) as stipulated in the contractor’s LMP/C-ESMP, and
       ensuring full compliance with these measures.
   -   Reporting incidents at the construction site and labor/OHS implementation status to the
       PMU in a timely manner.
Incident Reporting
Contractors shall notify the Construction Supervision Consultant and the Project Owner of any
incidents listed below within the agreed timeframe:
   -   Any violation of national laws, regulations, or international agreements.
   -   Any serious accidents or fatalities.
   -   Significant impacts resulting in personal property damage, such as traffic accidents,
       damage to houses/roads, or other incidents.
   -   Severe surface water/groundwater pollution.
   -   Failures of dikes/embankments at temporary disposal sites causing serious
       environmental pollution.
   -   Forest fires linked to worker behavior or labor influx.
   -   Any complaints related to sexual harassment, child abuse, or other child-related
       incidents.
   -   Complaints received regarding pollution or damages.
5.3.5. Responsibilities of the Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant
       (IESMC)
Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant (IESMC) will be contracted by
the PMU to provide several environmental protection training courses for relevant parties and
to carry out environmental inspections. The IESMC will perform monitoring tasks, including:

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                              Page 306
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
   -   Providing support to the PMU in establishing and operating the environmental and
       social management system, coordinating with the CSC team to train Contractors on the
       Project’s ESHS management requirements;
   -   Organizing training courses and implementing capacity-building activities for the PMU,
       CSC, Contractors, and relevant stakeholders on ESHS during Project implementation.
       This includes conducting training sessions to raise awareness on Gender/Labor Influx
       issues for Contractor staff, CSC staff, and PMU officers;
   -   Carrying out random field inspections to certify compliance or propose timely
       corrective/improvement actions;
   -   Conduct environmental quality monitoring sampling and prepare periodic
       environmental monitoring reports, including reports on the implementation status of the
       ESMP for the PMU and prepare environmental monitoring reports during the
       construction phase.
   -   Reviewing the Contractor’s environmental compliance, including the implementation
       of mitigation measures and related documentation;
   -   Reviewing the compliance of the PMU and CSC with the ESMP;
   -   The IESMC will also provide guidance and technical support to the PMU and EO
       regarding environmental and social issues.
5.3.6. Responsibilities of Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC)
During the construction phase, the Construction Supervision Consultant (CSC), qualified as
required by the PMU, will carry out environmental supervision. The CSC will assign
Environmental and Social (ES) staff, who will be responsible for inspecting and supervising all
construction activities to ensure that the mitigation measures set out in the ESMP are properly
implemented and that the Project’s adverse environmental and social impacts are minimized.
The CSC will recruit a sufficient number of Environmental Supervision Engineers with
adequate knowledge of environmental protection and construction project management to
perform the required tasks and supervise the Contractor’s implementation. Specifically, the
CSC’s ES staff will:
   -   Review and assess, on behalf of the PMU, whether the construction design meets the
       requirements of mitigation measures and impact management under the ESMP;
   -   Review and approve the Contractor’s Environmental and Social Management Plan
       (CESMP);
   -   Coordinate with the PMU’s Environmental Officer (EO) in reviewing environmental
       compliance at proposed new material and quarry sites, and advise the PMU on whether
       these extraction sites qualify for Project use;
   -   Verify and confirm with the PMU’s environmental monitoring procedures, including
       parameters, monitoring locations, equipment, and monitoring results;
   -   Supervise the Contractor’s implementation of the CESMP, including enforcement,
       proposing timely additional measures to improve mitigation efforts, and providing
       corrective guidance;
   -   Prepare monthly reports on the Contractor’s ESHS compliance status;
   -   Review payment requests related to environmental mitigation costs, if any.
5.3.7. Compliance with laws and contractual requirements
Construction activities will comply not only with the contractual requirements on pollution

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                              Page 307
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
control and environmental protection but also with the laws on pollution control and
environmental protection of the Socialist Republic of Viet Nam.
All reports on construction methods submitted by the Contractor to the CSC for approval will
also be sent to the ES for review to determine whether pollution control and environmental
protection measures are adequately included.
The ES will also review the progress of work items to check for any violations of relevant
environmental laws, thereby preventing potential breaches.
The Contractor will periodically provide copies of relevant documents to the PMU’s
environmental and social (E&S) staff and the CSC. These documents shall at least include
Construction Progress Reports, updated Construction Method Statements, applications for
permits/registrations under environmental protection laws, and all valid permits/registrations.
The Contractor’s on-site E&S staff (C-ES) and the CSC’s ES will also have the right to access
the Site Logbook upon request.
Upon receipt of these documents, the C-ES and ES will advise the PMU’s Environmental
Officer (EO) and the Contractor on cases of non-compliance with laws and contractual
requirements on environmental protection and pollution control, in order to guide subsequent
actions. If the SEO and ES conclude that a permit/registration application or any preparatory
work for pollution control and environmental protection may not comply with the method
statement, or may potentially lead to violations of pollution control and environmental
protection requirements, they will provide recommendations to the Contractor and the PMU.
5.3.8. Environmental complaints and the penalty system
The bidding documents will require the Contractor to arrange an Environmental, Social, Health,
and Safety (ESHS) Performance Security in the amount of 1–3% of the contract value. In the
event of serious non-compliance, environmental pollution, or social issues that the Contractor
fails to remedy within the permitted timeframe, funds from the ESHS Performance Security
will be used to pay a third party mobilized by the PMU to resolve these issues.
On the other hand, if CSC/ES/IESMC/PMU detect non-compliance by the Contractor with the
ESMP and environmental regulations during site supervision, up to 2% of the Contractor’s
monthly interim payment will be withheld. The Contractor will be granted a grace period
(determined by CSC / PMU) to correct the violation. If the Contractor satisfactorily implements
corrective actions within the grace period (confirmed by CSC / PMU), no penalties will be
applied and the withheld funds will be released to the Contractor. However, if the Contractor
fails to carry out the necessary corrective measures within the grace period, the Contractor shall
bear the costs of engaging a third party to rectify the damage (deducted from their payments).
In the event that IESMC/CSC/PMU fail to detect the Contractor’s non-compliance with
environmental regulations, these units shall be responsible for covering the costs of remedying
the violations.
Based on this Environmental and Social Compliance Framework, which includes the penalty
system, CSC will develop a detailed penalty system for environmental and social non-
compliance and submit it to the PMU for approval and implementation.
5.3.9. Reporting mechanizm
The Project Management Unit (PMU) is responsible for conducting internal supervision of the
implementation of the reporting regime. In addition, the PMU will hire an independent
monitoring agency (EMA) to carry out independent monitoring of the implementation of the
Resettlement Plan (RP) and to assess the living standards of those affected during and after the
completion of resettlement.


Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                 Page 308
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
Both internal and independent monitoring will be regular (on a monthly basis for internal
monitoring and six months for independent monitoring). A final project assessment of the
implementation of the resettlement is mandatory and a report will be prepared to confirm
whether the ESF's objectives are being achieved. Tools for monitoring and reporting on ESMP
implementation are summarized in the Table below.

5.4.     Environmental monitoring program
Based on Articles 111 and 112 of the Environmental Protection Law No. 72/2020/QH14, the
project is not required to implement a periodic environmental monitoring program during the
construction phase and the project operation phase.
However, due to the specific nature of the dredging activity with a large dredging volume, the
project will carry out monitoring of the quality of dredged materials (sediment quality). The
contractor will be required to sample and monitor the quality of the dredged material as
proposed below:
     •   Location: Two samples at each subproject with dredging activity (subproject 1 – Hoằng
         Trường fishing port and subproject 2 – Lạch Trường fishing port) and one sample for
         the remaining subprojects (subproject 3 -> subproject 9).
     •   Monitoring parameters: Salinity (excluding subproject 3 --> subproject 9), As, Hg, Cd,
         Cu, Pb, Zn.
     •   Monitoring frequency: Every 6 months (2 times during the entire construction period)
         for subprojects 1,2 and once throughout the construction period for subproject 3 -->
         subproject 9.
     •   Comparison standard: QCVN 43: 2017/BTNMT.

5.6.4. Project-level grievance redress mechanism
Within the framework of Vietnamese law, citizens who make complaints have the right to
protection. As part of the overall implementation of the project, the complaint resolution
mechanism (GRM) will be developed by the E-O, SO of the PMU, according to the procedure,
the responsible person and contact information will be prepared. It will be easy to access to
ensure that complaints will be handled and resolved at the lowest level as quickly as possible.
This mechanism will provide a framework in which complaints about environmental and safety
issues can be handled, complaints can be resolved, and disputes can be resolved in a timely
manner. The GRM will be launched before the start of construction.
During the development process, the Complaint Resolution Mechanism (GRM) will also be
managed by contractors under the supervision of the CSC. Contractors will inform affected
communities and communes of the availability of a complaint settlement mechanism to resolve
complaints and problems related to the subproject. This will be done through a public
consultation and information disclosure process under which contractors will regularly
communicate with the affected community and relevant agencies. Meetings will be held at least
quarterly, monthly, project fact sheets will be published, announcements will be posted in local
media, and announcements of upcoming activities will be posted,... Contractors should provide
contact information for any complaints when contractors communicate construction progress
to the local community.
All complaints and corresponding actions taken by contractors will be recorded in the
subproject's monitoring report. Claims and claims for damages can be made in the following
ways:
   -   Verbally: directly to CSC and/or the contractor's security guard or representative at the
       regional office.
   -   In writing: by handing over or sending a complaint to specific addresses.
   -   By phone, fax, e-mail: to the CSC, security guard or the contractor's representative.
Upon receipt of the complaint, CSC and the Contractor's safety officer or representative will
register the complaint in the complaint file and will record the facts related to the complaint
thereafter, until the complaint is resolved. Immediately after receiving the complaint, it is
necessary to copy the complaint in 4 copies. The original will be kept in the file, a copy will be
kept by the contractor safety officer, a copy will be sent to CSC and the final copy will be sent
to the PMU within 24 hours of receiving the complaint.
The information documented in the complaint will include:

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                      Page 314
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
    -   Date and time of complaint.
    -   The complainant's name, address, and contact details.
    -   A short description of the complaint.
    -   Actions are taken to resolve the complaint, including who to contact and detect at each
        stage of the complaint settlement process.
    -   The date and time when the complainant were contacted during the settlement process.
    -   Final decision of the complaint.
    -   The date, time and manner in which the complainant was notified.
    -   Signature of the complainant when the decision is issued.
Small complaints will be resolved within a week. Within two weeks (and weeks thereafter), a
written response will be sent to the complainant (by hand, post, fax, e-mail) outlining the
procedures to be carried out and the process by date.
The main goal is to solve the problem as quickly as possible by the simplest means, involving
as few people as possible and at the lowest possible level. Only if the problem cannot be solved
at the simplest level and / or within 15 days, other authorities will step in. Such a situation may
arise, for example, when a claim for damages is made, the amount payable cannot be settled, or
the causes of the damage are identified.
Complaint and complaint settlement procedures shall be carried out as follows:
Level 1 – People's Committee of commune/ward
The affected household shall submit its complaint to one of the officials of the commune/ward
People's Committee, through the village/hamlet head or directly to the commune People's
Committee, in writing or orally. The officer(s) of the commune/ward People's Committee or
the village head shall notify the ward/commune People's Committee of the complaint. The
People's Committee of the commune/ward will work directly with the affected households and
will come up with a plan to settle the complaint within 5 working days after receiving the
complaint. The Secretariat of the People's Committee of the commune/ward is responsible for
making records and recording all complaints that are being resolved. After the People's
Committee of the commune/ward issues a settlement decision, the household can continue to
complain within 30 working days. In case the second decision has been issued and the above-
mentioned household is still not satisfied with that decision, such household may lodge a
complaint with the provincial-level People's Committee.
Second level – Provincial People's Committee
Upon receiving complaints from households, the Provincial People's Committee will have 30
working days to resolve the case. The Provincial People's Committee is responsible for
compiling and archiving records of all complaints sent. After the Provincial People's Committee
issues a decision, households can complain within 45 working days. In case a second decision
has been issued but the household is still not satisfied with that decision, it has the right to lodge
a complaint with the People's Court within 45 working days. After that, the Provincial People's
Committee will have to deposit the compensation money into an account.
Third Level - Courts
In case the complainant initiates a lawsuit to the commune or provincial People's Court and the
Court shall settle in favor of the complainant. The complaint settlement decision must be sent
to the complainant and related parties and publicly posted at the head office of the relevant
People's Committee. The complainant will receive such decision after 3 days from the date of

Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                                    Page 315
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province
complaint settlement results at the ward and commune levels and 7 working days for communes
and provinces.
The GRM Officer will need to coordinate with the above levels to maintain a database of
complaints related to the Project from relevant parties, including information such as: the
content of the complaint, the source of the complaint and the date of receipt of the complaint,
etc. the full name and address of the complainant, the handling plan and existing status,
including complaints received orally and in writing. In case of necessity, the GRM Officer may
contact the complainant directly and instruct them to carry out the project's formal complaint
settlement process.
Complaint Resolution Mechanism for Workers:
Project workers (all three types: direct workers, contract workers, and main supply workers)
can directly submit their grievances to the GRM for workers. Members of this GRM will be
trained to handle worker complaints, including workplace grievances, effectively and
reasonably, in compliance with national labor regulations and the World Bank’s Environmental
and Social Standard 2 (ESS2).
         Complainant                          GRM Functions                      Timeframe
 Workers or labor unions will     • Refer worker-related complaints to the
 submit their complaints to GRM     worker's GRM.
 for Workers through one of the   • Registration, classification, and analysis
 communication channels.            of complaints.
                                  • Convene the GRM for a workers'
                                    meeting to analyze and resolve the
                                    complaint.
                                  • Provide written feedback to the
                                    complainant/contractor.
                                  • Provide           guidance           with
                                    recommendations to contractors to
                                    improve working conditions/labor
                                    management issues.

World Bank Grievance Redress Mechanism:
Individuals and communities who believe that a subproject supported by the World Bank
adversely affects their lives can submit their grievances to the existing subproject-level
grievance mechanism or directly to the World Bank’s Grievance Redress Service (GRS). This
mechanism ensures that submitted grievances are reviewed promptly to address issues related
to the subproject. Individuals or organizations affected by a subproject may also submit
complaints to the World Bank Inspection Panel to determine whether any harm has occurred or
could occur due to non-compliance with World Bank policies and procedures. Complaints can
be submitted at any time when the issue is of concern to the World Bank, and the Bank’s
management is responsible for responding. For more information on how to submit a complaint
to the World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS), please visit www.worldbank.org/grs. For
more information on submitting a complaint to the World Bank Inspection Panel, please visit
www.inspectionpanel.org.




Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Report                               Page 316
Sustainable Fishery Development Project in Thanh Hoa province




 CHAPTER 6.PUBLIC CONSULTATION AND INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
6.1.   Summary of the public consultation process
The World Bank's policies on Environmental and Social Standard No. 5 (ESS5) and
Environmental and Social Standard No. 10 (ESS10) require consultation and notification to
affected people and local authorities on environmental and social issues during the preparation
of the Project. Public consultation on the ESIA report must also comply with the requirements
of Point a, Clause 3, Article 26 of Decree 08/2022/ND-CP of the Government detailing a
number of articles of the Law on Environmental Protection.
The public consultation was conducted with the purpose of: (i) sharing information on the
proposed project items and activities with the project area community and stakeholders; and (ii)
Collecting comments and concerns on local specificities and sensitive environmental and social
issues in the project area (including 7 communes and wards in the project area) from local
authorities and communities. On that basis, the community concerns will be included in the
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment report and project solutions.
Consult with People's Committees of communes/wards and residential communities in the
project area
Direct consultation with the People's Committee of the commune/ward is carried out from date
to date, including the following main contents:
   -    Overview of the project, project investment items, identification of villages/areas within
        the project area.
   - Introduces the core requirements of the World Bank's Environmental and Social
        Framework.
   - Current status of environmental and social issues related to investment projects in
        commune/wards and remaining issues.
   - Potential environmental and social impacts of the project on the local community.
   - Community-led solutions to limit and minimize environmental and social impacts
        during project implementation.
   - Comments on the construction of the project.
Participants in the community consultation meetings include:
   - Project Management Board Representative;
   - ESIA consulting representative;
   - Representative of the People's Committee of the commune/ward;
   - Representatives of local departments and organizations
   - Village Chief/Deputy
   - Representatives of affected households
6.2.   Results of public consultation
During the preparation of the ESIA report, the project owner, with the support of the consulting
unit, organized 1 consultation meeting with the communities in the area where the 9 subprojects
will be implemented. The consultation meetings will be held in November 2024, with a total of
11 meetings and 306 participants (171 male and 135 female). Details are summarized in the
following table.

   Overall, the opinions are very supportive of the project, with a desire for the project to be implemented effectively, quickly, and with high quality.
   Some key feedback includes: (i) ensuring the minimization of environmental impacts during the dredging of the channel; (ii) minimizing the impacts
   on aquaculture activities; (iii) ensuring traffic safety (both waterway and roadway); and (iv) ensuring a reasonable construction schedule and restoring
   the original condition in case of any damage along the main transport route for materials to the subprojects.
   The prject owner has acknowledged comments of the community; This ESIA report has assessed these impacts and proposed corresponding mitigation
   measures in Chapter 5 – ESMP and commit to seriously implementing environmental impact mitigation measures proposed in the project's ESIA,
   EIA report.

6.3.   Information Disclosure
The first draft of the ESIA, SEP, LMP, and ESCP in Vietnamese was published locally at the communes
within the project area and by the Project Management Board in November 2024 for community
consultation. Based on the contents of the ESIA consulted during the meetings, the local people were
informed about the Project and provided their feedback/comments on related environmental and social
issues.
The final draft of the ESIA, along with the final drafts of the RP, SEP, LMP, and ESCP in English, was
published on the World Bank's external website in September 2025 for public consultation. The final
ESIA in both Vietnamese and English will be published locally at project sites and on the World Bank
website, with an expected release in October 2025.
APPENDICES
            APPENDIX 1. DREDGED MATERIALS MANAGEMENT PLAN (DMMP)
This DMMP (Dredged Material Management Plan) is prepared during the feasibility study (FS) phase.
The DMMP will be updated by the Detailed Design Consultant, then included in the bidding documents
and contracts of the construction packages with dredging activities of 9 sub-projects along with IESMC,
worker code of conduct and other mitigation requirements in the ESMP. After signing the contract, the
Contractor will prepare the Contractor's DMMP (C-DMMP) based on the DMMP in the contract and
submit it to the Project owner/construction supervision consultant for approval before construction
commencement.
In addition, this DMMP proposes measures to minimize impacts on water quality/biology through
selection of dredging methods and procedures; mitigation measures and environmental quality
monitoring programs.
      Introduce
Thanh Hoa SFDP Project consists of 9 subprojects, each of which will have dredging activities in the
navigation channel/fishing port/storm shelter area and irrigation canals.
In addition, this DMMP recommends measures to reduce water quality/biological impacts through
dredging method and procedure selection; mitigation measures and environmental quality monitoring
programs.
Overall, the above mentioned infrastructure investment items will bring about positive impacts.
However, negative environmental and social impacts will also occur during the construction phase of
the works. In particular, for the dredging and embankment of ports/anchorage/irrigation canals, the site
clearance and dredging process will generate a large volume of excavated and dredged materials that
need to be disposed of (about 462,142 m3). Specifically as follows.
        Table Annex 1: Volume of excavated soil, dredged materials generated by the project

                                                                                Volume of         Volume of
                       Demolition                                Volume of
                                      Volume of     Dredging                  excavated soil    excavated and
                        volume                                    leveling
                                      excavated      volume                    used for on-        dredged
 No.     Subprojects                                              (backfill
                         (m3)          soil (m3)      (m3)                      site filling    materials to be
                                                                 soil) (m3)
                                                                                   (m3)          dumped (m3)

                           1              2            3             4              5              6=2+3-5
  1     Subproject 1             0       14,290       18,800         26,952          14,290              18,800
  2     Subproject 2           1511       5,135      365,000          41307             5,135          365,000
  3     Subproject 3             0            253         94         20,049              253                 94
  4     Subproject 4            49       13,683       12,219         38,598          11,611              22,892
  5     Subproject 5           135        9,813       10,829         11,737             4,924            15,717
  6     Subproject 6           146        8,728        4,142         14,517             2,894             9,976
  7     Subproject 7            18        9,392        8,931         17,687             3,645            14,677
  8     Subproject 8           297        7,599        8,731         12,604             4,813            11,517
  9     Subproject 9           420       17,789        1,240         18,860          15,560               3,469
        Total               2,576        86,682      429,986        202,311          63,125            462,142

                                                               (Source: Project Basic Design Estimate, 2025)
The project’s volume of excavated soil and excess dredged materials during the construction phase must
be transported to landfills to avoid environmental impacts and to be utilized as resources in the future.
Most of the dredged volume (mainly sand) can be utilized as sand resources (filling sand - provided it
meets the standards for construction materials) on the basis of future resource auctions according to the
provisions of the Law on Minerals. However, without appropriate management plan, excavated and
dredged materials will affect the surrounding environment as well as the exploitation and aquaculture
activities of fishermen and aquaculture workers in the area.
Therefore, this Dredged Materials Management Plan (DMMP) is prepared to comprehensively manage
the impacts and risks during dredging and construction of the Project’s works.
DMMP covers the main contents as follows:
         i.   Dredging scope and method
        ii.   Key characteristics of the current status of rivers/seas/canals to noted
       iii.   Volume and properties of excavated and dredged materials
       iv.    Description of proposed storage areas and transportation options for excavated and dredged
              materials
        v.    Environmental and social impacts and risks of dredging activities on rivers/seas
       vi.    Environmental and social impact and risk mitigation plan
       vii.   Environmental monitoring and supervision plan
    viii.     Reporting requirements.
1. Dredging scope and method
1.1 Dredging scope
Subproject 1 – Hoang Truong port
Dredging of anchorage area and navigation channel with total dredging volume of about 18,800 m3,
including:
   -      Dredging the anchorage area: The dredging area is the water area in front of the wharf with a
          length of about 800 m, the width of the berthing area ranges from 12-20 m and of the channel is
          about 40 m; The anchorage area for ships L=12 m – 24 m; The dredged bottom elevation is from
          -2.9 m to -4.0 m.
   -      Dredging the access channel to the anchorage area: crest elevation according to Article 4.4
          TCVN 11820-5:2021 is: (i) Main conditions: Wharf surface elevation = H50% + 2.0m = 0.27 +
          2 = +2.27 (m); H50%: the water level monitored over many years (not less than 10 years) with
          50% probability of occurrence; (ii) Inspection conditions: Wharf Crest Elevation = H1% + 1.5m
          = 1.53 + 1.5 = +3.03 (m); H1%: the water level monitored over many years years (not less than
          10 years) with 1% probability of occurrence; In addition, the wharf crest elevation also takes
          account into the 2020 Climate Change Scenario, the sea level rise in the Hon Dau - Deo Ngang
          area by 2080 is about 50cm on average (from 34cm to 71cm according to the RCP8.5 scenario -
          taken as 73cm); and the elevation of detailed construction planning at a scale of 1/500 of the area
          has been approved in the area, connecting to the regional infrastructure network with a planned
          elevation of +2.35m. At the same time, taking consideration into current elevation of the fishing
          ports in operation along with the projected sea level rise due to climate change, the wharf crest
          elevation as +2.35m (State system) is recommended.
   -      Turning basin and channel: The project area has 4 turning areas with diameters of 2 turning areas
          for ships up to 12m, each with a diameter of D40m, 1 turning area for ships up to 17m with a
          diameter 55m and 1 turning area for ships up to 24m, with a diameter 75m. The dredging
          elevation of turning basin and channel is -2.9m (State system).
Subproject 2 – Lach Truong:
   -   The dredging volume of water area, turning area and ship channel is about 48,380 m3 and storm
       shelter area is about 321,620 m3.
   -   Dredging the water area in front of the wharf: Width of about B = (40~50) m, length of 330m
       (The dredging area in front of the wharf does not affect the inland waterway of Lach Truong
       river). The dredged bottom elevation from -3.6m to -4.1m.
   -   Dredging the access channel to the anchorage area and storm shelter anchorage area for fishing
       boats in Lach Truong: Length of approximately L=1,150.0m; bottom width of B=89-96m.
       Dredged bottom elevation from -3.6m to -4.1m.

       area: trapezoidal canal cross-section: (12 × 2.5) m, canal slope m = 2.0.

       2.25) m, canal slope m = 1.5. (ii) Section 2: 1,225.60 m – cross-section (8 × 2.35) m, canal slope

       management road with 90m long and 3m wide.
1.2. Dredging method
General method
   -   Before dredging, it is necessary to conduct surveys, clearance of unexploded ordnance (UXO)
       and removal of obstacles according to the regulations applicable to each subproject scope.
   -   Surveying works:
        + Re-survey the site, verify existing benchmarks, and establish additional benchmarks (if
          necessary);
        + Develop a construction control grid as the basis for all surveying work as the basis for all
          geodetic activities; use this network to perform elevation profiling and control vertical
          deviations and elevation levels of structural components.
        + Accurately position the project according to the design, check the deviation in elevation of
          the project components;
        + Positioning for construction of all project items;
        + Subsidence monitoring, deformation observation;
        + Perform as-built drawings and measurements upon completion of construction; Comply with
          specific contents specified in construction standards for surveying work.
   -   For subproject 1,2: Before carrying out the above steps, and before commencing dredging
       activities, handing over the site and control benchmarks for the construction process, execute
       procedures to apply for construction permits, and approve marine safety plans;
   -   Depending on the geology of the dredging area, an appropriate construction method is selected:
       hydraulic suction dredger or a grab bucket system will be used combined with barge to transport
       to the dredging materials to the disposal site which must be accurately identified in line with the
       requirements for dredging collection, especially those rated to environmental protection.
   -   Supervision of construction and transportation of dredged materials must be in compliance with
       legal regulations; ensuring the disposal of dredged materials takes place at the designated
       locations.
Specific dredging methods
   * For dredging of wharf and storm shelter anchorages – Subproject 1,2
   -   Subproject 1: Construction method: use a grab bucket system combined with barge to transport
       the dredged materials to the disposal site, the disposal distance is L=3km; the dredged materials
       will be pumped from the barge onto the disposal site at the low-efficiency salt field in Van Loc
       commune.
   -   Subproject 2: For dredging the storm shelter area, hydraulic suction dredgers are used to pump
       the dredged materials to the disposal site through a pipeline system with pipeline distance of
       L=0.5 km. The dredged material consists mainly of clayey silt containing organic humus, which
       is pumped directly into the disposal site located in the low-efficiency salt field area of Van Loc
       Commune, adjacent to the storm shelter anchorage area. nclosed by a containment embankment
       system to prevent sediment leakage and is equipped with wastewater filtration outlets, allowing
       water to drain into the existing canal system before flowing into the river.
   -      Construction method in dredged material disposal site: this disposal site will be enclosed by
          sandbags (s and may be sourced from dredged material if it meets quality requirements), with
          the crest elevation built according to the approved planning elevation, averaging approximately
          4 m); During the disposal process, a primary settling pit and a water collection pit will be
          constructed. Leachate from the dredged materials will flow through a temporary drainage ditch
          to the water collection pit, then flow to the receiving source.
   * For dredging irrigation canals – Subproject 3 → Subproject 9
Step 1:
Due to the extended alignment of the project’s longitudinal components, construction will be carried out
using a segmented approach. Branch roads are ultilized for transporting soil, materials, and equipment
to the site. The existing canal bed will be utilized for flow diversion during construction. The existing
canal bed will be utilized for flow diversion during construction. For sections located on the existing
canal bed, anti-softening mats will be used to support excavators during operation. Average mud
dredging depth is 0.50m, based on geotechnical data from the approved design phase. Equipment and
machinery will be mobilized at both ends of the canal, with construction proceeding from both ends
toward the center:
   -      For Phu Son 2 canal, section from K - K0+809.89 m: Use Lassen III sheet piles to enclose the
          construction area of the right bank embankment, and divert the flow to allow construction of the
          remaining section of the drainage canal.
   -      For For Bao Trieu Canal (Ong Ty Canal): During construction, a pontoon is used to support the
          excavator for dredging the canal according to the design cross-section, allowing flow diversion
          through the existing drainage canal.
   -      For Subprojects 4, 5, 6, 7 in Hoang Yen, Hoang Luu, Hoang Phong, and Hoang Chau communes:
          During construction, a pontoon is used to support the excavator for dredging the canal bed
          according to the design cross-section, so flow diversion is not required.
   -      Main drainage canal, segment from Ong Ninh sluice to Quay Co sluice, section from K0+429.14
          – K0+488.90 m (right bank embankment, downstream of Ong Hong sluice): Use bamboo piles
          and woven bamboo screens to form twin-pile cofferdams for constructing the right bank
          embankment and divert the flow for the remaining section of the drainage canal.
   -      Dredging in-field canal branch: During dredging, a pontoon is used to support the excavator for
          dredging the canal according to the design cross-section, allowing flow diversion through the
          existing drainage canal.
   - Canal from Ong Thuoc sluice to Ong Vanh sluice: Flow diversion follows the existing canal.
Step 2:
   -      Drive bamboo piles to reinforce the foundation within the construction area of the toe beam for
          the riverbank revetment.
   -      Construct the toe beam, complete the cross-section of the revetment and canal bank foundation,
          and simultaneously build the operation and maintenance road according to the design cross-
          section.
Step 3:
   -      Dredging the main canal bank according to design: Excavators are positioned on both sides of
          the canal, shape the canal slopes as designed. Excavated material is loaded onto trucks and
          transported to designated disposal site.
Step 4:
-   Complete embankment foundation according to design cross-section. Finish the reinforcement
    of crushed stone layers and concrete on the canal bank.
-   The machinery and equipment expected to be used by for subprojects with dredging activities
    will be as shown in the following table:

2. Main characteristics of current status of rivers/seas/canals and related activities
Before developing the construction method, the Contractor must thoroughly review the technical
documents of each subproject related to dredging activities in rivers, seas, and irrigation canals, including
the creation of navigation channels, turning basins, and irrigation canal, as well as the Environmental and
Social Impact Assessment (ESIA). The following characteristics should be carefully considered when
preparing the C-DMMP:
Subproject 1 – Hoang Truong:
The planning study area of Subproject 1 is a section of nearshore water surface, including part of the
land area currently undergoing leveling. The terrain elevation varies from the shoreline to the boundary
of the study area, ranging from 3.6 m to –7.25 m. The slope direction of the shoreline area gently
descends from the Southwest to the Northeast. The water surface occupies the majority of the area, while
the shoreline is divided into two zones: (i) Eastern zone: Existing stone revetment system and mooring
pillars for port vessels. (ii) Western zone: Spontaneously leveling area including a water basin and open
land used by private individuals for boat repair, interspersed with temporary tin-covered structures of
simple construction. Total area of the subproject 1 is 125,091 m², including: Water surface: approx
121,879.2 m², Creek bank: approx 2,870.6 m², Existing stone revetment land: approx 341.2 m².
Current status of port infrastructure includes:

   -   The embankment of the wharf is about 400 m long, is located within the boundary of the planning
       study area. The embankment structure consists of tightly stacked rubble stones. Many sections
       of the embankment have been degraded, with sediment accumulation along the embankment
       accompanied by unauthorized land reclamation by surrounding residents for informal business
       activities over the years, reducing its operational effectiveness.
   -   The private boat repair area is scattered along the Linh Truong foothill, located outside the
       boundary of the planning study area, and is arranged alongside spontaneously reclaimed land
       plots and ship-repair rail tracks that fall within the planning study boundary. This spontaneous
       development reflects shortcomings in management due to the port area not yet being fully
       invested and properly planned.
   -   The navigation channel is shallow due to lack of dredging, making it very difficult for high-
       capacity vessels to enter and unload cargo.
   -   Port infrastructure system has not been invested in (wharf, access roads, lighting system, roof,
       etc.).
   -   Currently, it is estimated that this location serves as the unloading point for more than 300 fishing
       vessels from Hoang Truong Commune and neighboring areas, with an annual throughput of
       approximately 2,000–3,000 tons.
Subproject 2 – Lach Truong:
   -   Current status of Hoa Loc Fishing Port: It is a Class II fishing port with an area of about 3.5ha;
       the storm shelter basin has a total area of 19.5 ha, capable of accommodating vessels with
       capacities up to 400 CV. Hoa Loc Fishing Port is located in Hau Loc District (currently Hoa Loc
       Commune), Thanh Hoa Province, and is one of the province’s major fishing centers. The port
       was invested in and put into operation in 2007 as a Class II structure, designed to serve 200 to
       300 vessels with capacities ranging from 135 to 450 CV. The current length of the port is about
       270.0m, of which 120m are designated for fishing vessels over 400 CV, and 150m are designated
       for vessels from 90 to 400CV.
   -   Lach Truong storm shelter anchorage area was invested in and put into operation in 2012, with
       a designed capacity of 264 vessels. (i) Zone 1 for vessels with capacities from 250 to 400 CV:
       this zone starts from the entrance of De Canal, runs along the canal, and ends at a point 425
       meters from the canal entrance. (ii) Zone 2 for vessels with capacities from 90 to 250 CV: this
       zone begins at the end of Zone 1 and extends to La La Temple, with a length of 725 meters.
       Vessels are arranged to anchor on both sides. (iii) (iii) Zone 3 for vessels with capacities up to
       90 CV: this zone begins at La La Temple Wharf, runs along the De Canal dike to the north,
       passing through Vich ferry terminal, with a total length of 680m. Zones 1 and 2, with a combined
       length of about 1.15 km, were invested in and put into operation in 2014, including the
       components of: channel expansion and dredging, dike construction, and installation of mooring
       systems for fishing vessels. After nearly 10 years of effective use and operation, the anchorage
       area has now been heavily affected by flooding and the lack of regular dredging to clear the
       channel. This has led to significant sediment accumulation within the basin. As a result, when
       the tide is low, mooring buoys rest on the mud surface. The anchorage area has been reduced in
       size, while the number of vessels arriving and docking continues to increase, making entry and
       exit for storm sheltering increasingly difficult and hazardous.
   -   According to the current survey, the two anchorage areas have experienced significant
       sediment accumulation. At the entrance to the basin, when the tide is low, the water level is
       approximately elevation –1, allowing only small vessels under 50 CV to enter. The two
       anchorage zones have been severely silted, with specific details as follows:

        ➢ Zone 1: Length of 425m, accommodates vessels up to 400 CV. Currently, at low tide, the
          water depth in the mooring channel is only about 0.5m; during high tide, the depth reaches
          approximately 1.8m. The area near the ferry terminal from Hoa Loc to Hai Loc and Hoang
          Hoa has been severely silted.
        ➢ Zone 2: Length of 725m, accommodates vessels up to 250 CV. Currently, at low tide, the
          water depth in the mooring channel is only about 0.2 meters; during high tide, the depth
          reaches approximately 1.5m.
   -   According to Decision No. 5109/QD-UBND dated December 30, 2016 approved by Thanh Hoa
       Provincial People’s Committee. The land area assigned to Hoa Loc Fishing Port Management
       Board for management and use is: 220,791.1 m2. It is used for the purpose of building the Fishing
       Port Headquarters and storm shelter anchorage area, specifically: The area for building the Hoa
       Loc Fishing Port Management Board Headquarters is 2,237.6 m2; Hoa Loc Fishing Port land is
       30,007.3 m2; Land for Lach Truong storm shelter anchorage (land in the storm shelter basin area)
       is 188,516.2 m2.
Subproject 3 – Nga Tien commune:
   -   Current land use status: It is the land designated for construction works running along the
       drainage canal bank, integrated with the operation and maintenance road, internal access roads,
       and the protection corridor of the structure in accordance with current legal regulations.
   -   Dredging of main and branch drainage canals, upgrading and expanding canal banks combined
       with construction of operation and maintenance roads are all aligned with the existing
       infrastructure routes. Therefore, the land area and residential houses along the route are not
       affected. The affected land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds along the canal route, as well
       as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones located adjacent to the dike foot.
Subproject4 – Hoang Tien commune:
   -   Current land use status: The land for construction works along the banks of the drainage canal
       combined with the operation and management road, internal traffic road and the construction
       protection corridor according to the provisions of current law.
   -   The Hung Tien canal currently has a narrow channel bed and is heavily silted, with many sections
       of the canal banks eroded. As a result, drainage is slow, causing waterlogging and insufficient
       water supply for aquaculture. The existing culvert under the dike remains in good condition and
       still ensures adequate drainage capacity, so its current state will be maintained.
   -   Dredging of the main and branch drainage canals, upgrading and expanding of canal banks
       combined with the construction of operation and maintenance roads are all aligned with the
       existing infrastructure routes. Therefore, the land area and residential houses along the route are
       not affected. The affected land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds along the canal route, as
       well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones located adjacent to the dike foot.
Subproject 5 – Hoang Chau commune:
   -   Current land use status: The land for construction works along the banks of the drainage canal
       combined with management and operation roads, internal traffic roads and construction
       protection corridors according to current regulatory laws.
   -   Dredging Dong Coi canal, Cau Dua canal, Hoi Trieu canal and Tay canal, which are the main
       drainage canals, branches, upgrading and expanding the canal banks combined with building
       management and operation roads are all along the existing construction route. Therefore, the land
       area and residential houses along the route are not affected. The majority of the land area consists
       of aquaculture ponds located along the canal route, as well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones
       situated adjacent to the dike foot.
Subproject 6 – Hoang Chau commune:
   -   Current land use status: The land for construction works running along the canal bank from the
       Tay dike of the Cung River to the Con Voi sluice and the village access road—Ngo Hao, Phuong
       Ngo 2 Village. This land serves the function of water supply and drainage, integrated with the
       operation and maintenance road, internal access roads, and the protection corridor of the structure
       in accordance with current legal regulations.
   -   Dredging of the main and branch drainage canals, upgrading and widening of canal banks
       combined with the construction of operation and maintenance roads are all aligned with the
       existing infrastructure routes. Therefore, the land area and residential houses along the route are
       not affected. The affected land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds located along the canal
       route, as well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones situated adjacent to the dike foot.
   -   Serves drainage for over 200ha aquaculture. The project area includes 170 ha of aquaculture
       land. Currently, the main supply and drainage canal lacks inlet and outlet culverts at the canal
       head, resulting in sediment accumulation and canal bank erosion. In addition to supplying and
       draining water for aquaculture, the main canal also serves as a drainage channel for the local
       sedge cultivation area. However, the canal system still lacks culverts for water regulation.
Subproject 7 – Hoang Chau commune:
   -   Khe River Canal provides drainage for more than 400 hectares of aquaculture. The project area
       has an aquaculture area of 60 hectares. The current status of the farming area is there is no any
       drainage culvert at the beginning of the canal, so the canal is silted up and the canal banks are
       eroded. In addition to supplying water to the farming area, the main canal also drains water for
       the local sedge growing area, but the system lacks sluices to regulate water.
   -   The Song Khe canal provides water supply and drainage for over 400 hectares of aquaculture.
       The project area includes 60 hectares of aquaculture land. Currently, the main supply and
       drainage canal lacks inlet and outlet culverts at the canal head, resulting in sediment
       accumulation and canal bank erosion. In addition to supplying and draining water for
       aquaculture, the main canal also serves as a drainage channel for the local sedge cultivation area.
       However, the canal system still lacks culverts for water regulation.
   -   Current land use status: is the land designated for construction works running along the drainage
       canal bank, integrated with the operation and maintenance road, internal access roads, and the
       protection corridor of the structure in line with current legal regulations.
   -   Dredging of main drainage canal—1 main canal and 3 branch canals—along with upgrading and
       widening of canal banks combined with the construction of operation and maintenance roads, all
       follow the alignment of existing infrastructure. Therefore, the land area and residential houses
       along the route are not affected. The impacted land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds located
       along the canal route, as well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones situated adjacent to the dike
       foot. The total length is about 3.05 km, including 1.5 km of main canal and 1.55 km of branch
       canals.
Subproject 8 – Van Loc commune:
   -   The Da Loc commune project area is 64 ha, which has been planned as a concentrated
       aquaculture area under the sustainable aquaculture project since 2003. Currently, the main supply
       and drainage canal, which sources water from the Quay Co sluice under the right dike of Len
       River, has been silted and lacks inlet and outlet sluice at the canal head. The Ong Ninh sluice,
       located at the end of the main supply canal, was constructed in 2006. It is a circular culvert with
       a small diameter that no longer meets the water supply and drainage requirements. The operating
       gate has rusted, and the sluice is now severely degraded.
   -   Current land use status: This is the land for construction works running along the drainage canal
       bank, integrated with the operation and maintenance road, internal access roads, and the
       protection corridor of the structure in accordance with current legal regulations.
   -   Dredging of the main and branch drainage canals, upgrading and widening of canal banks
       combined with the construction of operation and maintenance roads are all aligned with the
       existing infrastructure routes. Therefore, the land area and residential houses along the route are
       not affected. The affected land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds located along the canal
       route, as well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones situated adjacent to the dike foot.
Subproject 9 – Quang Chinh commune:

   -   The project area in Quang Trung Commune covers 172 ha and has been zoned as a centralized
       aquaculture area under the sustainable fishery development project since 1990. Currently, the
       main supply and drainage canal, which sources water from the Ong Thuoc sluice under the right
       dike of the Hoang River, has been silted. The inlet and outlet sluices at the canal head have
       severely deteriorated and no longer have sufficient operational capacity. The saltwater control
       sluice gate, Ong Vanh, located at the end of the main canal, has also degraded.
   -   Current land use status: This is the land for construction works running along the drainage canal
       bank, integrated with the operation and maintenance road, internal access roads, and the
       protection corridor of the structure in accordance with current legal regulation
   -   Dredging of the main and branch drainage canals, upgrading and widening of canal banks
       combined with the construction of operation and maintenance roads are all aligned with the
       existing infrastructure routes. Therefore, the land area and residential houses along the route are
       not affected. The affected land mainly consists of aquaculture ponds located along the canal
       route, as well as irrigation surfaces and buffer zones situated adjacent to the dike foot.

VIMCERTS 320 certificate) at each subproject area. Environmental sampling was carried out on 23, 24
and 30/10/2024. There were 11 excavated soil samples and 11 sediment samples (1 sample for each
subproject, 3 samples for subproject 3). The quality analysis results are shown below.

     4. Planned landfills and main transportation routes
The project’s volume of excess dredged material during the construction phase will have to be
transported to landfills to avoid environmental impacts and to be utilized as resources in the future.
Based on the factors such as volume generated, nature/composition, transportation distance and current
status of the project area, landfills for excess materials of each subproject have been selected as follows:
