   5. Summary of potential social and environmental impacts and risks
5.1 Overview
Chapter 4 of the Social Environmental Impact Assessment analyzed and assessed detailed social
environmental impacts of the dredging activities of each subproject, specifically:
Dredging and material transportation activities:
      - Generate noise from dredging equipment
      - Generate dust, exhaust gases, wastewater and about 462,142 m3 of dredged material.
      - Cause disturbance to the water area, increase turbidity, affect aquatic life and water-dependent
        activities such as agriculture and aquaculture, and lead to disruption or reduced productivity in
        aquaculture and fishing operations.
      - Disrupt waterway traffic and increase risks to waterway traffic safety
      - Cause damage or disruption to existing structures
      - Restrict people’s access from the shore to the river and vice versa (during port embankments)
      - Pose risks to workers’ and community’s health and safety
      - Pose risks of accident, incident.
Dumping activities:
      - Risks of material spillage into surrounding areas
      - Risk of flooding of surrounding areas due to water discharge from the disposal site
      - Risks to the community safety
In addition, there are other site-specific impacts and risks depending on the construction location. Below
is the summary of typical impacts and risks associated with dredging activities, transportation, and
material disposal at the disposal site.
5.2. Impacts and risks along the transportation route
-   Dust from the process of collection and transportation of excavated/dredged materials along the
    transportation route at the disposal site;
-   Increased noise from the process of transporting excavated/dredged soil and people around the
    disposal site;
-   Generation of construction wastewater from leakage during the transportation process by barges
    equipped with bottom discharge gates to the disposal site. – subproject 1,2;
-   Wastewater leakage from dredged materials during transportation from subproject 3 to subproject 9:
    The dredged material contains organic components and soil-sand sediments. If leakage occurs along
    the transportation route, it can negatively impact the air environment (due to road transportation by
    trucks) and affect the water environment when runoff enters the canal system along the transportation
    corridor to the disposal sites. It also cause loss of beautiful landscape along the transportation route;
-   Increased risk of collisions, congestion, and even traffic accidents along the transportation routes to
    the receiving sites for surplus and dredged materials, including Hai Loc Commune (now Van Loc)
    – subproject 1,2; Nga Tien commune for subproject 3 (Mr. Mai Sy Tuan’s site and Long Son site);
    Son Trang village, Hoang Yen commune (subproject 4); Hoang Phong commune – now Hoang Chau
    (subproject 5); Hoang Luu commune – now Hoang Chau (subproject 6); Hoang Chau commune
    (subproject 7); Da Loc commune – now Hoa Loc – now Van Loc (subproject 8); and Quang Trung
    commune – now Quang Chinh (subproject 9).
5.3 Impacts and risks at disposal site
In general, the volume of excavated and dredged materials that need to be disposal of from the project
is relatively large (462,142 m3), requiring a large area of land for storage and treatment. The project has
identified 09 disposal site (including 1 site for excavated and dredged materials of subproject 1,2 and 2
sites designated for subproject 3) to store and manage these materials. At each site, the following key
environmental and social risks and impacts may potentially arise:
Risk of overflow and erosion. When the disposal site is significantly elevated compared to the
surrounding ground level, dredged material may overflow into adjacent areas, covering vacant land or
productive land, vegetation, or infrastructure—thereby affecting local livelihoods and agricultural or
aquaculture activities. If the surface of the disposal site is not reinforced or covered after dredged
material is deposited, exposure to air and wind may further increase the risk of erosion.
Risk of localized flooding. Processed material at the disposal site may disrupt the existing drainage
system. Rainwater can accumulate on the material and then flow into the surrounding environment,
potentially causing localized flooding. Additionally, after wet material is pumped into the disposal site,
leakage water will be discharged. This water may have high turbidity, thereby reducing the quality of
receiving water bodies and affecting the development of aquatic species;
Wastewater leakage and its impacts. After being pumped from the barge to the disposal site, the wet
dredged material will gradually settle and release water. The leakage water will flow out of the disposal
site by gravity. Experience from other river, bay, and coastal dredging projects shows that: (i) the
sediment-to-water ratio in dredged material is 8:2, meaning water accounts for 20% of the total dredged
volume; (ii) in bays and coastal areas, the sediment-to-water ratio can be around 6:4, meaning water
accounts for 40% of the total dredged volume. This indicates that the volume of leakage water from
dredged material is quite large. If not properly controlled, this leakage water can cause localized flooding
around the disposal sites. It may also have relatively high turbidity (due to fine particles from excavated
and dredged material), leading to sedimentation in water bodies near wharves, canals, irrigation ditches,
and receiving waters.
Spill/discharge of dredged material into the surrounding environment. Each disposal site will have a
specific design height for the storage area. When waste material is deposited at the edge or top of the site,
it may overflow beyond the designated boundaries. Additionally, the slopes formed at the disposal site
may collapse under load, causing the material to spill outside the intended area, covering vegetation or
filling existing infrastructure—and in some cases, potentially causing accidents involving people.
Safety risks to local communities. Since barges will anchor at a distance from the final disposal site,
pipelines will be installed to send the material to the site. The pipeline themselves, wet material leaking
from pipeline pose safety risks to the local community—for example, residents may accidentally collide
with the pipes for various reasons, or pipeline failures may cause accidents for people nearby. After
disposal, the dredged material and ground at the site will only become stable once most of the
moisture/water has been drained from the dredged material. If someone enters unstable disposal areas,
accidents such as slipping or even drowning may occur.
   6. Impactsand risk mitigation plan
During the construction process, the Contractor shall comply with the requirements and take measures
to minimize environmental and social impacts as stated in the Environmental and Social Code of Practice
(ESCOP), the Code of Conduct for workers, requirements on minimizing environmental and social
impacts of dredging and construction of quaysides. The Contractor shall also comply with the measures
and requirements set forth in this DMMP, including.
6.1 Personnel arrangement
In the bidding documents, the contractor must propose at least one staff member with appropriate
qualifications, certifications, and experience to be responsible for safety, environment, and health for the
package implemented by the contractor. These personnel must be mobilized and work throughout the
contract implementation period to carry out supervision and reporting tasks within their assigned field.
The contractor shall facilitate the participation of these personnel in training courses relevant to their
responsibilities, organized by the Project.
6.2 Design of the disposal site for dredged materials
  -   The disposal site must be determined in advance, in accordance with approved construction
      specifications. Drainage ditches will be constructed around the site to prevent wastewater from
      flowing directly into the surrounding environment.
  -   Store excavated/dredged soil in the designated disposal site approved by the competent authority
      as mentioned above with the required height, area and reserve.
  -   Appropriately calculate and design embankments and surrounding drainage ditches. Regularly
      inspect and reinforce embankments and drainage systems to maintain stability.
  -   Regularly monitor the waste disposal site by the construction contractor, construction supervisor
      and Project Owner.
  -   Manage according to a separate plan, ensuring no release into the environment.
  -   Set out plan for covering the surface during the period when the disposal site area is not yet in use,
      in accordance with local regulations/planning. After the disposal site is filled, it is necessary to
      consider covering it with a layer of soil (if needed) to plant greenery in order to stabilize the
      surface, prevent erosion, and reduce dust.
  -   Have a design and planning option for the excavated/dredged material disposal site that are filled
      and stabilized. These sites may be rehabilitated into construction land or green space, depending
      on the approved planning.
6.3 Mitigation measures to be taken by contractors during dredging
To manage the potential environmental and social impacts and health and safety risks of the dredging
process, including material handling and transportation, the Contractor must perform and/or comply with
the following requirements:
  -   Mitigation measures are outlined in the detailed design and specifications of the tender documents,
      including dredging methods, transportation procedures and final proposals. The Contractor shall
      not propose alternative dredging methods, in particular the use of suction pumps to deliver liquid
      sludge from the riverbed directly onto the disposal site, which may lead to many risks and adverse
      environmental and social impacts;
  -   All ESHS (environmental, social, health and safety) terms and conditions in the tender/contract
      documents including ESCOP, Employee Code of Conduct;
  -   Designate staff to be responsible for ESHS, including GRM.
  -   Develop a dredging schedule that avoids the flood season, especially from September to November
      when floods from rivers and the sea may coincide with high tides caused by storms on certain
      days.
  -   In coordination with the PMU and CSC, conduct consultation with the community and local
      authorities who are likely to be affected by dredging activities such as aquaculture, fishing or using
      water transport when preparing the contractor’s detailed construction schedule. Discuss and agree
      with local people on the time of water and seed intake into the pond. The contractor may agree to
      avoid or stop dredging at certain times of the day so that people can take water from the river if
      they wish.
  -   Coordinate with the PMU to notify the community and local authorities along each specific river
      section about the dredging plan at least two weeks before construction.
  -   Prepare Contractor’s DMMP and submit to Construction Supervisor for review and approval.
      Update C-DMMP every six months or when issues arise that need to be resolved.
  -   Prepare emergency response plans for contractors in case of incidents or waterway traffic
          accidents;
    -     Register transportation routes, equipment, and construction deadlines with relevant authorities to
          serve as a basis for inspection and supervision of dredging, transportation, and disposal activities.
    -     Operators of pontoon bridges, excavators, barges, and suction vessels must undergo extensive
          training and possess relevant certifications to be approved by the CSC.
    -     Provide adequate relevant PPE for workers to use and enforce their use, especially wearing life
          jackets when working underwater. Assign someone to monitor each shift to respond promptly in
          case of emergency;
    -     Provide training for workers on ESHS regulations before commencing actual contract work.
          Weekly ESHS briefings are required;
    -     All measures specified in the Water Traffic Control prepared as part of the approved Detailed
          Design shall be implemented prior to the commencement of dredging. Warning signs must be
          installed and maintained during construction, including buoys on the water surface and signs on
          the river bank according to the approved plan.
    -     Re-establish and demarcate the dredging corridor using buoys when the pontoon bridge and barges
          move to the next river segment for dredging, in order to minimize risks to waterway traffic safety—
          particularly collisions between dredging equipment and local fishing gear.
-       Signal lights are provided on the pontoon bridge, barges, and suction vessels.
    -     Conduct monitoring of disturbed water areas. Conduct daily water turbidity monitoring in the
          dredged area. Agree with CSC on water turbidity thresholds. Suspend dredging when turbidity is
          observed to reach thresholds or may be harmful to aquatic life until water turbidity is restored to
          acceptable levels. Limit dredging during high tides to reduce sediment and pollutant dispersal in
          the water.
    -     Oil and lubricants stored on barges and pontoons shall be kept to a minimum and contained in
          sealed, waterproof containers placed in covered areas. It is mandatory to ensure no oil leakage into
          the river. Materials contaminated with oil and lubricants shall be stored in secure containers for
          collection by licensed hazardous waste treatment facilities.
    -     Check and maintain equipment and machinery to ensure they are in good condition and renew
          their registration before they expire.
    -     No maintenance or overhaul of excavators is allowed on the construction site but must be carried
          out in a dedicated maintenance workshop.
    -     It is not permitted to overload dredged material onto barges, holds or any means of transport.
    -     Move pontoons, excavators, and barges to safe shelters before the flood season arrives. Cover and
          protect this construction equipment to avoid fuel leaks or being blown away due to storm winds.
    -     Safely remove all equipment and materials introduced into the river and river prior to completion
          of dredging work
    -     The Contractor shall provide riverside accommodation with adequate water supply, electricity,
          sanitation and basic facilities for the health and safety of the workers. Register the workers with
          the local authorities.
    -     Facilitate inspection by IESMC and relevant authorities.
    -     Prepare contractor logbook with records of ESHS implementation, and monthly ESHS
          implementation reports.
In addition, the contractor needs to:
  -       Develop specific construction methods for each river segment, tailored to the actual conditions of
          the river in terms of flow dynamics, sediment levels, tidal influences, and flood currents.
  -       Establish a construction schedule for each location considering: (i) water levels, flows, weather
          conditions; (ii) recommendations from local residents and authorities on water and surface water
          needs for aquaculture and fishing activities; and (iii) construction schedules for other packages
          sharing the same construction site, including transportation routes and storage areas. Minimize
          safety risks for workers and construction equipment: avoid adverse natural conditions such as
          storms, floods, and natural disasters.
  -       Work with the Project Owner and supervision consultant to communicate regularly with local
          authorities and communities during construction planning and throughout the implementation
          phase.
  -       The Contractor shall be responsible for repairing and compensating for damages caused by
          construction activities at the Contractor’s expense.
  -       Minimize disturbance to the ground and riverbed;
  -       When a storm is forecast, dredging and embankment construction activities will be restricted, and
          site protection measures will be implemented; all construction activities will be suspended during
          stormy weather;
  -       Operate dredging at low speed during periods that allow for sedimentation;
  -       Provide warning signs in hazardous areas, such as underwater currents, high erosion potential, or
          deep excavations;
  -       The Contractor shall excavate and retain existing vegetation to cover the surface of the dredged
          material upon completion at the impoundment.
Measures to mitigate specific impacts caused by dredging
Subprojects involving dredging activities will result in adverse impacts on aquatic life and water quality
degradation. To manage potential environmental and social impacts and health and safety risks during
dredging, including material handling, the contractor must comply with the following requirements:
Planning and approval
      •    The Contractor shall develop a Contractor’s Detailed Dredged Material Management Plan
           (CDMMP), based on the detailed design and DMMP of the project (Appendix 2). The C-DMMP
           includes the volume of dredged material, physical, biological and chemical characteristics of the
           dredged material; dredging methods and plans; transportation, disposal and pollution control
           procedures; and risk mitigation measures at the disposal site. This plan shall be approved by the
           Owner, updated as necessary, and monitored regularly by CSC during implementation.
      •    Establish construction schedule for each location, considering water level, flow, weather,
           people’s recommendations and progress of related bid packages.
      •    Avoid construction during flood season, high tides or adverse weather, especially at times when
           vessels anchor to avoid storms.
      •    Consider the cumulative risk from concurrent dredging activities at neighboring subprojects (if
           any) to adjust construction progress and methods.
Community consultation and notification
      •    Consultation with communities and local authorities affected by dredging activities.
      •    Announce dredging plans at least two weeks before commencement.
      •    Discuss with people about the time to get water and seeds into the pond, can adjust the dredging
       time accordingly.
   •   Work with the Project Owner and supervising consultant to communicate regularly with the
       community throughout the implementation process.
   •   Establish a local environmental and social grievance redress mechanism with a clear point of
       contact and defined resolution timeline.

Equipment and human resources
   •   Equipment operators must be properly trained and certified.
   •   Fully equipped with PPE, especially life jackets for workers working underwater.
   •   Assign a supervisor to each shift to respond when needed.
Traffic and waterway safety
   •   Comply with waterway traffic control measures according to approved detailed designs.
   •   Install warning signs, buoys and signal lights on construction equipment to ensure safety,
       especially at night.
   •   Clearly demarcate the dredging corridor when equipment moves to a different river segment.

Waste and incident management
   •   Limit dredging during high tide to reduce the dispersion of sludge and pollutants.
   •   Do not use the pump suction method to blow/stir the bottom layer, due to potential environmental
       and social risks.
   •   Do not overload the barge or transport vehicles with dredged materials.
   •   Oils and lubricants must be stored safely, in closed, covered containers, to prevent leakage into
       the aquatic environment.
   •   Prepare full equipment to respond to incidents, especially to prevent the spread of grease.
   •   Move equipment to a safe shelter before heavy rain, flood or storm. After completion, remove
       all equipment and materials from the job site.
Dredged material storage management
   •   The disposal site must be pre-identified and comply with approved construction specifications.
       It should avoid areas near sensitive receiving water bodies or any zones close to water sources.
       Drainage ditches will be constructed around the storage area to block wastewater from flowing
       out.
   •   Retaining walls are established around the disposal site if necessary;
   •   The retaining wall must be completed before the start of disposal and checked periodically during
       construction. The retaining wall must have a minimum height equal to the disposal elevation to
       ensure overflow prevention, erosion prevention and stability of dredged material.
   •   Conduct regular monitoring of the disposal site to ensure that materials are not washed away.
   •   Fully set up mitigation measures such as embankments, sluice gates, drainage ditches, warning
       signs, and cofferdams before transporting sludge to the landfill.
   •   The storage site must have a system to collect leachate and treat it before discharging it into the
       environment.
    •    Comply with the procedures for monitoring, transporting, storing and handling dredged materials
         according to C-DMMP and construction methods.
    •    If pipelines are used to transport slurry, warning signs must be placed along the pipeline route,
         along with traffic direction indicators for pedestrians and road users.
    •    Immediately address any leakage or overflow incidents before continuing storage operations, and
         coordinate with local authorities and communities when necessary.
    •    Update the disposal site operation log, recording the volume, time, and condition of the material.
Control of incidents in the disposal site
    •    Consider potential incidents such as erosion, flooding, subsidence, and natural disaster in the
         design of the disposal site.
    •    Establish a general incident control plan for the project, including incidents related to dredging,
         transportation and disposal.
    •    Establish an incident response team of at least 3 people.
    •    Set up a hotline or accessible communication channel to enable the community to easily report
         incidents promptly.

(viii)   Measures to minimize disruption to existing fishing port operations – Subproject 1,2
However, stakeholder consultations indicate that the local area has experience in coordinating port
upgrading and dredging activities while maintaining safe vessel operations and port functionality. Key
mitigation measures include:
  Avoidance
    •    Pre-construction inter-agency consultation: Coordinate with the waterway police, port
         management board, fisheries department and local community to understand port operations and
         coordinate construction accordingly.
    •    Information disclosure and risk communication: Post construction information, schedule,
         complaint mechanism; broadcast weather information via loudspeakers.
    •    Maintenance existing port operations: No construction or gathering of machinery or materials in
         the operating area.
    •    Construction outside peak hours: Avoid high traffic hours (1–4am and 6–9am).
  Minimization
    •    The storage area must be pre-determined in accordance with approved construction
         specifications. Areas near sensitive receiving waters, or areas near water sources, should be
         avoided. Trenches must be constructed around the disposal to contain wastewater.
    •    Retaining walls are installed around the disposal area if necessary.
    •    Retaining walls must be completed before commencing disposal activities and inspected
         regularly throughout the construction process. The walls must have a minimum height equal to
         the disposal elevation to prevent overflow, erosion, and ensure stability of the dredged material.
    •    Conduct regular monitoring of the disposal area to ensure that materials are not washed away.
    •    Strictly adhere to the approved Dredged Materials Management Plan (DMMP), which includes
         provisions for material transportation, storage, pollution control and safety at the material yard.
    •    Port access and security management: Control access to the construction area, especially zones
         adjacent to fishing vessel docking and cargo loading/unloading activities. Vessels or
       organizations that do not comply with port regulations are not permitted to enter the construction
       zone.
   •   Construction in sections, spatial separation: Construction in sections, with clear fences separating
       the construction area and the port’s operating area.
   •   Waterway traffic coordination: Coordinate with maritime agencies to inform about the situation
       of waterways, buoys, and wharves so as to ensure fishing vessels enter and exit the port safely
       during the construction period.
   •   Signs and identification signals. Install signs, flags, and lighting in moving and anchoring areas
       to help fishermen, traders, and residents identify the construction area, especially at night.
   •   Environmental incident monitoring and handling: Organize on-duty shifts, hang warning signals
       when there are storms or tropical depressions; handle on-site accidents and environmental
       pollution incidents if they occur in the port area.
  Restoration
   •   Post-construction restoration: Clean up, restore the site and completed locations before moving
       on to the next phase.

(ix) Measures to minimize impacts on waterway traffic – Subproject area 1,2
  Avoidance
   •   Conduct consultations and develop a safety and emergency response plan prior to construction:
          + Develop a plan to ensure maritime and inland waterway safety and submit it to the
          competent authority for approval.
          + Work in advance with local medical facilities to be ready to respond in case of an incident.
          + Establish an early warning system: Contact meteorological stations and local authorities to
          update weather, proactively adjust construction when there are storms, thunderstorms, and
          rough seas.
          + Equip all construction vessels with complete rescue gear: lifeboats, life buoys, signal lights,
          communication devices, and first aid kits.
          + Clearly assign responsibilities and conduct regular training sessions for workers on
          waterway traffic incident response procedures.
   •   Publicize construction information and safety warnings through mass media.
   •   Avoid construction during time frames with high vessel traffic density (1-4am and 6-9am).
   •   • Do not carry out construction during adverse weather conditions: suspend operations during
       thunderstorms, whirlwinds, fog, or rough seas to ensure safety.
  Minimization
   •   Fully comply with regulations on construction vehicles and waterway traffic.
          + Vehicles and boat must be registered, have license plates, and obtain operation licenses in
          accordance with regulations.
          + Operators must hold a valid boat driving license in accordance with regulations.
   •   Install warning system and limit construction area:
          + Provide life buoys, signal lights, and warning signs at the construction site, dredging area,
          and navigation channel.
          + Construction vessels shall operate only within the boundaries marked by buoys. Absolutely
          no movement outside the designated water area is allowed to prevent collisions and
          obstruction of waterway traffic.
          + Do not place equipment or materials outside the project area or in areas that potential a risk
          of obstructing waterway traffic.
          + Successive construction method: Minimize the occupied area of the river/sea surface at any
          given time.
   •   Safety and communication equipment:
          + Equip signal complete signal lights, lifeboats, lifebuoys, and fire-fighting equipment.
          + Use and regularly inspect VHF communication equipment operating 24/7 on channel 9.
   •   Compliance with construction and safety plans:
          + Regularly check communication equipment to ensure smooth operation.
          + Assign traffic guards throughout the entire construction process.
          + Move construction vehicles and machinery to safe locations in storm shelters during bad
          weather conditions, thunderstorms.
  Restoration
   •   Cleaning up and site reinstatement: After each construction phase, immediately remove
       equipment and clear the navigation channel.
   •   Update signaling system: Reinstall buoys and signs to ensure waterway traffic safety after
       construction.

(x) Measures to minimize impacts on aquaculture activities for the subproject 3->9
Temporary mitigation measures for aquaculture households are as follows:
  Avoidance
   •   Early notification and community consultation: The project owner coordinates with local
       authorities to inform the construction plan at least 2 weeks in advance and discuss with people
       about the water intake and seed stocking times, avoiding overlapping with peak construction
       time.
   •   Restrict water intake from the construction area: Aquaculture households should draw water
       from external river sources or other alternatives located far from the construction area.
  Minimization
   •   Successive construction in small sections (100–200m): Minimize the extent of impact and allow
       the aquatic environment to recover between phases.
   •   Use appropriate construction equipment: In terms of capacity and volume to limit stirring up
       the river bottom but still ensures construction progress.
   •   Install buoys to mark the construction area: Mark the construction area to warn of pollution
       risks and ensure waterway safety.
   •   Use silt curtains in dredging areas near water intake points: Enclose the construction area
       with silt curtains to limit the spread of turbidity and pollutants, helping to protect aquatic
       ecosystems and maintain water quality for aquaculture.
   •   Construct protective barriers around aquaculture water intake areas: Install temporary
       silt curtains around the farming zone to prevent sediment and pollutants from entering the
       intake point, thereby minimizing impacts on incoming water quality.
   •   Adjust farming season: Agree with local people on the time of water intake and seed stocking
       to avoid peak construction time.
   •   Water quality monitoring: Monitor turbidity, TSS and water environmental parameters at water
       intake areas serving aquaculture, apply appropriate measurement methods when necessary.
   •   Utilize existing water intake treatment processes of aquaculture households: Farming ponds
       apply filtration, sedimentation, disinfection, and water quality testing systems before introducing
       water into the ponds, helping to minimize risks from the water source.
  Restoration
   •   Restoring water channel after construction: Clean up, remove equipment, ensure stable flow after
       each dredging stage.
   •   Post-construction monitoring: Monitor water quality at the water intake points to ensure there
       are no lasting effects.
  Compensation
   •   Compensation and support payments if damage occurs: In case the impact cannot be avoided,
       the project will compensate households for loss of income due to disruption of farming activities.
   •   Participate in the livelihood restoration program: Severely affected households will be supported
       through the income restoration program according to the project's Resettlement Plan (if any).

(xi) Mitigation measures to reduce environmental and marine ecosystem impacts from dredging
     activities
To prevent impacts of the dredging activities on the environment and marine ecosystem, apart from
complying with the measures described in Chapter 5, the Project is required to follow the with the
following specific measures to prevent collisions between vessels within the port as well as clashes
between vessels and construction equipment, increasing the risk of vessel sink, oil and grease leakage
and other impacts affecting the environment and marine ecosystem:
  Avoidance
   •   Dredging activities must be carried out successively within the port. A soft barrier using buoys
       and warning signs must be established around the dredging area.
   •   Suspend dredging operations at night, during rough seas, high tides, or adverse weather
       conditions to allow vessels to seek safe shelter and to limit the spread of pollutants from the port
       to the surrounding marine protected area. During suspension periods, dredging equipment such
       as grab dredgers and barges must be relocated to a designated area along the port shoreline to
       avoid obstructing vessel movement. This area must also be equipped with warning signs and
       nighttime lighting.
   •   Conduct monitoring and surveillance of seawater quality, dredged materials, and biological
       characteristics in both the port area and surrounding environment to track the growth and
       development of aquatic species, thereby enabling appropriate preventive measures.
   •   Arrange the transportation route and disposal site reasonably.
  Minimization
   •   Use appropriate dredging equipment to minimize breaking up weathered rock and stirring up the
       bottom mud layer excessively.
   •   Dredged materials are transferred to barges immediately after being brought to the surface,
       without prolonged accumulation in the dock area.
   •   Do not overload the barge or transportation vehicle with materials, ensure safety and avoid
       spillage into the environment.
   •   Do not store oil and lubricants on barges or dredging machinery; oil-contaminated materials must
       be collected and treated by a licensed entity.
   •   Dredging equipment operators must be appropriately trained and certified, supervised and
       certified by a supervising consultant (CSC).
   •   Provide adequate PPE for workers, especially life jackets when working on water; assign people
       to monitor each shift for timely rescue.
   Emergency Preparedness
   •   Establish a response plan for waterway traffic incidents, including collisions, engine failures,
       capsizing, or oil spills. All response plans must be approved by the competent authorities.
   •   Suspend construction and relocate equipment to a safe shoreline area during adverse weather
       conditions; this area must be equipped with warning signs and nighttime lighting.
   •   Regularly train crew on rescue, evacuation and underwater incident handling procedures.
   •   Notify and coordinate with border guards, fisheries surveillance, coast guard, waterway traffic
       and port authorities to provide timely support when incidents occur.
   •   Establish an emergency contact point between the transportation vessel, project owner,
       contractor and authorities. Ability to quickly handle unusual situations.
  Restoration
   •   Clean up and collect spilled materials and waste after each construction phase, ensuring no
       residuals or spills remain that could affect water quality.
   •   Restore water quality and benthic environment by monitoring and adjusting construction
       progress if prolonged signs of degradation are detected.
   •   Report periodic environmental monitoring results to the authorities and the Marine Protected
       Area Management Board.
6.4 Preparation of C-DMMP by Contractor
After signing the contract, must prepare and submit the C-DMMP along with the construction plan and
methods. These documents must include measures and actions to mitigate Environmental and Social
impacts, considering the following issues:
   -   Construction methods differ between dredging riverbeds and riverside excavations as well as
       irrigation canal areas, describing the equipment and labor used.
   -   The dredging process, transportation and transportation route to the disposal sites of
       excavated/dredged materials are disposed of with specific and detailed plans.
   -   Allocate excavated/dredged materials to each disposal site based on additional information about
       sediment quality from rivers/canals to the disposal areas, which will be available during the
       detailed design phase.
6.5 Requirements for disposal activities and disposal site management
Waste Treatment and Management Process
   -    The Contractor shall cancel all provisions in their C-DMMP regarding the connection of barges that
        may need to anchor at a certain distance from the disposal sites (typically several hundred meters,
        although it is technically feasible to transfer materials from barges to disposal sites over distances of
        up to two kilometers; (the actual distance from where barges can be moored to each disposal site
        will be determined during the detailed design process).
   -    If pipelines are installed to connect barges to disposal sites (through which slurry will be
        pumped), the C-DMMP must include mitigation measures such as warning signs along the
        pipeline route.
   -    All approved mitigation measures, including embankments, discharge outlets, drainage ditches,
        warning signs, and fences if necessary for disposal sites must be established prior to the
        commencement of material handling.
   -    The pumping of materials into designated cells and the drainage process shall follow the
        procedures and sequences specified in the C-DMMP and/or the construction plan.
   -    Conduct regular monitoring at disposal sites. Any leakage or overflow incidents must be
        promptly addressed before resuming material handling operations.
Incident control
   -    The design of disposal site must take account into incidents such as erosion, flooding, subsidence,
        natural disasters, storms and floods.
   -    Establish a general incident control plan for subprojects, including incidents related to dredging,
        transportation and disposal at selected sites.
   -    Establish a Safety Committee or incident response team of at least 3 people to be ready to respond
        when an incident occurs.
   -    Establish an accessible hotline or communication channel between stakeholders and the
        community to enable prompt incident reporting.
   7. Environmental and safety monitoring and supervision plan
        7.1 Environmental Monitoring Plan
Based on Articles 111 and 112 of the Environmental Protection Law No. 72/2020/QH14, the project is
not required to implement a periodic environmental monitoring program during the project construction
and operation phase.
However, due to the specific nature of the dredging activities with a large dredging volume, the project
will carry out monitoring of the quality of dredged materials (sediment quality). The construction
contractor will be required to conduct sampling and quality monitoring of the proposed dredged
materials as follows:
     + Location: collect 02 samples at each subproject area with dredging activities (subproject 1-
Hoang Truong fishing port and subproject 2 - Lach Truong fishing port) and 01 sample at each remaining
subproject (subproject 3-> subproject 9).
       + Monitoring parameters: Salinity (except subproject 3-> subproject 9), As, Hg, Cd, Cu, Pb, Zn.
      + Monitoring frequency: 06 months/time (2 times during the construction period for subproject
1,2 and 1 time during the construction period for subproject 3 -> subproject 9).
       + Comparison standard: QCVN 43: 2017/BTNMT.
        7.2 Compliance Monitoring Plan and Reporting Requirements
The Contractor shall be responsible for complying with the requirements and taking the mitigation
measures stated in the ESIA, ESMP and DMMP and shall prepare monthly ESHS report on the
implementation situtation. The Environmental, Social, Health and Safety (ESHS) compliance report,
following the Project’s standard template, will be developed during implementation in alignment with
the requirements stated in the bidding documents, and submitted to the Project Owner/Supervision
Consultant for review and approval. The key aspects to be reported by the Contractor include the
following:
          -   Scope of work under the contract package
          -   Status of C-ESMP and C-DMMP and related environmental documents submitted to the
              Project ơner
          -   Monthly construction progress, construction location and volume of work completed
          -   Mitigation measures implemented during the month
          -   Emerging issues and outstanding problems
          -   Environmental, social, safety and health training activities attended
          -   Inspection and monitoring activities of stakeholders
          -   The Construction Supervision Consultant shall be responsible for daily monitoring of the
              Contractor’s compliance and preparing monthly supervision reports. The Independent
              Supervision Consultant shall conduct quarterly monitoring during the construction phase.
